544	/-- **Auxiliary subsingleton lemma for `HModule_flasque_eq_zero`**
545	(iter-193 Lane H prover dispatch).
546	
547	For a flasque sheaf `F` of `kbar`-modules on `X` and any `n : ℕ`, the
548	`(n+1)`-th derived global-sections cohomology `HModule kbar F (n+1)` is
549	subsingleton (i.e., has at most one element). This statement is the
550	strong-induction-on-`n` carrier of the main theorem
551	`HModule_flasque_eq_zero`: the `F`-generalised quantifier inside the
552	`induction` block lets the inductive step apply the IH on the flasque
553	quotient sheaf at one lower degree. Once subsingleton is established,
554	`Module.finrank_zero_of_subsingleton` gives the `finrank = 0` conclusion
555	in the main statement. -/
556	private theorem Scheme.HModule_flasque_subsingleton_aux
557	    {kbar : Type u} [Field kbar] {X : TopCat.{u}}
558	    [HasSheafify (Opens.grothendieckTopology X) (ModuleCat.{u} kbar)]
559	    [HasExt (Sheaf (Opens.grothendieckTopology X) (ModuleCat.{u} kbar))]
560	    (n : ℕ) :
561	    ∀ {F : Sheaf (Opens.grothendieckTopology X) (ModuleCat.{u} kbar)},
562	      Scheme.IsFlasque F → Subsingleton (Scheme.HModule kbar F (n + 1)) := by
563	  induction n with
564	  | zero =>
565	    intro F hF
566	    refine ⟨fun x y => ?_⟩
567	    -- i = 1 case: use ext_one_eq_zero_of_hom_surjective_of_injective on the
568	    -- canonical injective SES `0 → F → Injective.under F → cokernel → 0`.
569	    have hSES := Scheme.injectiveSES_shortExact F
570	    -- Injective.under F is injective by Mathlib's instance.
571	    have hI_inj : Injective (Scheme.injectiveSES F).X₂ := Injective.injective_under F
572	    -- 1.16(b) at U = ⊤ gives section-level surjectivity, which we lift to
573	    -- Hom-from-(constantSheaf k)-surjectivity via the explicit `constantSheaf
574	    -- ⊣ (sheafSections at ⊤)` adjunction (`constantSheafAdj` with terminal
575	    -- `⊤ ∈ Opens X` via `Preorder.isTerminalTop`) and the rank-1 free
576	    -- structure of `ModuleCat.of kbar kbar` (a kbar-linear hom from `kbar`
577	    -- is determined by its image of `1`, with the inverse map
578	    -- `LinearMap.toSpanSingleton kbar _ s` for any chosen lift `s`).
579	    have hsurj : Function.Surjective
580	        (fun (f : (constantSheaf (Opens.grothendieckTopology X)
581	            (ModuleCat.{u} kbar)).obj (ModuleCat.of kbar kbar) ⟶
582	            (Scheme.injectiveSES F).X₂) => f ≫ (Scheme.injectiveSES F).g) := by
583	      intro g
584	      -- Set up the adjunction at the terminal `⊤ ∈ Opens X`.
585	      let hT : Limits.IsTerminal (⊤ : TopologicalSpace.Opens X) :=
586	        Preorder.isTerminalTop (TopologicalSpace.Opens X)
587	      let adj := constantSheafAdj (Opens.grothendieckTopology X)
588	        (ModuleCat.{u} kbar) hT
589	      -- 1.16(b) at U = ⊤ gives section-level surjectivity.
590	      have h_b_top : Function.Surjective
591	          (((Scheme.injectiveSES F).g.hom.app
592	            (Opposite.op (⊤ : TopologicalSpace.Opens X))).hom) :=
593	        Scheme.IsFlasque.shortExact_app_surjective hSES hF ⊤
594	      -- Convert `g` to a section-level morphism via `adj.homEquiv`.
595	      let g_sec : ModuleCat.of kbar kbar ⟶
596	          (Scheme.injectiveSES F).X₃.val.obj
597	            (Opposite.op (⊤ : TopologicalSpace.Opens X)) :=
598	        adj.homEquiv _ _ g
599	      -- Pick the value of `g_sec` at `1 : kbar`.
600	      let s₃ : (Scheme.injectiveSES F).X₃.val.obj
601	          (Opposite.op (⊤ : TopologicalSpace.Opens X)) := g_sec.hom 1
602	      -- Apply 1.16(b) at ⊤ to get a lift in `X₂`.
603	      obtain ⟨s₂, hs₂⟩ := h_b_top s₃
604	      -- Construct the section-level lift `f_sec : kbar ⟶ X₂.val.obj (op ⊤)`.
605	      let f_sec : ModuleCat.of kbar kbar ⟶
606	          (Scheme.injectiveSES F).X₂.val.obj
607	            (Opposite.op (⊤ : TopologicalSpace.Opens X)) :=
608	        ModuleCat.ofHom (LinearMap.toSpanSingleton kbar _ s₂)
609	      -- Lift `f_sec` back to a sheaf-level morphism via `adj.homEquiv.symm`.
610	      refine ⟨(adj.homEquiv _ _).symm f_sec, ?_⟩
611	      -- Verify `(adj.homEquiv.symm f_sec) ≫ S.g = g` by applying `adj.homEquiv`.
612	      apply (adj.homEquiv _ _).injective
613	      rw [Adjunction.homEquiv_naturality_right, Equiv.apply_symm_apply]
614	      change f_sec ≫ ((sheafSections (Opens.grothendieckTopology X)
615	        (ModuleCat.{u} kbar)).obj (Opposite.op ⊤)).map (Scheme.injectiveSES F).g = g_sec
616	      -- Verify the section-level equation at the linear map level.
617	      -- Two `kbar`-linear maps from `kbar` agree iff they agree at `1`.
618	      apply ModuleCat.hom_ext
619	      ext
620	      -- Goal (post `LinearMap.ext_ring`): both sides applied at `1`.
621	      change ((Scheme.injectiveSES F).g.hom.app (Opposite.op ⊤)).hom
622	          ((LinearMap.toSpanSingleton kbar _ s₂) 1) = g_sec.hom 1
623	      rw [LinearMap.toSpanSingleton_apply_one]
624	      exact hs₂
625	    rw [ext_one_eq_zero_of_hom_surjective_of_injective _ hSES hsurj x,
626	        ext_one_eq_zero_of_hom_surjective_of_injective _ hSES hsurj y]
627	  | succ m ih =>
628	    intro F hF
629	    refine ⟨fun x y => ?_⟩
630	    -- i = m + 2, m + 1 ≥ 1. Use the LES iso via the canonical injective SES,
631	    -- with the flasque quotient G inheriting flasqueness from F + Injective.under F
632	    -- (Hartshorne III Lemma 2.4 + II Ex 1.16(c)). Reduce to IH at degree m + 1.
633	    have hSES := Scheme.injectiveSES_shortExact F
634	    have hI_inj : Injective (Scheme.injectiveSES F).X₂ := Injective.injective_under F
635	    have hI_flasque : Scheme.IsFlasque (Scheme.injectiveSES F).X₂ :=
636	      Scheme.IsFlasque.injective_flasque _
637	    have h_b : ∀ U, Function.Surjective
638	        (((Scheme.injectiveSES F).g.hom.app (Opposite.op U)).hom) :=
639	      fun U => Scheme.IsFlasque.shortExact_app_surjective hSES hF U
640	    have hG_flasque : Scheme.IsFlasque (Scheme.injectiveSES F).X₃ :=
641	      Scheme.IsFlasque.cokernel_of_shortExact_flasque_flasque hI_flasque h_b
642	    have hG_sub : Subsingleton (Scheme.HModule kbar (Scheme.injectiveSES F).X₃ (m + 1)) :=
643	      ih hG_flasque
644	    have hX₃_zero :
645	        ∀ y : Abelian.Ext
646	            ((constantSheaf (Opens.grothendieckTopology X)
647	              (ModuleCat.{u} kbar)).obj (ModuleCat.of kbar kbar))
648	            (Scheme.injectiveSES F).X₃ (m + 1), y = 0 :=
649	      fun y => Subsingleton.elim (α := Scheme.HModule kbar (Scheme.injectiveSES F).X₃ (m + 1)) y 0
650	    have h_n₀ : 1 ≤ m + 1 := Nat.succ_le_succ (Nat.zero_le _)
651	    rw [ext_succ_eq_zero_of_injective_of_lower_zero _ hSES h_n₀ hX₃_zero x,
652	        ext_succ_eq_zero_of_injective_of_lower_zero _ hSES h_n₀ hX₃_zero y]
653	
654	/-- **Flasque sheaves have vanishing higher cohomology** (Hartshorne
655	III.2, Proposition 2.5).
656	
657	For a topological space `X` and a flasque sheaf `F` of `kbar`-modules on
658	`X`, the `kbar`-flavoured derived global-sections cohomology
659	`HModule kbar F i` is the zero `kbar`-module for every `i ≥ 1`. In
660	particular, `dim_{kbar} HModule kbar F 1 = 0`.
661	
662	The proof structure mirrors Hartshorne III §2 verbatim: embed `F` into
663	an injective `I` of the abelian sheaf category, form the quotient short
664	exact sequence `0 → F → I → G → 0` (Mathlib: `Scheme.injectiveSES`,
665	axiom-clean), observe that `G` inherits flasqueness from `F` and `I`
666	(the latter by Hartshorne III Lemma 2.4), read off the short exact
667	sequence on global sections from the flasque input
668	(Hartshorne II Ex. 1.16(b)), and apply the long exact sequence
669	(`Abelian.Ext.covariant_sequence_exact₁`) to get `HModule kbar F 1 = 0`
670	and a reduction `HModule kbar F i ≅ HModule kbar G (i - 1)` for `i ≥ 2`;
671	iteration closes the higher-degree cases.
672	
673	**iter-193 Lane H prover dispatch** — body fully closed structurally;
674	sorries are isolated to two named substrate helpers
675	(`shortExact_app_surjective` and `injective_flasque`, both Tier-3).
676	The proof decomposes cleanly into the two cases via the auxiliary
677	subsingleton lemma `Scheme.HModule_flasque_subsingleton_aux`:
678	* `i = 1`: invoke `ext_one_eq_zero_of_hom_surjective_of_injective` on the
679	  `injectiveSES F` short-exact data, supplying the Hartshorne II Ex 1.16(b)
680	  input. The Hom-from-constant-sheaf surjectivity is derived axiom-clean
681	  from `IsFlasque.shortExact_app_surjective` at `U = ⊤` via the explicit
682	  `constantSheafAdj` (at terminal `⊤ ∈ Opens X` via
683	  `Preorder.isTerminalTop`) + the rank-1 free structure of
684	  `ModuleCat.of kbar kbar` (`LinearMap.toSpanSingleton` lift).
685	* `i ≥ 2`: invoke `ext_succ_eq_zero_of_injective_of_lower_zero` on
686	  `injectiveSES F`, supplying `Subsingleton (HModule kbar G (i-1))` via the
687	  IH applied to the flasque quotient `G` (the latter via
688	  `IsFlasque.cokernel_of_shortExact_flasque_flasque` and
689	  `IsFlasque.injective_flasque`).
690	
691	Blueprint reference: `thm:H1_vanishing_flasque`. -/
692	theorem Scheme.HModule_flasque_eq_zero
693	    {kbar : Type u} [Field kbar] {X : TopCat.{u}}
694	    [HasSheafify (Opens.grothendieckTopology X) (ModuleCat.{u} kbar)]
695	    [HasExt (Sheaf (Opens.grothendieckTopology X) (ModuleCat.{u} kbar))]
696	    {F : Sheaf (Opens.grothendieckTopology X) (ModuleCat.{u} kbar)}
697	    (hF : Scheme.IsFlasque F) (i : ℕ) (hi : 1 ≤ i) :
698	    Module.finrank kbar (Scheme.HModule kbar F i) = 0 := by
699	  obtain ⟨n, rfl⟩ : ∃ n, n + 1 = i := ⟨i - 1, by omega⟩

700	  have hsub : Subsingleton (Scheme.HModule kbar F (n + 1)) :=
701	    Scheme.HModule_flasque_subsingleton_aux n hF
702	  exact Module.finrank_zero_of_subsingleton
703	
704	/-! ## §2. Skyscraper sheaves are flasque -/
705	
706	/-- **Skyscraper sheaf as pushforward of a constant sheaf** (Hartshorne
707	II.1, Exercise 1.17).
708	
709	The closed-point skyscraper sheaf `skyscraperSheaf P A` on a topological
710	space `X` is naturally isomorphic to the pushforward, along the constant
711	map `PUnit → X` at `P`, of the constant sheaf with value `A` on
712	`PUnit`. The constant sheaf on `PUnit` is itself isomorphic to the
713	skyscraper sheaf at `PUnit.unit`, so this is the sheaf-level
714	counterpart of Mathlib's presheaf-level
715	`skyscraperPresheaf_eq_pushforward` (Mathlib snapshot `b80f227`
716	`Topology/Sheaves/Skyscraper.lean`).
717	
718	For our usage on a curve, `PUnit` plays the role of `closure {P}`
719	(which on a Noetherian space with `P` closed is a singleton, hence
720	homeomorphic to `PUnit`).
721	
722	**iter-191 Lane H file-skeleton** — Tier-3 honest typed sorry; closure
723	is iter-192+. Blueprint reference:
724	`lem:skyscraperSheaf_eq_pushforward`. -/
725	theorem Scheme.skyscraperSheaf_eq_pushforward_const
726	    (kbar : Type u) [Field kbar]
727	    {X : TopCat.{u}} (P : X)
728	    [∀ U : TopologicalSpace.Opens X, Decidable (P ∈ U)]
729	    [∀ U : TopologicalSpace.Opens (TopCat.of PUnit.{u + 1}),
730	      Decidable (PUnit.unit ∈ U)]
731	    (A : ModuleCat.{u} kbar) :
732	    Nonempty
733	      (skyscraperSheaf (C := ModuleCat.{u} kbar) P A ≅
734	        (TopCat.Sheaf.pushforward (ModuleCat.{u} kbar)
735	            (TopCat.ofHom (ContinuousMap.const (TopCat.of PUnit.{u + 1}) P))).obj
736	          ((constantSheaf
737	              (Opens.grothendieckTopology (TopCat.of PUnit.{u + 1}))
738	              (ModuleCat.{u} kbar)).obj A)) := by
739	  sorry
740	
741	/-- **Closure of the support point of a `PrimeDivisor` is irreducible**.
742	
743	For any scheme `X` and any `P : X.PrimeDivisor`, the topological
744	closure of the singleton `{P.point}` is an irreducible subset of `X`.
745	This is a project-bespoke ancillary that holds in full generality: the
746	closure of any irreducible set in a topological space is irreducible,
747	and a singleton `{x}` is irreducible (it is nonempty and contains no
748	proper non-empty closed subsets).
749	
750	**iter-191 Lane H prover dispatch** — closed via
751	`isIrreducible_singleton.closure`. Blueprint reference:
752	`lem:closedPoint_closure_irreducible`. -/
753	theorem Scheme.PrimeDivisor.closure_isIrreducible
754	    {X : Scheme.{u}} (P : X.PrimeDivisor) :
755	    IsIrreducible (closure ({P.point} : Set X)) :=
756	  isIrreducible_singleton.closure
757	
758	/-- **The closed-point skyscraper sheaf is flasque**.
759	
760	For a smooth proper geometrically irreducible curve `C / kbar` and a
761	prime divisor `P : C.left.PrimeDivisor` (equivalently, a closed point
762	on the curve), the closed-point skyscraper sheaf
763	`skyscraperSheaf P.point (ModuleCat.of kbar kbar)` is flasque as a
764	sheaf of `kbar`-modules on the underlying topological space of `C`.
765	
766	The proof originally was planned to compose the four lemma-blocks of this
767	chapter (`skyscraperSheaf_eq_pushforward_const`,
768	`PrimeDivisor.closure_isIrreducible`, `IsFlasque.constant_of_irreducible`,
769	`IsFlasque.pushforward`). The iter-191 prover dispatch takes a more direct
770	route: unfold `skyscraperSheaf.val = skyscraperPresheaf`, then on the
771	`p ∈ V` branch the restriction map is an `eqToHom` (hence iso, hence
772	surjective in `ConcreteCategory`); on the `p ∉ V` branch the codomain is
773	the terminal object of `ModuleCat kbar`, which is the zero object, hence
774	`Subsingleton` on the underlying type. Either branch closes the
775	`Function.Surjective` obligation. This bypasses (3) and (5).
776	
777	**iter-191 Lane H prover dispatch** — closed directly via
778	`skyscraperPresheaf_map`. Blueprint reference:
779	`lem:skyscraperSheaf_isFlasque`. -/
780	theorem Scheme.skyscraperSheaf_isFlasque
781	    {kbar : Type u} [Field kbar] [IsAlgClosed kbar]
782	    (C : Over (Spec (.of kbar))) [IsProper C.hom]
783	    [SmoothOfRelativeDimension 1 C.hom]
784	    [GeometricallyIrreducible C.hom] [IsIntegral C.left]
785	    (P : C.left.PrimeDivisor)
786	    [∀ U : TopologicalSpace.Opens C.left, Decidable (P.point ∈ U)] :
787	    Scheme.IsFlasque
788	      (skyscraperSheaf (C := ModuleCat.{u} kbar) P.point
789	        (ModuleCat.of kbar kbar)) := by
790	  intro U V h
791	  change Function.Surjective
792	    (((skyscraperPresheaf P.point (ModuleCat.of kbar kbar)).map
793	      (homOfLE h).op).hom)
794	  by_cases hV : P.point ∈ V
795	  · -- restriction is `eqToHom`, hence an iso, hence surjective
796	    have heq : (skyscraperPresheaf P.point (ModuleCat.of kbar kbar)).obj
797	        (Opposite.op U) =
798	        (skyscraperPresheaf P.point (ModuleCat.of kbar kbar)).obj
799	        (Opposite.op V) := by
800	      simp [skyscraperPresheaf, h hV, hV]
801	    have hmap := skyscraperPresheaf_map P.point (ModuleCat.of kbar kbar)
802	      (i := (homOfLE h).op)
803	    rw [dif_pos hV] at hmap
804	    rw [hmap]
805	    have : IsIso (eqToHom heq) := inferInstance
806	    exact (ConcreteCategory.bijective_of_isIso (eqToHom heq)).2
807	  · -- codomain is zero (terminal in ModuleCat), so surjective trivially
808	    have hzero : Limits.IsZero
809	        ((skyscraperPresheaf P.point (ModuleCat.of kbar kbar)).obj
810	          (Opposite.op V)) := by
811	      simp [skyscraperPresheaf, hV]
812	      exact (terminalIsTerminal).isZero
813	    have : Subsingleton ((skyscraperPresheaf P.point
814	        (ModuleCat.of kbar kbar)).obj (Opposite.op V)) :=
815	      ModuleCat.subsingleton_of_isZero hzero
816	    intro y; exact ⟨0, Subsingleton.elim _ _⟩
817	
818	/-! ## §3. The closed-point skyscraper sheaf has vanishing `H¹` -/
819	
820	/-- **`H¹` of the closed-point skyscraper sheaf vanishes** (Hartshorne
821	III.2.5 applied to the flasque skyscraper).
822	
823	For a smooth proper geometrically irreducible curve `C / kbar` and a
824	prime divisor `P : C.left.PrimeDivisor` (a closed point on the curve),
825	`dim_{kbar} H¹(C, skyscraperSheaf P (ModuleCat.of kbar kbar)) = 0`.

833	`HModule_flasque_eq_zero` (still typed-`sorry` pending iter-192+
834	Hartshorne III.2.5 closure) and `skyscraperSheaf_isFlasque` (now closed

840	below. Blueprint reference:
841	`lem:H1_skyscraperSheaf_finrank_eq_zero_main`. -/
842	theorem Scheme.H1_skyscraperSheaf_finrank_eq_zero
843	    {kbar : Type u} [Field kbar] [IsAlgClosed kbar]
844	    (C : Over (Spec (.of kbar))) [IsProper C.hom]
845	    [SmoothOfRelativeDimension 1 C.hom]
846	    [GeometricallyIrreducible C.hom] [IsIntegral C.left]
847	    (P : C.left.PrimeDivisor)
848	    [∀ U : TopologicalSpace.Opens C.left, Decidable (P.point ∈ U)] :
849	    Module.finrank kbar
850	        (Scheme.HModule kbar
851	          (skyscraperSheaf (C := ModuleCat.{u} kbar) P.point
852	            (ModuleCat.of kbar kbar)) 1) = 0 :=

2	
3	\chapter{Vanishing of \(H^1\) for skyscraper sheaves on a curve (RR.2.H\(^1\))}
4	\label{chap:RR_H1Vanishing}
5	
6	% SOURCE: Hartshorne, \emph{Algebraic Geometry}, II.1, Exercise~1.16
7	% (flasque sheaves), Exercise~1.17 (skyscraper sheaves); III.2,
8	% Proposition~2.5 (flasque sheaves are acyclic) (read from
9	% references/hartshorne-algebraic-geometry.pdf, PDF pages 67--68 and
10	% 225). All verbatim quotes pasted in the declaration blocks below were
11	% copied character-by-character from the rendered page images of the
12	% scanned PDF (no text layer is present).
13	
14	\section{Setup and motivation}
15	
16	This chapter is the project-side build of the
17	\texttt{RR.2.H\(^1\)} sub-phase, committed in iter-188 of the strategy
18	revision: \emph{the first cohomology of a closed-point skyscraper sheaf
19	on a smooth proper curve vanishes}. Concretely, the chapter pins the
20	identity
21	\[
22	  \dim_{\bar k}\, H^1\bigl(C,\, \mathtt{skyscraperSheaf}\;P\;
23	    (\Module.\mathtt{of}\;\bar k\;\bar k)\bigr) \;=\; 0
24	\]
25	on a smooth proper geometrically irreducible curve \(C / \bar k\), where
26	\(P \in C\) is a closed point and the cohomology is computed through the
27	project's \(\Module \bar k\)-flavoured \(\Ext\)-cohomology pipeline
28	\(\HModule \bar k\) (chapter~REF, sibling
29	\texttt{Cohomology\_StructureSheafModuleK.tex}).
30	
31	\paragraph{Why a dedicated chapter.} Mathlib (snapshot
32	\texttt{b80f227}) does \emph{not} ship a flasque-cohomology vanishing
33	lemma at the \(\Ext\)-cohomology level used by
34	\(\HModule \bar k\), nor a Serre-duality theorem for curves. The
35	genus-\(0\) critical path nevertheless needs the skyscraper
36	\(H^1\)-vanishing twice: once inside the Hartshorne~IV.1.3
37	\(\chi\)-additivity assembly
38	(\cref{chap:RiemannRoch_RRFormula}, the parent lemma
39	\texttt{eulerCharacteristic\_skyscraperSheaf}), and once inside the
40	\texttt{RR.3} chain that produces a non-constant section
41	(\cref{chap:RiemannRoch_OCofP}, the downstream consumer
42	\texttt{h1\_vanishing\_genusZero}). The present chapter unifies the
43	substrate behind both consumers into a single short, self-contained
44	project-side build.
45	
46	\paragraph{Strategy of the chapter.} The argument is the classical
47	Hartshorne~III~\S2 one: an injective resolution of a flasque sheaf
48	yields a flasque quotient at each stage (since both the injective object
49	and the original sheaf are flasque), and the global-sections functor is
50	exact on a short exact sequence of flasque sheaves. Iterating, every
51	derived functor of \(\Gamma\) vanishes on a flasque input in positive
52	degree. The chapter then specialises to the closed-point skyscraper
53	sheaf, which is flasque by inspection (it is the pushforward, along the
54	inclusion of a one-point closed subspace, of the constant sheaf on an
55	irreducible base — and pushforwards of flasque sheaves are flasque).
56	
57	\paragraph{Standing hypotheses.} Throughout this chapter, \(\bar k\) is
58	an algebraically closed field and \(C\) is a smooth proper geometrically
59	irreducible curve over \(\bar k\). The Lean signatures (planned location
60	\texttt{AlgebraicJacobian/RiemannRoch/H1Vanishing.lean}) carry the same
61	typeclass package as the rest of the project's curve API
62	(\(\mathtt{IsProper}\), \(\mathtt{SmoothOfRelativeDimension}\, 1\),
63	\(\mathtt{GeometricallyIrreducible}\), \(\mathtt{IsIntegral}\)). The
64	flasque-vanishing lemma of \S2 below is stated more generally for a
65	noetherian topological space (the natural generality of
66	Hartshorne~III.2.5); the curve specialisation is read off by instance
67	synthesis on the underlying space \(C.\mathtt{left.toTopCat}\).
68	
69	\section{Flasque sheaves and their cohomology vanishing}
70	\label{sec:H1Vanishing_flasque}
71	
72	\begin{definition}
73	
74	\leanok
75	[Flasque sheaf]
76	  \label{def:isFlasque_sheaf}
77	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.IsFlasque}
78	  % SOURCE: [Hartshorne], II.1, Exercise~1.16 (definition of flasque
79	  % sheaf, opening paragraph) (read from
80	  % references/hartshorne-algebraic-geometry.pdf, PDF page 67); III.2,
81	  % paragraph preceding Lemma~2.4 (Hartshorne's own recall of the
82	  % definition) (PDF page 224).
83	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Flasque Sheaves. A sheaf \(\mathscr{F}\) on a
84	  % topological space \(X\) is \emph{flasque} if for every inclusion
85	  % \(V \subseteq U\) of open sets, the restriction map
86	  % \(\mathscr{F}(U) \to \mathscr{F}(V)\) is surjective."
87	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Recall (II, Ex. 1.16) that a sheaf \(\mathscr{F}\) on
88	  % a topological space \(X\) is \emph{flasque} if for every inclusion
89	  % of open sets \(V \subseteq U\), the restriction map
90	  % \(\mathscr{F}(U) \to \mathscr{F}(V)\) is surjective."
91	  \textit{Source: Hartshorne, II.1, Exercise~1.16 (definition); recalled
92	  III.2 preceding Lemma~2.4.}
93	  Let \((X, \mathcal O_X)\) be a ringed space and let \(\mathcal F\) be a
94	  sheaf of \(\mathcal O_X\)-modules on \(X\). We say that \(\mathcal F\)
95	  is \emph{flasque} if, for every inclusion of open sets
96	  \(V \subseteq U\) in \(X\), the restriction map
97	  \(\mathcal F(U) \to \mathcal F(V)\) is surjective.
98	
99	  In the project's \(\Module \bar k\)-flavoured setup the predicate is
100	  applied to a sheaf
101	  \(\mathcal F : \mathrm{Sheaf}\;J\;(\Module \bar k)\), where \(J\) is the
102	  Grothendieck topology of opens on the underlying topological space of
103	  the curve \(C\). The surjectivity condition is read on the underlying
104	  presheaf of \(\bar k\)-modules; the project predicate
105	  \texttt{Scheme.IsFlasque} packages exactly this condition.
106	
107	  \paragraph{Lean signature scope.} The Lean predicate
108	  \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.IsFlasque} takes a sheaf
109	  \(\mathcal F : \mathrm{Sheaf}\;(\Opens.\mathtt{grothendieckTopology}\;X)\;
110	  (\Module \bar k)\) and asserts that for every pair \(V \le U\) of opens
111	  of \(X\), the restriction morphism
112	  \((\mathcal F.\mathtt{val.map}\;(\mathrm{leOp}\;V\;U)) :
113	  \mathcal F.\mathtt{val.obj}\;(\mathrm{op}\;U) \to
114	  \mathcal F.\mathtt{val.obj}\;(\mathrm{op}\;V)\) is surjective as a map of
115	  \(\bar k\)-modules.
116	\end{definition}
117	
118	\begin{lemma}
119	
120	\leanok
121	[Pushforward of a flasque sheaf is flasque]
122	  \label{lem:isFlasque_pushforward}
123	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.IsFlasque.pushforward}
124	  \uses{def:isFlasque_sheaf}
125	  % SOURCE: [Hartshorne], II.1, Exercise~1.16(d) (read from
126	  % references/hartshorne-algebraic-geometry.pdf, PDF page 67).
127	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "If \(f: X \to Y\) is a continuous map, and if
128	  % \(\mathscr{F}\) is a flasque sheaf on \(X\), then \(f_*\mathscr{F}\)
129	  % is a flasque sheaf on \(Y\)."
130	  \textit{Source: Hartshorne, II.1, Exercise~1.16(d).}
131	  Let \(f \colon X \to Y\) be a continuous map of topological spaces and
132	  let \(\mathcal F\) be a flasque sheaf on \(X\). Then the pushforward
133	  sheaf \(f_*\mathcal F\) is flasque on \(Y\).
134	\end{lemma}
135	
136	\begin{proof}
137	  \uses{def:isFlasque_sheaf}
138	  For an inclusion of opens \(V \subseteq U\) in \(Y\), the restriction
139	  map of \(f_*\mathcal F\) is by definition the restriction map of
140	  \(\mathcal F\) on the open sets \(f^{-1}(V) \subseteq f^{-1}(U)\) of
141	  \(X\):
142	  \[
143	    (f_*\mathcal F)(U) \;=\; \mathcal F(f^{-1}(U))
144	    \;\xrightarrow{\;\mathcal F(f^{-1}(V) \subseteq f^{-1}(U))\;}\;
145	    \mathcal F(f^{-1}(V)) \;=\; (f_*\mathcal F)(V).
146	  \]
147	  Since \(\mathcal F\) is flasque on \(X\), the right-hand restriction is
148	  surjective, hence so is the left-hand one. Therefore \(f_*\mathcal F\)
149	  is flasque on \(Y\).
150	\end{proof}
151	
152	\begin{lemma}
153	
154	\leanok
155	[Constant sheaf on an irreducible space is flasque]
156	  \label{lem:isFlasque_constant_irreducible}
157	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.IsFlasque.constant_of_irreducible}
158	  \uses{def:isFlasque_sheaf}
159	  % SOURCE: [Hartshorne], II.1, Exercise~1.16(a) (read from
160	  % references/hartshorne-algebraic-geometry.pdf, PDF page 67).
161	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Show that a constant sheaf on an irreducible
162	  % topological space is flasque. See (I, \S1) for irreducible
163	  % topological spaces."
164	  \textit{Source: Hartshorne, II.1, Exercise~1.16(a).}
165	  Let \(X\) be an irreducible topological space and let \(A\) be a
166	  \(\bar k\)-module. Then the constant sheaf
167	  \((\mathrm{constantSheaf}\;J\;\_)\;A\) on \(X\) is flasque.
168	\end{lemma}
169	
170	\begin{proof}
171	  \uses{def:isFlasque_sheaf}
172	  On an irreducible topological space every non-empty open is connected
173	  and dense, so the constant presheaf
174	  \(U \mapsto A\) (with all non-empty restriction maps the identity)
175	  already satisfies the sheaf condition, and equals its sheafification.
176	  For every inclusion of non-empty opens \(V \subseteq U\), the
177	  restriction map is the identity on \(A\) — in particular surjective.
178	  For an inclusion involving the empty open, the target is the zero
179	  module, so surjectivity is immediate. Therefore the constant sheaf is
180	  flasque.
181	\end{proof}
182	
183	% NOTE (iter-194 reviewer): The companion Lean substrate helper
184	% `Scheme.IsFlasque.shortExact_app_surjective` (the
185	% Hartshorne~II.1~Exercise~1.16(b) consequence packaged here as the
186	% surjectivity hypothesis) was structurally decomposed iter-194 via a
187	% `forget₂` bridge to `Sheaf J AddCommGrpCat`: 3 axiom-clean substrate
188	% helpers landed (`sheafCompose_additive`,
189	% `sheafCompose_preservesZero`, `Scheme.IsFlasque.toAddCommGrpCat`);
190	% `Mono SAb.f` + `Epi SAb.g` are now proven axiom-clean inline.  Only
191	% the typeclass `(sheafCompose (forget₂ ModuleCat AddCommGrpCat))%
192	% .PreservesHomology` remains as the focused residual gap; iter-195
193	% closure target.
194	\begin{lemma}
195	
196	\leanok
197	[Cokernel of a flasque-by-flasque short exact sequence is flasque]
198	  \label{lem:flasque_cokernel_short_exact}
199	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.IsFlasque.cokernel_of_shortExact_flasque_flasque}
200	  \uses{def:isFlasque_sheaf}
201	  % SOURCE: Project-bespoke substrate helper for the
202	  % Hartshorne~II.1~Exercise~1.16(c) / III.2.5 chain (iter-193 Lane H
203	  % prover-side construction). The classical reference is Hartshorne
204	  % II.1, Exercise~1.16(c); see the verbatim quote in the proof of
205	  % \cref{thm:H1_vanishing_flasque} below.
206	  \textit{Source: project-bespoke iter-193 substrate (Hartshorne~II.1,
207	  Exercise~1.16(c) packaged with the sections-surjectivity input from
208	  II.1, Exercise~1.16(b) as a hypothesis).}
209	  Let $X$ be a topological space and let
210	  \[
211	    0 \to \mathcal F_1 \to \mathcal F_2 \to \mathcal F_3 \to 0
212	  \]
213	  be a short exact sequence in
214	  $\mathrm{Sheaf}\;(\Opens.\mathtt{grothendieckTopology}\;X)\;
215	  (\Module \bar k)$. Suppose:
216	  \begin{enumerate}
217	    \item $\mathcal F_2$ is flasque (in the sense of
218	      \cref{def:isFlasque_sheaf}); and
219	    \item the section-level map
220	      $\mathcal F_2(U) \to \mathcal F_3(U)$ is surjective for every open
221	      $U \subseteq X$ (the Hartshorne~II.1, Exercise~1.16(b) input,
222	      packaged as a hypothesis rather than invoked internally — this
223	      keeps the lemma's axiom-set clean and lets the consumer site
224	      supply the surjectivity from its own substrate).
225	  \end{enumerate}
226	  Then the cokernel sheaf $\mathcal F_3$ is flasque.
227	
228	  Concretely, given $V \le U$ in $\Opens X$ and a section
229	  $t \in \mathcal F_3(V)$: lift $t$ to $\tilde t \in \mathcal F_2(V)$
230	  via hypothesis~(2) at $V$, extend $\tilde t$ to
231	  $\tilde T \in \mathcal F_2(U)$ via flasqueness of $\mathcal F_2$, and
232	  push $\tilde T$ through the map $\mathcal F_2 \to \mathcal F_3$ at
233	  $U$. Naturality of the morphism on opens identifies the restriction
234	  of the resulting section in $\mathcal F_3(U)$ with the original $t$.
235	\end{lemma}
236	
237	\begin{lemma}
238	
239	\leanok
240	[$\Ext^{n+1}$ vanishes from $\Ext^n$ vanishing on the rightmost term, with injective middle term]
241	  \label{lem:ext_succ_zero_of_injective_lower_zero}
242	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.ext_succ_eq_zero_of_injective_of_lower_zero}
243	  \uses{def:Scheme_HModule}
244	  % SOURCE: Project-bespoke substrate helper for the
245	  % Hartshorne~III.2.5 induction (iter-193 Lane H prover-side
246	  % construction). The underlying mathematical statement is the
247	  % classical degree-shifting argument that, for an injective middle
248	  % term in a short exact sequence, the connecting morphism in the
249	  % long exact $\Ext$ sequence is surjective above the injective
250	  % dimension. No external source is cited verbatim.
251	  \textit{Source: project-bespoke iter-193 substrate.}
252	  Let $\mathcal C$ be an abelian category equipped with a
253	  \texttt{HasExt} instance, let $X$ be an object of $\mathcal C$, and
254	  let
255	  \[
256	    0 \to S_1 \to S_2 \to S_3 \to 0
257	  \]
258	  be a short exact sequence in $\mathcal C$ whose middle term $S_2$ is
259	  injective. Let $n_0 \ge 1$ be a natural number and assume that
260	  $\Ext^{n_0}(X, S_3) = 0$ (i.e.\ every element of $\Ext^{n_0}(X, S_3)$
261	  vanishes). Then $\Ext^{n_0 + 1}(X, S_1) = 0$.
262	
263	  The proof is the classical degree-shift. Since $S_2$ is injective and
264	  $n_0 + 1 \ge 2$, the injective-dimension subsingleton instance forces
265	  $\Ext^{n_0 + 1}(X, S_2)$ to be subsingleton; combined with exactness
266	  of the covariant $\Ext$ long exact sequence at $\Ext^{n_0 + 1}(X,
267	  S_1)$, every $x_1 \in \Ext^{n_0 + 1}(X, S_1)$ is in the image of the
268	  connecting morphism $\delta \colon \Ext^{n_0}(X, S_3) \to
269	  \Ext^{n_0 + 1}(X, S_1)$. The hypothesis
270	  $\Ext^{n_0}(X, S_3) = 0$ then gives $x_1 = 0$. The lemma is consumed
271	  in the $i \ge 2$ inductive case of \cref{thm:H1_vanishing_flasque}.
272	\end{lemma}
273	
274	\begin{theorem}
275	
276	\leanok
277	[Flasque sheaves have vanishing higher cohomology]
278	  \label{thm:H1_vanishing_flasque}
279	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.HModule_flasque_eq_zero}
280	  \uses{def:isFlasque_sheaf}
281	  % SOURCE: [Hartshorne], III.2, Proposition~2.5 (read from
282	  % references/hartshorne-algebraic-geometry.pdf, PDF page 225).
283	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Proposition 2.5. \emph{If \(\mathscr{F}\) is a
284	  % flasque sheaf on a topological space \(X\), then
285	  % \(H^i(X, \mathscr{F}) = 0\) for all \(i > 0\).}"
286	  \textit{Source: Hartshorne, III.2, Proposition~2.5, p.~208.}
287	  Let \(X\) be a topological space and let \(\mathcal F\) be a flasque
288	  sheaf of \(\bar k\)-modules on \(X\), viewed as an object of
289	  \(\mathrm{Sheaf}\;(\Opens.\mathtt{grothendieckTopology}\;X)\;
290	  (\Module \bar k)\). Then, for every \(i \ge 1\),
291	  \[
292	    \HModule\;\bar k\;\mathcal F\;i \;=\; 0.
293	  \]
294	  In particular, \(\dim_{\bar k} \HModule\;\bar k\;\mathcal F\;1 = 0\).
295	\end{theorem}
296	
297	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: "Proof. Embed \(\mathscr{F}\) in an injective
298	% object \(\mathscr{I}\) of \(\mathfrak{Ab}(X)\) and let \(\mathscr{G}\)
299	% be the quotient:
300	% \(0 \to \mathscr{F} \to \mathscr{I} \to \mathscr{G} \to 0\).
301	% Then \(\mathscr{F}\) is flasque by hypothesis, \(\mathscr{I}\) is
302	% flasque by (2.4), and so \(\mathscr{G}\) is flasque by (II, Ex. 1.16c).
303	% Now since \(\mathscr{F}\) is flasque, we have an exact sequence (II,
304	% Ex. 1.16b)
305	% \(0 \to \Gamma(X, \mathscr{F}) \to \Gamma(X, \mathscr{I}) \to
306	% \Gamma(X, \mathscr{G}) \to 0\).
307	% On the other hand, since \(\mathscr{I}\) is injective, we have
308	% \(H^i(X, \mathscr{I}) = 0\) for \(i > 0\) (1.1Ae). Thus from the long
309	% exact sequence of cohomology, we get \(H^1(X, \mathscr{F}) = 0\) and
310	% \(H^i(X, \mathscr{F}) \cong H^{i-1}(X, \mathscr{G})\) for each
311	% \(i \geq 2\). But \(\mathscr{G}\) is also flasque, so by induction on
312	% \(i\) we get the result."
313	\begin{proof}
314	  \uses{def:isFlasque_sheaf}
315	  We follow Hartshorne's induction. Embed \(\mathcal F\) into an
316	  injective object \(\mathcal I\) of the abelian category
317	  \(\mathrm{Sheaf}\;(\Opens.\mathtt{grothendieckTopology}\;X)\;
318	  (\Module \bar k)\) — such an injective exists by the
319	  enough-injectives instance for sheaves of \(\bar k\)-modules on opens
320	  (\cref{chap:Cohomology_StructureSheafModuleK}). Form the quotient
321	  short exact sequence
322	  \[
323	    0 \to \mathcal F \to \mathcal I \to \mathcal G \to 0
324	  \]
325	  in the sheaf category.
326	
327	  \emph{Step 1: the quotient \(\mathcal G\) is flasque.} The hypothesis
328	  gives that \(\mathcal F\) is flasque. The injective object
329	  \(\mathcal I\) is flasque by the Hartshorne~III~Lemma~2.4 input:
330	  any injective \(\mathcal O_X\)-module is flasque, where the
331	  \(\mathcal O_X\) here is the constant sheaf of rings \(\bar k\) on
332	  \(X\), so the sheaf category over the constant sheaf of rings is the
333	  category of sheaves of \(\bar k\)-modules and the lemma applies. From
334	  the two flasqueness inputs the quotient \(\mathcal G\) inherits
335	  flasqueness by Hartshorne~II~Exercise~1.16(c): \emph{if
336	  \(0 \to \mathcal F' \to \mathcal F \to \mathcal F'' \to 0\) is an
337	  exact sequence and \(\mathcal F', \mathcal F\) are flasque, then
338	  \(\mathcal F''\) is flasque}.
339	
340	  \emph{Step 2: the global-sections functor is exact on the chosen
341	  short exact sequence.} Since the leftmost term \(\mathcal F\) is
342	  flasque, the long exact sequence of cohomology starts with a short
343	  exact sequence on global sections
344	  (Hartshorne~II~Exercise~1.16(b)):
345	  \[
346	    0 \to \Gamma(X, \mathcal F) \to \Gamma(X, \mathcal I) \to
347	    \Gamma(X, \mathcal G) \to 0.
348	  \]
349	
350	  \emph{Step 3: the long exact sequence gives the vanishing.}
351	  The object \(\mathcal I\) is injective in the abelian sheaf category,
352	  hence its right-derived global-sections cohomology vanishes in
353	  positive degree:
354	  \(\HModule\;\bar k\;\mathcal I\;i = 0\) for all \(i \ge 1\). The long
355	  exact sequence of the short exact sequence above, applied to the
356	  derived functor \(\HModule\;\bar k\;{-}\;{\bullet}\) of \(\Gamma\),
357	  reads in degrees \(0\) and \(1\) as
358	  \[
359	    \Gamma(X, \mathcal I) \to \Gamma(X, \mathcal G)
360	    \to \HModule\;\bar k\;\mathcal F\;1
361	    \to \HModule\;\bar k\;\mathcal I\;1 = 0.
362	  \]
363	  The leftmost map is surjective by Step~2, so the connecting morphism
364	  to \(\HModule\;\bar k\;\mathcal F\;1\) is the zero map, and the kernel
365	  of \(\HModule\;\bar k\;\mathcal F\;1 \to 0\) is all of
366	  \(\HModule\;\bar k\;\mathcal F\;1\); hence
367	  \(\HModule\;\bar k\;\mathcal F\;1 = 0\).
368	
369	  \emph{Step 4: higher degrees by induction.} For \(i \ge 2\), the long
370	  exact sequence yields
371	  \(\HModule\;\bar k\;\mathcal F\;i \cong \HModule\;\bar k\;\mathcal G\;
372	  (i - 1)\); the inductive hypothesis on the flasque quotient
373	  \(\mathcal G\) gives the right-hand side is zero. This closes the
374	  vanishing in all positive degrees. The chapter consumes only the
375	  degree-\(1\) instance, but the lemma is stated and proved in full
376	  generality so it can support later sub-builds (e.g.\ Grothendieck
377	  vanishing on a one-dimensional scheme).
378	
379	  % NOTE: the proof structure mirrors Hartshorne~III~Proposition~2.5
380	  % verbatim. The Lean closure is gated on three substrate inputs:
381	  % (a) the enough-injectives instance for `Sheaf J (ModuleCat kbar)`
382	  % already provided by chapter
383	  % `Cohomology_StructureSheafModuleK.tex`; (b) the project-side
384	  % flasque-quotient lemma (Hartshorne~II~Ex.~1.16(c)) restated for
385	  % `ModuleCat kbar`-valued sheaves; (c) the long-exact-sequence
386	  % carrier for `Abelian.Ext.covariantSequence` at the `HModule kbar`
387	  % level (the same LES carrier consumed by
388	  % `eulerCharacteristic_shortExact_add` in
389	  % `RiemannRoch_RRFormula.tex`). All three are project-side ~50-100
390	  % LOC apiece.
391	\end{proof}

393	\section{Skyscraper sheaves are flasque}
394	\label{sec:H1Vanishing_skyscraperFlasque}
395	
396	\begin{lemma}
397	
398	\leanok
399	[Skyscraper sheaf as pushforward from a closed point]
400	  \label{lem:skyscraperSheaf_eq_pushforward}
401	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.skyscraperSheaf_eq_pushforward_const}
402	  % SOURCE: [Hartshorne], II.1, Exercise~1.17 (read from
403	  % references/hartshorne-algebraic-geometry.pdf, PDF page 68).
404	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Skyscraper Sheaves. Let \(X\) be a topological space,
405	  % let \(P\) be a point, and let \(A\) be an abelian group. Define a
406	  % sheaf \(i_P(A)\) on \(X\) as follows: \(i_P(A)(U) = A\) if \(P \in
407	  % U\), \(0\) otherwise. \ldots Show that this sheaf could also be
408	  % described as \(i_*(A)\), where \(A\) denotes the constant sheaf
409	  % \(A\) on the closed subspace \(\{P\}^-\), and \(i\colon \{P\}^- \to
410	  % X\) is the inclusion."
411	  \textit{Source: Hartshorne, II.1, Exercise~1.17.}
412	  Let \(X\) be a topological space, let \(P \in X\) be a point with
413	  closure \(\overline{\{P\}}\), and let \(A\) be a \(\bar k\)-module.
414	  Write \(i \colon \overline{\{P\}} \hookrightarrow X\) for the closed
415	  embedding. Then the skyscraper sheaf
416	  \(\mathtt{skyscraperSheaf}\;P\;A\) on \(X\) is naturally isomorphic to
417	  the pushforward \(i_*\bigl((\mathrm{constantSheaf}\;{-}\;\_)\;A\bigr)\)
418	  of the constant sheaf with value \(A\) on \(\overline{\{P\}}\).
419	\end{lemma}
420	
421	\begin{proof}
422	  Both presheaves send an open \(U \subseteq X\) to \(A\) when
423	  \(P \in U\) (equivalently, when
424	  \(U \cap \overline{\{P\}}\) is non-empty, since the closed subset
425	  \(\overline{\{P\}}\) is irreducible and any open meeting it contains
426	  \(P\)), and to \(0\) when \(P \notin U\) (equivalently,
427	  \(U \cap \overline{\{P\}} = \varnothing\)). The restriction maps agree
428	  by inspection: in the \(P \in V \subseteq U\) case both are the
429	  identity on \(A\); in the \(P \notin V\) case both factor through
430	  \(0\). The natural identification on opens is a sheaf morphism, and
431	  on stalks it is the identity (the stalk at any \(Q \neq P\) outside
432	  \(\overline{\{P\}}\) is zero on both sides; the stalk at any point of
433	  \(\overline{\{P\}}\) is \(A\) on both sides). Hence the comparison is
434	  an isomorphism of sheaves.
435	\end{proof}
436	
437	\begin{lemma}
438	
439	\leanok
440	[Closure of a closed point is irreducible]
441	  \label{lem:closedPoint_closure_irreducible}
442	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.PrimeDivisor.closure_isIrreducible}
443	
444	  Let \(C\) be a Noetherian scheme (in particular the smooth proper
445	  curve \(C / \bar k\) of this chapter), and let \(P \in C\) be a closed
446	  point. Then the closure \(\overline{\{P\}} = \{P\}\) is irreducible
447	  (trivially: a singleton is irreducible as a topological space).
448	
449	  \textit{Project-bespoke ancillary} (immediate set-theoretic fact;
450	  no external source pinned).
451	\end{lemma}
452	
453	\begin{proof}
454	
455	  A singleton topological space is irreducible: its only non-empty open
456	  is the whole space, which trivially intersects every other non-empty
457	  open in a non-empty set (in fact in itself). For a closed point
458	  \(P \in C\) the closure \(\overline{\{P\}}\) is \(\{P\}\), which is
459	  homeomorphic to a singleton, hence irreducible.
460	\end{proof}
461	
462	\begin{lemma}
463	
464	\leanok
465	[Closed-point skyscraper sheaf is flasque]
466	  \label{lem:skyscraperSheaf_isFlasque}
467	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.skyscraperSheaf_isFlasque}
468	  \uses{def:isFlasque_sheaf, lem:skyscraperSheaf_eq_pushforward,
469	        lem:closedPoint_closure_irreducible,
470	        lem:isFlasque_pushforward, lem:isFlasque_constant_irreducible}
471	
472	  Let \(C\) be a smooth proper geometrically irreducible curve over
473	  \(\bar k\) and let \(P \in C\) be a closed point. Then the
474	  closed-point skyscraper sheaf
475	  \[
476	    k(P) \;:=\; \mathtt{skyscraperSheaf}\;P\;
477	      (\Module.\mathtt{of}\;\bar k\;\bar k)
478	  \]
479	  is flasque as a sheaf of \(\bar k\)-modules on \(C\).
480	
481	  \textit{Project-bespoke packaging} (no separately named external
482	  source; the constituent inputs are quoted in their own lemma blocks
483	  above).
484	\end{lemma}
485	
486	\begin{proof}
487	  \uses{def:isFlasque_sheaf, lem:skyscraperSheaf_eq_pushforward, lem:closedPoint_closure_irreducible, lem:isFlasque_pushforward, lem:isFlasque_constant_irreducible}
488	  By
489	  \cref{lem:skyscraperSheaf_eq_pushforward}, the skyscraper sheaf is
490	  isomorphic to the pushforward
491	  \(i_*\bigl((\mathrm{constantSheaf}\;{-}\;\_)\;
492	  (\Module.\mathtt{of}\;\bar k\;\bar k)\bigr)\) along the inclusion
493	  \(i \colon \overline{\{P\}} \hookrightarrow C\). The base
494	  \(\overline{\{P\}} = \{P\}\) is irreducible
495	  (\cref{lem:closedPoint_closure_irreducible}), so the constant sheaf on
496	  it is flasque (\cref{lem:isFlasque_constant_irreducible}). Pushforward
497	  of a flasque sheaf is flasque
498	  (\cref{lem:isFlasque_pushforward}). Flasqueness transports along the
499	  isomorphism of \cref{lem:skyscraperSheaf_eq_pushforward}, so
500	  \(k(P)\) is flasque.
501	\end{proof}

503	\section{\(H^1\) of a closed-point skyscraper sheaf vanishes}
504	\label{sec:H1Vanishing_skyscraperH1}
505	
506	\begin{theorem}
507	
508	\leanok
509	[\(H^1\) of a closed-point skyscraper sheaf has dimension zero]
510	  \label{lem:H1_skyscraperSheaf_finrank_eq_zero_main}
511	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.H1_skyscraperSheaf_finrank_eq_zero}
512	  \uses{thm:H1_vanishing_flasque, lem:skyscraperSheaf_isFlasque}
513	
514	  Let \(C\) be a smooth proper geometrically irreducible curve over
515	  \(\bar k\) and let \(P \in C\) be a closed point. Then
516	  \[
517	    \dim_{\bar k}\, H^1\bigl(C,\, \mathtt{skyscraperSheaf}\;P\;
518	      (\Module.\mathtt{of}\;\bar k\;\bar k)\bigr) \;=\; 0,
519	  \]
520	  where the left-hand side is the \(\bar k\)-finrank of
521	  \(\HModule\;\bar k\;(\mathtt{skyscraperSheaf}\;P\;(\Module.\mathtt{of}\;
522	  \bar k\;\bar k))\;1\).
523	
524	  \textit{Project-bespoke assembly} of the two substrate inputs of this
525	  chapter (\cref{thm:H1_vanishing_flasque} and
526	  \cref{lem:skyscraperSheaf_isFlasque}). The chapter's external source
527	  is Hartshorne~III.2 Proposition~2.5 applied to the flasque skyscraper
528	  sheaf — both inputs are quoted verbatim in their own lemma blocks
529	  above.
530	\end{theorem}
531	
532	\begin{proof}
533	  \uses{thm:H1_vanishing_flasque, lem:skyscraperSheaf_isFlasque}
534	  The closed-point skyscraper sheaf
535	  \(k(P) = \mathtt{skyscraperSheaf}\;P\;(\Module.\mathtt{of}\;\bar k\;
536	  \bar k)\) is flasque (\cref{lem:skyscraperSheaf_isFlasque}). By the
537	  flasque-cohomology vanishing theorem
538	  (\cref{thm:H1_vanishing_flasque}) applied at \(i = 1\) on the
539	  underlying topological space \(C.\mathtt{left.toTopCat}\),
540	  \[
541	    \HModule\;\bar k\;(k(P))\;1 \;=\; 0.
542	  \]
543	  Taking \(\Module.\mathtt{finrank}\;\bar k\) of the zero module gives
544	  the claimed identity \(\dim_{\bar k} H^1(C, k(P)) = 0\).
545	\end{proof}

625	    \(\dim H^1(C, \mathcal O_C) = 0\) this delivers
626	    \(\dim H^0(C, \mathcal O_C([P])) = 2\), the non-constant-section
627	    output of \texttt{RR.3}.
628	\end{itemize}
629	
630	\section{Out of scope}
631	
632	The following items lie outside this chapter and are scheduled into
633	sibling sub-builds:
634	\begin{itemize}
635	  \item \emph{General coherent-sheaf cohomology vanishing.} Higher
636	    cohomology vanishing for general coherent sheaves on a curve
637	    requires Grothendieck vanishing on a one-dimensional scheme
638	    (Hartshorne~III.2.7), which the chapter does \emph{not} use; the
639	    chapter is narrowly tailored to the flasque/skyscraper case
640	    \(H^1(C, k(P)) = 0\) that the genus-\(0\) critical path needs.
641	  \item \emph{Serre duality for curves.} The general-\(g\) Riemann--Roch
642	    statement \(\ell(D) - \ell(K - D) = \deg(D) + 1 - g\) routes through
643	    Serre duality \(H^1(C, \mathcal L) \cong H^0(C, \omega_C \otimes
644	    \mathcal L^{-1})^*\); Mathlib has neither the dualizing sheaf
645	    \(\omega_C\) on a scheme nor the duality theorem. The chapter
646	    sidesteps duality entirely by using flasqueness directly (the
647	    Hartshorne~III~\S2 order of presentation, not the
648	    Hartshorne~III~\S7 one).
649	  \item \emph{The \(H^0\) half of \(\chi(k(P)) = 1\).} The
650	    one-dimensionality identification \(\dim H^0(C, k(P)) = 1\) is the
651	    sibling substrate lemma
652	    REF of
653	    \cref{chap:RiemannRoch_RRFormula}, closed axiom-clean iter-188
654	    against the project's four-step \(\bar k\)-linear-equivalence chain.
655	    The present chapter is the complementary H\(^1\) half.
656	\end{itemize}

155:instance hasSheafCompose_of_preservesLimitsOfSize [PreservesLimitsOfSize.{v₁, max u₁ v₁} F] :

1	/-
2	Copyright (c) 2021 Adam Topaz. All rights reserved.
3	Released under Apache 2.0 license as described in the file LICENSE.
4	Authors: Adam Topaz
5	-/
6	module
7	
8	public import Mathlib.CategoryTheory.Sites.Sheaf
9	public import Mathlib.CategoryTheory.ConcreteCategory.Forget
10	
11	/-!
12	
13	In this file we construct the functor `Sheaf J A ⥤ Sheaf J B` between sheaf categories
14	obtained by composition with a functor `F : A ⥤ B`.
15	
16	In order for the sheaf condition to be preserved, `F` must preserve the correct limits.
17	The lemma `Presheaf.IsSheaf.comp` says that composition with such an `F` indeed preserves the
18	sheaf condition.
19	
20	The functor between sheaf categories is called `sheafCompose J F`.
21	Given a natural transformation `η : F ⟶ G`, we obtain a natural transformation
22	`sheafCompose J F ⟶ sheafCompose J G`, which we call `sheafCompose_map J η`.
23	
24	-/
25	
26	@[expose] public section
27	
28	
29	namespace CategoryTheory
30	
31	open CategoryTheory.Limits Functor
32	
33	universe v₁ v₂ v₃ u₁ u₂ u₃
34	
35	variable {C : Type u₁} [Category.{v₁} C]
36	variable {A : Type u₂} [Category.{v₂} A]
37	variable {B : Type u₃} [Category.{v₃} B]
38	variable (J : GrothendieckTopology C)
39	variable {U : C} (R : Presieve U)
40	variable (F G H : A ⥤ B) (η : F ⟶ G) (γ : G ⟶ H)
41	
42	/-- Describes the property of a functor to "preserve sheaves". -/
43	class GrothendieckTopology.HasSheafCompose : Prop where
44	  /-- For every sheaf `P`, `P ⋙ F` is a sheaf. -/
45	  isSheaf (P : Cᵒᵖ ⥤ A) (hP : Presheaf.IsSheaf J P) : Presheaf.IsSheaf J (P ⋙ F)
46	
47	variable [J.HasSheafCompose F] [J.HasSheafCompose G] [J.HasSheafCompose H]
48	
49	/-- Composing a functor which `HasSheafCompose`, yields a functor between sheaf categories. -/
50	@[simps! obj_obj map_hom]
51	def sheafCompose : Sheaf J A ⥤ Sheaf J B :=
52	  ObjectProperty.lift _
53	    (sheafToPresheaf _ _ ⋙ (Functor.whiskeringRight _ _ _).obj F)
54	      (fun P ↦ GrothendieckTopology.HasSheafCompose.isSheaf _ P.property)
55	
56	instance [F.Faithful] : (sheafCompose J F ⋙ sheafToPresheaf _ _).Faithful :=
57	  show (sheafToPresheaf _ _ ⋙ (whiskeringRight Cᵒᵖ A B).obj F).Faithful from inferInstance
58	
59	instance [F.Faithful] [F.Full] : (sheafCompose J F ⋙ sheafToPresheaf _ _).Full :=
60	  show (sheafToPresheaf _ _ ⋙ (whiskeringRight Cᵒᵖ A B).obj F).Full from inferInstance
61	
62	variable {F} in
63	/-- If `F : A ⥤ B` is fully faithful, then `sheafCompose J F ⋙ sheafToPresheaf J B` is fully
64	faithful. -/
65	def fullyFaithfulSheafComposeCompSheafToPresheaf (hF : F.FullyFaithful) :
66	    (sheafCompose J F ⋙ sheafToPresheaf J B).FullyFaithful :=
67	  (fullyFaithfulSheafToPresheaf J A).comp (hF.whiskeringRight Cᵒᵖ)
68	
69	instance [F.Faithful] : (sheafCompose J F).Faithful :=
70	  Functor.Faithful.of_comp (sheafCompose J F) (sheafToPresheaf _ _)
71	
72	instance [F.Full] [F.Faithful] : (sheafCompose J F).Full :=
73	  Functor.Full.of_comp_faithful (sheafCompose J F) (sheafToPresheaf _ _)
74	
75	variable {F} in
76	/-- If `F : A ⥤ B` is fully faithful, then `sheafCompose J F` is fully faithful. -/
77	def fullyFaithfulSheafCompose (hF : F.FullyFaithful) :
78	    (sheafCompose J F).FullyFaithful :=
79	  (fullyFaithfulSheafComposeCompSheafToPresheaf J hF).ofCompFaithful
80	
81	instance [F.ReflectsIsomorphisms] : (sheafCompose J F).ReflectsIsomorphisms where
82	  reflects {G₁ G₂} f _ := by
83	    rw [← isIso_iff_of_reflects_iso _ (sheafToPresheaf _ _),
84	      ← isIso_iff_of_reflects_iso _ ((whiskeringRight Cᵒᵖ A B).obj F)]
85	    change IsIso ((sheafToPresheaf _ _).map ((sheafCompose J F).map f))
86	    infer_instance
87	
88	variable {F G}
89	
90	/--
91	If `η : F ⟶ G` is a natural transformation then we obtain a morphism of functors
92	`sheafCompose J F ⟶ sheafCompose J G` by whiskering with `η` on the level of presheaves.
93	-/
94	def sheafCompose_map : sheafCompose J F ⟶ sheafCompose J G where
95	  app := fun _ => .mk <| whiskerLeft _ η
96	
97	@[simp]
98	lemma sheafCompose_id : sheafCompose_map (F := F) J (𝟙 _) = 𝟙 _ := rfl
99	
100	@[simp]
101	lemma sheafCompose_comp :
102	    sheafCompose_map J (η ≫ γ) = sheafCompose_map J η ≫ sheafCompose_map J γ := rfl
103	
104	namespace GrothendieckTopology.Cover
105	
106	variable (F G) {J}
107	variable (P : Cᵒᵖ ⥤ A) {X : C} (S : J.Cover X)
108	
109	/-- The multicospan associated to a cover `S : J.Cover X` and a presheaf of the form `P ⋙ F`
110	is isomorphic to the composition of the multicospan associated to `S` and `P`,
111	composed with `F`. -/
112	@[simps!]
113	def multicospanComp : (S.index (P ⋙ F)).multicospan ≅ (S.index P).multicospan ⋙ F :=
114	  NatIso.ofComponents
115	    (fun t =>
116	      match t with
117	      | WalkingMulticospan.left _ => Iso.refl _
118	      | WalkingMulticospan.right _ => Iso.refl _)
119	    (by
120	      rintro (a | b) (a | b) (f | f | f)
121	      all_goals cat_disch)
122	
123	/-- Mapping the multifork associated to a cover `S : J.Cover X` and a presheaf `P` with
124	respect to a functor `F` is isomorphic (upto a natural isomorphism of the underlying functors)
125	to the multifork associated to `S` and `P ⋙ F`. -/
126	def mapMultifork :
127	    F.mapCone (S.multifork P) ≅
128	      (Limits.Cone.postcompose (S.multicospanComp F P).hom).obj (S.multifork (P ⋙ F)) :=
129	  Cone.ext (Iso.refl _)
130	
131	end GrothendieckTopology.Cover
132	
133	/--
134	Composing a sheaf with a functor preserving the limit of `(S.index P).multicospan` yields a functor
135	between sheaf categories.
136	-/
137	instance (priority := high) hasSheafCompose_of_preservesMulticospan (F : A ⥤ B)
138	    [∀ (X : C) (S : J.Cover X) (P : Cᵒᵖ ⥤ A), PreservesLimit (S.index P).multicospan F] :
139	    J.HasSheafCompose F where
140	  isSheaf P hP := by
141	    rw [Presheaf.isSheaf_iff_multifork] at hP ⊢
142	    intro X S
143	    obtain ⟨h⟩ := hP X S
144	    replace h := isLimitOfPreserves F h
145	    replace h := Limits.IsLimit.ofIsoLimit h (S.mapMultifork F P)
146	    exact ⟨Limits.IsLimit.postcomposeHomEquiv (S.multicospanComp F P) _ h⟩
147	
148	/--
149	Composing a sheaf with a functor preserving limits of the same size as the hom sets in `C` yields a
150	functor between sheaf categories.
151	
152	Note: the size of the limit that `F` is required to preserve in
153	`hasSheafCompose_of_preservesMulticospan` is in general larger than this.
154	-/
155	instance hasSheafCompose_of_preservesLimitsOfSize [PreservesLimitsOfSize.{v₁, max u₁ v₁} F] :
156	    J.HasSheafCompose F where
157	  isSheaf _ hP := Presheaf.isSheaf_comp_of_isSheaf J _ F hP
158	
159	variable {J}
160	
161	lemma Sheaf.isSeparated {FA : A → A → Type*} {CA : A → Type*}
162	    [∀ X Y, FunLike (FA X Y) (CA X) (CA Y)] [ConcreteCategory A FA] [J.HasSheafCompose (forget A)]
163	    (F : Sheaf J A) : Presheaf.IsSeparated J F.obj := by
164	  rintro X S hS x y h
165	  exact (((isSheaf_iff_isSheaf_of_type _ _).1
166	    ((sheafCompose J (forget A)).obj F).2).isSeparated S hS).ext (fun _ _ hf => h _ _ hf)
167	
168	lemma Presheaf.IsSheaf.isSeparated {F : Cᵒᵖ ⥤ A} {FA : A → A → Type*} {CA : A → Type*}
169	    [∀ X Y, FunLike (FA X Y) (CA X) (CA Y)] [ConcreteCategory A FA]
170	    [J.HasSheafCompose (forget A)] (hF : Presheaf.IsSheaf J F) :
171	    Presheaf.IsSeparated J F :=
172	  Sheaf.isSeparated ⟨F, hF⟩
173	
174	end CategoryTheory

16	We define and prove basic properties about flasque sheaves on topological spaces.

21	
22	## Main results
23	
24	* `TopCat.Sheaf.IsFlasque.epi_of_shortExact`: Given a short exact sequence of sheaves,
25	  `0 ⟶ 𝓕 ⟶ 𝓖 ⟶ 𝓗 ⟶ 0`, if `𝓕` is flasque then `𝓖(U) ⟶ 𝓗(U)` is surjective, for any open `U`.
26	
27	* `TopCat.Sheaf.IsFlasque.of_shortExact_of_isFlasque₁₂ `: Given a short exact sequence of
28	  sheaves, `0 ⟶ 𝓕 ⟶ 𝓖 ⟶ 𝓗 ⟶ 0`, if `𝓕` and `𝓖` are flasque, then `𝓗` is flasque.

92	      grind [Subtype.ext_iff, h i.property j.property]
93	    · rfl
94	    all_goals
95	      rw [← CategoryOfElements.map_snd h₁.some.2]
96	      dsimp
97	      rw [← Functor.map_comp_apply]
98	      rfl
99	  have le₁ : iSup f ≤ U := iSup_le <| fun j => leOfHom j.1.hom.1.unop
100	  have le₂ : ∀ i, i ∈ c → unop i.right.1 ≤ iSup f := fun i hi ↦ le_iSup f ⟨i, hi⟩
101	  use StructuredArrow.mk (CategoryOfElements.homMk _ _ (homOfLE le₁).op (eq_app_of_locally_eq ht
102	      (fun i ↦ leOfHom i.1.hom.1.unop) (fun i ↦ (CategoryOfElements.map_snd i.1.hom).symm)).symm :
103	      ⟨op U, s⟩ ⟶ (Functor.whiskerRight g.hom
104	      (CategoryTheory.forget AddCommGrpCat)).mapElements.obj ⟨op (iSup f), t⟩)
105	  exact fun i hi => Nonempty.intro (StructuredArrow.homMk (CategoryOfElements.homMk _ _
106	    (homOfLE (le₂ i hi)).op (ht ⟨i, hi⟩)) (by cat_disch))
107	
108	set_option backward.isDefEq.respectTransparency false in
109	/-- Given a short exact sequence of sheaves, `0 ⟶ 𝓕 ⟶ 𝓖 ⟶ 𝓗 ⟶ 0`, if `𝓕` is flasque then
110	`𝓖(U) ⟶ 𝓗(U)` is surjective, for any open `U`. -/
111	theorem epi_of_shortExact {S : ShortComplex (Sheaf AddCommGrpCat X)} (hS : S.ShortExact)
112	    [IsFlasque S.X₁] : Epi (S.g.1.app (op U)) := by
113	  refine (AddCommGrpCat.epi_iff_surjective _).mpr (fun s ↦ ?_)
114	  -- We want to find a preimage of `s` by `S.g`.
115	  -- We apply Zorn's lemma to obtain a term `t` of `Under S.g s` that is maximal.
116	  obtain ⟨t, ht⟩ := exists_maximal_of_chains_bounded
117	    (structured_arrows_elements_sheaf_chains_bounded S.g s)
118	    (fun ⟨f⟩ ⟨g⟩ ↦ ⟨g ≫ f⟩)
119	  have tle : t.right.1.unop ≤ U := leOfHom t.hom.1.unop
120	  have tcomp : s |_ t.right.1.unop = S.g.hom.app t.right.1 t.right.2 :=
121	      CategoryOfElements.map_snd t.hom
122	  -- We get a section `t.right.2` of `S.g` defined on an open subset `t.right.1.unop` of `U`,
123	  -- that is sent to the restriction of `s` by `S.g`.
124	  have : U ≤ t.right.1.unop := by
125	  -- Prove that the set of definition of `t.right.2` contains `U`.
126	    intro x hx
127	    have := (isLocallySurjective_iff_epi S.g).mpr hS.epi_g
128	    -- We use local surjectivity to find a section `t₁` of `S.X₂` on a neighborhood `W` of `x`
129	    -- that maps to `s |_ W` by `S.g`.
130	    obtain ⟨W, Wle, ⟨t₁, ht₁⟩, hW⟩ := (isLocallySurjective_iff S.g.hom).mp this U s x hx
131	    --`t.right.2` and `t₁` need not agree on their overlap so we need to deal with their
132	    -- difference `t₂`
133	    let t₂ := t.right.2 |_ (t.right.1.unop ⊓ W) - t₁ |_ (t.right.1.unop ⊓ W)
134	    have : (S.g.hom.app (op (t.right.1.unop ⊓ W))) t₂ = 0 := by
135	      simp [map_restrict, ← tcomp, restrict_restrict, ht₁, t₂]
136	    -- Since `S` is exact and `t₂` maps to zero, we can lift it to a section `t₃` of `S.X₁`
137	    obtain ⟨t₃, ht₃⟩ := Sheaf.sections_exact_of_left_exact hS.1 hS.2 t₂ this
138	    -- Using that `S.X₁` is flasque, we can lift `t₃` to a section on `W`.
139	    obtain ⟨t₄, (ht₄ : t₄ |_ (t.right.1.unop ⊓ W) = t₃)⟩ := (AddCommGrpCat.epi_iff_surjective
140	      (S.X₁.obj.map (homOfLE inf_le_right).op)).mp inferInstance t₃
141	    let f : Fin 2 → Opens X := ![t.right.1.unop, W]
142	    let sf : (i : Fin 2) → S.X₂.obj.obj (op (f i))
143	    | 0 => t.right.2
144	    | 1 => t₁ + (S.f.hom.app (op W)) t₄
145	    have : sf 0 |_ (t.right.1.unop ⊓ W) = sf 1 |_ (t.right.1.unop ⊓ W) := by
146	      dsimp [sf, f]
147	      simp only [restrict_sum, ← map_restrict, ht₄, ht₃, t₂, add_sub_cancel]
148	    -- We glue `t.right.2` and `t₁ + (S.f.hom.app (op W)) t₄` together to form `t₅`
149	    obtain ⟨t₅, ht₅, _⟩ : ∃! t₅, IsGluing S.X₂.obj f sf t₅ := by
150	      apply Sheaf.existsUnique_gluing
151	      simp only [IsCompatible, Fin.forall_fin_two]
152	      refine ⟨⟨rfl, this⟩, Eq.symm ?_, rfl⟩
153	      apply_fun (fun s ↦ restrictOpen s (W ⊓ t.right.1.unop) (le_of_eq (inf_comm _ _))) at this
154	      rw [restrict_restrict, restrict_restrict] at this
155	      exact this
156	    have le : iSup f ≤ U := iSup_le_iff.mpr (Fin.forall_fin_two.mpr ⟨tle, Wle⟩)
157	    -- We upgrade `t₅` to an object in `Under S.g s` that is defined on `t.right.1.unop ⊔ W`.
158	    let t₆ : Under S.g s :=
159	      StructuredArrow.mk (S := ⟨op U, s⟩)
160	        (T := (Functor.whiskerRight S.g.hom (CategoryTheory.forget AddCommGrpCat)).mapElements)
161	        (Y := ⟨op (iSup f), t₅⟩) <| CategoryOfElements.homMk _ _ (homOfLE le).op (by
162	          refine (eq_app_of_locally_eq ht₅ (by rw [Fin.forall_fin_two]; exact ⟨tle, Wle⟩) ?_).symm
163	          rw [Fin.forall_fin_two]
164	          refine ⟨tcomp.symm, ?_⟩
165	          simp only [Fin.isValue, Functor.comp_obj, map_add, homOfLE_leOfHom, sf, f]
166	          have : (S.f.hom.app (op W) ≫ S.g.hom.app (op W)) = 0 := by
167	            rw [← NatTrans.comp_app, ← ObjectProperty.FullSubcategory.comp_hom, S.zero]
168	            rfl
169	          simp [← CategoryTheory.comp_apply, this, ht₁]
170	          rfl)
171	    -- We prove that `t₆` is bigger than `t` for the preorder used on `Under S.g s`.
172	    have : Nonempty (t₆ ⟶ t) := Nonempty.intro (StructuredArrow.homMk (CategoryOfElements.homMk _ _
173	      (homOfLE (le_iSup f 0)).op (ht₅ 0)) (by cat_disch))
174	    exact leOfHom ((ht t₆) this).some.right.1.unop ((le_iSup f 1) hW)
175	  exact ⟨t.right.2 |_ U, by simp [map_restrict, ← tcomp, restrict_restrict]⟩
176	
177	/-- Given a short exact sequence of sheaves, `0 ⟶ 𝓕 ⟶ 𝓖 ⟶ 𝓗 ⟶ 0`, if `𝓕` and `𝓖` are flasque,
178	then `𝓗` is flasque. -/
179	theorem of_shortExact_of_isFlasque₁₂ {S : ShortComplex (Sheaf AddCommGrpCat X)}
180	    (hS : S.ShortExact) [IsFlasque S.X₁] [IsFlasque S.X₂] : IsFlasque S.X₃ where
181	  epi {U V} i := by
182	    have : Epi (S.g.1.app U ≫ S.X₃.obj.map i) := by
183	      rw [← S.g.hom.naturality i]
184	      exact CategoryTheory.epi_comp' inferInstance (epi_of_shortExact hS)
185	    exact CategoryTheory.epi_of_epi (S.g.1.app U) (S.X₃.obj.map i)
186	
187	end TopCat.Sheaf.IsFlasque

import Mathlib

open CategoryTheory CategoryTheory.Limits Functor

universe u v
variable {C : Type u} [Category.{v} C] {A : Type u} [Category.{v} A] {B : Type u} [Category.{v} B]

instance my_sheafCompose_preservesFiniteLimits (J : GrothendieckTopology C) 
    (F : A ⥤ B) [J.HasSheafCompose F] [HasFiniteLimits A] [HasFiniteLimits B]
    [PreservesFiniteLimits F] :
    PreservesFiniteLimits (sheafCompose J F) := by
  have h1 : (sheafCompose J F ⋙ sheafToPresheaf J B) =
      (sheafToPresheaf J A ⋙ (Functor.whiskeringRight _ _ _).obj F) := rfl
  -- the right side preserves finite limits
  have h2 : PreservesFiniteLimits (sheafToPresheaf J A ⋙ (Functor.whiskeringRight _ _ _).obj F) := by
    infer_instance
  rw [← h1] at h2
  -- sheafToPresheaf reflects isomorphisms, so it reflects limits
  apply preservesFiniteLimits_of_reflects_of_preserves _ (sheafToPresheaf J B)

import Mathlib

open CategoryTheory CategoryTheory.Limits Functor

universe u v
variable {C : Type u} [Category.{v} C] {A : Type u} [Category.{v} A] {B : Type u} [Category.{v} B]
  [HasFiniteLimits A] [HasFiniteLimits B] (J : GrothendieckTopology C)

-- the goal: build PreservesFiniteLimits on sheafCompose J F
example (F : A ⥤ B) [J.HasSheafCompose F] [PreservesFiniteLimits F] :
    PreservesFiniteLimits (sheafCompose J F) := by
  have heq : (sheafCompose J F ⋙ sheafToPresheaf J B) =
      (sheafToPresheaf J A ⋙ (Functor.whiskeringRight _ _ _).obj F) := rfl
  haveI hcomp : PreservesFiniteLimits (sheafCompose J F ⋙ sheafToPresheaf J B) := by
    rw [heq]
    exact ⟨fun K _ _ => inferInstance⟩
  exact preservesFiniteLimits_of_reflects_of_preserves _ (sheafToPresheaf J B)

25:We provide the instance `CategoryTheory.Category (TopCat.Sheaf C X)` as the full subcategory of
26:presheaves, and the fully faithful functor `Sheaf.forget : TopCat.Sheaf C X ⥤ TopCat.Presheaf C X`.
105:/-- A `TopCat.Sheaf C X` is a presheaf of objects from `C` over a (bundled) topological space `X`,
128:def forget : TopCat.Sheaf C X ⥤ TopCat.Presheaf C X :=

100	
101	end Presheaf
102	
103	variable (C X)
104	
105	/-- A `TopCat.Sheaf C X` is a presheaf of objects from `C` over a (bundled) topological space `X`,
106	satisfying the sheaf condition.
107	-/
108	nonrec def Sheaf : Type max u v w :=
109	  Sheaf (Opens.grothendieckTopology X) C
110	deriving Category
111	
112	variable {C X}
113	
114	/-- The underlying presheaf of a sheaf -/
115	abbrev Sheaf.presheaf (F : X.Sheaf C) : TopCat.Presheaf C X :=
116	  F.1
117	
118	variable (C X)
119	
120	-- Let's construct a trivial example, to keep the inhabited linter happy.
121	instance sheafInhabited : Inhabited (Sheaf (CategoryTheory.Discrete PUnit) X) :=
122	  ⟨⟨Functor.star _, Presheaf.isSheaf_unit _⟩⟩
123	
124	namespace Sheaf
125	
126	/-- The forgetful functor from sheaves to presheaves.
127	-/
128	def forget : TopCat.Sheaf C X ⥤ TopCat.Presheaf C X :=
129	  sheafToPresheaf _ _
130	
131	-- The following instances should be constructed by a deriving handler.
132	-- https://github.com/leanprover-community/mathlib4/issues/380
133	
134	instance forget_full : (forget C X).Full where
135	  map_surjective f := ⟨ObjectProperty.homMk f, rfl⟩
136	
137	instance forgetFaithful : (forget C X).Faithful where
138	  map_injective := Sheaf.hom_ext
139	
140	-- Note: These can be proved by simp.
141	theorem id_app (F : Sheaf C X) (t) : (𝟙 F : F ⟶ F).1.app t = 𝟙 _ :=
142	  rfl
143	
144	theorem comp_app {F G H : Sheaf C X} (f : F ⟶ G) (g : G ⟶ H) (t) :
145	    (f ≫ g).1.app t = f.1.app t ≫ g.1.app t :=
146	  rfl
147	
148	end Sheaf

132	lemma GrothendieckTopology.preservesSheafification_iff_of_adjunctions
133	    (adj₂ : G₂ ⊣ sheafToPresheaf J B) :
134	    J.PreservesSheafification F ↔ ∀ (P : Cᵒᵖ ⥤ A),
135	      IsIso (G₂.map (whiskerRight (adj₁.unit.app P) F)) := by

137	  constructor

139	    apply W_of_preservesSheafification
140	    rw [J.W_iff_isIso_map_of_adjunction adj₁]
141	    infer_instance
142	  · intro h
143	    constructor
144	    intro P₁ P₂ f hf
145	    rw [J.W_iff_isIso_map_of_adjunction adj₁] at hf
146	    dsimp [MorphismProperty.inverseImage]
147	    rw [← (W _).postcomp_iff _ _ (h P₂), ← whiskerRight_comp]
148	    erw [adj₁.unit.naturality f]
149	    dsimp only [Functor.comp_map]
150	    rw [whiskerRight_comp, (W _).precomp_iff _ _ (h P₁)]
151	    apply ObjectProperty.isLocal_of_isIso

153	section HasSheafCompose
154	
155	variable (adj₂ : G₂ ⊣ sheafToPresheaf J B) [J.HasSheafCompose F]
156	
157	/-- The canonical natural transformation
158	`(whiskeringRight Cᵒᵖ A B).obj F ⋙ G₂ ⟶ G₁ ⋙ sheafCompose J F`
159	when `F : A ⥤ B` is such that `J.HasSheafCompose F`, and that `G₁` and `G₂` are
160	left adjoints to the forget functors `sheafToPresheaf`. -/
161	def sheafComposeNatTrans :
162	    (whiskeringRight Cᵒᵖ A B).obj F ⋙ G₂ ⟶ G₁ ⋙ sheafCompose J F where
163	  app P := (adj₂.homEquiv _ _).symm (whiskerRight (adj₁.unit.app P) F)
164	  naturality {P Q} f := by
165	    dsimp
166	    erw [← adj₂.homEquiv_naturality_left_symm,
167	      ← adj₂.homEquiv_naturality_right_symm]
168	    congr 1
169	    ext X
170	    have := NatTrans.congr_app (adj₁.unit.naturality f) X
171	    dsimp at this ⊢
172	    grind
173	
174	set_option backward.isDefEq.respectTransparency false in
175	lemma sheafComposeNatTrans_fac (P : Cᵒᵖ ⥤ A) :
176	    adj₂.unit.app (P ⋙ F) ≫
177	      (sheafToPresheaf J B).map ((sheafComposeNatTrans J F adj₁ adj₂).app P) =
178	        whiskerRight (adj₁.unit.app P) F := by
179	  simp [sheafComposeNatTrans, -ObjectProperty.ι_obj, -ObjectProperty.ι_map,
180	    Adjunction.homEquiv_counit]
181	
182	set_option backward.isDefEq.respectTransparency false in
183	lemma sheafComposeNatTrans_app_uniq (P : Cᵒᵖ ⥤ A)
184	    (α : G₂.obj (P ⋙ F) ⟶ (sheafCompose J F).obj (G₁.obj P))
185	    (hα : adj₂.unit.app (P ⋙ F) ≫ (sheafToPresheaf J B).map α =
186	        whiskerRight (adj₁.unit.app P) F) :
187	    α = (sheafComposeNatTrans J F adj₁ adj₂).app P := by
188	  apply (adj₂.homEquiv _ _).injective
189	  dsimp [sheafComposeNatTrans]
190	  erw [Equiv.apply_symm_apply]
191	  rw [← hα]
192	  apply adj₂.homEquiv_unit
193

195	lemma GrothendieckTopology.preservesSheafification_iff_of_adjunctions_of_hasSheafCompose :
196	    J.PreservesSheafification F ↔ IsIso (sheafComposeNatTrans J F adj₁ adj₂) := by
197	  rw [J.preservesSheafification_iff_of_adjunctions F adj₁ adj₂,

202	    ← sheafComposeNatTrans_fac J F adj₁ adj₂,

import Mathlib

open CategoryTheory CategoryTheory.Limits Functor

universe u

-- Try writing the PreservesFiniteLimits instance via the factorization trick.
instance my_pf {kbar : Type u} [Field kbar] {X : TopCat.{u}} :
    PreservesFiniteLimits (sheafCompose (Opens.grothendieckTopology X)
      (forget₂ (ModuleCat.{u} kbar) AddCommGrpCat.{u})) := by
  set Fforget := sheafCompose (Opens.grothendieckTopology X)
      (forget₂ (ModuleCat.{u} kbar) AddCommGrpCat.{u}) with hF
  set J := Opens.grothendieckTopology X with hJ
  -- factorization equality (already verified to hold via rfl)
  have heq : (Fforget ⋙ sheafToPresheaf J AddCommGrpCat.{u}) =
      (sheafToPresheaf J (ModuleCat.{u} kbar) ⋙
        (Functor.whiskeringRight _ _ _).obj (forget₂ (ModuleCat.{u} kbar) AddCommGrpCat.{u})) := rfl
  -- The right side preserves finite limits:
  haveI hcomp : PreservesFiniteLimits (Fforget ⋙ sheafToPresheaf J AddCommGrpCat.{u}) := by
    rw [heq]
    refine ⟨fun K _ _ => ?_⟩
    infer_instance
  -- sheafToPresheaf reflects all limits (fully faithful), so it reflects isomorphisms
  exact preservesFiniteLimits_of_reflects_of_preserves _ (sheafToPresheaf J AddCommGrpCat.{u})

1	/-
2	Copyright (c) 2024 Joël Riou. All rights reserved.
3	Released under Apache 2.0 license as described in the file LICENSE.
4	Authors: Joël Riou
5	-/
6	module
7	
8	public import Mathlib.CategoryTheory.Sites.Localization
9	public import Mathlib.CategoryTheory.Sites.CompatibleSheafification
10	public import Mathlib.CategoryTheory.Sites.Whiskering
11	public import Mathlib.CategoryTheory.Sites.Sheafification
12	
13	/-! # Functors which preserve sheafification
14	
15	In this file, given a Grothendieck topology `J` on `C` and `F : A ⥤ B`,
16	we define a type class `J.PreservesSheafification F`. We say that `F` preserves
17	the sheafification if whenever a morphism of presheaves `P₁ ⟶ P₂` induces
18	an isomorphism on the associated sheaves, then the induced map `P₁ ⋙ F ⟶ P₂ ⋙ F`
19	also induces an isomorphism on the associated sheaves. (Note: it suffices to check
20	this property for the map from any presheaf `P` to its associated sheaf, see
21	`GrothendieckTopology.preservesSheafification_iff_of_adjunctions`).
22	
23	In general, we define `Sheaf.composeAndSheafify J F : Sheaf J A ⥤ Sheaf J B` as the functor
24	which sends a sheaf `G` to the sheafification of the composition `G.val ⋙ F`.
25	If `J.PreservesSheafification F`, we show that this functor can also be thought of
26	as the localization of the functor `_ ⋙ F` on presheaves: we construct an isomorphism
27	`presheafToSheafCompComposeAndSheafifyIso` between
28	`presheafToSheaf J A ⋙ Sheaf.composeAndSheafify J F` and
29	`(whiskeringRight Cᵒᵖ A B).obj F ⋙ presheafToSheaf J B`.
30	
31	Moreover, if we assume `J.HasSheafCompose F`, we obtain an isomorphism
32	`sheafifyComposeIso J F P : sheafify J (P ⋙ F) ≅ sheafify J P ⋙ F`.
33	
34	We show that under suitable assumptions, the forgetful functor from a concrete
35	category preserves sheafification; this holds more generally for
36	functors between such concrete categories which commute both with
37	suitable limits and colimits.
38	
39	## TODO
40	* construct an isomorphism `Sheaf.composeAndSheafify J F ≅ sheafCompose J F`
41	
42	-/
43	
44	@[expose] public section
45	
46	universe v u
47	
48	namespace CategoryTheory
49	
50	open Category Limits Functor
51	
52	variable {C : Type u} [Category.{v} C] (J : GrothendieckTopology C)
53	  {A B : Type*} [Category* A] [Category* B] (F : A ⥤ B)
54	
55	namespace GrothendieckTopology
56	
57	/-- A functor `F : A ⥤ B` preserves the sheafification for the Grothendieck
58	topology `J` on a category `C` if whenever a morphism of presheaves `f : P₁ ⟶ P₂`
59	in `Cᵒᵖ ⥤ A` is such that becomes an iso after sheafification, then it is
60	also the case of `whiskerRight f F : P₁ ⋙ F ⟶ P₂ ⋙ F`. -/
61	class PreservesSheafification : Prop where
62	  le : J.W ≤ J.W.inverseImage ((whiskeringRight Cᵒᵖ A B).obj F)
63	
64	variable [PreservesSheafification J F]
65	
66	lemma W_of_preservesSheafification
67	    {P₁ P₂ : Cᵒᵖ ⥤ A} (f : P₁ ⟶ P₂) (hf : J.W f) :
68	    J.W (whiskerRight f F) :=
69	  PreservesSheafification.le _ hf
70	
71	variable [HasWeakSheafify J B]
72	
73	lemma W_isInvertedBy_whiskeringRight_presheafToSheaf :
74	    J.W.IsInvertedBy (((whiskeringRight Cᵒᵖ A B).obj F) ⋙ presheafToSheaf J B) := by
75	  intro P₁ P₂ f hf
76	  dsimp
77	  rw [← W_iff]
78	  exact J.W_of_preservesSheafification F _ hf
79	
80	end GrothendieckTopology
81	
82	section
83	
84	variable [HasWeakSheafify J B]
85	
86	/-- This is the functor sending a sheaf `X : Sheaf J A` to the sheafification
87	of `X.val ⋙ F`. -/
88	noncomputable abbrev Sheaf.composeAndSheafify : Sheaf J A ⥤ Sheaf J B :=
89	  sheafToPresheaf J A ⋙ (whiskeringRight _ _ _).obj F ⋙ presheafToSheaf J B
90	
91	variable [HasWeakSheafify J A]
92	
93	/-- The canonical natural transformation from
94	`(whiskeringRight Cᵒᵖ A B).obj F ⋙ presheafToSheaf J B` to
95	`presheafToSheaf J A ⋙ Sheaf.composeAndSheafify J F`. -/
96	@[simps!]
97	noncomputable def toPresheafToSheafCompComposeAndSheafify :
98	    (whiskeringRight Cᵒᵖ A B).obj F ⋙ presheafToSheaf J B ⟶
99	      presheafToSheaf J A ⋙ Sheaf.composeAndSheafify J F :=
100	  whiskerRight (sheafificationAdjunction J A).unit
101	    ((whiskeringRight _ _ _).obj F ⋙ presheafToSheaf J B)
102	
103	variable [J.PreservesSheafification F]
104	
105	instance : IsIso (toPresheafToSheafCompComposeAndSheafify J F) := by
106	  rw [NatTrans.isIso_iff_isIso_app]
107	  intro X
108	  dsimp
109	  simpa only [← J.W_iff] using J.W_of_preservesSheafification F _ (J.W_toSheafify X)
110	
111	/-- The canonical isomorphism between `presheafToSheaf J A ⋙ Sheaf.composeAndSheafify J F`
112	and `(whiskeringRight Cᵒᵖ A B).obj F ⋙ presheafToSheaf J B` when `F : A ⥤ B`
113	preserves sheafification. -/
114	@[simps! inv_app]
115	noncomputable def presheafToSheafCompComposeAndSheafifyIso :
116	    presheafToSheaf J A ⋙ Sheaf.composeAndSheafify J F ≅
117	      (whiskeringRight Cᵒᵖ A B).obj F ⋙ presheafToSheaf J B :=
118	  (asIso (toPresheafToSheafCompComposeAndSheafify J F)).symm
119	
120	noncomputable instance : Localization.Lifting (presheafToSheaf J A) J.W
121	    ((whiskeringRight Cᵒᵖ A B).obj F ⋙ presheafToSheaf J B) (Sheaf.composeAndSheafify J F) :=
122	  ⟨presheafToSheafCompComposeAndSheafifyIso J F⟩
123	
124	end
125	
126	section
127	
128	variable {G₁ : (Cᵒᵖ ⥤ A) ⥤ Sheaf J A} (adj₁ : G₁ ⊣ sheafToPresheaf J A)
129	  {G₂ : (Cᵒᵖ ⥤ B) ⥤ Sheaf J B}

lemma isLocallyInjective_of_whisker (hH : CoverPreserving J K H)
    [H.IsCoverDense K] [IsLocallyInjective J (whiskerLeft H.op f)] : IsLocallyInjective K f where
  equalizerSieve_mem {X} a b h := by
    apply K.transitive (H.is_cover_of_isCoverDense K X.unop)
    intro Y g ⟨⟨Z, lift, m, fac⟩⟩
    rw [← fac, Sieve.pullback_comp]
    apply K.pullback_stable
    refine K.superset_covering (Sieve.functorPullback_pushforward_le H _) ?_
    refine K.superset_covering (Sieve.functorPushforward_monotone H _ ?_)
      (hH.cover_preserve <| equalizerSieve_mem J (whiskerLeft H.op f)
        (F.map m.op a) (F.map m.op b) ?_)
--
  ⟨fun _ ↦ isLocallyInjective_of_whisker J K H f hH,
    fun _ ↦ isLocallyInjective_whisker J K H f⟩

lemma isLocallySurjective_whisker [H.IsCocontinuous J K] [IsLocallySurjective K f] :
    IsLocallySurjective J (whiskerLeft H.op f) where
  imageSieve_mem a := H.cover_lift J K (imageSieve_mem K f a)

lemma isLocallySurjective_of_whisker (hH : CoverPreserving J K H)
    [H.IsCoverDense K] [IsLocallySurjective J (whiskerLeft H.op f)] : IsLocallySurjective K f where
  imageSieve_mem {X} a := by
    apply K.transitive (H.is_cover_of_isCoverDense K X)
    intro Y g ⟨⟨Z, lift, m, fac⟩⟩
    rw [← fac, Sieve.pullback_comp]
    apply K.pullback_stable
    have hh := hH.cover_preserve <| imageSieve_mem J (whiskerLeft H.op f) (G.map m.op a)
    refine K.superset_covering (Sieve.functorPullback_pushforward_le H _) ?_
    refine K.superset_covering (Sieve.functorPushforward_monotone H _ ?_) hh
    intro W q ⟨x, h⟩
--

import Mathlib

open CategoryTheory CategoryTheory.Limits Functor Presheaf

universe u

example {kbar : Type u} [Field kbar] {X : TopCat.{u}}
    {P₁ P₂ : (TopologicalSpace.Opens X)ᵒᵖ ⥤ ModuleCat.{u} kbar} (f : P₁ ⟶ P₂)
    [IsLocallyInjective (Opens.grothendieckTopology X) f] :
    IsLocallyInjective (Opens.grothendieckTopology X)
      (whiskerRight f (forget₂ (ModuleCat.{u} kbar) AddCommGrpCat.{u})) := by
  -- Use forget_iff: locally injective iff after forget (to types)
  rw [← isLocallyInjective_forget_iff (D := AddCommGrpCat.{u})]
  -- (whiskerRight f forget₂) ⋙ forget = whiskerRight f (forget₂ ⋙ forget) = whiskerRight f (forget _)
  -- forget₂ ⋙ forget AddCommGrpCat = forget (ModuleCat) via HasForget₂
  have h := HasForget₂.forget_comp (C := ModuleCat.{u} kbar) (D := AddCommGrpCat.{u})
  -- h : forget₂ ⋙ forget = forget
  have heq : whiskerRight (whiskerRight f (forget₂ (ModuleCat.{u} kbar) AddCommGrpCat.{u}))
      (forget AddCommGrpCat.{u}) =
      whiskerRight f (forget (ModuleCat.{u} kbar)) := by
    rw [← whiskerRight_comp]
    congr 1
  rw [heq]
  -- locally injective after forget to types iff locally injective
  rw [isLocallyInjective_forget_iff (D := ModuleCat.{u} kbar)]
  infer_instance

import Mathlib

open CategoryTheory CategoryTheory.Limits Functor Presheaf

universe u

-- Provide a project-local instance for PreservesFiniteLimits (sheafCompose ... forget₂)
-- Then ask for PreservesHomology
instance my_sheafCompose_pf {kbar : Type u} [Field kbar] {X : TopCat.{u}} :
    PreservesFiniteLimits (sheafCompose (Opens.grothendieckTopology X)
      (forget₂ (ModuleCat.{u} kbar) AddCommGrpCat.{u})) := by
  have heq :
    (sheafCompose (Opens.grothendieckTopology X)
        (forget₂ (ModuleCat.{u} kbar) AddCommGrpCat.{u}) ⋙
      sheafToPresheaf (Opens.grothendieckTopology X) AddCommGrpCat.{u}) =
    (sheafToPresheaf (Opens.grothendieckTopology X) (ModuleCat.{u} kbar) ⋙
      (Functor.whiskeringRight _ _ _).obj
        (forget₂ (ModuleCat.{u} kbar) AddCommGrpCat.{u})) := rfl
  haveI hcomp : PreservesFiniteLimits
      (sheafCompose (Opens.grothendieckTopology X)
        (forget₂ (ModuleCat.{u} kbar) AddCommGrpCat.{u}) ⋙
      sheafToPresheaf (Opens.grothendieckTopology X) AddCommGrpCat.{u}) := by
    rw [heq]
    refine ⟨fun K _ _ => ?_⟩
    infer_instance
  exact preservesFiniteLimits_of_reflects_of_preserves _
    (sheafToPresheaf (Opens.grothendieckTopology X) AddCommGrpCat.{u})

-- Try to see if there's some auto-instance now

    (hS : S.Exact) :
    (S.map (sheafCompose (Opens.grothendieckTopology X)
      (forget₂ (ModuleCat.{u} kbar) AddCommGrpCat.{u}))).Exact := by
  exact hS.map _

import Mathlib

open CategoryTheory CategoryTheory.Limits Functor Presheaf

universe u

-- I'll try a direct approach using `isLocallyInjective` via composing through `forget`.
-- Key fact: forget₂ ModuleCat AddCommGrpCat composed with forget AddCommGrpCat = forget ModuleCat.
example {kbar : Type u} [Field kbar] {X : TopCat.{u}}
    {P₁ P₂ : (TopologicalSpace.Opens X)ᵒᵖ ⥤ ModuleCat.{u} kbar} (f : P₁ ⟶ P₂)
    (h_li : IsLocallyInjective (Opens.grothendieckTopology X) f) :
    IsLocallyInjective (Opens.grothendieckTopology X)
      (whiskerRight f (forget₂ (ModuleCat.{u} kbar) AddCommGrpCat.{u})) := by
  haveI := h_li
  -- locally injective iff locally injective after forget (to types)
  rw [← isLocallyInjective_forget_iff (D := AddCommGrpCat.{u})]
  -- whiskerRight (whiskerRight f forget₂) forget = whiskerRight f (forget₂ ⋙ forget)
  -- = whiskerRight f (forget ModuleCat)
  have heq : (whiskerRight (whiskerRight f (forget₂ (ModuleCat.{u} kbar) AddCommGrpCat.{u}))
      (forget AddCommGrpCat.{u})) =
      whiskerRight f (forget (ModuleCat.{u} kbar)) := by
    ext U y
    rfl
  rw [heq]
  infer_instance  -- IsLocallyInjective J (whiskerRight f (forget ModuleCat)) from h_li

import Mathlib

open CategoryTheory CategoryTheory.Limits Functor Presheaf

universe u

example {kbar : Type u} [Field kbar] {X : TopCat.{u}}
    {P₁ P₂ : (TopologicalSpace.Opens X)ᵒᵖ ⥤ ModuleCat.{u} kbar} (f : P₁ ⟶ P₂)
    [hf : IsLocallyInjective (Opens.grothendieckTopology X) f] :
    IsLocallyInjective (Opens.grothendieckTopology X)
      (whiskerRight f (forget₂ (ModuleCat.{u} kbar) AddCommGrpCat.{u})) := by
  -- Get IsLocallyInjective of whisker with forget (to types)
  haveI : IsLocallyInjective (Opens.grothendieckTopology X)
      (whiskerRight f (forget (ModuleCat.{u} kbar))) := inferInstance
  -- Show heq: the equation between two whiskerings
  have heq : (whiskerRight (whiskerRight f (forget₂ (ModuleCat.{u} kbar) AddCommGrpCat.{u}))
      (forget AddCommGrpCat.{u})) =
      whiskerRight f (forget (ModuleCat.{u} kbar)) := by
    ext U y
    rfl
  -- Use the iff lemma to convert  
  rw [← isLocallyInjective_forget_iff (D := AddCommGrpCat.{u}), heq]
