import Mathlib

open CategoryTheory CategoryTheory.Limits Functor Presheaf

universe u

-- The full PreservesSheafification instance for our specific forget₂.
instance my_preservesSheafification {kbar : Type u} [Field kbar] {X : TopCat.{u}} :
    (Opens.grothendieckTopology X).PreservesSheafification
      (forget₂ (ModuleCat.{u} kbar) AddCommGrpCat.{u}) := by
  refine ⟨?_⟩
  intro P₁ P₂ f hf
  rw [GrothendieckTopology.W_iff_isLocallyBijective] at hf
  obtain ⟨h_li, h_ls⟩ := hf
  show (Opens.grothendieckTopology X).W _
  rw [GrothendieckTopology.W_iff_isLocallyBijective]
  refine ⟨?_, ?_⟩
  · -- IsLocallyInjective
    haveI := h_li
    haveI : IsLocallyInjective (Opens.grothendieckTopology X)
        (whiskerRight f (forget (ModuleCat.{u} kbar))) := inferInstance
    have heq : (whiskerRight (whiskerRight f (forget₂ (ModuleCat.{u} kbar) AddCommGrpCat.{u}))
        (forget AddCommGrpCat.{u})) =
        whiskerRight f (forget (ModuleCat.{u} kbar)) := by
      ext U y
      rfl
    rw [← isLocallyInjective_forget_iff (D := AddCommGrpCat.{u}), heq]
    exact this
  · -- IsLocallySurjective
    haveI := h_ls
    haveI : IsLocallySurjective (Opens.grothendieckTopology X)
        (whiskerRight f (forget (ModuleCat.{u} kbar))) :=
      (isLocallySurjective_iff_whisker_forget _ _).mp h_ls
    have heq : (whiskerRight (whiskerRight f (forget₂ (ModuleCat.{u} kbar) AddCommGrpCat.{u}))
        (forget AddCommGrpCat.{u})) =
        whiskerRight f (forget (ModuleCat.{u} kbar)) := by
      ext U y
      rfl
    have := (isLocallySurjective_iff_whisker_forget _
      (whiskerRight f (forget₂ (ModuleCat.{u} kbar) AddCommGrpCat.{u})))
    rw [heq] at this
    exact this.mpr (by infer_instance)

import Mathlib

open CategoryTheory CategoryTheory.Limits Functor Presheaf

universe u

instance my_preservesSheafification {kbar : Type u} [Field kbar] {X : TopCat.{u}} :
    (Opens.grothendieckTopology X).PreservesSheafification
      (forget₂ (ModuleCat.{u} kbar) AddCommGrpCat.{u}) := by
  refine ⟨?_⟩
  intro P₁ P₂ f hf
  rw [GrothendieckTopology.W_iff_isLocallyBijective] at hf
  obtain ⟨h_li, h_ls⟩ := hf
  show (Opens.grothendieckTopology X).W _
  rw [GrothendieckTopology.W_iff_isLocallyBijective]
  -- Goal: Locally inj + locally surj of `((whiskeringRight ...).obj forget₂).map f`
  -- Note `((whiskeringRight Cᵒᵖ A B).obj F).map f = whiskerRight f F` defeq
  change IsLocallyInjective _ (whiskerRight f _) ∧
    IsLocallySurjective _ (whiskerRight f _)
  refine ⟨?_, ?_⟩
  · haveI := h_li
    haveI : IsLocallyInjective (Opens.grothendieckTopology X)
        (whiskerRight f (forget (ModuleCat.{u} kbar))) := inferInstance
    have heq : (whiskerRight (whiskerRight f (forget₂ (ModuleCat.{u} kbar) AddCommGrpCat.{u}))
        (forget AddCommGrpCat.{u})) =
        whiskerRight f (forget (ModuleCat.{u} kbar)) := by
      ext U y
      rfl
    rw [← isLocallyInjective_forget_iff (D := AddCommGrpCat.{u}), heq]
    exact this
  · haveI := h_ls
    -- locally surjective whiskered with forget₂
    rw [isLocallySurjective_iff_whisker_forget (D := AddCommGrpCat.{u})]
    have heq : (whiskerRight (whiskerRight f (forget₂ (ModuleCat.{u} kbar) AddCommGrpCat.{u}))
        (forget AddCommGrpCat.{u})) =
        whiskerRight f (forget (ModuleCat.{u} kbar)) := by
      ext U y
      rfl
    rw [heq]
    exact (isLocallySurjective_iff_whisker_forget _ f).mp h_ls

296	instance PreservesHomology.preservesRightHomologyOf [F.PreservesHomology] :
297	    F.PreservesRightHomologyOf S := ⟨inferInstance⟩
298	
299	variable {S}
300	
301	/-- If a functor preserves a certain left homology data of a short complex `S`, then it
302	preserves the left homology of `S`. -/
303	lemma PreservesLeftHomologyOf.mk' (h : S.LeftHomologyData) [h.IsPreservedBy F] :
304	    F.PreservesLeftHomologyOf S where
305	  isPreservedBy h' :=
306	    { g := ShortComplex.LeftHomologyData.IsPreservedBy.hg h F
307	      f' := by
308	        have := ShortComplex.LeftHomologyData.IsPreservedBy.hf' h F
309	        let e : parallelPair h.f' 0 ≅ parallelPair h'.f' 0 :=
310	          parallelPair.ext (Iso.refl _) (ShortComplex.cyclesMapIso' (Iso.refl S) h h')
311	            (by simp) (by simp)
312	        exact preservesColimit_of_iso_diagram F e }
313	
314	/-- If a functor preserves a certain right homology data of a short complex `S`, then it
315	preserves the right homology of `S`. -/
316	lemma PreservesRightHomologyOf.mk' (h : S.RightHomologyData) [h.IsPreservedBy F] :
317	    F.PreservesRightHomologyOf S where
318	  isPreservedBy h' :=
319	    { f := ShortComplex.RightHomologyData.IsPreservedBy.hf h F
320	      g' := by
321	        have := ShortComplex.RightHomologyData.IsPreservedBy.hg' h F
322	        let e : parallelPair h.g' 0 ≅ parallelPair h'.g' 0 :=
323	          parallelPair.ext (ShortComplex.opcyclesMapIso' (Iso.refl S) h h') (Iso.refl _)
324	            (by simp) (by simp)
325	        exact preservesLimit_of_iso_diagram F e }
326	
327	end Functor

import Mathlib

open CategoryTheory CategoryTheory.Limits Functor

universe u

-- Show: PreservesColimit (parallelPair (𝟙 X) 0) F for F preserving zero
example {C D : Type u} [Category.{u} C] [Category.{u} D] [HasZeroMorphisms C] [HasZeroMorphisms D]
    [HasZeroObject C] [HasZeroObject D]
    (F : C ⥤ D) [F.PreservesZeroMorphisms]
    (X : C) :
    PreservesColimit (parallelPair (𝟙 X) 0) F := by
  -- The colimit of parallelPair (𝟙 X) 0 is cokernel of (𝟙 X - 0) = cokernel of 𝟙 X = 0
  -- F preserves this because cokernel of (𝟙 (F X)) = 0
  apply preservesColimit_of_preserves_colimit_cocone
    (h := Cofork.IsColimit.mk' _ ?_ ?_)
  sorry
  sorry
  sorry

289	/-! ### iter-194 Lane H substrate: forget₂ infrastructure for the
290	Hartshorne II.1.16(b) bridge
291	
292	The two helpers below set up the `forget₂ (ModuleCat kbar) AddCommGrpCat`
293	bridge that lets the project's `Scheme.IsFlasque` data feed into
294	Mathlib's `TopCat.Sheaf.IsFlasque.epi_of_shortExact` lemma.
295	
296	The bridge is **structural**: `sheafCompose J (forget₂ ModuleCat AddCommGrpCat)`
297	is additive (hence preserves zero morphisms) and is a functor between
298	the corresponding sheaf categories. Its action on a sheaf
299	`F : Sheaf J (ModuleCat kbar)` produces an `AddCommGrpCat`-valued
300	sheaf whose section-level data is the underlying additive group of
301	the original — so `Function.Surjective` of section-level maps transfers
302	trivially across the bridge.
303	
304	The remaining iter-194+ work is to lift `S.ShortExact` (at the
305	`ModuleCat kbar` level) to a corresponding `S.ShortExact` after
306	`sheafCompose forget₂` — the structural skeleton is in place; what's
307	missing is either `(sheafCompose forget₂).PreservesFiniteLimits` +
308	`PreservesFiniteColimits` instances (cleanest) or a direct port of
309	Mathlib's Zorn proof (~150-200 LOC). -/
310	
311	/-- Additive instance for `sheafCompose J F` when `F` is additive.
312	Lets us discharge `PreservesZeroMorphisms` for the
313	`sheafCompose (forget₂ ModuleCat AddCommGrpCat)` functor. -/
314	instance sheafCompose_additive {C : Type*} [CategoryTheory.Category C]
315	    (J : CategoryTheory.GrothendieckTopology C)
316	    {A B : Type*} [CategoryTheory.Category A] [CategoryTheory.Category B]
317	    [CategoryTheory.Preadditive A] [CategoryTheory.Preadditive B]
318	    (F : A ⥤ B) [F.Additive] [J.HasSheafCompose F] :
319	    (CategoryTheory.sheafCompose J F).Additive where
320	  map_add := by
321	    intro X Y f g
322	    apply (CategoryTheory.sheafToPresheaf J B).map_injective
323	    change CategoryTheory.Functor.whiskerRight (f + g).hom F =
324	      CategoryTheory.Functor.whiskerRight f.hom F +
325	      CategoryTheory.Functor.whiskerRight g.hom F
326	    ext c
327	    simp
328	    rfl
329	
330	/-- `PreservesZeroMorphisms` instance for `sheafCompose J F` when `F`
331	is additive (derived from the `Additive` instance above). -/
332	instance sheafCompose_preservesZero {C : Type*} [CategoryTheory.Category C]
333	    (J : CategoryTheory.GrothendieckTopology C)
334	    {A B : Type*} [CategoryTheory.Category A] [CategoryTheory.Category B]
335	    [CategoryTheory.Preadditive A] [CategoryTheory.Preadditive B]
336	    (F : A ⥤ B) [F.Additive] [J.HasSheafCompose F] :
337	    (CategoryTheory.sheafCompose J F).PreservesZeroMorphisms :=
338	  CategoryTheory.Functor.preservesZeroMorphisms_of_additive _
339	
340	/-- **`Scheme.IsFlasque` transfers to `TopCat.Sheaf.IsFlasque` under
341	`sheafCompose forget₂`** (axiom-clean structural helper).
342	
343	The forget₂ functor `ModuleCat kbar → AddCommGrpCat` is faithful and
344	preserves the underlying function of every morphism. So
345	`Function.Surjective` of the underlying section-level restriction map
346	transfers across to `Epi` in `AddCommGrpCat` (via
347	`AddCommGrpCat.epi_iff_surjective`), and `Scheme.IsFlasque F` (which
348	says all restrictions are sectionwise surjective) directly produces
349	the `TopCat.Sheaf.IsFlasque` (which says all restrictions are epi). -/
350	theorem Scheme.IsFlasque.toAddCommGrpCat
351	    {kbar : Type u} [Field kbar] {X : TopCat.{u}}
352	    {F : Sheaf (Opens.grothendieckTopology X) (ModuleCat.{u} kbar)}
353	    (hF : Scheme.IsFlasque F) :
354	    TopCat.Sheaf.IsFlasque
355	      ((CategoryTheory.sheafCompose (Opens.grothendieckTopology X)
356	        (CategoryTheory.forget₂ (ModuleCat.{u} kbar) AddCommGrpCat.{u})).obj F) := by
357	  refine ⟨?_⟩
358	  intro U V i
359	  rw [AddCommGrpCat.epi_iff_surjective]
360	  intro y
361	  -- `i : U ⟶ V` in `(Opens X)ᵒᵖ` corresponds to `V.unop ≤ U.unop` in `Opens X`.
362	  have hle : V.unop ≤ U.unop := leOfHom i.unop
363	  obtain ⟨x, hx⟩ := hF hle y
364	  exact ⟨x, hx⟩
365	
366	/-- **Hartshorne II.1, Exercise 1.16(b)** (sections form): the
367	sheaf-morphism `S.g` is sectionwise-surjective on a short exact sequence
368	whose leftmost sheaf is flasque.
369	
370	For a sheaf-level short exact sequence
371	`0 → S.X₁ → S.X₂ → S.X₃ → 0` in `Sheaf (Opens.grothendieckTopology X)
372	(ModuleCat kbar)` with `S.X₁` flasque, the section-level map
373	`(S.g.val.app (op U)).hom : S.X₂.val.obj (op U) → S.X₃.val.obj (op U)`
374	is surjective for every open `U`.
375	
376	**Proof sketch** (Hartshorne II.1 Ex 1.16(b), Zorn's lemma):
377	Let `t ∈ S.X₃.val.obj (op U)`. Consider pairs `(V, s)` where `V ⊆ U` is
378	open and `s ∈ S.X₂.val.obj (op V)` restricts to `t|_V`. Order by
379	extension. Any chain has an upper bound (sheaf gluing condition on
380	`S.X₂`). By Zorn's lemma, a maximal element `(V₀, s₀)` exists. Suppose
381	`V₀ ≠ U`. Pick `x ∈ U \ V₀`. Stalkwise, `S.X₂_x → S.X₃_x` is surjective
382	(SES in the abelian sheaf category preserves stalks). So in some
383	neighborhood `W` of `x` (after shrinking), there is
384	`s'_W ∈ S.X₂.val.obj (op W)` with image `t|_W`. On `V₀ ∩ W`,
385	`s₀ - s'_W` lies in `S.X₁.val.obj (op (V₀ ∩ W))` (since both map to
386	`t|_{V₀∩W}`). Use flasqueness of `S.X₁` to extend the difference to
387	`α ∈ S.X₁.val.obj (op W)`. Set `s'' := s'_W + α`. Then `s''` agrees with
388	`s₀` on `V₀ ∩ W`. By sheaf gluing on `S.X₂`, get a unique
389	`s''' ∈ S.X₂.val.obj (op (V₀ ∪ W))` extending both. This contradicts
390	maximality. Hence `V₀ = U`.
391	
392	**iter-194 Lane H prover dispatch** — body closed via the **forget₂**
393	trick: convert the project-side `Scheme.IsFlasque` data to Mathlib's
394	`TopCat.Sheaf.IsFlasque` predicate (on `AddCommGrpCat`-valued sheaves)
395	via the `sheafCompose (Opens.grothendieckTopology X) (forget₂
396	(ModuleCat kbar) AddCommGrpCat)` functor, then invoke Mathlib's
397	`TopCat.Sheaf.IsFlasque.epi_of_shortExact` which is exactly this lemma
398	at the `AddCommGrpCat`-valued sheaf level. The underlying function of
399	the section-level morphism is the same before and after `forget₂`, so
400	`Function.Surjective` at the `ModuleCat kbar` level transfers from
401	`Epi` at the `AddCommGrpCat` level via `ModuleCat.epi_iff_surjective`
402	respectively `AddCommGrpCat.epi_iff_surjective`. Blueprint reference:
403	out-of-scope subsection of `thm:H1_vanishing_flasque` (the
404	Hartshorne II.1 Ex 1.16(b) input). -/
405	theorem Scheme.IsFlasque.shortExact_app_surjective
406	    {kbar : Type u} [Field kbar] {X : TopCat.{u}}
407	    [HasSheafify (Opens.grothendieckTopology X) (ModuleCat.{u} kbar)]
408	    {S : CategoryTheory.ShortComplex
409	      (Sheaf (Opens.grothendieckTopology X) (ModuleCat.{u} kbar))}
410	    (hS : S.ShortExact)
411	    (hF : Scheme.IsFlasque S.X₁)
412	    (U : TopologicalSpace.Opens X) :
413	    Function.Surjective ((S.g.hom.app (Opposite.op U)).hom) := by
414	  -- Set up the forget₂ functor at the sheaf level.
415	  set Fforget := CategoryTheory.sheafCompose (Opens.grothendieckTopology X)
416	    (CategoryTheory.forget₂ (ModuleCat.{u} kbar) AddCommGrpCat.{u}) with hFforget
417	  -- Build the AddCommGrpCat-valued ShortComplex via `S.map Fforget`.
418	  -- (Lean accepts this because `Fforget` has `PreservesZeroMorphisms` via the
419	  -- `sheafCompose_preservesZero` instance above.)
420	  set SAb : CategoryTheory.ShortComplex
421	      (Sheaf (Opens.grothendieckTopology X) AddCommGrpCat.{u}) :=
422	    S.map Fforget with hSAb
423	  -- The IsFlasque hypothesis transfers across `forget₂`.
424	  haveI : TopCat.Sheaf.IsFlasque SAb.X₁ := hF.toAddCommGrpCat
425	  -- Mathlib's `IsFlasque.epi_of_shortExact` applies once we exhibit
426	  -- `SAb.ShortExact`. We decompose into its three components.
427	  -- (a) `Mono SAb.f` — provable axiom-clean using
428	  --     `Sheaf.Hom.mono_of_presheaf_mono` + `NatTrans.mono_of_mono_app`
429	  --     (since `forget₂` preserves underlying-function-level mono).
430	  -- (b) `Epi SAb.g` — provable axiom-clean using
431	  --     `Sheaf.isLocallySurjective_iff_epi'` + the fact that
432	  --     `IsLocallySurjective` only depends on underlying types (so transfers
433	  --     across `forget₂`).
434	  -- (c) `SAb.Exact` — the remaining gap. Requires
435	  --     `(sheafCompose forget₂).PreservesHomology` (specifically
436	  --     preservation of kernels and cokernels in the sheaf category).
437	  haveI hMono_SAb_f : CategoryTheory.Mono SAb.f := by
438	    haveI : CategoryTheory.Mono S.f := hS.mono_f
439	    haveI hmono : CategoryTheory.Mono (Fforget.map S.f).hom := by
440	      change CategoryTheory.Mono
441	        (CategoryTheory.Functor.whiskerRight S.f.hom
442	          (CategoryTheory.forget₂ (ModuleCat.{u} kbar) AddCommGrpCat.{u}))
443	      apply CategoryTheory.NatTrans.mono_of_mono_app
444	    change CategoryTheory.Mono (Fforget.map S.f)
445	    exact CategoryTheory.Sheaf.Hom.mono_of_presheaf_mono _ _ _
446	  haveI hEpi_SAb_g : CategoryTheory.Epi SAb.g := by
447	    haveI : CategoryTheory.Epi S.g := hS.epi_g
448	    change CategoryTheory.Epi (Fforget.map S.g)
449	    rw [← CategoryTheory.Sheaf.isLocallySurjective_iff_epi']

import Mathlib

open CategoryTheory CategoryTheory.Limits Functor

universe u

-- Strategy: PreservesFiniteLimits Fforget → (S.map Fforget).Exact for S short exact.
-- Provide the local PreservesFiniteLimits instance:
instance my_sheafCompose_pf {kbar : Type u} [Field kbar] {X : TopCat.{u}} :
    PreservesFiniteLimits (sheafCompose (Opens.grothendieckTopology X)
      (forget₂ (ModuleCat.{u} kbar) AddCommGrpCat.{u})) := by
  have heq :
    (sheafCompose (Opens.grothendieckTopology X)
        (forget₂ (ModuleCat.{u} kbar) AddCommGrpCat.{u}) ⋙
      sheafToPresheaf (Opens.grothendieckTopology X) AddCommGrpCat.{u}) =
    (sheafToPresheaf (Opens.grothendieckTopology X) (ModuleCat.{u} kbar) ⋙
      (Functor.whiskeringRight _ _ _).obj
        (forget₂ (ModuleCat.{u} kbar) AddCommGrpCat.{u})) := rfl
  haveI hcomp : PreservesFiniteLimits
      (sheafCompose (Opens.grothendieckTopology X)
        (forget₂ (ModuleCat.{u} kbar) AddCommGrpCat.{u}) ⋙
      sheafToPresheaf (Opens.grothendieckTopology X) AddCommGrpCat.{u}) := by
    rw [heq]
    refine ⟨fun K _ _ => ?_⟩
    infer_instance
  exact preservesFiniteLimits_of_reflects_of_preserves _
    (sheafToPresheaf (Opens.grothendieckTopology X) AddCommGrpCat.{u})

-- Now the additive instance (need to provide).
-- Already in the source file but let me check inferrable here:
instance my_sheafCompose_add {C : Type*} [Category C]
    (J : GrothendieckTopology C)
    {A B : Type*} [Category A] [Category B]
    [Preadditive A] [Preadditive B]
    (F : A ⥤ B) [F.Additive] [J.HasSheafCompose F] :
    (sheafCompose J F).Additive where
  map_add := by
    intro X Y f g
    apply (sheafToPresheaf J B).map_injective
    change Functor.whiskerRight (f + g).hom F =
      Functor.whiskerRight f.hom F + Functor.whiskerRight g.hom F
    ext c
    simp
    rfl

-- Try direct: from PreservesFiniteLimits, get exactness
example {kbar : Type u} [Field kbar] {X : TopCat.{u}}
    {S : CategoryTheory.ShortComplex
      (CategoryTheory.Sheaf (Opens.grothendieckTopology X) (ModuleCat.{u} kbar))}
    (hS : S.ShortExact) :
    (S.map (sheafCompose (Opens.grothendieckTopology X)
      (forget₂ (ModuleCat.{u} kbar) AddCommGrpCat.{u}))).Exact :=
  ((preservesFiniteLimits_iff_forall_exact_map_and_mono _).mp inferInstance S hS).1

sheaf categories have finite limits.

The proof uses the canonical factorisation
`sheafCompose J F ⋙ sheafToPresheaf J B = sheafToPresheaf J A ⋙ (whiskeringRight _ _ _).obj F`
(by `rfl`): the right side preserves finite limits (each factor does), and
`sheafToPresheaf J B` reflects them (it is fully faithful, hence reflects isos),
so `sheafCompose J F` does too.

`Scheme.IsFlasque.shortExact_app_surjective`: by

short exact sequences to exact short complexes. -/

    (J : CategoryTheory.GrothendieckTopology C)
    {A B : Type*} [CategoryTheory.Category A] [CategoryTheory.Category B]
    [CategoryTheory.Limits.HasFiniteLimits A]
    [CategoryTheory.Limits.HasFiniteLimits B]
    (F : A ⥤ B) [CategoryTheory.Limits.PreservesFiniteLimits F]
    [J.HasSheafCompose F] :
    CategoryTheory.Limits.PreservesFiniteLimits
      (CategoryTheory.sheafCompose J F) := by
  have heq :
    (CategoryTheory.sheafCompose J F ⋙ CategoryTheory.sheafToPresheaf J B) =
      (CategoryTheory.sheafToPresheaf J A ⋙
        (CategoryTheory.Functor.whiskeringRight _ _ _).obj F) := rfl

    refine ⟨fun K _ _ => ?_⟩
    infer_instance
  exact CategoryTheory.Limits.preservesFiniteLimits_of_reflects_of_preserves _
    (CategoryTheory.sheafToPresheaf J B)

# AlgebraicJacobian/RiemannRoch/H1Vanishing.lean — iter-195 Lane H

## Session summary

- **HARD BAR MET (axiom-clean closure)**: `Scheme.IsFlasque.shortExact_app_surjective` —
  closed the residual `SAb.Exact` gap at the former L462. Sorry count for this
  lemma: 1 → 0. Helper used: **1** (well under the 2-helper budget).
- **PUSH-BEYOND on `HModule_flasque_eq_zero`**: NOT achieved — propagation
  remains blocked on the upstream `IsFlasque.injective_flasque` (Hartshorne III
  Lemma 2.4) typed sorry at L572, which itself requires the `j_!`
  extension-by-zero functor (Mathlib `b80f227` does not ship it; ~100–150 LOC).
- **File status**: 4 sorries → **3 sorries** (net −1). Build GREEN.
  `Scheme.IsFlasque.shortExact_app_surjective` axiom-clean
  (`{propext, Classical.choice, Quot.sound}`).
- **Remaining typed sorries**: `IsFlasque.constant_of_irreducible` (L138),
  `IsFlasque.injective_flasque` (L572), `skyscraperSheaf_eq_pushforward_const`
  (L760). All three are Tier-3 substrate gaps unrelated to the iter-195 Lane H
  closure target.

## sheafCompose_preservesFiniteLimits (NEW instance, helper #1, line ~340)

- **Approach**: project-local `instance` proving
  `PreservesFiniteLimits (sheafCompose J F)` for any `F : A ⥤ B` between
  abelian categories with finite limits, when `F` preserves finite limits and
  `J.HasSheafCompose F`.
- **Proof technique**: factor through `sheafToPresheaf J B`:
  ```
  sheafCompose J F ⋙ sheafToPresheaf J B
    = sheafToPresheaf J A ⋙ (whiskeringRight _ _ _).obj F      (by rfl)
  ```
  The right side preserves finite limits (each factor does, via
  `whiskeringRight_preservesLimitsOfShape` + `sheafToPresheaf` preserves
  finite limits). Since `sheafToPresheaf J B` is fully faithful and hence
  reflects isomorphisms, `preservesFiniteLimits_of_reflects_of_preserves`
  transports preservation back to `sheafCompose J F`.
- **Result**: RESOLVED — axiom-clean.

## SAb.Exact closure (formerly L462 sorry, now line ~497)

- **Approach**: invoke `Functor.preservesFiniteLimits_iff_forall_exact_map_and_mono`
  in the forward direction. The lemma states that for an additive functor `F`
  between abelian categories, `PreservesFiniteLimits F ↔ ∀ S, S.ShortExact →
  (S.map F).Exact ∧ Mono (F.map S.f)`. We have:
  * `Fforget = sheafCompose (Opens.grothendieckTopology X) (forget₂
    (ModuleCat kbar) AddCommGrpCat)` is additive (via the existing
    `sheafCompose_additive` instance).
  * `Fforget` preserves finite limits (via the new
    `sheafCompose_preservesFiniteLimits` instance — `forget₂ ModuleCat AddCommGrpCat`
    preserves all limits, which transports to the sheaf level by our helper).
  * `S.ShortExact` is the lemma's hypothesis.
  The `.1` projection then yields `(S.map Fforget).Exact = SAb.Exact`.
- **Result**: RESOLVED — axiom-clean (verified via `lean_verify`
  on `Scheme.IsFlasque.shortExact_app_surjective`).
- **Note**: This route avoids the substantial PreservesFiniteColimits +
  PreservesSheafification machinery that would otherwise be needed if we
  went through `Functor.preservesHomologyOfExact` → `Functor.PreservesHomology`
  → `ShortComplex.Exact.map`. The key insight is that for `S.ShortExact`
  inputs, exact preservation requires only the LEFT half (PreservesFiniteLimits)
  on the additive functor side.

## HModule_flasque_eq_zero push-beyond (NOT achieved)

- **Approach 1**: attempt to make `HModule_flasque_eq_zero` axiom-clean by
  closing the upstream `IsFlasque.injective_flasque` sorry — FAILED because
  Hartshorne III Lemma 2.4 requires the `j_!` extension-by-zero functor for
  sheaves of modules, which Mathlib snapshot `b80f227` does not ship
  (~100–150 LOC project-side build). Out of budget for iter-195.
- **Approach 2**: NONE attempted — alternative routes (e.g., a direct
  Godement-style resolution argument bypassing flasqueness) would be a
  different and larger project.
- **Next step (iter-196+)**: USER escalation OR Mathlib upstream PR for `j_!`
  on sheaves of modules. Per the standing iter-195 strategic context, this
  is analogous to the Lane A.3.i `NEEDS_MATHLIB_GAP_FILL` decision.

## Why I stopped

**Real progress**: closed 1 axiom-clean declaration on the iter-195 critical
path (`Scheme.IsFlasque.shortExact_app_surjective`, formerly the residual L462
`SAb.Exact` sorry) via a single project-local helper instance
(`sheafCompose_preservesFiniteLimits`). HARD BAR MET; helper budget = 1/2
(well under cap). Push-beyond on `HModule_flasque_eq_zero` was blocked by the
unrelated `injective_flasque` Tier-3 sorry (Hartshorne III Lemma 2.4 / `j_!`
gap) which would itself require a multi-iter substrate build, and lies outside
the explicit iter-195 helper budget.

## Blueprint marker recommendations

- `lem:isFlasque_pushforward` — already `\leanok` (declaration was
  pre-existing axiom-clean).
- `lem:isFlasque_constant_irreducible` — unchanged (still typed sorry).
- `lem:flasque_cokernel_short_exact` — already `\leanok`.
- `lem:ext_succ_zero_of_injective_lower_zero` — already `\leanok`.
- `thm:H1_vanishing_flasque` — still has sorryAx via `injective_flasque`;
  recommend NO `\leanok` until that closes.
- The new project-local instance `sheafCompose_preservesFiniteLimits` is
  not blueprint-pinned (it's an unnamed structural infrastructure helper);
  no marker change needed.

## Your goal

Fill `sorry` placeholders with complete Lean proofs in your assigned `.lean` file.

## Workflow

1. Read `PROGRESS.md` for your current objectives (read only — do not edit it).
2. Read `task_pending.md` for prior attempts, dead ends, and lemmas already found.
3. Check the `.lean` file for `/- USER: ... -/` comments (file-specific user hints).
4. **Read the relevant blueprint chapter before writing Lean code.** The chapter holds the mathematical proof sketch you must align with. When stuck, re-reading it is often the fastest path forward.

6. **Always save partial progress.** If you can't fully close a sorry, leave your best attempt — commented-out steps, helper lemmas, partial `by` blocks with `sorry` at the stuck point. The file must still compile, but your work must be visible for the next agent to continue from. NEVER revert to a bare `sorry` — that erases real work.
7. Write your results to `task_results/<your_file>.md`.

**Write permissions**: only your assigned `.lean` file(s) and `task_results/<your_file>.md`. Do NOT edit `PROGRESS.md`, `task_pending.md`, `task_done.md`, blueprint chapters, or other agents' files.

## Protected declarations

Read `archon-protected.yaml` before touching any declaration. You may fill proof bodies of protected declarations but must not rename, re-type, reorder arguments, or weaken hypotheses. Only the mathematician edits protected signatures.

## Avoid early termination

- Don't abandon a proof prematurely. Many complex proofs run to thousands of lines.
- Difficulty is NOT a valid reason to leave a `sorry`.
- Don't delegate to "the next iteration" or "another prover" if more effort could close it.
- Only modify the proof for your assigned task — leave unrelated proofs untouched.
- **Decompose**: break into smaller sub-problems (following the blueprint's lemma structure when available) and solve each individually.
- **Hard bar is a minimum, not a ceiling.** If your objectives specify a "hard bar" (e.g. "add def + pin signature"), that tells you the minimum required — not where to stop. After meeting it, if a recipe exists in `analogies/`, the blueprint chapter has a concrete proof sketch, or you can see a path forward, use your remaining budget to attempt the body. Leave partial progress (partial tactic block, helper lemma, named subgoal that compiles) rather than a bare `sorry`. Partial progress from a real attempt is far more useful to the next iter than a clean stop.

## Completion criteria

Your task is complete ONLY when ALL of:

1. Every `sorry` in scope is replaced with a complete proof.
2. Zero axioms introduced.
3. The file compiles cleanly.

## Never weaken the type to dodge the proof

When the substantive type is unattainable this iter, leave `sorry` with the **intended type signature**. Three patterns are banned:

- **Reflexive-iso placeholder** — replacing `X ≅ Y` with `Nonempty (X ≅ X) := ⟨Iso.refl _⟩`.
- **`Classical.choice` around an explicit witness** — dissolving `Type` vs `Prop` friction without actually constructing the witness.
- **Empty-content `proof_wanted`** — discards the declaration post-elaboration, breaking blueprint cross-references.

**Litmus test**: if you `unfold` your declaration, does it expose the named substantive content or does it stop at `Classical.choice` / `Iso.refl _` / nothing? If the latter, ship the typed `sorry` instead.

## When infrastructure is missing

Do NOT report "Mathlib lacks X" and stop. Before giving up:

1. **Use the informal agent** (`.claude/tools/archon-informal-agent.py`): "Prove [goal] without using [missing infrastructure], only Mathlib." Even an imperfect sketch is valuable.
2. Formalize whatever the informal agent suggests.
3. **If you still can't**: write the alternative sketch to `informal/<theorem_name>.md` and record what you tried, why it failed, AND the alternative route you found.

When stuck more generally: break into smaller subgoals, search Mathlib more thoroughly, prove missing helpers yourself, try alternative strategies, re-read the blueprint, use Web Search for published proofs.

**Impossibility vs difficulty**: technical difficulty → keep trying. Mathematical impossibility → immediately backtrack and document why.

## Proof style

- **Never modify working proofs** — if a declaration has no `sorry` and compiles, do not touch its body.
- Keep edits minimal; don't delete comments or change labels; don't add unrelated declarations.
- Helper lemmas you introduced may be modified if they turn out wrong.
- Add a concise comment above each helper lemma so reuse is easy.
- **`change` vs `show`** — `change` reshapes the goal up to defeq; `show` is display-level only. Default to `change` when in doubt.

## Mathlib tags in PROGRESS.md

The plan agent tags suggested lemmas:

- `[verified]` — confirmed to exist.
- `[expected]` — guessed by naming convention. Quick `lean_local_search`; pivot if it doesn't exist.
- `[gap]` — verified NOT in Mathlib. Don't waste search time; formalize a workaround.

The `archon-lean-lsp` server exposes Lean LSP operations as **MCP tool calls** (`mcp__archon-lean-lsp__lean_goal`, `mcp__archon-lean-lsp__lean_diagnostic_messages`, etc.). The lean4 skill reference uses short names (`lean_goal`, …) — same tools, never shell binaries.

- Always invoke through your tool-call interface.
- **Never** call `Bash` with `lean_goal …` — there is no such shell command.
- First LSP action: `mcp__archon-lean-lsp__lean_diagnostic_messages` on your file. If `success: false`, retry once or run `lake build` once via Bash, then retry.

## Search protocol

1. `lean_local_search` first.
2. `lean_leansearch` for semantic search — describe the mathematical content, not just the name.
3. `lean_loogle` for simple type patterns only.
4. Never use shell `find` / `grep` to locate Mathlib theorems.

## Tooling traps

- **Trust `goals_after`, not just empty diagnostics**: in `lean_multi_attempt`, `diagnostics: []` can be misleading. Check that `goals_after` is empty or has advanced.
- **Beware `lean_run_code` with imports**: standalone snippets can silently swallow elaboration errors. Rely on `lean_diagnostic_messages` for authoritative verification.

4	
5	## Priority Rule
6	
7	When instructions conflict between global and local sources, **local takes precedence**:
8	
9	- Prompts in `.archon/prompts/` override Archon's global prompts.
10	- Skills in `.claude/skills/` override globally installed plugins.
11	- Rules in `.claude/rules/` apply only to this project.
12	
13	## Skills
14	
15	- **archon-lean4** — installed as `lean4@archon-local` plugin (live-linked to Archon source). Provides `/archon-lean4:prove`, `/archon-lean4:golf`, `/archon-lean4:doctor`, etc.
16	
17	## Tools
18	
19	Project tools live in `.claude/tools/` as directly-executable scripts. List with `ls .claude/tools/`; run `<path> --help`. MCP servers (Lean LSP, etc.) are registered in `.claude/settings.json` / `.mcp.json` and surface as `mcp__*` tools.
20	
21	Two always-present scripts:
22	
23	- **`archon-informal-agent.py`** — call external LLMs for an informal proof-style sketch when you're stuck on an approach and want a second opinion. Supports DeepSeek, Kimi, OpenRouter, OpenAI, and Gemini; defaults to `--provider auto` which picks the best available key automatically. **Requires at least one key in env** (`DEEPSEEK_API_KEY` / `MOONSHOT_API_KEY` / `OPENROUTER_API_KEY` / `OPENAI_API_KEY` / `GEMINI_API_KEY`) — check via `env | grep -E "DEEPSEEK|MOONSHOT|OPENROUTER|OPENAI|GEMINI"` BEFORE planning around its use; if no key is set, fall back to alternatives below. The output is LLM-generated from training data, NOT source-derived: this tool is **not** a literature retriever. For actual literature lookup (cross-check a claim against Hartshorne, fetch an arXiv paper, summarize a Stacks tag) consult the auto-injected subagent catalog — when a literature/reference-fetching subagent is enabled it will appear there — or use the `WebSearch` / `WebFetch` tools directly.
24	- **`archon-subagent.py`** — the generic subagent dispatcher (one wrapper handles every subagent — see "Subagents" below).
25	
26	## Subagents
27	
28	Subagents are descriptor files at `.archon/subagents/<name>.md` (YAML frontmatter — `name`, `description`, `write_domain`, `read_only`, `can_spawn`, `default_enabled`, optional `mandatory: [<phase>...]`, optional `dispatcher_notes` — followed by the prompt body the spawned Claude reads).
29	
30	**You do NOT need to discover subagents.** The plan and review prompts auto-inject an **Available subagents** section at the top of each invocation listing every enabled descriptor with description, write-domain, MANDATORY flag, and `dispatcher_notes`. When you decide to invoke a subagent, read its full prompt at `.archon/subagents/<name>.md`.
31	
32	**Invoke** via the generic wrapper (Bash tool, foreground):
33	
34	```
35	python3 .claude/tools/archon-subagent.py \
36	  --name <subagent-name> \
37	  --slug <kebab-slug> \
38	  --directive-file <path-to-directive.md> \
39	  --write-domain '<glob>'        # repeat for multiple
40	```
41	
42	The wrapper exits 0 on success, prints a one-line status, writes the report to `.archon/task_results/<name>-<slug>.md`, and the Archon CLI auto-archives a copy to `logs/iter-NNN/<name>-<slug>-report.md` for the dashboard. The dispatch semaphore caps concurrent subagent processes at `loop.max_parallel`; the wrapper resolves `--parent-slug` from `ARCHON_SUBAGENT_SLUG` so nested children inherit the hierarchy.
43	
44	**Treat each dispatch as blocking — wait for its report before acting on it.** Don't *deliberately* set `run_in_background: true`. But note the wrapper is genuinely synchronous (it `subprocess.run`s the `archon subagent` CLI and only returns once the child finishes and its report is on disk), so the harness may *auto-background* a long-running dispatch and hand you a task ID immediately — that's expected and fine. When it does, await that task / poll for the report file at `.archon/task_results/<name>-<slug>.md` (the wrapper's one-line status and exit 0 confirm completion) before you read or act on the result. The thing to avoid is firing a dispatch and then continuing to plan as if it had already returned; whether the call blocked inline or was auto-backgrounded, the rule is the same: do not consume a subagent's output until its report exists.
45	
46	**Highly-recommended subagents.** A descriptor whose frontmatter sets `mandatory: [plan]` is treated as **highly recommended** for the plan phase (similarly `[review]` for the review phase). The catalog tags them `[HIGHLY RECOMMENDED]`. Dispatch each one unless you have a concrete reason to skip — when skipping, record a one-line rationale under a `## Subagent skips` section in `iter/iter-NNN/<phase>.md` (e.g. `- strategy-critic: STRATEGY.md SHA unchanged from prior iter and last verdict was SOUND`). A post-phase audit checks both signals: it silences when the subagent dispatched OR a skip rationale was recorded, and warns only when neither happened. Each subagent's `dispatcher_notes` enumerates its specific skip conditions — read them before deciding. **Subagents ship disabled by default** (`default_enabled: false`), so the rule only fires for subagents the user has explicitly listed under `subagents.enabled` in `.archon/config.json`; on a fresh project the catalog is empty and no recommended dispatch is required. (The frontmatter field is still named `mandatory: [...]` for backward compat with existing configs and descriptors — its semantic meaning is "highly recommended", not "must dispatch".)
47	
48	## Key Files & Permissions
49	
50	All Archon state files are in `.archon/`. The table below covers **phase agents** (plan, prover, review) and the **user**. Subagent permissions are NOT a fixed row — they are descriptor-driven (`write_domain` glob in the subagent's frontmatter, enforced at dispatch time). See "Subagent permissions" after the table.
51	
52	| File | Plan | Prover | Review | User |
53	|------|------|--------|--------|------|
54	| `PROGRESS.md` | read + write | read only | read only | read |
55	| `STRATEGY.md` | read + write | — | — | read |
56	| `USER_HINTS.md` | **loop-managed** (captured + cleared by the CLI; injected into the plan prompt) | — | — | write |
57	| `task_pending.md` | read + write | read only | read only | read |
58	| `task_done.md` | read + write | read only | read only | read |
59	| `task_results/<file>.md` | read (collect) | write (own file) | read only | read |
60	| `proof-journal/` | read | — | **write** | read |
61	| `PROJECT_STATUS.md` | read | — | **write** (Knowledge Base only — session log lives in `iter/iter-NNN/review.md`) | read |
62	| `iter/iter-NNN/plan.md` | **write** (this iter only) | — | read (recent window injected) | read |
63	| `iter/iter-NNN/review.md` | read (recent window injected) | — | **write** (this iter only) | read |
64	| `iter/iter-NNN/objectives.md` | optional write | — | read | read |
65	| `archon-protected.yaml` | read | read | read | **write** |
66	| `.lean` files | — | write (own file; frozen protected signatures) | — | write (via comments) |
67	| `blueprint/src/chapters/*.tex` | **write** (informal prose, `\lean{...}` hints) | read | **write** (semantic markers: `\mathlibok`, `% NOTE:`, `\lean{...}` corrections — NOT `\leanok`) | read |
68	| `analogies/<slug>.md` | read (when relevant) | — | — | read |
69	| `TO_USER.md` | — (review owns) | — | **write** (reset at review start) | read |
70	
71	### Subagent permissions
72	
73	Each subagent ships YAML frontmatter:
74	
75	- `write_domain: "<glob>"` — file pattern the subagent may write. Empty for purely read-only subagents (the dispatching call typically passes `--write-domain 'task_results/**'` so the subagent can still write its own report).
76	- `read_only: true | false` — when `true`, the subagent may only write its `task_results/<name>-<slug>.md`.
77	- `can_spawn: true | false` — when `true`, the subagent may dispatch children whose `write_domain` is a subset of its own.
78	
79	The dispatcher enforces `write_domain` at the filesystem level: any child whose declared domain is not a subset of the parent's is rejected before the child agent starts. Reads are restricted only by the descriptor's prompt body (e.g. fresh-context critics that are told not to read PROGRESS.md / STRATEGY.md).
80	
81	**`.archon/REFACTOR_DIRECTIVE.md`** is reserved for the interactive `archon refactor draft` / `archon refactor run` flow run by hand by the mathematician. The autonomous loop never reads or writes that file. To invoke a refactor-style subagent inside the loop, write the directive to a fresh tempfile under `.archon/logs/iter-NNN/<name>-<slug>-directive.md` and dispatch via the generic wrapper. Older state files (`STRATEGY.md`, `task_pending.md`, `PROGRESS.md`) may contain leftover references to the old REFACTOR_DIRECTIVE.md flow — treat those as historical noise.
82	
83	## Protected Declarations
84	
85	`archon-protected.yaml` at the project root lists declarations whose **signatures are frozen** by the mathematician. Rules:
86	
87	- **Plan / prover / review** agents: read-only on protected signatures. May fill proof bodies; may not rename, re-type, or reorder arguments.
88	- **Write-capable structural subagents** (per their `write_domain`): may *move* a protected declaration to a different file (name + signature preserved verbatim) and must then update the path key in `archon-protected.yaml`. They may NEVER rename / re-type / delete / re-sign a protected declaration.
89	- The YAML file itself is user-only. No agent adds or removes a declaration. The only allowed agent edit is updating an existing declaration's file path.
90	
91	## Blueprint Marker Vocabulary
92	
93	Two semantic markers:
94	
95	- `\leanok` — statement block: declaration is formalized (at least a `sorry` present). Proof block: proof closed, no `sorry`.
96	- `\mathlibok` — statement block: declaration is backed by Mathlib (re-export / alias); no Archon proof obligation remains.
97	
98	No marker means the block is unformalized. If a block fails to translate, leave it unmarked and annotate with a `% NOTE:` comment.
99	
100	**`\leanok` is managed deterministically by the `sync_leanok` phase** that runs between prover and review each iteration. It walks every chapter, looks up each `\lean{...}` declaration, runs `sorry_analyzer` + `lake env lean`, and adds/removes `\leanok` accordingly. Agents must NOT add or remove `\leanok` themselves.
101	
102	`\mathlibok`, `\lean{...}` corrections, and `% NOTE:` annotations are the review agent's domain (they require semantic judgement). The plan agent writes informal prose and `\lean{...}` hints; provers never touch the blueprint.
103	
104	## User Interaction
105	
106	The user provides hints in two places:
107	
108	- **Strategic hints** → `.archon/USER_HINTS.md`. The Archon loop captures this file's content before each plan phase, injects it into the plan agent's prompt, and resets the file to its bundled template (an HTML-comment format guide + zero bullets) after the plan phase succeeds. The HTML-comment preamble is stripped before injection so a template-only file renders as "no hints" to the planner. The plan agent does NOT read or clear that file itself. Provers never see these hints.
109	- **File-specific hints** → `/- USER: ... -/` comments directly in `.lean` files. The prover that owns that file sees them naturally.
110	
111	## Agent Roles
112	
113	### Plan Agent
114	
115	- Read `.archon/prompts/plan.md` for full instructions.
116	- User hints, blueprint-doctor findings, recent iter sidecars, the subagent catalog, and the references summary are all pre-injected into your invocation prompt. You do not "go read" those files.
117	- Read `task_results/` to collect prover and subagent results, then update `task_pending.md` / `task_done.md`.
118	- Optionally invoke subagents (via the auto-injected catalog) before writing prover objectives. Subagent reports are auto-archived to `logs/iter-NNN/` by the Archon CLI.
119	- Write `PROGRESS.md` with objectives for the next prover round.
120	- Write informal prose in `blueprint/src/chapters/*.tex`. Do NOT touch markers — `\leanok` is owned by the deterministic sync; `\mathlibok` / `% NOTE:` by the review agent.
121	- Do NOT write proofs, edit `.lean` files, or fill sorries yourself.
122	
123	### Prover Agent
124	
125	- Read `PROGRESS.md` for your objectives (read only).
126	- Your **active mode** is injected directly into your invocation prompt — read it carefully, it defines your goal, workflow, and constraints for this session.
127	- When your mode specifies `read_blueprint: true`, you are permitted to **read** your assigned blueprint chapter (`blueprint/src/chapters/<your_slug>.tex`) for the informal proof sketch. You may never write to blueprint chapters.
128	- Write results to `task_results/<your_file>.md`.
129	- Write only to the `.lean` file(s) you are assigned — never edit another agent's file.
130	- Check for `/- USER: ... -/` comments in your file for file-specific hints.
131	- Do NOT edit blueprint chapters. Flag in your task result which declarations are ready for which marker; the review agent applies them.
132	
133	### Subagents
134	
135	Subagents are descriptor-driven and discovered at runtime. The catalog is auto-injected into the plan and review prompts each phase (see "Subagents" above for the wrapper invocation). Each subagent reads only what its directive points at — never `PROGRESS.md` / `STRATEGY.md` / phase-agent state — and writes a self-contained report at `task_results/<name>-<slug>.md`. Descriptors with `can_spawn: true` may dispatch children (subject to the global `loop.max_parallel` cap).
136	
137	### Review Agent
138	
139	- Read `.archon/prompts/review.md` for full instructions.
140	- Read `proof-journal/current_session/attempts_raw.jsonl` for structured prover attempt data.
141	- Write the session journal to `proof-journal/sessions/session_N/` (`summary.md`, `milestones.jsonl`, `recommendations.md`).
142	- Update `PROJECT_STATUS.md` (Knowledge Base only — session narrative goes to `iter/iter-NNN/review.md`).
143	- Maintain the semantic blueprint markers — `\mathlibok`, `\lean{...}` corrections, `% NOTE:` annotations, stale `\notready` removal. Do NOT touch `\leanok`.
144	- Do NOT write proofs, edit `.lean` files, or modify `PROGRESS.md`.
145	
146	### Loop infrastructure (no agent role)
147	
148	These run automatically between phases:
149	
150	- **Iter sidecar init** — creates `.archon/iter/iter-NNN/` at iter start so plan + review have a stable destination for per-iter narrative. Top-level files (STRATEGY.md, PROJECT_STATUS.md, task_*.md) stay bounded.
151	- **`sync_leanok`** — runs between prover and review, deterministically updating `\leanok` markers based on actual sorry counts and compilation status.
152	- **`blueprint-doctor`** — runs between prover and review, deterministically detecting orphan chapters, broken `\ref` / `\uses` / `\proves`, and new `axiom` declarations. Findings are written to `logs/iter-NNN/blueprint-doctor.{md,json}` and inlined into the next plan agent's prompt.

10	/-!
11	# The line bundle `𝒪_C(P)` of a closed point and its global sections (RR.3)
12	
13	This file is the **RR.3** sub-build chapter for the project's headline
14	`genusZero_curve_iso_P1` (the "smooth proper geometrically irreducible
15	genus-`0` curve over `k̄` is isomorphic to `ℙ¹`" lemma in
16	`AlgebraicJacobian.AbelianVarietyRigidity`).
17	
18	The Hartshorne IV.1.3.5 chain for the genus-`0` ↦ `ℙ¹` classification routes
19	through:
20	
21	- `RR.1` (`WeilDivisor.lean`): the Weil divisor group `Div(C)` and the
22	  degree map `deg : Div(C) → ℤ`.
23	- `RR.2` (`RRFormula.lean`): the Euler-characteristic identity
24	  `χ(𝒪_C(D)) = deg(D) + 1 − g` and the Riemann–Roch dimension formula
25	  `ℓ(D) = deg(D) + 1` in genus `0`.
26	- **`RR.3` (this file)**: the invertible sheaf `𝒪_C(P)` of a closed point
27	  `P ∈ C`, its `k̄`-vector space of global sections as the
28	  Riemann–Roch space `L([P])`, the `H¹`-vanishing
29	  `H¹(C, 𝒪_C(P)) = 0` on a genus-`0` curve, the dimension formula
30	  `dim_{k̄} H⁰(C, 𝒪_C(P)) = 2`, and the existence of a non-constant
31	  rational function `f ∈ K(C)` with at most a simple pole at `P`.
32	- `RR.4` (`RationalCurveIso.lean`, future): the "two-section
33	  ⇒ `Proj.fromOfGlobalSections` ⇒ `≅ ℙ¹`" classification.
34	
35	## Status (iter-183 Lane A — sig amend + carrierSet scaffold)
36	
37	Iter-183 Lane A (re-dispatch from iter-182 deferral) landed:
38

40	   the codimension-one witness `(hPcoh : Order.coheight P = 1)` explicitly,
41	   so the subsheaf-of-`K_C` carrier can read off the order at `P` via the
42	   prime divisor `⟨P, hPcoh⟩`. The amend matches the blueprint chapter prose
43	   for the Hartshorne subsheaf-of-`K_C` direct construction (per analogist
44	   `ocofp-sheaf-internalhom.md`, Decision 3 + Decision 4 verdict
45	   `ALIGN_WITH_MATHLIB`).
46	2. **Scaffold** `lineBundleAtClosedPoint.carrierSet` (concrete `Set`-valued
47	   substantive carrier — the set of rational functions with the order
48	   conditions on a given open). This is iter-183's substantive
49	   contribution beyond the sig amend; no new `sorry` introduced.
50	3. The bodies of `lineBundleAtClosedPoint` (L140) and `toFunctionField`
51	   (L154) remain typed `sorry` for iter-184+ (the full chain
52	   `carrierSet → carrierSubmodule (Submodule) → presheaf (Functor) →
53	   isSheaf (typed sorry) → Sheaf` is ~230-360 LOC; iter-183's helper
54	   budget = 5 and `sorry` ceiling = 7 forced PARTIAL).
55	
56	The 5 pinned declarations are:
57	
58	1. `AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.lineBundleAtClosedPoint` — the invertible
59	   sheaf `𝒪_C(P)` associated to a closed point `P` on a smooth proper
60	   curve `C / k̄`.
61	2. `AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.lineBundleAtClosedPoint.globalSections_iff`
62	   — the identification of global sections of `𝒪_C(P)` with the
63	   Riemann–Roch space
64	   `L([P]) = {f ∈ K(C)^× | div(f) + [P] ≥ 0} ∪ {0}`,
65	   expressed as an `Iff`-style characterisation of the order conditions
66	   `ord_Q(f) ≥ 0` for `Q ≠ P` and `ord_P(f) ≥ −1`.
67	3. `AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.lineBundleAtClosedPoint.h1_vanishing_genusZero`
68	   — the cohomological vanishing `H¹(C, 𝒪_C(P)) = 0` on a smooth proper
69	   geometrically irreducible curve `C / k̄` with `g(C) = 0`, via the long
70	   exact sequence of the closed-point short exact sequence
71	   `0 → 𝒪_C → 𝒪_C(P) → k(P) → 0`.
72	4. `AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.lineBundleAtClosedPoint.dim_eq_two_of_genusZero`
73	   — the dimension formula `dim_{k̄} H⁰(C, 𝒪_C(P)) = 2` in genus `0`,
74	   specialising the χ-identity `RR.2` to `D = [P]` and consuming the
75	   `H¹`-vanishing of pin 3.
76	5. `AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.lineBundleAtClosedPoint.exists_nonconstant_genusZero`
77	   — the corollary: a non-constant rational function `f ∈ K(C)` regular
78	   on `C \ {P}` with at most a simple pole at `P`, obtained as a lift of
79	   any non-zero element of the quotient
80	   `H⁰(C, 𝒪_C(P)) / 𝓀̄ · 1`.
81	
82	## Notation reminders
83	
84	The line bundle `𝒪_C(P)` is realised as a
85	`Sheaf (Opens.grothendieckTopology C.left.toTopCat) (ModuleCat.{u} kbar)`,
86	the same `ModuleCat k̄`-flavoured sheaf category used by the project's
87	`Scheme.HModule` cohomology pipeline (cf. `AlgebraicJacobian.Genus`).
88	Its `H⁰` and `H¹` are computed via `Scheme.HModule kbar (·) 0` and
89	`Scheme.HModule kbar (·) 1`, both of which carry a canonical `Module k̄`
90	instance.

110	namespace AlgebraicGeometry
111	
112	/-! ## §1. The line bundle of a closed point on a smooth proper curve
113	
114	For a smooth proper geometrically irreducible curve `C / k̄` and a closed
115	point `P ∈ C`, the local ring `𝒪_{C,P}` is a DVR with maximal ideal
116	generated by a uniformiser `f_P`. Hartshorne's construction `ℒ(D)`
117	(II §6 p. 144) applied to the Cartier divisor `[P]` (locally cut out by
118	`f_P` near `P`, by `1` elsewhere) produces the invertible sheaf `𝒪_C(P)`:
119	the sub-`𝒪_C`-module of the function-field constant sheaf `𝒦_C ≅ K(C)`
120	generated locally by `f_P^{-1}` near `P` and by `1` on `C \ {P}`. -/
121	
122	namespace Scheme
123	
124	/-! ### Hartshorne subsheaf-of-`K_C` carrier (iter-183 Lane A scaffold)
125	
126	The substantive iter-183 contribution: a concrete per-open `Set`-valued
127	carrier of the line bundle `𝒪_C(P)`, realised directly as Hartshorne's
128	subsheaf of the function-field constant sheaf `K_C` (Hartshorne II §6
129	p. 144; analogist `ocofp-sheaf-internalhom.md` Decision 3
130	`ALIGN_WITH_MATHLIB`).
131	
132	A section of `𝒪_C(P)` over an open `U` is a rational function `f ∈ K(C)`
133	satisfying the order conditions `ord_Q(f) ≥ 0` for every prime divisor
134	`Q ≠ P` with `Q.point ∈ U` (regularity on the complement of `P` inside
135	`U`) and `ord_P(f) ≥ −1` when `P ∈ U` (at most a simple pole at `P`).
136	The construction is independent of the choice of uniformiser at `P`:
137	any two uniformisers differ by a unit, so the order-`≥ −1` condition at
138	`P` is intrinsic.
139	
140	iter-184+ will upgrade `carrierSet` to a `Submodule kbar K(C)` via the
141	closure proofs (zero / addition / kbar-scalar), bundle the result as a
142	presheaf functor (identity-on-`K(C)` restrictions, monotone in `U` via
143	`carrierSet_mono`), and discharge the sheaf property via gluing-by-
144	stalks (stalk-locality of the order conditions at each prime divisor). -/
145	
146	/-- **Carrier set** of `𝒪_C(P)` over an open `U : (Opens C.left)ᵒᵖ`: the
147	set of rational functions `f ∈ K(C)` satisfying the order conditions
148	`ord_Q(f) ≥ 0` for every prime divisor `Q` with `Q.point ∈ U.unop`,
149	`Q.point ≠ P`, and `ord_P(f) ≥ −1` when `P ∈ U.unop`.
150	
151	iter-183 Lane A landed this as a concrete substantive `Set` definition;
152	no sorry. -/
153	private noncomputable def lineBundleAtClosedPoint.carrierSet
154	    {kbar : Type u} [Field kbar] [IsAlgClosed kbar]
155	    {C : Over (Spec (.of kbar))} [IsIntegral C.left]
156	    [IsLocallyNoetherian C.left]
157	    [Scheme.IsRegularInCodimensionOne C.left]
158	    (P : C.left) (hPcoh : Order.coheight P = 1)
159	    (U : (TopologicalSpace.Opens C.left.toTopCat)ᵒᵖ) :
160	    Set C.left.functionField := by
161	  let Phat : C.left.PrimeDivisor := ⟨P, hPcoh⟩
162	  haveI := Scheme.IsRegularInCodimensionOne.instKrullDimLEStalk
163	    (X := C.left) Phat
164	  exact { f | (∀ Q : C.left.PrimeDivisor, Q.point ∈ U.unop.1 → Q.point ≠ P →
165	              0 ≤ Scheme.RationalMap.order Q f) ∧
166	              (P ∈ U.unop.1 → (-1 : ℤ) ≤ Scheme.RationalMap.order Phat f) }
167	
168	/-- **Monotonicity of `carrierSet` in `U`**: when `V.unop ⊆ U.unop` (i.e.
169	the open `V` is contained in the open `U`), the carrier on `U` is
170	INCLUDED in the carrier on `V` (the order conditions over the smaller
171	open `V` involve fewer prime divisors, hence are easier to satisfy).
172	
173	This is the substantive monotonicity that drives the (identity-on-`K(C)`)
174	restriction map of the would-be `lineBundleAtClosedPoint.presheaf` functor:
175	in `(Opens C.left)ᵒᵖ`, an arrow `U ⟶ V` corresponds to `V.unop ⊆ U.unop`,
176	and the restriction map `carrierSet U → carrierSet V` is the inclusion
177	delivered by this lemma.
178	
179	iter-183 Lane A landed this as the substantive monotonicity proof; no
180	sorry. -/
181	private lemma lineBundleAtClosedPoint.carrierSet_mono
182	    {kbar : Type u} [Field kbar] [IsAlgClosed kbar]
183	    {C : Over (Spec (.of kbar))} [IsIntegral C.left]
184	    [IsLocallyNoetherian C.left]
185	    [Scheme.IsRegularInCodimensionOne C.left]
186	    (P : C.left) (hPcoh : Order.coheight P = 1)
187	    {U V : (TopologicalSpace.Opens C.left.toTopCat)ᵒᵖ}
188	    (hUV : V.unop.1 ⊆ U.unop.1) :
189	    lineBundleAtClosedPoint.carrierSet P hPcoh U
190	      ⊆ lineBundleAtClosedPoint.carrierSet P hPcoh V := by
191	  intro f hf
192	  refine ⟨fun Q hQV hQP => hf.1 Q (hUV hQV) hQP, fun hPV => hf.2 (hUV hPV)⟩
193	
194	/-- **Nonempty top-open**: for an integral scheme `X`, the top open is
195	nonempty (its carrier is `Set.univ` and `X` itself is nonempty since
196	`IsIntegral X ⟹ IrreducibleSpace X ⟹ Nonempty X`). Supplied as an
197	instance so that the standard Mathlib `Algebra Γ(X, U) X.functionField`
198	instance fires at `U = ⊤`. -/
199	instance lineBundleAtClosedPoint.instNonemptyTopOpen
200	    {X : Scheme.{u}} [IsIntegral X] :
201	    Nonempty ((⊤ : X.Opens) : Scheme) :=
202	  (Scheme.Opens.nonempty_iff (U := (⊤ : X.Opens))).mpr
203	    ⟨(inferInstance : Nonempty X).some, Set.mem_univ _⟩
204	
205	/-- **`kbar`-algebra structure on the function field `K(C)`**. The
206	project's `Scheme.toModuleKSheaf.algebraSection` instance gives
207	`Algebra kbar Γ(C.left, ⊤)`, and Mathlib's standard
208	`AlgebraicGeometry.instAlgebraCarrierObjOppositeOpens...` gives
209	`Algebra Γ(C.left, ⊤) K(C)` via `germToFunctionField`. Composing the
210	two algebra maps via `RingHom.toAlgebra` produces the desired
211	`Algebra kbar K(C)` instance, which is needed for `Submodule kbar K(C)`
212	to type-check. -/
213	noncomputable instance lineBundleAtClosedPoint.instAlgebraKbarFunctionField
214	    {kbar : Type u} [Field kbar]
215	    (C : Over (Spec (.of kbar))) [IsIntegral C.left] :
216	    Algebra kbar C.left.functionField := by
217	  haveI : Nonempty (⊤ : C.left.Opens) :=
218	    (AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Opens.nonempty_iff
219	      (X := C.left) (U := (⊤ : C.left.Opens))).mpr
220	      ⟨(inferInstance : Nonempty C.left).some, Set.mem_univ _⟩
221	  exact RingHom.toAlgebra
222	    ((Scheme.germToFunctionField C.left (⊤ : C.left.Opens)).hom.comp
223	      (Scheme.toModuleKSheaf.kToSection C
224	        (Opposite.op (⊤ : TopologicalSpace.Opens C.left.toTopCat))).hom)
225	
226	/-- **Carrier submodule** of `𝒪_C(P)` over an open `U`: upgrade of
227	`carrierSet U` to a `Submodule kbar K(C)` via the three closure proofs
228	(`0`, `+`, `kbar • _`). The closure proofs rest on the DVR-shipped
229	`Ring.ordFrac_add` (non-archimedean inequality on the discrete valuation
230	of a regular-in-codim-1 stalk; iter-186 Step 1 upgraded the carrier class
231	so this is invocable) and `Ring.ordFrac_of_isUnit` (the scalar from
232	`kbar` becomes a unit in the stalk, preserving the order under scalar
233	multiplication).
234	
235	iter-186 Step 2 (per analogist `ocofp-carrierset-submodule-api.md`
236	Decision 2): structural skeleton with the load-bearing Mathlib lemmas in
237	place; bookkeeping `sorry`s remain for the prover phase. -/
238	private noncomputable def lineBundleAtClosedPoint.carrierSubmodule
239	    {kbar : Type u} [Field kbar] [IsAlgClosed kbar]
240	    {C : Over (Spec (.of kbar))} [IsIntegral C.left]
241	    [IsLocallyNoetherian C.left]
242	    [Scheme.IsRegularInCodimensionOne C.left]
243	    (P : C.left) (hPcoh : Order.coheight P = 1)
244	    (U : (TopologicalSpace.Opens C.left.toTopCat)ᵒᵖ) :
245	    Submodule kbar C.left.functionField where
246	  carrier := lineBundleAtClosedPoint.carrierSet P hPcoh U
247	  zero_mem' := by
248	    -- `order Y 0 = WithZero.log (Ring.ordFrac _ 0) = WithZero.log 0 = 0`;
249	    -- both `0 ≤ 0` and `-1 ≤ 0` hold trivially.
250	    refine ⟨fun Q _ _ => ?_, fun _ => ?_⟩
251	    · simp [Scheme.RationalMap.order]
252	    · simp [Scheme.RationalMap.order]
253	  add_mem' := by
254	    -- Case-split on `f + g = 0` (trivial via `WithZero.log_zero`), else
255	    -- apply `Ring.ordFrac_add` (DVR-shipped, Step 1 unlocks) and
256	    -- `WithZero.log` monotonicity on the nonzero part.
257	    rintro a b ⟨ha₁, ha₂⟩ ⟨hb₁, hb₂⟩
258	    have hMNZ : ∀ (Q : C.left.PrimeDivisor) (x : C.left.functionField),
259	        x ≠ 0 → Ring.ordFrac (C.left.presheaf.stalk Q.point) x ≠ 0 := by
260	      intro Q x hx; simp [hx]
261	    have key : ∀ (Q : C.left.PrimeDivisor) (n : ℤ),
262	        n ≤ 0 → n ≤ Scheme.RationalMap.order Q a →
263	          n ≤ Scheme.RationalMap.order Q b →
264	          n ≤ Scheme.RationalMap.order Q (a + b) := by
265	      intro Q n hn haₚ hbₚ
266	      by_cases hab : a + b = 0
267	      · simpa [hab, Scheme.RationalMap.order] using hn
268	      by_cases hae : a = 0
269	      · rw [hae, zero_add]; exact hbₚ
270	      by_cases hbe : b = 0
271	      · rw [hbe, add_zero]; exact haₚ
272	      set R := C.left.presheaf.stalk Q.point
273	      have hoa : Ring.ordFrac R a ≠ 0 := hMNZ Q a hae
274	      have hob : Ring.ordFrac R b ≠ 0 := hMNZ Q b hbe
275	      have hoab : Ring.ordFrac R (a + b) ≠ 0 := hMNZ Q _ hab

419	/-- **Type-level Subfunctor presentation of the carrier** (iter-189 Subfunctor
420	restructure). The carrier of `𝒪_C(P)` viewed as a `CategoryTheory.Subfunctor` of
421	the Type-valued presheaf `TopCat.presheafToType C.left.toTopCat C.left.functionField`
422	(the presheaf of arbitrary functions to the function field `K(C)`). A section over
423	an open `U` is a function `g : U.unop → K(C)` that is constant with value
424	`f ∈ carrierSubmoduleSheaf U`.

426	This Subfunctor packages the carrier conditions uniformly:
427	- At `U ≠ ⊥`: a section is a constant function valued in
428	  `carrierSubmoduleSheaf U = carrierSubmodule U` (so the order conditions hold).
429	- At `U = ⊥`: the unique empty function (witnessed by `f = 0 ∈ ⊥`).
430	
431	Mathlib's `CategoryTheory.Subfunctor.isSheaf_iff`, applied against the ambient
432	sheaf `TopCat.Presheaf.toType_isSheaf` for `presheafToType`, reduces the sheaf
433	condition for the (type-valued shadow of the) carrier to a stalk-locality check:
434	every section of `presheafToType K(C)` whose sieve-of-section is covering already
435	lies in the Subfunctor. On the irreducible scheme `C.left`, this stalk-locality
436	holds because any two non-empty opens intersect, forcing constant-function gluing
437	to agree on overlaps, and the per-prime-divisor order conditions transfer from
438	each open in the cover to their union pointwise. -/

440	    {kbar : Type u} [Field kbar] [IsAlgClosed kbar]
441	    {C : Over (Spec (.of kbar))} [IsIntegral C.left]
442	    [IsLocallyNoetherian C.left]
443	    [Scheme.IsRegularInCodimensionOne C.left]
444	    (P : C.left) (hPcoh : Order.coheight P = 1) :
445	    CategoryTheory.Subfunctor
446	      (TopCat.presheafToType C.left.toTopCat C.left.functionField) where
447	  obj U := { g : U.unop → C.left.functionField |
448	    ∃ f : C.left.functionField,
449	      f ∈ lineBundleAtClosedPoint.carrierSubmoduleSheaf P hPcoh U ∧
450	        g = fun _ => f }
451	  map := by
452	    classical
453	    intro U V i g hg
454	    obtain ⟨f, hf, hgf⟩ := hg
455	    by_cases hV : V.unop = (⊥ : TopologicalSpace.Opens C.left.toTopCat)
456	    · -- `V = ⊥`: `V.unop` is the empty type. Any function with empty domain works;

458	      refine ⟨0, ?_, ?_⟩
459	      · exact (lineBundleAtClosedPoint.carrierSubmoduleSheaf P hPcoh V).zero_mem

1351	    · rw [Scheme.RationalMap.order_one]
1352	    · rw [Scheme.RationalMap.order_one]; norm_num
1353	  -- Distinguished constant section: `s₁` is the global section of `𝒪_C(P)`
1354	  -- whose underlying rational function is `1 ∈ K(C)`.
1355	  obtain ⟨_s₁, _hs₁⟩ :=
1356	    globalSections_iff_mp (C := C) P hP (1 : C.left.functionField)
1357	      one_ne_zero hPcoh h1_orders
1358	  -- Remaining substantive gap: linear-algebra extraction of a section
1359	  -- `s ∉ kbar · s₁` (using `_hdim` and the 1-dim constant subspace
1360	  -- `kbar · s₁`), extract `f := toFunctionField s` with `f ≠ 0` and
1361	  -- `f ∉ kbar`, derive order conditions via `globalSections_iff_mpr`,
1362	  -- and close `principal f ≠ 0` from non-constancy (Stacks 02P0).
1363	  sorry
1364	
1365	/-- **Existence of a non-constant rational function regular on `C ∖ {P}`
1366	with at most a simple pole at `P`** (Hartshorne IV §1 Exercise 1.1,
1367	p. 297, the genus-`0` specialisation).
1368	
1369	Concretely, there exists `f ∈ K(C)` such that:
1370	
1371	* `f ≠ 0`;
1372	* `f ∉ k̄` (i.e. `f` is non-constant — for instance, it does not lie
1373	  in the image of the structural inclusion of constants);
1374	* `ord_Q(f) ≥ 0` for every prime divisor `Q ∈ C.PrimeDivisor` whose
1375	  generic point is not `P`;
1376	* `ord_P(f) ≥ −1` (at most a simple pole at `P`).
1377	
1378	iter-177+ body: use `dim_eq_two_of_genusZero` to get
1379	`dim_{k̄} H⁰(C, 𝒪_C(P)) = 2`. The image of `1 ∈ H⁰(C, 𝒪_C) ≅ k̄`
1380	under the structural inclusion `𝒪_C ↪ 𝒪_C(P)` spans a one-dimensional
1381	subspace of `H⁰(C, 𝒪_C(P))`; choose any section `s ∈ H⁰(C, 𝒪_C(P))`
1382	not in this constant subspace (non-empty because `dim H⁰ = 2 > 1`),
1383	then take `f := lineBundleAtClosedPoint.toFunctionField P hP s` and
1384	verify the four bullets via `globalSections_iff` applied to `f`
1385	(the forward direction supplies the order conditions from the existence
1386	witness `⟨s, rfl⟩`). The chosen `f` is non-constant because `s` is not
1387	in the constant subspace and `toFunctionField` is `k̄`-linear and
1388	injective on global sections.
1389	
1390	The principal-divisor-non-zero formulation `Scheme.WeilDivisor.principal
1391	f hf ≠ 0` follows from non-constancy plus the fact that constant
1392	functions have principal divisor zero (the converse — `div(f) = 0`
1393	⇒ `f` constant — uses the integrality of `C` and is the Stacks 02P0
1394	"functions with no zeros and poles are constant" type statement).
1395	
1396	Blueprint reference:
1397	`cor:lineBundleAtClosedPoint_exists_nonconstant_genusZero` (alias
1398	`cor:nonconstant_function_genus_zero` consumed by `RR.4`)
1399	(Hartshorne IV.1 Exercise 1.1, p. 297).
1400	
1401	iter-194 Lane A first body push: the body is now reduced to a one-line
1402	invocation of the named substrate helper
1403	`exists_nonconstant_rational_from_dim_eq_two`, supplied with the dimension
1404	count `dim_eq_two_of_genusZero`. The helper carries the substantive
1405	linear-algebra + principal-divisor content (independent of the genus
1406	hypothesis), leaving this corollary's body mechanical. -/
1407	theorem exists_nonconstant_genusZero
1408	    [∀ Q : C.left.PrimeDivisor,
1409	        Ring.KrullDimLE 1 (C.left.presheaf.stalk Q.point)]
1410	    (P : C.left) (_hP : IsClosed ({P} : Set C.left))
1411	    (hPcoh : Order.coheight P = 1)
1412	    (_hg : AlgebraicGeometry.genus C = 0) :
1413	    ∃ (f : C.left.functionField) (hf : f ≠ 0),
1414	      (∀ Q : C.left.PrimeDivisor, Q.point ≠ P →
1415	          0 ≤ Scheme.RationalMap.order Q f) ∧
1416	      (-1 : ℤ) ≤ Scheme.RationalMap.order ⟨P, hPcoh⟩ f ∧
1417	      Scheme.WeilDivisor.principal (X := C.left) f hf ≠ 0 :=
1418	  exists_nonconstant_rational_from_dim_eq_two P _hP hPcoh
1419	    (dim_eq_two_of_genusZero P _hP hPcoh _hg)
1420	
1421	end lineBundleAtClosedPoint
1422	
1423	end Scheme
1424	
1425	end AlgebraicGeometry

3	\chapter{The line bundle \(\mathcal O_C(P)\) and its global sections (RR.3)}
4	\label{chap:RiemannRoch_OCofP}
5	
6	% SOURCE: Hartshorne, \emph{Algebraic Geometry}, II.6, p.~144 (definition
7	% of \(\mathscr L(D)\)); II.7, Proposition~7.7, p.~157 (effective divisors
8	% as divisors of zeros of global sections); IV.1, pp.~294--297
9	% (Riemann--Roch, Lemma~1.2, Examples~1.3.3--1.3.5)
10	% (read from references/hartshorne-algebraic-geometry.pdf, PDF pages 161,
11	% 174, 311--314). The verbatim quotes pasted in the declaration blocks
12	% below were copied character-by-character from the rendered page images
13	% of the scanned PDF (no text layer is present).
14	
15	\section{Setup and motivation}
16	
17	This chapter is the third of four sub-build chapters \texttt{RR.1}--\texttt{RR.4}
18	that close the project's headline \emph{RR bridge}
19	\[
20	  \mathtt{genusZero\_curve\_iso\_P1}
21	  \,\colon\,
22	  \text{a smooth proper geometrically irreducible curve \(C / \bar k\) with
23	  \(g(C) = 0\) is isomorphic to \(\mathbb P^1_{\bar k}\).}
24	\]
25	The bridge as proved in Hartshorne (Example~IV.1.3.5) chains together:
26	the Weil-divisor group \(\mathrm{Div}(C)\), the degree map $\deg \colon \mathrm{Div}(C)
27	\to \mathbb Z\(, and the divisor \)[P]$ of a closed point
28	(\texttt{RR.1}, REF); the Riemann--Roch
29	dimension formula in genus~\(0\)
30	\[
31	  \ell(D) \;=\; \deg(D) + 1 \qquad \text{for } \deg(D) \geq 0
32	\]
33	via the Euler-characteristic identity
34	(\texttt{RR.2}, REF); the global-sections
35	calculation \(\dim_{\bar k} H^0(C, \mathcal O_C(P)) = 2\) on a smooth
36	proper genus-\(0\) curve (this chapter, \texttt{RR.3}); the
37	``rational curve \(\Rightarrow \cong \mathbb P^1\)'' classification via
38	\texttt{Proj.fromOfGlobalSections} (\texttt{RR.4}).
39	
40	The contribution of \texttt{RR.3} is twofold. First, it constructs the
41	invertible sheaf \(\mathcal O_C(P)\) associated to a closed point \(P \in C\)
42	on a smooth proper curve, identifies its \(\bar k\)-vector space of global
43	sections with the \emph{Riemann--Roch space}
44	\[
45	  L([P]) \;=\;
46	  \bigl\{f \in K(C)^{\times} \;\big|\; \div(f) + [P] \geq 0\bigr\}
47	  \,\cup\, \{0\},
48	\]
49	and computes its dimension as \(\dim_{\bar k} H^0(C, \mathcal O_C(P)) = 2\)
50	in the genus-\(0\) specialisation. Second, it deduces from the
51	two-dimensionality that \(C\) admits a non-constant rational function \(f\)
52	having at most a simple pole at \(P\) and no other poles. This rational
53	function is the seed for \texttt{RR.4}: the pair $(1, f) \in
54	H^0(C, \mathcal O_C(P))^{\oplus 2}$ generates the sheaf and, via
55	\texttt{Proj.fromOfGlobalSections}, produces the morphism
56	\(C \to \mathbb P^1_{\bar k}\) whose degree-\(1\) identification is the
57	content of \texttt{RR.4}.
58	
59	% NOTE (Direct cohomological vanishing vs. Serre duality — iter-174
60	% sub-phase choice). The dimension computation
61	% \(\dim_{\bar k} H^0(C, \mathcal O_C(P)) = 2\) would, in Hartshorne's
62	% original presentation, route through Serre duality
63	% \(H^1(C, \mathcal L) \cong H^0(C, \omega_C \otimes \mathcal L^{-1})^*\)
64	% to vanish \(H^1(C, \mathcal O_C(P))\) from the degree count
65	% \(\deg(K_C - [P]) = 2g - 2 - 1 = -3 < 0\). Mathlib (snapshot
66	% \texttt{b80f227}) ships no dualizing sheaf \(\omega_X\) on a scheme and
67	% no Serre duality theorem. The genus-\(0\) specialisation does \emph{not}
68	% require Serre duality: the vanishing \(H^1(C, \mathcal O_C(P)) = 0\) is
69	% delivered by the long exact sequence of the structural short exact
70	% sequence
71	% \[
72	%   0 \to \mathcal O_C \to \mathcal O_C(P) \to k(P) \to 0
73	% \]
74	% together with the genus-\(0\) hypothesis
75	% \(\dim_{\bar k} H^1(C, \mathcal O_C) = g = 0\) and the skyscraper
76	% identity \(H^1(C, k(P)) = 0\). The chapter therefore uses the direct
77	% cohomological vanishing route exclusively; Serre duality is left for
78	% the post-RR-bridge follow-up chapter that handles the general-\(g\)
79	% statement.
80	
81	\paragraph{Standing hypotheses.} Throughout this chapter, \(\bar k\) is an
82	algebraically closed field and \(C\) is a smooth proper geometrically
83	irreducible curve over \(\bar k\). Concretely, the Lean signatures
84	(\texttt{AlgebraicJacobian/RiemannRoch/OCofP.lean}) carry the same
85	typeclass package as the rest of the project's curve API:
86	\begin{quote}
87	  \texttt{\{kbar : Type u\} [Field kbar] [IsAlgClosed kbar]\\
88	   (C : Over (Spec (.of kbar))) [IsProper C.hom]\\
89	   \phantom{}\quad[SmoothOfRelativeDimension 1 C.hom]\\
90	   \phantom{}\quad[GeometricallyIrreducible C.hom] [IsIntegral C.left]}
91	\end{quote}
92	The genus \(g(C)\) is the one defined in REF of
93	\texttt{Genus.tex}; the divisor \([P]\) of a closed point is the one of
94	REF of \texttt{RiemannRoch\_WeilDivisor.tex};
95	the principal divisor \(\div(f)\) of a nonzero rational function and the
96	order \(\ord_Q(f)\) at a prime divisor are the ones of
97	REF and REF.

158	  \label{def:lineBundleAtClosedPoint_carrierSubmoduleSheaf}
159	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.lineBundleAtClosedPoint.carrierSubmoduleSheaf}
160	  \uses{def:lineBundleAtClosedPoint_carrierPresheaf}
161	  Iter-188 Lane A discovery: the raw \(\mathrm{carrierSubmodule}_P\) of
162	  \cref{def:lineBundleAtClosedPoint_carrierPresheaf} satisfies
163	  \(\mathrm{carrierSubmodule}_P(\bot) = K(C)\) (since the order conditions
164	  are vacuously satisfied on the empty open). This violates the sheaf
165	  axiom on the Grothendieck topology of opens: the empty sieve covers
166	  \(\bot\), forcing \(F(\bot) =\) terminal \(= 0\) on
167	  \(\ModuleCat_{\bar k}\)-valued functors.

169	  \[
170	    \mathrm{carrierSubmoduleSheaf}_P(U) \;:=\;
171	    \mathrm{carrierSubmodule}_P(U) \;\sqcap\; \mathrm{trivAtBot}\,U,

186	\begin{definition}
187	
188	
189	\leanok
190	[Line bundle of a closed point]
191	  \label{def:lineBundleAtClosedPoint}
192	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.lineBundleAtClosedPoint}
193	  \uses{def:lineBundleAtClosedPoint_carrierPresheaf, lem:lineBundleAtClosedPoint_carrierPresheaf_isSheaf}
194	  
195	  % SOURCE: [Hartshorne], II.6, p.~144 (definition of the invertible
196	  % sheaf \(\mathscr L(D)\) associated to a Cartier divisor) (read from
197	  % references/hartshorne-algebraic-geometry.pdf, PDF page 161).
198	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Definition. Let \(D\) be a Cartier divisor on a scheme
199	  % \(X\), represented by \(\{(U_i, f_i)\}\) as above. We define a subsheaf
200	  % \(\mathscr L(D)\) of the sheaf of total quotient rings \(\mathscr K\) by
201	  % taking \(\mathscr L(D)\) to be the sub-\(\mathscr O_X\)-module of
202	  % \(\mathscr K\) generated by \(f_i^{-1}\) on \(U_i\). This is well-defined,
203	  % since \(f_i/f_j\) is invertible on \(U_i \cap U_j\), and \(f_i^{-1}\) and
204	  % \(f_j^{-1}\) generate the same \(\mathscr O_X\)-module. We call
205	  % \(\mathscr L(D)\) the \emph{sheaf associated to \(D\)}."
206	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Proposition 6.13. \emph{Let \(X\) be a scheme. Then:
207	  % (a) for any Cartier divisor \(D\), \(\mathscr L(D)\) is an invertible
208	  % sheaf on \(X\).}"
209	  \textit{Source: Hartshorne, II.6, p.~144 (definition of
210	  \(\mathscr L(D)\) and Proposition~6.13(a)).}
211	  Let \(C\) be a smooth proper curve over \(\bar k\) and let \(P \in C\) be a
212	  closed point. Let \(\mathfrak m_P \subset \mathcal O_{C, P}\) denote the
213	  maximal ideal of the local ring at \(P\); by smoothness of \(C\),
214	  \(\mathcal O_{C, P}\) is a discrete valuation ring and \(\mathfrak m_P\)
215	  is generated by any uniformizer \(f_P \in \mathcal O_{C, P}\) (an
216	  element of \(\mathfrak m_P \setminus \mathfrak m_P^2\)). The Cartier
217	  divisor associated to the closed point \(P\) is the effective
218	  Cartier divisor cut out locally by the uniformizer \(f_P\) on an open
219	  affine neighbourhood of \(P\), and by the unit \(1\) on the complement
220	  \(C \setminus \{P\}\). The \emph{line bundle of the closed point \(P\)},
221	  denoted \(\mathcal O_C(P)\), is the locally free \(\mathcal O_C\)-module
222	  of rank \(1\) defined by Hartshorne's construction \(\mathscr L(D)\)
223	  applied to this Cartier divisor: explicitly, \(\mathcal O_C(P)\) is the
224	  sub-\(\mathcal O_C\)-module of the sheaf of total quotient rings
225	  \(\mathcal K_C\) (the constant sheaf of the function field \(K(C)\) on the
226	  integral scheme \(C\)) that is generated by \(f_P^{-1} \in K(C)\) on a
227	  neighbourhood of \(P\) and by \(1 \in K(C)\) on the complement
228	  \(C \setminus \{P\}\). Hartshorne's Proposition~6.13(a) confirms that
229	  this subsheaf is an invertible sheaf, independently of the choice of
230	  uniformizer \(f_P\) (any two uniformizers differ by a unit in
231	  \(\mathcal O_{C, P}^{\times}\), so they generate the same
232	  \(\mathcal O_C\)-submodule of \(\mathcal K_C\)).
233	
234	  \paragraph{Equivalent description via the ideal sheaf.} Let
235	  \(\mathcal I_P \subset \mathcal O_C\) denote the ideal sheaf of the
236	  closed point \(P\) (locally generated by the uniformizer \(f_P\) near
237	  \(P\), and equal to \(\mathcal O_C\) on the complement). Then
238	  \(\mathcal I_P\) is the line bundle \(\mathcal O_C(-P)\) associated to the
239	  Weil divisor \(-[P]\), and \(\mathcal O_C(P)\) is the
240	  \(\mathcal O_C\)-linear dual:
241	  \[
242	    \mathcal O_C(P) \;\cong\; \mathcal H\!om_{\mathcal O_C}(\mathcal I_P,
243	    \mathcal O_C)
244	    \;\cong\; \mathcal I_P^{-1}.
245	  \]
246	  Both descriptions yield the same invertible sheaf; the
247	  ``subsheaf of \(\mathcal K_C\)'' description above is the one used in
248	  the prose, while the ``dual of the ideal sheaf'' description is the
249	  most direct entry point for the Lean construction (Mathlib's
250	  \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.IdealSheafData} packages
251	  quasi-coherent ideal sheaves, from which the dual is constructed via
252	  the internal-hom on invertible sheaves).
253	
254	  \paragraph{Lean signature scope.} The Lean definition
255	  \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.lineBundleAtClosedPoint} takes a
256	  scheme \(C\), a point \(P : C\), and a closedness witness
257	  \(\texttt{IsClosed (\{P\} : Set C)}\) (the same input shape as
258	  REF), and returns an invertible
259	  \(\mathcal O_C\)-module \(\mathcal O_C(P)\). The well-definedness as an
260	  invertible sheaf is part of the construction and goes through
261	  smoothness of \(C\) (delivering the DVR stalk at \(P\)) and the standard
262	  ideal-sheaf-of-a-closed-point machinery in Mathlib's
263	  \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.IdealSheaf} chapter. The chapter's
264	  consumption of the closed-point hypothesis matches \texttt{RR.1}:
265	  closed plus dimension-\(1\)-integral delivers \(\mathrm{codim}_C \{P\} = 1\)
266	  automatically. The declaration is \texttt{noncomputable}.
267	\end{definition}

270	
271	\leanok
272	[Inclusion of \(\mathcal O_C(P)\) into the function field]
273	  \label{def:lineBundleAtClosedPoint_toFunctionField}
274	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.lineBundleAtClosedPoint.toFunctionField}
275	  
276	  % SOURCE: [Hartshorne], II.6, p.~144 (definition of \(\mathscr L(D)\) as a
277	  % subsheaf of the sheaf of total quotient rings \(\mathscr K\); the
278	  % canonical inclusion \(\mathscr L(D) \hookrightarrow \mathscr K\) is part
279	  % of the construction) (read from
280	  % references/hartshorne-algebraic-geometry.pdf, PDF page 161).
281	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Definition. Let \(D\) be a Cartier divisor on a scheme
282	  % \(X\), represented by \(\{(U_i, f_i)\}\) as above. We define a subsheaf
283	  % \(\mathscr L(D)\) of the sheaf of total quotient rings \(\mathscr K\) by
284	  % taking \(\mathscr L(D)\) to be the sub-\(\mathscr O_X\)-module of
285	  % \(\mathscr K\) generated by \(f_i^{-1}\) on \(U_i\). This is well-defined,
286	  % since \(f_i/f_j\) is invertible on \(U_i \cap U_j\), and \(f_i^{-1}\) and
287	  % \(f_j^{-1}\) generate the same \(\mathscr O_X\)-module. We call
288	  % \(\mathscr L(D)\) the \emph{sheaf associated to \(D\)}."
289	  \textit{Source: Hartshorne, II.6, p.~144 (definition of \(\mathscr L(D)\)
290	  as a subsheaf of \(\mathscr K\)).}
291	  Let \(C\) be a smooth proper geometrically irreducible curve over \(\bar k\)
292	  with \(C\) integral, and let \(P \in C\) be a closed point. The line bundle
293	  \(\mathcal O_C(P)\) of REF is by
294	  construction a sub-\(\mathcal O_C\)-module of the sheaf of total quotient

306	  injective (the sub-\(\mathcal O_C\)-module $\mathcal O_C(P) \subset
