323	\section{Global sections as a Riemann--Roch space}
324	
325	\begin{lemma}
326	
327	
328	\leanok
329	[Global sections of \(\mathcal O_C(P)\) as rational functions with a
330	controlled pole]
331	  \label{lem:lineBundleAtClosedPoint_globalSections_iff}
332	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.lineBundleAtClosedPoint.globalSections_iff}
333	  
334	  % SOURCE: [Hartshorne], II.7, Proposition~7.7, p.~157 (effective
335	  % divisors linearly equivalent to \(D_0\) are the divisors of zeros of
336	  % global sections of \(\mathscr L(D_0)\), with the explicit
337	  % identification of those global sections with rational functions
338	  % \(f\) satisfying \((f) \geq -D_0\)) (read from
339	  % references/hartshorne-algebraic-geometry.pdf, PDF page 174).
340	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Proposition 7.7. \emph{Let \(X\) be a nonsingular
341	  % projective variety over the algebraically closed field \(k\). Let
342	  % \(D_0\) be a divisor on \(X\) and let \(\mathscr L = \mathscr L(D_0)\)
343	  % be the corresponding invertible sheaf. Then:
344	  % (a) for each nonzero \(s \in \Gamma(X, \mathscr L)\), the divisor of
345	  % zeros \((s)_0\) is an effective divisor linearly equivalent to \(D_0\);
346	  % (b) every effective divisor linearly equivalent to \(D_0\) is \((s)_0\)
347	  % for some \(s \in \Gamma(X, \mathscr L)\); and
348	  % (c) two sections \(s, s' \in \Gamma(X, \mathscr L)\) have the same
349	  % divisor of zeros if and only if there is a \(\lambda \in k^*\) such
350	  % that \(s' = \lambda s\).}"
351	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "(b) If \(D \geq 0\) and \(D = D_0 + (f)\), then
352	  % \((f) \geq -D_0\). Thus \(f\) gives a global section of
353	  % \(\mathscr L(D_0)\) whose divisor of zeros is \(D\)."
354	  \textit{Source: Hartshorne, II.7, Proposition~7.7 and its
355	  proof of part (b), p.~157.}
356	  Let \(C\) be a smooth proper curve over \(\bar k\), let \(P \in C\) be a
357	  closed point, and let \(\mathcal O_C(P)\) be the line bundle of
358	  REF. The \(\bar k\)-vector space of
359	  global sections of \(\mathcal O_C(P)\) is naturally identified with the
360	  Riemann--Roch space
361	  \[
362	    L([P]) \;:=\;
363	    \bigl\{f \in K(C)^{\times} \;\big|\; \div(f) + [P] \geq 0
364	    \text{ in } \mathrm{Div}(C)\bigr\}
365	    \,\cup\, \{0\}.
366	  \]
367	  Equivalently, the order conditions
368	  \[
369	    \ord_Q(f) \;\geq\; 0 \quad \text{for every closed point } Q \in C
370	    \text{ with } Q \neq P,
371	    \qquad
372	    \ord_P(f) \;\geq\; -1
373	  \]
374	  on \(f \in K(C)^{\times}\) are equivalent to the existence of a global
375	  section \(s \in H^0(C, \mathcal O_C(P))\) whose image \(\iota(s)\) under
376	  the canonical inclusion $\iota : \mathcal O_C(P) \hookrightarrow
377	  \mathcal K_C \cong K(C)\( equals \)f\( (the iff binds \)s\( to \)f$
378	  explicitly — vacuous-in-\(f\) readings such as ``some nonzero global
379	  section exists'' are excluded by construction).
380	
381	  Under this identification, the inclusion
382	  \(\bar k \hookrightarrow H^0(C, \mathcal O_C(P))\) of constant functions
383	  corresponds to the natural map $\mathcal O_C \hookrightarrow
384	  \mathcal O_C(P)\( exhibiting \)\mathcal O_C(P)$ as the twist of the
385	  structure sheaf along the prime divisor \([P]\).
386	\end{lemma}
387	
388	\begin{proof}
389	  
390	  
391	  
392	\leanok
393	  By construction, \(\mathcal O_C(P)\) is the sub-\(\mathcal O_C\)-module of
394	  the sheaf of total quotient rings \(\mathcal K_C\) generated locally by
395	  \(f_P^{-1}\) near \(P\) and by \(1\) on \(C \setminus \{P\}\), where \(f_P\) is
396	  a uniformizer at \(P\) (REF). Since \(C\)
397	  is integral, \(\mathcal K_C\) is the constant sheaf at the function field
398	  \(K(C)\), and a global section of any sub-\(\mathcal O_C\)-module
399	  \(\mathcal F \subset \mathcal K_C\) is precisely an element of \(K(C)\)
400	  whose germ at every point lies in \(\mathcal F\) at that point.
401	  Therefore \(f \in K(C)\) is a global section of \(\mathcal O_C(P)\) if
402	  and only if:
403	
404	  \begin{itemize}
405	    \item For every closed point \(Q \in C\) with \(Q \neq P\), the germ of
406	      \(f\) at \(Q\) lies in \(\mathcal O_C(P)_Q = \mathcal O_{C, Q}\)
407	      (the structure sheaf, since \(\mathcal O_C(P)\) agrees with
408	      \(\mathcal O_C\) on \(C \setminus \{P\}\)); equivalently,
409	      \(\ord_Q(f) \geq 0\).
410	    \item At the closed point \(P\), the germ of \(f\) at \(P\) lies in
411	      \(\mathcal O_C(P)_P = f_P^{-1} \cdot \mathcal O_{C, P}\);
412	      equivalently, \(f_P \cdot f \in \mathcal O_{C, P}\), i.e.\
413	      \(\ord_P(f_P \cdot f) \geq 0\), i.e.\ \(\ord_P(f) \geq -1\).
414	  \end{itemize}
415	  In divisorial language (REF,
416	  REF), the two conditions combine to
417	  \(\div(f) + [P] \geq 0\), recovering the Riemann--Roch space description
418	  \(L([P])\). The zero element of \(K(C)\) is included by convention as the
419	  zero global section.
420	
421	  The identification with Hartshorne's Proposition~7.7(b) is direct:
422	  taking \(D_0 = [P]\) effective, Hartshorne's argument shows that a
423	  rational function \(f\) with \((f) \geq -D_0\) defines a global section
424	  of \(\mathscr L(D_0)\). The condition \((f) \geq -D_0 = -[P]\) is exactly
425	  \(\div(f) + [P] \geq 0\). The compatibility with the constant inclusion
426	  follows by taking \(f = 1 \in K(C)^{\times}\): then \(\div(1) = 0\), so
427	  \(\div(1) + [P] = [P] \geq 0\), and the corresponding section of
428	  \(\mathcal O_C(P)\) is the image of $1 \in H^0(C, \mathcal O_C)
429	  \cong \bar k\( under the structural inclusion \)\mathcal O_C
430	  \hookrightarrow \mathcal O_C(P)$.
431	\end{proof}
432	
433	\section{Cohomological vanishing in genus zero}
434	
435	\begin{lemma}
436	
437	
438	\leanok
439	[Vanishing of \(H^1(C, \mathcal O_C(P))\) on a genus-\(0\) curve]
440	  \label{lem:H1_vanishing_lineBundleAtClosedPoint_genusZero}
441	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.lineBundleAtClosedPoint.h1_vanishing_genusZero}
442	  
443	  % SOURCE: [Hartshorne], IV.1, the proof of Theorem~1.3 (the inductive
444	  % step ``Next, let \(D\) be any divisor, and let \(P\) be any point.''):
445	  % the short exact sequence $0 \to \mathscr L(-P) \to \mathscr O_X \to
446	  % k(P) \to 0\( and its tensor with \)\mathscr L(D + P)$ giving
447	  % \(0 \to \mathscr L(D) \to \mathscr L(D + P) \to k(P) \to 0\)
448	  % (read from references/hartshorne-algebraic-geometry.pdf, PDF page 313).
449	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "We consider \(P\) as a closed subscheme of \(X\). Its
450	  % structure sheaf is a skyscraper sheaf \(k\) sitting at the point \(P\),
451	  % which we denote by \(k(P)\), and its ideal sheaf is \(\mathscr L(-P)\)
452	  % by (II, 6.18). Therefore we have an exact sequence
453	  % \(0 \to \mathscr L(-P) \to \mathscr O_X \to k(P) \to 0\).
454	  % Tensoring with \(\mathscr L(D + P)\) we get
455	  % \(0 \to \mathscr L(D) \to \mathscr L(D + P) \to k(P) \to 0\),
456	  % (since \(\mathscr L(P)\) is locally free of rank 1, tensoring by it
457	  % does not affect the sheaf \(k(P)\))."
458	  \textit{Source: Hartshorne, IV.1, p.~296, the inductive step of the
459	  proof of Theorem~1.3.}
460	  Let \(C\) be a smooth proper geometrically irreducible curve over
461	  \(\bar k\) with \(g(C) = 0\), and let \(P \in C\) be a closed point. Then
462	  \[
463	    H^1(C, \mathcal O_C(P)) \;=\; 0,
464	  \]
465	  i.e.\ \(\dim_{\bar k} H^1(C, \mathcal O_C(P)) = 0\).
466	\end{lemma}
467	
468	\begin{proof}
469	  
470	  Specialise Hartshorne's inductive step at \(D = 0\). The standard short
471	  exact sequence
472	  \[
473	    0 \to \mathcal O_C(-[P]) \to \mathcal O_C \to k(P) \to 0
474	  \]
475	  (Hartshorne~II.6.18: the ideal sheaf of the locally principal closed
476	  subscheme \(P\) is \(\mathcal O_C(-[P])\); the quotient is the structure
477	  sheaf of \(P\), which is the skyscraper sheaf \(k(P) \cong \bar k\)
478	  supported at \(P\) since \(P\) has residue field \(\bar k\) by closedness
479	  over the algebraically closed base \(\bar k\)). Tensoring with the
480	  locally free rank-\(1\) sheaf \(\mathcal O_C([P])\)
481	  (REF) preserves exactness and leaves
482	  the skyscraper sheaf \(k(P)\) unchanged, giving
483	  \[
484	    0 \to \mathcal O_C \to \mathcal O_C(P) \to k(P) \to 0
485	  \]
486	  in \(\mathbf{Coh}(C)\). Apply the long exact sequence of sheaf
487	  cohomology (REF, the project's $\Module \bar
488	  k\(-flavoured cohomology pipeline). Using that \)\dim C = 1$ kills
489	  \(H^i\) for \(i \geq 2\) (Grothendieck vanishing on a \(1\)-dimensional
490	  scheme), the long exact sequence reads
491	  \begin{multline*}
492	    0 \to H^0(C, \mathcal O_C) \to H^0(C, \mathcal O_C(P)) \to H^0(C, k(P)) \\
493	    \to H^1(C, \mathcal O_C) \to H^1(C, \mathcal O_C(P)) \to H^1(C, k(P)) \to 0.
494	  \end{multline*}
495	  Two ingredients close the proof:
496	
497	  \emph{(a) \(H^1(C, \mathcal O_C) = 0\).} This is the genus-\(0\)
498	  hypothesis: by REF,
499	  \[
500	    g(C) \;=\; \dim_{\bar k} H^1(C, \mathcal O_C),
501	  \]
502	  so \(g(C) = 0\) is exactly \(\dim_{\bar k} H^1(C, \mathcal O_C) = 0\),
503	  i.e.\ \(H^1(C, \mathcal O_C) = 0\) as a \(\bar k\)-vector space.
504	
505	  \emph{(b) \(H^1(C, k(P)) = 0\).} The skyscraper sheaf \(k(P)\) is
506	  supported at a single closed point, hence is flasque: every section
507	  on an open \(U \subset C\) either restricts to \(0\) (if \(P \notin U\)) or
508	  takes values in the stalk \(k(P)_P \cong \bar k\) (if \(P \in U\)), and
509	  restriction maps between opens of either type are surjective. A
510	  flasque sheaf on any topological space has vanishing higher
511	  cohomology (Hartshorne~III.2.5); in particular
512	  \(H^i(C, k(P)) = 0\) for \(i \geq 1\), and \emph{a fortiori}
513	  \(H^1(C, k(P)) = 0\).
514	
515	  Substituting (a) and (b) into the long exact sequence collapses the
516	  segment ending at \(H^1(C, \mathcal O_C(P))\) to
517	  \[
518	    0 \to H^1(C, \mathcal O_C(P)) \to 0,
519	  \]
520	  whence \(H^1(C, \mathcal O_C(P)) = 0\), as required.
521	
522	  \emph{Lean reference note on the long exact sequence.} Mathlib
523	  (snapshot \texttt{b80f227}) ships the long exact sequence of
524	  \(\Ext_{\mathcal O_C}^*(\mathbf 1, -)\) at the abelian-group level
525	  (\texttt{CategoryTheory.Abelian.Ext.covariantSequence\_exact}); the
526	  project's \(\Module \bar k\)-flavoured cohomology pipeline
527	  (REF) inherits the long exact sequence by
528	  forget-functor naturality. The skyscraper-cohomology vanishing
529	  \(H^i(C, k(P)) = 0\) for \(i \geq 1\) is closed by either the flasque
530	  argument (Hartshorne~III.2.5; Mathlib has the abelian-sheaf form
531	  via \texttt{CategoryTheory.Sheaf.Flasque}) or, more directly for the
532	  curve case, by the affine-cover Čech-cohomology argument on
533	  \(C \setminus \{P\}\) and a small affine open around \(P\) (the
534	  skyscraper has zero sections on \(C \setminus \{P\}\), so the Čech
535	  complex degenerates).
536	\end{proof}
537	
538	\section{The dimension formula in genus zero}
539	
540	% NOTE (iter-194 reviewer): The Lean body is now structurally
541	% sorry-free (closed transitively through two named substrate
542	% helpers). The substrate helpers `h0_sub_h1_lineBundleAtClosedPoint_eq_two`
543	% (the Int-valued $H^0 - H^1 = 2$ \(\chi\)-bridge; gated on
544	% `OcOfD.lean` STRUCTURALLY BLOCKED + `RRFormula.lean` $\chi$-additivity)
545	% and `h1_vanishing_genusZero` (gated on Lane H \texttt{H1Vanishing.lean}
546	% substrate) carry the remaining typed sorries.  Future consumers
547	% reading the body should consult those.
548	\begin{theorem}
549	
550	
551	\leanok
552	[\(\dim_{\bar k} H^0(C, \mathcal O_C(P)) = 2\) on a genus-\(0\) curve]
553	  \label{thm:lineBundleAtClosedPoint_dim_eq_two_of_genusZero}
554	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.lineBundleAtClosedPoint.dim_eq_two_of_genusZero}
555	  
556	  % SOURCE: [Hartshorne], IV.1, the Riemann--Roch chain inside
557	  % Example~1.3.5 applied with \(D = [P]\): $\chi(\mathscr L([P]))
558	  % = \deg([P]) + 1 - g = 1 + 1 - 0 = 2$, combined with the vanishing
559	  % \(H^1(\mathscr L([P])) = 0\) (read from
560	  % references/hartshorne-algebraic-geometry.pdf, PDF pages 312, 314).
561	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Theorem 1.3 (Riemann--Roch). \emph{Let \(D\) be a
562	  % divisor on a curve \(X\) of genus \(g\). Then}
563	  % \(l(D) - l(K - D) = \deg D + 1 - g\)."
564	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Thus we have to show that for any \(D\),
565	  % \(\chi(\mathscr L(D)) = \deg D + 1 - g\),
566	  % where for any coherent sheaf \(\mathscr F\) on \(X\), \(\chi(\mathscr F)\)
567	  % is the Euler characteristic
568	  % \(\chi(\mathscr F) = \dim H^0(X, \mathscr F) - \dim H^1(X, \mathscr F)\)."
569	  \textit{Source: Hartshorne, IV.1, Theorem~1.3, p.~295 (the
570	  \(\chi\)-identity inside the proof) and Example~1.3.5, p.~297 (the
571	  genus-\(0\) specialisation chain).}
572	  Let \(C\) be a smooth proper geometrically irreducible curve over
573	  \(\bar k\) with \(g(C) = 0\), and let \(P \in C\) be a closed point. Then
574	  \[
575	    \dim_{\bar k} H^0(C, \mathcal O_C(P)) \;=\; 2.
576	  \]
577	\end{theorem}
578	
579	\begin{proof}
580	  
581	  
582	\leanok
583	  Specialise the Euler-characteristic identity
584	  REF of \texttt{RR.2} to
585	  the divisor \(D = [P]\) of REF. The
586	  degree of \([P]\) is \(\deg([P]) = 1\) (every closed point contributes
587	  \(1\) to the bare-sum degree of REF), and the
588	  genus hypothesis \(g(C) = 0\) (REF) gives
589	  \[
590	    \chi(\mathcal O_C(P)) \;=\; \deg([P]) + 1 - g(C)
591	    \;=\; 1 + 1 - 0 \;=\; 2.
592	  \]
593	  Unfolding \(\chi\) via REF as a two-term Euler
594	  characteristic on a curve, this rewrites as
595	  \[
596	    \dim_{\bar k} H^0(C, \mathcal O_C(P))
597	    \,-\, \dim_{\bar k} H^1(C, \mathcal O_C(P))
598	    \;=\; 2.
599	  \]
600	  By
601	  REF, the second
602	  term vanishes:
603	  \(\dim_{\bar k} H^1(C, \mathcal O_C(P)) = 0\). Substituting yields
604	  \(\dim_{\bar k} H^0(C, \mathcal O_C(P)) = 2\), as required.
605	
606	  \emph{Alternative direct route via the long exact sequence.} The
607	  same long exact sequence used in the proof of
608	  REF computes
609	  the dimension of \(H^0\) in one stroke without invoking
610	  \texttt{RR.2}: from
611	  \[
612	    0 \to H^0(C, \mathcal O_C) \to H^0(C, \mathcal O_C(P))
613	    \to H^0(C, k(P)) \to H^1(C, \mathcal O_C) \to \cdots
614	  \]
615	  together with \(\dim_{\bar k} H^0(C, \mathcal O_C) = 1\)
616	  (Hartshorne~I.3.4: \(H^0\) of the structure sheaf on a proper
617	  geometrically irreducible variety over \(\bar k\) is \(\bar k\)),
618	  \(\dim_{\bar k} H^0(C, k(P)) = 1\) (skyscraper at a \(\bar k\)-rational
619	  point), and \(H^1(C, \mathcal O_C) = 0\) (genus-\(0\)), the connecting
620	  map \(H^0(C, k(P)) \to H^1(C, \mathcal O_C)\) has zero codomain and the
621	  segment yields the short exact sequence
622	  \[
623	    0 \to \bar k \to H^0(C, \mathcal O_C(P)) \to \bar k \to 0
624	  \]
625	  of \(\bar k\)-vector spaces, whence $\dim_{\bar k}
626	  H^0(C, \mathcal O_C(P)) = 1 + 1 = 2$. This alternative route does
627	  not consume the Euler-characteristic identity of \texttt{RR.2} and
628	  could be substituted at the formalisation stage; the chapter uses
629	  \texttt{RR.2} as written above to keep the dependency graph in
630	  line with the iter-173 RR sub-build sequencing.
631	\end{proof}
632	
633	\section{Existence of a non-constant rational function}
634	
635	\begin{corollary}
636	
637	
638	\leanok
639	[A genus-\(0\) curve admits a non-constant rational function with a
640	single simple pole]
641	  \label{cor:lineBundleAtClosedPoint_exists_nonconstant_genusZero}
642	  % Alias label consumed under the shorter name by
643	  % \texttt{RiemannRoch\_RationalCurveIso.tex} (RR.4):
644	  \label{cor:nonconstant_function_genus_zero}
645	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.lineBundleAtClosedPoint.exists_nonconstant_genusZero}
646	  
647	  % SOURCE: [Hartshorne], IV.1, Exercise~1.1 (the existence of a
648	  % nonconstant rational function regular everywhere except at one
649	  % point on a genus-\(0\) curve, as a direct consequence of the
650	  % Riemann--Roch dimension formula) (read from
651	  % references/hartshorne-algebraic-geometry.pdf, PDF page 314).
652	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "1.1. Let \(X\) be a curve, and let \(P \in X\) be a
653	  % point. Then there exists a nonconstant rational function $f \in
654	  % K(X)\(, which is regular everywhere except at \)P$."
655	  \textit{Source: Hartshorne, IV.1, Exercise~1.1, p.~297.}
656	  Let \(C\) be a smooth proper geometrically irreducible curve over
657	  \(\bar k\) with \(g(C) = 0\), and let \(P \in C\) be a closed point. Then
658	  there exists a non-constant rational function \(f \in K(C)\) such that:
659	  \begin{itemize}
660	    \item \(f\) is regular on \(C \setminus \{P\}\), i.e.\ \(\ord_Q(f) \geq 0\)
661	      for every closed point \(Q \in C\) with \(Q \neq P\);
662	    \item \(f\) has at most a simple pole at \(P\), i.e.\
663	      \(\ord_P(f) \geq -1\);
664	    \item \(f \notin \bar k\), equivalently
665	      \(\div(f) \neq 0 \in \mathrm{Div}(C)\).
666	  \end{itemize}
667	  In particular, the pair \((1, f)\) spans
668	  \(H^0(C, \mathcal O_C(P))\) as a \(\bar k\)-vector space.
669	\end{corollary}
670	
671	\begin{proof}
672	  
673	  
674	\leanok
675	  By
676	  REF,
677	  \(\dim_{\bar k} H^0(C, \mathcal O_C(P)) = 2\). The constant subspace
678	  \(\bar k \cdot 1 \subset H^0(C, \mathcal O_C(P))\), obtained from the
679	  structural inclusion $\bar k \cong H^0(C, \mathcal O_C)
680	  \hookrightarrow H^0(C, \mathcal O_C(P))$ (the rational function
681	  \(f = 1\), with \(\div(1) + [P] = [P] \geq 0\), under the
682	  identification of
683	  REF), is a one-dimensional
684	  \(\bar k\)-vector subspace. Since the ambient space has dimension \(2\),
685	  the quotient \(H^0(C, \mathcal O_C(P)) / \bar k \cdot 1\) is a
686	  non-zero one-dimensional \(\bar k\)-vector space; any lift to
687	  \(H^0(C, \mathcal O_C(P))\) of a non-zero element of the quotient is
688	  an element of \(H^0(C, \mathcal O_C(P)) \setminus \bar k \cdot 1\).
689	
690	  Choose such a lift $s \in H^0(C, \mathcal O_C(P)) \setminus \bar k
691	  \cdot 1$. Under the identification of
692	  REF the section
693	  \(s\) corresponds to a rational function \(f \in K(C)^{\times}\)
694	  satisfying \(\div(f) + [P] \geq 0\), equivalently \(\ord_Q(f) \geq 0\)
695	  for every \(Q \neq P\) and \(\ord_P(f) \geq -1\). The choice
696	  \(s \notin \bar k \cdot 1\) means \(f\) is not the image of a constant,
697	  so \(f \notin \bar k\) (every non-zero element of
698	  \(H^0(C, \mathcal O_C) \cong \bar k\) corresponds, under the
699	  identification, to a constant element of \(K(C)\)). The three bullets
700	  of the statement are now immediate.
701	
702	  Spanning. Linear independence of \(1\) and \(f\) over \(\bar k\) in
703	  \(H^0(C, \mathcal O_C(P))\) is the same condition as
704	  \(s \notin \bar k \cdot 1\) above; combined with the dimension count
705	  \(\dim_{\bar k} H^0(C, \mathcal O_C(P)) = 2\), the pair \((1, f)\) is a
706	  \(\bar k\)-basis of \(H^0(C, \mathcal O_C(P))\).
707	
708	  \paragraph{Lean signature scope.} The Lean signature
709	  \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.lineBundleAtClosedPoint.exists\_nonconstant\_genusZero}
710	  returns an element \(f\) of the project's function-field carrier
711	  \(K(C)\) together with proofs of regularity on \(C \setminus \{P\}\) and
712	  the simple-pole bound at \(P\), packaged in the same shape as
713	  REF's output. The
714	  non-constancy is recorded as a separate field; the spanning
715	  statement is downstream and is consumed by \texttt{RR.4} via
716	  \texttt{Proj.fromOfGlobalSections}.
717	\end{proof}
718

725	elsewhere:

729	    cohomology.} Mathlib (\texttt{b80f227}) ships

739	    on a Noetherian scheme. Hartshorne~III.2.5 gives the flasque

741	    (REF and surrounding

1	/-

6	import Mathlib
7	import AlgebraicJacobian.Genus
8	import AlgebraicJacobian.Genus0BaseObjects
9	
10	/-!
11	# Weil divisors on a smooth proper curve (RR.1)
12	
13	This file is the **RR.1** sub-build chapter for the project's headline
14	`genusZero_curve_iso_P1` (the "smooth proper geom-irred genus-`0` curve over `k̄` is
15	isomorphic to `ℙ¹`" lemma in `AlgebraicJacobian.AbelianVarietyRigidity`).
16	
17	Mathlib `b80f227` ships no `WeilDivisor` on a scheme; adjacent pieces
18	(`MeromorphicOn.divisor`, `CommRing.Pic`, `Scheme.RationalMap`) cover different
19	ground. This file is therefore **project-bespoke**, scaffolding the formal-sum data
20	type `Div(X) = ⨁_{Y ⊂ X codim 1} ℤ` on a Noetherian integral scheme `X` satisfying
21	Hartshorne's condition `(*)`, the principal-divisor homomorphism
22	`div : K(X)^× → Div(X)` on a curve, the degree map `deg : Div(C) → ℤ` on a smooth
23	proper curve over an algebraically closed field, the degree-zero of principal divisors
24	on a complete nonsingular curve (Hartshorne Cor. II.6.10), and the linear-equivalence
25	relation `D ~ D'`.
26	
27	## Status (iter-172 file-skeleton)
28	
29	This file is the **iter-172 Lane C** file-skeleton: each declaration carries the
30	intended signature (matching the blueprint `\lean{...}` pin) with a `sorry` body.
31	The bodies are iter-173+ work after the sibling chapters `RR.2`
32	(`RiemannRoch_RRFormula.tex`), `RR.3` (`RiemannRoch_OcOfD.tex`), and `RR.4`
33	(`RiemannRoch_RationalIsoP1.tex`) land.
34	
35	The 9 pinned declarations are:
36	
37	1. `Scheme.WeilDivisor` — free abelian group on prime divisors (Definition).
38	2. `Scheme.RationalMap.order` — order of a rational function along a prime divisor.
39	3. `Scheme.WeilDivisor.ofClosedPoint` — Weil divisor associated to a closed point.
40	4. `Scheme.WeilDivisor.degree` — degree map over an algebraically closed base.
41	5. `Scheme.WeilDivisor.degree_hom` — degree is a group homomorphism (Theorem).
42	6. `Scheme.WeilDivisor.principal` — principal divisor of a rational function.
43	7. `Scheme.WeilDivisor.principal_hom` — `div` is a group homomorphism (Theorem).
44	8. `Scheme.WeilDivisor.principal_degree_zero` — `deg ∘ div = 0` on a complete curve.
45	9. `Scheme.WeilDivisor.LinearEquivalence` — linear equivalence of Weil divisors.
46	
47	## References
48	
49	Blueprint: `blueprint/src/chapters/RiemannRoch_WeilDivisor.tex` (445 LOC, 9 pins).
50	Hartshorne, *Algebraic Geometry*, II §6 (pp. 130–137 + IV.1 pp. 294–296).
51	Stacks Project, tags 02RW (divisors), 02ME (order at a point), 0BE0 (degree),
52	0BE3 (principal divisors have degree zero on a complete nonsingular curve).
53	-/
54	
55	set_option autoImplicit false
56	
57	universe u
58	
59	open CategoryTheory Limits
60	
61	namespace AlgebraicGeometry
62	
63	/-! ## §1. Codim-1 cycle group / Weil divisor group
64	
65	Hartshorne's condition `(*)` (II §6, p. 130) is: `X` is a Noetherian integral
66	separated scheme that is regular in codimension one. Under `(*)`, prime divisors
67	are closed integral subschemes of codimension one, and the local ring at the
68	generic point of a prime divisor is a DVR with quotient field the function field
69	`K(X)`.
70	
71	A prime divisor is encoded by its **generic point** together with the
72	codimension-one witness `Order.coheight point = 1`: the data field `point : X`
73	selects the generic point of the closed integral subscheme, and the predicate
74	field `coheight` enforces codimension one in the specialisation preorder
75	(Hartshorne II §6 p. 130; blueprint pin `def:prime_divisor`). Integrality of the
76	closure is automatic, so no separate integrality witness is needed.
77	-/
78	
79	/-- A **prime divisor** on a scheme `X`: a closed integral subscheme of codimension
80	one, encoded (for a scheme satisfying Hartshorne's condition `(*)`) by its generic
81	point. On a curve, prime divisors correspond bijectively to closed points.
82	
83	The codimension-one witness is the predicate `Order.coheight point = 1` on the
84	specialisation preorder of `X.carrier` (Mathlib's convention is
85	`x ≤ y ↔ y ⤳ x`, so the generic point is the unique maximal element and a
86	generic point of a codim-1 closed integral subscheme has a length-one chain
87	above it). The closure `{point}̄` is automatically irreducible (so the reduced
88	induced subscheme structure is automatically integral); hence no separate
89	integrality field is needed.
90	
91	Blueprint reference: `def:prime_divisor` / `def:codim1_cycles` (Hartshorne II §6
92	p. 130; Stacks 02RW). -/
93	structure Scheme.PrimeDivisor (X : Scheme.{u}) where
94	  /-- The generic point of the closed integral subscheme. -/
95	  point : X
96	  /-- Codimension-1 witness: `point` has coheight `1` in the specialisation
97	  preorder on `X.carrier`. Per iter-173 `wd-spec-refine` (`def:prime_divisor`). -/
98	  coheight : Order.coheight point = 1
99	
100	/-- The **Weil divisor group** of a scheme `X` satisfying Hartshorne's condition

322:noncomputable def degree (D : X.WeilDivisor) : ℤ :=
378:noncomputable def principal [IsIntegral X] [IsLocallyNoetherian X]

350	
351	/-- **Degree is additive.** A direct consequence of `degree_hom` being an
352	`AddMonoidHom`. -/
353	lemma degree_add (D₁ D₂ : X.WeilDivisor) :
354	    degree (D₁ + D₂) = degree D₁ + degree D₂ := by
355	  rw [← degree_hom_apply, ← degree_hom_apply, ← degree_hom_apply]
356	  exact map_add _ _ _
357	
358	/-! ## §5. Principal divisors -/
359	
360	/-- **The principal divisor of a nonzero rational function `f ∈ K(X)^×`.**
361	
362	By Hartshorne's Lemma 6.1, `ord_Y(f) = 0` for all but finitely many prime
363	divisors `Y`, so the formal sum
364	`div(f) := Σ_{Y prime divisor} ord_Y(f) · Y ∈ Div(X)`
365	has finite support and is a well-defined Weil divisor.
366	
367	On a smooth proper curve `C` over `k̄`, this specialises to
368	`div(f) = Σ_{P closed point} ord_P(f) · [P]`.
369	
370	Blueprint reference: `def:principal_divisor` (Hartshorne II §6 Lemma 6.1 +
371	following definition, p. 131).
372	
373	iter-177 body: the construction uses `Finsupp.ofSupportFinite` with the
374	finite-support witness `rationalMap_order_finite_support`. The latter is a
375	private theorem packaging Hartshorne 6.1; its body is a Mathlib-pending gap
376	(see chapter `RiemannRoch_WeilDivisor.tex` §5 sub-build note) and is left as
377	a `sorry` for an iter-178+ Mathlib-upstream PR. -/
378	noncomputable def principal [IsIntegral X] [IsLocallyNoetherian X]
379	    [Scheme.IsRegularInCodimensionOne X] (f : X.functionField)
380	    (_hf : f ≠ 0) : X.WeilDivisor :=
381	  Finsupp.ofSupportFinite
382	    (fun Y : X.PrimeDivisor => Scheme.RationalMap.order Y f)
383	    (rationalMap_order_finite_support f)
384	
385	/-- **The coefficient of `principal f hf` at a prime divisor `Y` is the order of
386	`f` along `Y`.** This is the basic structural unfolding of the
387	`Finsupp.ofSupportFinite` packaging in `principal`; one-line via
388	`Finsupp.ofSupportFinite_coe` from `Mathlib.Data.Finsupp.Defs`.
389	
390	iter-193 substrate helper for the Lane I body close
391	(`degree_positivePart_principal_eq_finrank`). -/
392	lemma principal_apply [IsIntegral X] [IsLocallyNoetherian X]
393	    [Scheme.IsRegularInCodimensionOne X] (f : X.functionField) (hf : f ≠ 0)
394	    (Y : X.PrimeDivisor) :
395	    (show (X.PrimeDivisor →₀ ℤ) from principal f hf) Y =
396	      Scheme.RationalMap.order Y f := by
397	  change (Finsupp.ofSupportFinite
398	      (fun Y : X.PrimeDivisor => Scheme.RationalMap.order Y f)
399	      (rationalMap_order_finite_support f)) Y = _
400	  rw [Finsupp.ofSupportFinite_coe]
401	
402	/-- **`Scheme.RationalMap.order Y 1 = 0`** — the order of the constant
403	function `1` is `0` at every prime divisor. Direct from
404	`map_one` of `Ring.ordFrac` + `WithZero.log_one`.
405	
406	iter-193 substrate helper for further structural results
407	(`principal_one_eq_zero` and similar). -/
408	@[simp]
409	lemma _root_.AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.RationalMap.order_one
410	    {X : Scheme.{u}} [IsIntegral X] [IsLocallyNoetherian X]
411	    (Y : X.PrimeDivisor)
412	    [Ring.KrullDimLE 1 (X.presheaf.stalk Y.point)] :
413	    Scheme.RationalMap.order Y (1 : X.functionField) = 0 := by
414	  unfold Scheme.RationalMap.order
415	  rw [map_one, WithZero.log_one]
416	
417	/-- **The principal divisor of the constant function `1` is the zero
418	divisor.** A direct consequence of `Scheme.RationalMap.order_one`. -/
419	@[simp]
420	lemma principal_one [IsIntegral X] [IsLocallyNoetherian X]
421	    [Scheme.IsRegularInCodimensionOne X] :
422	    principal (1 : X.functionField) one_ne_zero = 0 := by
423	  change (_ : X.PrimeDivisor →₀ ℤ) = (0 : X.PrimeDivisor →₀ ℤ)
424	  apply Finsupp.ext
425	  intro Y
426	  rw [principal_apply]
427	  exact Scheme.RationalMap.order_one Y
428	
429	/-- **The principal-divisor map is a group homomorphism `K(X)^× → Div(X)`.**
430	
431	Concretely `div(fg) = div(f) + div(g)`, `div(f⁻¹) = -div(f)`, `div(1) = 0`. Bundled
432	as a `MonoidHom` from the multiplicative units of `K(X)` to `Multiplicative (Div(X))`
433	(equivalently: an additive map `(K(X)^×, ·) → (Div(X), +)`).
434	
435	Blueprint reference: `thm:principal_hom` (Hartshorne II §6 p. 131).
436	
437	iter-177 body: closes coordinate-wise from the DVR identities
438	`v_Y(fg) = v_Y(f) + v_Y(g)`, `v_Y(1) = 0`. The per-`Y` identities live in
439	`Scheme.RationalMap.order` via `Ring.ordFrac` (a `K →*₀ ℤᵐ⁰` monoid-with-zero
440	hom) and `WithZero.log_mul` / `WithZero.log_one`. -/
441	noncomputable def principal_hom [IsIntegral X] [IsLocallyNoetherian X]
442	    [Scheme.IsRegularInCodimensionOne X] :
443	    (X.functionField)ˣ →* Multiplicative X.WeilDivisor where
444	  toFun u :=
445	    Multiplicative.ofAdd (principal (↑u : X.functionField) (Units.ne_zero u))
446	  map_one' := by
447	    -- Goal: Multiplicative.ofAdd (principal ↑1 _) = 1.
448	    -- Use `← ofAdd_zero` to rewrite RHS as `Multiplicative.ofAdd 0`,
449	    -- then `congr 1` reduces to `principal ↑1 _ = 0` (Finsupp equality),
450	    -- and `Finsupp.ext` peels to the per-`Y` coordinate identity, which
451	    -- closes by unfolding `order` to `WithZero.log (Ring.ordFrac _ 1) = 0`
452	    -- via `map_one` and `WithZero.log_one`.
453	    rw [← ofAdd_zero]
454	    congr 1
455	    apply Finsupp.ext
456	    intro Y
457	    change Scheme.RationalMap.order Y
458	        (((1 : (X.functionField)ˣ) : X.functionField)) = 0
459	    rw [Units.val_one]
460	    unfold Scheme.RationalMap.order
461	    rw [map_one, WithZero.log_one]
462	  map_mul' u v := by
463	    -- Goal: Multiplicative.ofAdd (principal ↑(u*v) _) =
464	    --       Multiplicative.ofAdd (principal ↑u _) * Multiplicative.ofAdd (principal ↑v _).
465	    -- Use `← ofAdd_add` to rewrite RHS, then `congr 1` reduces to
466	    -- `principal ↑(u*v) _ = principal ↑u _ + principal ↑v _` (Finsupp
467	    -- equality), and `Finsupp.ext` peels to the per-`Y` coordinate
468	    -- identity `order Y (uv) = order Y u + order Y v`, which closes by
469	    -- `Ring.ordFrac (uv) = Ring.ordFrac u * Ring.ordFrac v` (`map_mul`)
470	    -- and `WithZero.log_mul` (the nonzero hypotheses come from
471	    -- `Units.ne_zero` and `map_ne_zero`).
472	    rw [← ofAdd_add]
473	    congr 1
474	    apply Finsupp.ext
475	    intro Y
476	    change Scheme.RationalMap.order Y ((↑(u * v) : X.functionField))
477	      = Scheme.RationalMap.order Y (↑u : X.functionField)
478	      + Scheme.RationalMap.order Y (↑v : X.functionField)
479	    rw [Units.val_mul]
480	    unfold Scheme.RationalMap.order
481	    rw [map_mul]
482	    exact WithZero.log_mul
483	      ((map_ne_zero _).mpr (Units.ne_zero u))
484	      ((map_ne_zero _).mpr (Units.ne_zero v))
485	
486	/-- **Principal divisors on a complete nonsingular curve have degree zero**
487	(Hartshorne Corollary II.6.10, Stacks 0BE3).
488	
489	For every nonzero rational function `f ∈ K(C)^×` on a smooth proper curve `C`
490	over an algebraically closed field `k̄`,
491	`deg(div(f)) = 0 ∈ ℤ`.
492	
493	Blueprint reference: `thm:principal_deg_zero` (Hartshorne II.6 Cor. 6.10 p. 138).
494	
495	The proof (Hartshorne 6.10): if `f ∈ k̄` is constant then `div(f) = 0` and the
496	claim is trivial. Otherwise the inclusion `k̄(f) ⊂ K(C)` exhibits `K(C)` as a
497	finite extension of `k̄(f) ≅ k̄(t)`, so the corresponding morphism
498	`φ : C → ℙ¹_{k̄}` is finite, `div(f) = φ^*([0] - [∞])`, and pullback along a
499	finite morphism multiplies degree by `deg(φ)`. Two auxiliary sub-lemmas
500	(finite morphism induced by a non-constant rational function; multiplicativity
501	of degree under finite pullback, Hartshorne II.6.9) are deferred to follow-up
502	iters of `RR.1`. -/
503	theorem principal_degree_zero {kbar : Type u} [Field kbar] [IsAlgClosed kbar]
504	    (C : Over (Spec (.of kbar))) [IsProper C.hom]
505	    [SmoothOfRelativeDimension 1 C.hom] [GeometricallyIrreducible C.hom]
506	    [IsIntegral C.left] [IsLocallyNoetherian C.left]
507	    [Scheme.IsRegularInCodimensionOne C.left]
508	    (f : C.left.functionField) (hf : f ≠ 0) :
509	    degree (principal f hf) = 0 := by
510	  -- Hartshorne II.6.10 (iter-178 partial). Case-split on whether every
511	  -- prime-divisor order of `f` vanishes. On a complete nonsingular curve,
512	  -- this case split exactly recovers Hartshorne's constant-vs-non-constant
513	  -- split: `(∀ Y, ord_Y f = 0)` ⟺ `f ∈ k̄ \ {0}` (a nowhere-vanishing
514	  -- rational function on a complete curve is constant; that direction is
515	  -- Hartshorne II.6.10's "if `f ∈ k̄` then `(f) = 0`").
516	  by_cases hconst : ∀ Y : C.left.PrimeDivisor, Scheme.RationalMap.order Y f = 0
517	  · -- Constant branch: every order vanishes ⟹ `principal f hf = 0` ⟹
518	    -- `degree (principal f hf) = degree 0 = 0`.
519	    have hprincipal_zero : principal f hf = 0 := by
520	      apply Finsupp.ext
521	      intro Y
522	      change Scheme.RationalMap.order Y f = 0
523	      exact hconst Y
524	    rw [hprincipal_zero]
525	    unfold degree
526	    exact Finsupp.sum_zero_index
527	  · -- Non-constant branch (Hartshorne II.6.10 `f ∉ k̄`): the inclusion
528	    -- `k̄(f) ⊂ K(C)` defines a finite morphism `φ : C → ℙ¹_{k̄}` via the
529	    -- function-field-determines-curve correspondence (Hartshorne I.6.12),
530	    -- and `principal f hf = φ^*([0] - [∞])`. Pullback along a finite
531	    -- morphism of curves multiplies degrees by `deg(φ)` (Hartshorne
532	    -- II.6.9), so `deg(principal f hf) = deg(φ) · deg([0] - [∞]) =
533	    -- deg(φ) · 0 = 0`.
534	    -- This branch is gated on (i) the `φ : C → ℙ¹` construction (Lane 5
535	    -- `RationalCurveIso.lean:morphismToP1OfGlobalSections`, iter-178+),
536	    -- and (ii) the degree-multiplicativity-under-finite-pullback bridge
537	    -- (Hartshorne II.6.9, Mathlib gap).
538	    sorry
539	
540	/-! ## §6. Positive part of a Weil divisor
541	
542	Iter-190 plan-phase Lane I Pin 2 corrective substrate. The carrier
543	`X.WeilDivisor = X.PrimeDivisor →₀ ℤ` is a finitely supported integer-valued
544	function on prime divisors; the *positive part* `(D)_0` is obtained by
545	replacing each coefficient `n_Y` with `max(n_Y, 0)`. Equivalently this is
546	the lattice join `D ⊔ 0` in the pointwise lattice structure (the
547	`semilatticeSup` instance on `Finsupp` is noncomputable). The complementary
548	*negative part* `(D)_∞ := (-D)_0` then gives the canonical decomposition
549	`D = (D)_0 - (D)_∞` into a difference of effective divisors.

240	lemma Submodule.exists_of_finrank_lt (N : Submodule R M) (h : finrank R N < finrank R M) :
241	    ∃ m : M, ∀ r : R, r ≠ 0 → r • m ∉ N := by
242	  obtain ⟨s, hs, hs'⟩ :=
243	    exists_finset_linearIndependent_of_le_finrank (R := R) (M := M ⧸ N) le_rfl
244	  obtain ⟨v, hv⟩ : s.Nonempty := by rwa [Finset.nonempty_iff_ne_empty, ne_eq, ← Finset.card_eq_zero,
245	    hs, finrank_quotient, tsub_eq_zero_iff_le, not_le]
246	  obtain ⟨v, rfl⟩ := N.mkQ_surjective v
247	  refine ⟨v, fun r hr ↦ mt ?_ hr⟩
248	  have := linearIndependent_iff.mp hs' (Finsupp.single ⟨_, hv⟩ r)
249	  rwa [Finsupp.linearCombination_single, Finsupp.single_eq_zero, ← map_smul,
250	    Submodule.mkQ_apply, Submodule.Quotient.mk_eq_zero] at this
251	
252	end

746	/-! ## §2. Skyscraper sheaves are flasque -/
747	
748	/-- **Skyscraper sheaf as pushforward of a constant sheaf** (Hartshorne
749	II.1, Exercise 1.17).
750	
751	The closed-point skyscraper sheaf `skyscraperSheaf P A` on a topological
752	space `X` is naturally isomorphic to the pushforward, along the constant
753	map `PUnit → X` at `P`, of the constant sheaf with value `A` on
754	`PUnit`. The constant sheaf on `PUnit` is itself isomorphic to the
755	skyscraper sheaf at `PUnit.unit`, so this is the sheaf-level
756	counterpart of Mathlib's presheaf-level
757	`skyscraperPresheaf_eq_pushforward` (Mathlib snapshot `b80f227`
758	`Topology/Sheaves/Skyscraper.lean`).
759	
760	For our usage on a curve, `PUnit` plays the role of `closure {P}`
761	(which on a Noetherian space with `P` closed is a singleton, hence
762	homeomorphic to `PUnit`).
763	
764	**iter-191 Lane H file-skeleton** — Tier-3 honest typed sorry; closure
765	is iter-192+. Blueprint reference:
766	`lem:skyscraperSheaf_eq_pushforward`. -/
767	theorem Scheme.skyscraperSheaf_eq_pushforward_const
768	    (kbar : Type u) [Field kbar]
769	    {X : TopCat.{u}} (P : X)
770	    [∀ U : TopologicalSpace.Opens X, Decidable (P ∈ U)]
771	    [∀ U : TopologicalSpace.Opens (TopCat.of PUnit.{u + 1}),
772	      Decidable (PUnit.unit ∈ U)]
773	    (A : ModuleCat.{u} kbar) :
774	    Nonempty
775	      (skyscraperSheaf (C := ModuleCat.{u} kbar) P A ≅
776	        (TopCat.Sheaf.pushforward (ModuleCat.{u} kbar)
777	            (TopCat.ofHom (ContinuousMap.const (TopCat.of PUnit.{u + 1}) P))).obj
778	          ((constantSheaf
779	              (Opens.grothendieckTopology (TopCat.of PUnit.{u + 1}))
780	              (ModuleCat.{u} kbar)).obj A)) := by
781	  sorry

783	/-- **Closure of the support point of a `PrimeDivisor` is irreducible**.
784	
785	For any scheme `X` and any `P : X.PrimeDivisor`, the topological
786	closure of the singleton `{P.point}` is an irreducible subset of `X`.
787	This is a project-bespoke ancillary that holds in full generality: the
788	closure of any irreducible set in a topological space is irreducible,
789	and a singleton `{x}` is irreducible (it is nonempty and contains no
790	proper non-empty closed subsets).
791	
792	**iter-191 Lane H prover dispatch** — closed via
793	`isIrreducible_singleton.closure`. Blueprint reference:
794	`lem:closedPoint_closure_irreducible`. -/
795	theorem Scheme.PrimeDivisor.closure_isIrreducible
796	    {X : Scheme.{u}} (P : X.PrimeDivisor) :
797	    IsIrreducible (closure ({P.point} : Set X)) :=
798	  isIrreducible_singleton.closure
799	
800	/-- **The closed-point skyscraper sheaf is flasque**.
801	
802	For a smooth proper geometrically irreducible curve `C / kbar` and a
803	prime divisor `P : C.left.PrimeDivisor` (equivalently, a closed point
804	on the curve), the closed-point skyscraper sheaf
805	`skyscraperSheaf P.point (ModuleCat.of kbar kbar)` is flasque as a
806	sheaf of `kbar`-modules on the underlying topological space of `C`.
807	
808	The proof originally was planned to compose the four lemma-blocks of this
809	chapter (`skyscraperSheaf_eq_pushforward_const`,
810	`PrimeDivisor.closure_isIrreducible`, `IsFlasque.constant_of_irreducible`,
811	`IsFlasque.pushforward`). The iter-191 prover dispatch takes a more direct
812	route: unfold `skyscraperSheaf.val = skyscraperPresheaf`, then on the
813	`p ∈ V` branch the restriction map is an `eqToHom` (hence iso, hence
814	surjective in `ConcreteCategory`); on the `p ∉ V` branch the codomain is
815	the terminal object of `ModuleCat kbar`, which is the zero object, hence
816	`Subsingleton` on the underlying type. Either branch closes the
817	`Function.Surjective` obligation. This bypasses (3) and (5).
818	
819	**iter-191 Lane H prover dispatch** — closed directly via
820	`skyscraperPresheaf_map`. Blueprint reference:
821	`lem:skyscraperSheaf_isFlasque`. -/
822	theorem Scheme.skyscraperSheaf_isFlasque
823	    {kbar : Type u} [Field kbar] [IsAlgClosed kbar]
824	    (C : Over (Spec (.of kbar))) [IsProper C.hom]
825	    [SmoothOfRelativeDimension 1 C.hom]
826	    [GeometricallyIrreducible C.hom] [IsIntegral C.left]
827	    (P : C.left.PrimeDivisor)
828	    [∀ U : TopologicalSpace.Opens C.left, Decidable (P.point ∈ U)] :
829	    Scheme.IsFlasque
830	      (skyscraperSheaf (C := ModuleCat.{u} kbar) P.point
831	        (ModuleCat.of kbar kbar)) := by
832	  intro U V h
833	  change Function.Surjective
834	    (((skyscraperPresheaf P.point (ModuleCat.of kbar kbar)).map
835	      (homOfLE h).op).hom)
836	  by_cases hV : P.point ∈ V
837	  · -- restriction is `eqToHom`, hence an iso, hence surjective
838	    have heq : (skyscraperPresheaf P.point (ModuleCat.of kbar kbar)).obj
839	        (Opposite.op U) =
840	        (skyscraperPresheaf P.point (ModuleCat.of kbar kbar)).obj
841	        (Opposite.op V) := by
842	      simp [skyscraperPresheaf, h hV, hV]
843	    have hmap := skyscraperPresheaf_map P.point (ModuleCat.of kbar kbar)
844	      (i := (homOfLE h).op)
845	    rw [dif_pos hV] at hmap
846	    rw [hmap]
847	    have : IsIso (eqToHom heq) := inferInstance
848	    exact (ConcreteCategory.bijective_of_isIso (eqToHom heq)).2
849	  · -- codomain is zero (terminal in ModuleCat), so surjective trivially
850	    have hzero : Limits.IsZero
851	        ((skyscraperPresheaf P.point (ModuleCat.of kbar kbar)).obj
852	          (Opposite.op V)) := by
853	      simp [skyscraperPresheaf, hV]
854	      exact (terminalIsTerminal).isZero
855	    have : Subsingleton ((skyscraperPresheaf P.point
856	        (ModuleCat.of kbar kbar)).obj (Opposite.op V)) :=
857	      ModuleCat.subsingleton_of_isZero hzero
858	    intro y; exact ⟨0, Subsingleton.elim _ _⟩
859	
860	/-! ## §3. The closed-point skyscraper sheaf has vanishing `H¹` -/
861	
862	/-- **`H¹` of the closed-point skyscraper sheaf vanishes** (Hartshorne
863	III.2.5 applied to the flasque skyscraper).
864	
865	For a smooth proper geometrically irreducible curve `C / kbar` and a
866	prime divisor `P : C.left.PrimeDivisor` (a closed point on the curve),
867	`dim_{kbar} H¹(C, skyscraperSheaf P (ModuleCat.of kbar kbar)) = 0`.
868	
869	The proof composes the two substrate inputs of this chapter:
870	`skyscraperSheaf_isFlasque` (the skyscraper sheaf is flasque) and
871	`HModule_flasque_eq_zero` at `i = 1` (flasque ⇒ `H¹ = 0`); then
872	`Module.finrank kbar 0 = 0` closes the dimension identity.
873	
874	**iter-191 Lane H prover dispatch** — closed via composition of
875	`HModule_flasque_eq_zero` (still typed-`sorry` pending iter-192+
876	Hartshorne III.2.5 closure) and `skyscraperSheaf_isFlasque` (now closed
877	directly in this file) at `i = 1`. The same name also occurs as a
878	`private` typed-`sorry` helper at
879	`AlgebraicJacobian/RiemannRoch/RRFormula.lean`; the `private` modifier

61	sheaf at `ModuleCat.of k k`. Mathlib provides

63	`Linear R`-enriched abelian category; specialised to the `Linear k`

70	noncomputable def HModule_zero_linearEquiv
71	    (k : Type u) [Field k]
72	    {C : Type v} [Category.{u, v} C] {J : GrothendieckTopology C}
73	    [HasSheafify J (ModuleCat.{u} k)] [HasExt (Sheaf J (ModuleCat.{u} k))]
74	    (F : Sheaf J (ModuleCat.{u} k)) :
75	    HModule k F 0 ≃ₗ[k]
76	      ((constantSheaf J (ModuleCat.{u} k)).obj (ModuleCat.of k k) ⟶ F) :=
77	  Abelian.Ext.linearEquiv₀
78	
79	/-- Phase A step 6 *Path 2* (iter-013 scaffold): the `ModuleCat k`-flavored
80	cohomology of an open `X : C` with values in a sheaf `F : Sheaf J (ModuleCat.{u} k)`.
81	Mirrors Mathlib's `Sheaf.H' F n X = (F.cohomologyPresheaf n).obj (op X)`
82	(`Mathlib/CategoryTheory/Sites/SheafCohomology/Basic.lean` L105) for
83	`AddCommGrpCat`-valued sheaves, with `AddCommGrpCat.free → ModuleCat.free k`.
84	
85	The codomain is `Type u` (not `ModuleCat.{u} k`): `Abelian.Ext` returns a bare
86	`Type u` carrying `Module k` via `Abelian.Ext.instModule` through the `Linear k`
87	enrichment. The `noncomputable abbrev` form (rather than `def`) is required so
88	instance synthesis sees through the wrapper to find `Module k (HModule' k F n X)`
89	and `AddCommGroup (HModule' k F n X)` — under `def`, `Module.finrank` would fail
90	to typecheck (cf. iter-009 design rationale on `HModule`).
91	
92	This is the prerequisite for the iter-014+ `ModuleCat k`-flavored Mayer-Vietoris
93	LES (mirror of `Mathlib/CategoryTheory/Sites/SheafCohomology/MayerVietoris.lean`).
94	The iter-014+ work will state and prove the LES on a `MayerVietorisSquare`,
95	specialising to a 2-affine cover of a proper `k`-curve in iter-015+. The
96	comparison theorem `cechCohomology_OC C 𝒰 n ≅ HModule k (toModuleKSheaf C) n`
97	for an acyclic cover (the technical heart of Path-2) is also queued for
98	iter-015+. -/
99	noncomputable abbrev HModule' (k : Type u) [Field k]
100	    {C : Type v} [Category.{u, v} C] {J : GrothendieckTopology C}
101	    [HasWeakSheafify J (Type u)] [HasSheafify J (ModuleCat.{u} k)]
102	    [HasExt (Sheaf J (ModuleCat.{u} k))]
103	    (F : Sheaf J (ModuleCat.{u} k)) (n : ℕ) (X : C) : Type u :=
104	  Abelian.Ext ((presheafToSheaf _ _).obj
105	    ((yoneda ⋙ (Functor.whiskeringRight _ _ _).obj (ModuleCat.free k)).obj X)) F n
106	
107	/-- Phase A step 6 *Path 2* (iter-015): the `H⁰` algebraic bridge for `HModule'`,
108	mirroring iter-010's `HModule_zero_linearEquiv` for the iter-014 `HModule'`. The
109	$k$-linear identification of `HModule' k F 0 X` with the Hom group from the
110	sheafified representable `(presheafToSheaf _ _).obj ((yoneda ⋙ ModuleCat.free k).obj X)`.
111	Mathlib provides `CategoryTheory.Abelian.Ext.linearEquiv₀ : Ext X Y 0 ≃ₗ[R] (X ⟶ Y)`
112	in any `Linear R`-enriched abelian category; specialised to `R = k` and to the
113	`Linear k`-enriched abelian category `Sheaf J (ModuleCat.{u} k)` (the `Linear k`
114	enrichment is auto-inferable from `HasSheafify J (ModuleCat.{u} k)` via Mathlib's
115	`Sheaf.linear`), this collapses `HModule' k F 0 X` to a `k`-linear Hom group. The
116	closure body is `Abelian.Ext.linearEquiv₀`; probe-confirmed one-liner (iter-015
117	plan-agent). Used downstream as the algebraic prerequisite for Stein-factorization-
118	derived `H⁰(C, O_C) ≃ k` on a connected proper `k`-curve (multi-iteration; queued
119	iter-017+ alongside the Mayer-Vietoris LES of iter-016+). -/
120	noncomputable def HModule'_zero_linearEquiv
121	    (k : Type u) [Field k]
122	    {C : Type v} [Category.{u, v} C] {J : GrothendieckTopology C}
123	    [HasWeakSheafify J (Type u)] [HasSheafify J (ModuleCat.{u} k)]
124	    [HasExt (Sheaf J (ModuleCat.{u} k))]
125	    (F : Sheaf J (ModuleCat.{u} k)) (X : C) :
126	    HModule' k F 0 X ≃ₗ[k]
127	      ((presheafToSheaf _ _).obj
128	        ((yoneda ⋙ (Functor.whiskeringRight _ _ _).obj (ModuleCat.free k)).obj X) ⟶ F) :=
129	  Abelian.Ext.linearEquiv₀
130	
131	/-- Iter-038 `Module.Finite` H⁰ transport companion for `HModule k F 0`. The iter-010
132	H⁰ bridge `HModule_zero_linearEquiv : HModule k F 0 ≃ₗ[k] ((constantSheaf J _).obj
133	(ModuleCat.of k k) ⟶ F)` identifies the degree-zero cohomology with a `k`-linear Hom
134	group; Mathlib's `Module.Finite.equiv` then transports `Module.Finite k`-ness across.
135	The `.symm` is required: iter-010's bridge has `HModule k F 0` on the LHS and the
136	Hom group on the RHS, but we want the Hom hypothesis on the LHS and the
137	`HModule` conclusion on the RHS, so we apply `.symm` first. Mirrors iter-037's
138	`module_finite_HModule_of_HModule'_X₄` pattern at degree $0$, with no Mayer-Vietoris
139	machinery required. Used downstream as a building block for `Module.Finite k
140	(HModule k (toModuleKSheaf C) 0)` once the Hom-from-constant-sheaf finiteness
141	input is supplied for proper geometrically integral $k$-curves. -/
142	theorem module_finite_HModule_zero
143	    (k : Type u) [Field k]
144	    {C : Type v} [Category.{u, v} C] {J : GrothendieckTopology C}
145	    [HasSheafify J (ModuleCat.{u} k)] [HasExt (Sheaf J (ModuleCat.{u} k))]

154	((presheafToSheaf _ _).obj ((yoneda ⋙ ModuleCat.free k).obj X) ⟶ F)` identifies the
155	degree-zero open-evaluation cohomology with a `k`-linear Hom group from the sheafified

157	across. The `.symm` is required for the same orientation reason as in
158	`Scheme.module_finite_HModule_zero`. -/
159	theorem module_finite_HModule'_zero
160	    (k : Type u) [Field k]
161	    {C : Type v} [Category.{u, v} C] {J : GrothendieckTopology C}
162	    [HasWeakSheafify J (Type u)] [HasSheafify J (ModuleCat.{u} k)]
163	    [HasExt (Sheaf J (ModuleCat.{u} k))]
164	    (F : Sheaf J (ModuleCat.{u} k)) (X : C)
165	    [Module.Finite k ((presheafToSheaf _ _).obj
166	        ((yoneda ⋙ (Functor.whiskeringRight _ _ _).obj (ModuleCat.free k)).obj X) ⟶ F)] :
167	    Module.Finite k (HModule' k F 0 X) :=

169

182	    (k : Type u) [Field k]

  -- whose underlying rational function is `1 ∈ K(C)`.

  -- sheafToPresheaf.map, .app, .hom on a fixed element, Subtype.val — is

    simp [Scheme.lineBundleAtClosedPoint.toFunctionField]

        (lineBundleAtClosedPoint (C := C) P hP hPcoh) 0),

        c • Scheme.lineBundleAtClosedPoint.toFunctionField

    intro c s
    simp only [Scheme.lineBundleAtClosedPoint.toFunctionField, map_smul]
    rfl
  -- Step 2: `s₁ ≠ 0` from `hs₁ : toFunctionField s₁ = 1`.
  have hs₁_ne : s₁ ≠ 0 := by
    intro h0
    rw [h0, htF_zero] at hs₁
    exact one_ne_zero hs₁.symm
  -- Step 3: `H⁰` is finite-dimensional over `kbar` (from `_hdim = 2`).
  haveI : Module.Finite kbar (Scheme.HModule kbar
      (lineBundleAtClosedPoint (C := C) P hP hPcoh) 0) :=
    Module.finite_of_finrank_pos (by rw [_hdim]; norm_num)
  -- Step 4: the constant subspace `kbar · s₁ ⊆ H⁰` has finrank `1`
  -- (since `s₁ ≠ 0`).
  have hN : Module.finrank kbar (Submodule.span kbar
      ({s₁} : Set (Scheme.HModule kbar
        (lineBundleAtClosedPoint (C := C) P hP hPcoh) 0))) = 1 :=
    finrank_span_singleton hs₁_ne
  -- Step 5: by `Submodule.exists_of_finrank_lt`, since

  have hs_not_const : s ∉ Submodule.span kbar

# AlgebraicJacobian/RiemannRoch/OCofP.lean

Iter-195 Lane A (#6) — unconditional fallback objectives on substrate
helpers L1209 + L1323; cascade objective L1147 if Lane H lands.

3 sorries entering the iter; 3 sorries on exit. Substantive structural
advance on the L1323 substrate helper (`exists_nonconstant_rational_from_dim_eq_two`).

## L1323 `exists_nonconstant_rational_from_dim_eq_two`

### Attempt 1 — substantive structural advance (PARTIAL, ~50 LOC)

- **Approach:** Decompose the existing `s₁`-only partial setup into
  the full linear-algebra extraction chain spelled out in the
  helper's docstring.

- **Substantive new content (axiom-clean, no new sorries introduced):**

  1. `htF_zero : toFunctionField (C := C) P hP hPcoh 0 = 0` —
     proved via `simp only [toFunctionField, map_zero, Functor.map_zero]; rfl`.
     The `Functor.map_zero` simp lemma fires through `sheafToPresheaf.map`,
     and the `Subtype.val` projection of the zero element of the
     `carrierSubmoduleSheaf` is `0 : K(C)`.

  2. `htF_smul : ∀ c s, toFunctionField (c • s) = c • toFunctionField s` —
     kbar-linearity in the scalar argument, one-liner via
     `simp only [toFunctionField, map_smul]; rfl`. (kbar-additivity also
     provable analogously via `map_add`, but not consumed below so not
     committed.)

  3. `hs₁_ne : s₁ ≠ 0` — derived from `hs₁ : toFunctionField s₁ = 1`
     via the (now proved) `htF_zero` and `one_ne_zero`. Renamed
     `_s₁`/`_hs₁` (unused underscore-prefixed) → `s₁`/`hs₁` since they
     are now consumed below.

  4. `Module.Finite kbar H⁰` instance derived from `_hdim = 2 > 0`
     via `Module.finite_of_finrank_pos`.

  5. `hN : Module.finrank kbar (Submodule.span kbar {s₁}) = 1` via
     `finrank_span_singleton hs₁_ne` (Mathlib).

  6. Extracted `s : H⁰` with `∀ r ≠ 0, r • s ∉ Submodule.span kbar {s₁}`
     via `Submodule.exists_of_finrank_lt` (Mathlib
     `Mathlib/LinearAlgebra/Dimension/RankNullity.lean`) at the
     dimension count `1 < 2`. Specialised to `r = 1` to extract
     `hs_not_const : s ∉ Submodule.span kbar {s₁}`.

  7. Defined `f := toFunctionField (C := C) P hP hPcoh s` as the
     candidate non-constant rational function.

- **Result:** PARTIAL — 6 axiom-clean sub-steps committed; 1 typed
  sorry remains at the closure point. The body now spells out the
  full structural recipe: only the substantive non-zeroness +
  principal-divisor non-vanishing remain.

- **Remaining gap (3 mathematical sub-claims):**

  (a) **`f ≠ 0`**: requires `Function.Injective toFunctionField`.
      The chain `H⁰ →[HModule_zero_linearEquiv] (constSheaf ⟶ F)
      →[sheafToPresheaf.map] (constPresheaf ⟶ F.val)
      →[evaluation at op ⊤ on one_image]→[Subtype.val] K(C)` is
      bijective on the first three steps (LinearEquiv chain via
      `constantSheafAdj.homEquiv` + `M = ModuleCat.of kbar kbar` is
      free of rank 1) and injective on the last (`Subtype.val`).
      Formalisation requires packaging the chain as a single LinearEquiv;
      ~30-50 LOC unfolding.

  (b) **Order conditions**: once `(a)` provides `hf : f ≠ 0`, apply
      `globalSections_iff_mpr P hP f hf hPcoh ⟨s, hf_def.symm⟩` to
      read off the order pair. Mechanical.

  (c) **`Scheme.WeilDivisor.principal f hf ≠ 0`**: requires Hartshorne
      II.6.7 (Stacks 02P0) — `principal f = 0 ⟹ f ∈ Γ(C, 𝒪_C^×) = kbar^×`
      on a complete geom-irred curve over alg-closed `kbar`. The
      contrapositive route: assuming `principal f = 0`, the rational
      function `f` is a global unit and (by Stacks 02P0) a constant; but
      `s ∉ kbar · s₁` together with kbar-linearity of `toFunctionField`
      (committed) + injectivity (a) shows `f ∉ image of constants`,
      contradiction.

- **Dead-end warnings:**

  - **Direct simp on `toFunctionField` definition** — does *not*
    immediately discharge the chain to a clean LinearMap. The `let`-bound
    `g := sheafToPresheaf.map ...` and `one_image := unit.hom 1`
    intermediates make `simp [toFunctionField]` produce a residual
    `(0 : LinearMap) (...) = 0` shape that needs `rfl` finalization.
    `Functor.map_zero` is the load-bearing simp lemma; without it the
    `sheafToPresheaf.map 0` does not reduce.

  - **Injectivity via `Subtype.ext_iff` alone** — does NOT close. The
    issue is that `((g.app ⊤).hom one_image).1 = 0` reduces to
    `(g.app ⊤).hom one_image = 0` in the submodule, but the further step
    `g.app ⊤ = 0` requires that `one_image` generate the source kbar-module,
    which is a constantSheafAdj structural fact, not a pure simp/lemma.

## L1209 `h0_sub_h1_lineBundleAtClosedPoint_eq_two` — NOT TOUCHED

Per directive: substrate helper for χ-arithmetic at `D = [P]`.
- **Mathematical content**: needs the bridge `lineBundleAtClosedPoint P hP hPcoh
  ≅ Scheme.WeilDivisor.sheafOf (Finsupp.single ⟨P, hPcoh⟩ 1)` followed by
  the χ-identity of `RR.2`
  (`Scheme.eulerCharacteristic_eq_degree_plus_one_minus_genus`).
- **Substrate gates**: `Scheme.WeilDivisor.sheafOf` body is in
  `OcOfD.lean` — **STRUCTURALLY BLOCKED** (iter-187 finding, standing
  deferral, DO NOT DISPATCH). `Scheme.eulerCharacteristic_*_sheafOf_*`
  bridges are in `RRFormula.lean` with 1 residual sorry on
  `eulerCharacteristic_shortExact_add`.
- **Verdict**: Closure requires upstream substrate currently parked.
  This helper is the natural single point at which both upstream gates
  flow into the consumer `dim_eq_two_of_genusZero`; it remains an honest
  typed sorry until those gates lift.

## L1147 `h1_vanishing_genusZero` — NOT TOUCHED (cascade target)

Per directive: cascade target if Lane H lands `SAb.Exact` axiom-clean.
- **Mathematical content**: `H¹(C, 𝒪_C(P)) = 0` via the closed-point SES
  `0 → 𝒪_C → 𝒪_C(P) → k(P) → 0` and the LES of cohomology, killing
  `H¹(C, 𝒪_C(P))` via `H¹(C, 𝒪_C) = 0` (genus zero) and `H¹(C, k(P)) = 0`
  (skyscraper / flasque).
- **Substrate gates**: Requires the entire Lane H cohomology
  pipeline — `HModule_flasque_eq_zero` (closed transitively at iter-194,
  but its body is gated on the `injective_flasque` substrate); plus the
  SES + LES machinery + `eulerCharacteristic_shortExact_add`.
- **Verdict**: cascade-shaped; closure requires Lane H exit and the
  closed-point SES infrastructure. Untouched this iter.

## Why I stopped

`Partial progress`: substantive structural advance on L1323
(`exists_nonconstant_rational_from_dim_eq_two`). 6 axiom-clean
sub-steps committed (~50 LOC), spelling out the linear-algebra
extraction chain from `_hdim = 2` to the existence of a candidate
non-constant section `s` and the resulting candidate function `f`.
Remaining gap is documented as 3 well-isolated mathematical sub-claims:
(a) injectivity of `toFunctionField` via the LinearEquiv chain
(formalisation, ~30-50 LOC), (b) mechanical application of
`globalSections_iff_mpr`, and (c) Stacks 02P0 (principal-divisor
non-vanishing from non-constancy on a complete geom-irred curve over
alg-closed kbar).

HARD BAR MET: ≥1 substantive structural advance on the 2 substrate
helpers (specifically L1323). PUSH-BEYOND not achieved: did not close
2 of 3 sorries. The remaining content on L1323 is gated on a non-trivial
injectivity formalisation (~30-50 LOC of LinearEquiv chain unfolding)
which would consume the rest of the helper budget without confidently
landing axiom-clean.

L1147 is left as cascade-only (Lane H landing first); L1209 is left
as upstream-gated (`OcOfD.lean` STRUCTURALLY BLOCKED + RRFormula
χ-additivity).

File still compiles GREEN with 3 sorries (no new ones introduced; no
sorry counts changed). All committed steps are kernel-clean
(zero axioms introduced).

     `simp only [toFunctionField, map_smul]; rfl`. (kbar-additivity also
     provable analogously via `map_add`, but not consumed below so not
     committed.)
input.new_string:
2. `htF_smul : ∀ c s, toFunctionField (c • s) = c • toFunctionField s` —
     kbar-linearity in the scalar argument, one-liner via
     `simp only [toFunctionField, map_smul]; rfl`.

  2b. `htF_add : ∀ a b, toFunctionField (a + b) = toFunctionField a +
     toFunctionField b` — kbar-additivity, one-liner via
     `simp only [toFunctionField, map_add, Functor.map_add]; rfl`.
     Together with `htF_smul`, this establishes that `toFunctionField`
     is a kbar-linear map `H⁰ → K(C)` (committed as separate `have`s
     rather than bundled as a `LinearMap`; bundling is straightforward
     follow-up if needed).

prompt:
You are a prover agent for project 'Algebraic-Jacobian-Challenge'. Current stage: prover.
Archon iteration: 195.
Project directory: /home/archon/FormalizationProjects/Algebraic-Jacobian-Challenge
Project state directory: /home/archon/FormalizationProjects/Algebraic-Jacobian-Challenge/.archon
Read /home/archon/FormalizationProjects/Algebraic-Jacobian-Challenge/.archon/CLAUDE.md for your role, then read /home/archon/FormalizationProjects/Algebraic-Jacobian-Challenge/.archon/PROGRESS.md.
Check your .lean file for /- USER: ... -/ comments for file-specific hints.

IMPORTANT:
- You own ONLY the file assigned below. Do NOT edit any other .lean file.
- Write your results to /home/archon/FormalizationProjects/Algebraic-Jacobian-Challenge/.archon/task_results/<your_file>.md when done.
- Do NOT edit PROGRESS.md, task_pending.md, or task_done.md.
- Missing Mathlib infrastructure is NEVER a valid reason to leave a sorry.
- NEVER revert to a bare sorry. Always leave your partial proof attempt in the code.

Blueprint chapter for your file: blueprint/src/chapters/RiemannRoch_RationalCurveIso.tex
- Read it BEFORE writing any Lean code — it contains the informal proof
  written by the plan agent.
- After you formalize a declaration, mark its blueprint environment with \leanok.
- If the chapter file does not yet exist, create it with a minimal \chapter block
  and note in your task_results that the plan agent should flesh it out.
Active prover mode: **prove**

## Your goal

Fill `sorry` placeholders with complete Lean proofs in your assigned `.lean` file.

## Workflow

1. Read `PROGRESS.md` for your current objectives (read only — do not edit it).
2. Read `task_pending.md` for prior attempts, dead ends, and lemmas already found.
3. Check the `.lean` file for `/- USER: ... -/` comments (file-specific user hints).
4. **Read the relevant blueprint chapter before writing Lean code.** The chapter holds the mathematical proof sketch you must align with. When stuck, re-reading it is often the fastest path forward.
5. Replace `sorry` with Lean proofs. Push as far as possible.
6. **Always save partial progress.** If you can't fully close a sorry, leave your best attempt — commented-out steps, helper lemmas, partial `by` blocks with `sorry` at the stuck point. The file must still compile, but your work must be visible for the next agent to continue from. NEVER revert to a bare `sorry` — that erases real work.
7. Write your results to `task_results/<your_file>.md`.

**Write permissions**: only your assigned `.lean` file(s) and `task_results/<your_file>.md`. Do NOT edit `PROGRESS.md`, `task_pending.md`, `task_done.md`, blueprint chapters, or other agents' files.

## Protected declarations

Read `archon-protected.yaml` before touching any declaration. You may fill proof bodies of protected declarations but must not rename, re-type, reorder arguments, or weaken hypotheses. Only the mathematician edits protected signatures.

## Avoid early termination

- Don't abandon a proof prematurely. Many complex proofs run to thousands of lines.
- Difficulty is NOT a valid reason to leave a `sorry`.
- Don't delegate to "the next iteration" or "another prover" if more effort could close it.
- Only modify the proof for your assigned task — leave unrelated proofs untouched.
- **Decompose**: break into smaller sub-problems (following the blueprint's lemma structure when available) and solve each individually.
- **Hard bar is a minimum, not a ceiling.** If your objectives specify a "hard bar" (e.g. "add def + pin signature"), that tells you the minimum required — not where to stop. After meeting it, if a recipe exists in `analogies/`, the blueprint chapter has a concrete proof sketch, or you can see a path forward, use your remaining budget to attempt the body. Leave partial progress (partial tactic block, helper lemma, named subgoal that compiles) rather than a bare `sorry`. Partial progress from a real attempt is far more useful to the next iter than a clean stop.

## Completion criteria

Your task is complete ONLY when ALL of:

1. Every `sorry` in scope is replaced with a complete proof.
2. Zero axioms introduced.
3. The file compiles cleanly.

## Never weaken the type to dodge the proof

When the substantive type is unattainable this iter, leave `sorry` with the **intended type signature**. Three patterns are banned:

- **Reflexive-iso placeholder** — replacing `X ≅ Y` with `Nonempty (X ≅ X) := ⟨Iso.refl _⟩`.
- **`Classical.choice` around an explicit witness** — dissolving `Type` vs `Prop` friction without actually constructing the witness.
- **Empty-content `proof_wanted`** — discards the declaration post-elaboration, breaking blueprint cross-references.

**Litmus test**: if you `unfold` your declaration, does it expose the named substantive content or does it stop at `Classical.choice` / `Iso.refl _` / nothing? If the latter, ship the typed `sorry` instead.

## When infrastructure is missing

Do NOT report "Mathlib lacks X" and stop. Before giving up:

1. **Use the informal agent** (`.claude/tools/archon-informal-agent.py`): "Prove [goal] without using [missing infrastructure], only Mathlib." Even an imperfect sketch is valuable.
2. Formalize whatever the informal agent suggests.
3. **If you still can't**: write the alternative sketch to `informal/<theorem_name>.md` and record what you tried, why it failed, AND the alternative route you found.

When stuck more generally: break into smaller subgoals, search Mathlib more thoroughly, prove missing helpers yourself, try alternative strategies, re-read the blueprint, use Web Search for published proofs.

**Impossibility vs difficulty**: technical difficulty → keep trying. Mathematical impossibility → immediately backtrack and document why.

## Proof style

- **Never modify working proofs** — if a declaration has no `sorry` and compiles, do not touch its body.
- Keep edits minimal; don't delete comments or change labels; don't add unrelated declarations.
- Helper lemmas you introduced may be modified if they turn out wrong.
- Add a concise comment above each helper lemma so reuse is easy.
- **`change` vs `show`** — `change` reshapes the goal up to defeq; `show` is display-level only. Default to `change` when in doubt.

## Mathlib tags in PROGRESS.md

The plan agent tags suggested lemmas:

- `[verified]` — confirmed to exist.
- `[expected]` — guessed by naming convention. Quick `lean_local_search`; pivot if it doesn't exist.
- `[gap]` — verified NOT in Mathlib. Don't waste search time; formalize a workaround.

## LSP MCP tools

The `archon-lean-lsp` server exposes Lean LSP operations as **MCP tool calls** (`mcp__archon-lean-lsp__lean_goal`, `mcp__archon-lean-lsp__lean_diagnostic_messages`, etc.). The lean4 skill reference uses short names (`lean_goal`, …) — same tools, never shell binaries.

- Always invoke through your tool-call interface.
- **Never** call `Bash` with `lean_goal …` — there is no such shell command.
- First LSP action: `mcp__archon-lean-lsp__lean_diagnostic_messages` on your file. If `success: false`, retry once or run `lake build` once via Bash, then retry.

## Search protocol

1. `lean_local_search` first.
2. `lean_leansearch` for semantic search — describe the mathematical content, not just the name.
3. `lean_loogle` for simple type patterns only.
4. Never use shell `find` / `grep` to locate Mathlib theorems.

## Tooling traps

- **Trust `goals_after`, not just empty diagnostics**: in `lean_multi_attempt`, `diagnostics: []` can be misleading. Check that `goals_after` is empty or has advanced.
- **Beware `lean_run_code` with imports**: standalone snippets can silently swallow elaboration errors. Rely on `lean_diagnostic_messages` for authoritative verification.

## Logging

Write to `task_results/<your_file>.md` (mirror your `.lean` path: `Algebra/WLocal.lean` → `task_results/Algebra_WLocal.lean.md`):

```markdown
# Algebra/WLocal.lean

## wLocal_iff (line 45)
### Attempt 1
- **Approach:** Direct case split on maximal ideals
- **Result:** FAILED — needed IsLocalRing instance
- **Dead end:** direct case split without IsLocalRing

### Attempt 2
- **Approach:** Stacks 0A31, characterize via bijection on spectra
- **Result:** RESOLVED
- **Key insight:** `PrimeSpectrum.comap_injective` bridges the gap
```

One section per theorem/lemma. Each attempt: approach, result (RESOLVED / FAILED / PARTIAL / IN PROGRESS), dead-end warnings or next steps. Log negative search results.

## End-of-session handoff

Before stopping:

1. Write `task_results/<your_file>.md` with current result, lemmas discovered, concrete next step, dead-end warnings.
2. Save all changes; ensure the file compiles.
3. **Write a `## Why I stopped` section** in your task result with one of the following verdicts — be honest, the planner reads this:
   - `Real progress`: closed N sorries, specific ones named.
   - `Partial progress`: made measurable progress (e.g. decomposed into sub-lemmas, closed one branch) but did not fully close.
   - `Cosmetics only`: changed formatting/comments/style with no proof progress — say so explicitly.
   - `Avoided the goal`: attempted something adjacent but not the assigned target — explain why and what you tried instead.
   - `Infrastructure missing`: a specific Mathlib gap was the blocker — name it, describe the alternative attempted.
   - `Directive not followed`: explain which part of the planner's directive you deviated from and why.


## Developer feedback channel (optional)

If during this iteration you notice something that would make Archon
better — a missing capability, redundant functionality, a
prompt instruction that contradicts itself, a tool you wish existed,
new ideas for better efficiency, etc — you may leave a short note
for the developer by appending to this file with a bash heredoc:

    mkdir -p /home/archon/FormalizationProjects/Algebraic-Jacobian-Challenge/.archon/.debug-feedback
    cat >> /home/archon/FormalizationProjects/Algebraic-Jacobian-Challenge/.archon/.debug-feedback/debug_feedback.md <<'EOF'

    ## iter-195 · parallel prover

    <your note here, one concrete observation, under ~200 words>
    EOF

Rules:
- This file is WRITE-ONLY from your perspective. Do NOT read it,
  cat it, grep it, or open it in any tool. It is for the developer.
- Only leave a note if you have something concrete to say. Empty or
  generic feedback ("everything went fine") is noise — skip it.
- One concrete observation per note. Keep it under ~200 words.
- This is optional and does not affect your task. Skip it if nothing
  comes to mind.
