100	/-- The **Weil divisor group** of a scheme `X` satisfying Hartshorne's condition
101	`(*)`: the free abelian group on the set of prime divisors of `X`,
102	`Div(X) = ⨁_{Y prime divisor of X} ℤ · Y`. An element `D = Σ nᵢ · Yᵢ` with finitely
103	many nonzero coefficients is a **Weil divisor**; if all `nᵢ ≥ 0` it is **effective**.
104	
105	Blueprint reference: `def:codim1_cycles` (Hartshorne II §6 p. 130). -/
106	def Scheme.WeilDivisor (X : Scheme.{u}) : Type u := X.PrimeDivisor →₀ ℤ
107	
108	namespace Scheme.WeilDivisor
109	
110	noncomputable instance (X : Scheme.{u}) : AddCommGroup X.WeilDivisor :=
111	  inferInstanceAs (AddCommGroup (X.PrimeDivisor →₀ ℤ))
112	
113	instance (X : Scheme.{u}) : Inhabited X.WeilDivisor :=
114	  inferInstanceAs (Inhabited (X.PrimeDivisor →₀ ℤ))
115	
116	end Scheme.WeilDivisor
117	
118	/-! ## §2. Order of a rational function at a prime divisor
119	
120	For a scheme `X` satisfying `(*)`, every prime divisor `Y` carries a discrete
121	valuation `v_Y` on the function field `K(X)`. The order of a nonzero rational
122	function `f ∈ K(X)^×` along `Y` is the integer `ord_Y(f) := v_Y(f)`. -/
123	
124	namespace Scheme.RationalMap
125	
126	/-- **Order of a nonzero rational function `f ∈ K(X)^×` along a prime divisor `Y`.**
127	
128	For `X` satisfying Hartshorne's `(*)`, the local ring `O_{X,η}` at the generic
129	point `η` of `Y` is a discrete valuation ring with quotient field the function
130	field `K(X)`, and `ord_Y(f) = v_Y(f) ∈ ℤ` is the value of the associated normalised
131	discrete valuation on `f`.
132	
133	On a smooth proper curve `C` over `k̄`, every closed point `P ∈ C` is a prime
134	divisor and `ord_P(f) = v_P(f)` is the standard DVR valuation at `P`.
135	
136	Blueprint reference: `def:order_at_point` (Hartshorne II §6 pp. 130–131; Stacks 02ME).
137	
138	iter-176 body (per analogist `dvr-rationalmap-order`): the body uses Mathlib's
139	`Ring.ordFrac` (the canonical `K →*₀ ℤᵐ⁰` monoid-with-zero hom from
140	`Mathlib.RingTheory.OrderOfVanishing.Basic`, Stacks `02MD`) on the stalk
141	`X.presheaf.stalk Y.point`, then projects through `WithZero.log : ℤᵐ⁰ → ℤ`
142	(the canonical projection with junk-on-zero, `Mathlib.Algebra.GroupWithZero.WithZero`).
143	On `f = 0` this gives `order Y 0 = 0` (junk convention from `WithZero.log_zero`).
144	
145	The required Mathlib typeclasses on the stalk are:
146	- `IsNoetherianRing` — from `[IsLocallyNoetherian X]`.
147	- `IsDomain` ⟹ `Nontrivial` — from `[IsIntegral X]`.
148	- `IsFractionRing (X.presheaf.stalk Y.point) X.functionField` — from `[IsIntegral X]`.
149	- `Ring.KrullDimLE 1 (X.presheaf.stalk Y.point)` — threaded explicitly (the
150	  topological-coheight-to-Krull-dim bridge `Order.coheight Y.point = 1 ⟹
151	  Ring.KrullDimLE 1 (X.presheaf.stalk Y.point)` is a Mathlib-upstream-pending
152	  gap; see Stacks `02IZ` / `005X`). -/
153	noncomputable def order {X : Scheme.{u}} [IsIntegral X]
154	    [IsLocallyNoetherian X] (Y : X.PrimeDivisor)
155	    [Ring.KrullDimLE 1 (X.presheaf.stalk Y.point)]
156	    (f : X.functionField) : ℤ :=
157	  WithZero.log (Ring.ordFrac (X.presheaf.stalk Y.point) f)
158	
159	end Scheme.RationalMap
160	
161	/-! ### Regular-in-codimension-one bridge class
162	
163	Hartshorne's condition `(*)` includes "regular in codimension one", i.e.\ every
164	prime divisor `Y` of `X` has a DVR stalk `O_{X,Y.point}`. Mathlib has no direct
165	typeclass for this; we package it as the project-bespoke class
166	`Scheme.IsRegularInCodimensionOne`, with an instance synthesising the per-`Y`
167	Krull-dim-≤-1 condition required by `Scheme.RationalMap.order`. Blueprint pin
168	("Iter-173+ may introduce a `Scheme.IsRegularInCodimensionOne` predicate to
169	abbreviate this"; chapter `RiemannRoch_WeilDivisor.tex` §2 "Standing hypothesis
170	`(*)` in the Lean encoding"). -/
171	
172	/-- Project-bespoke class encoding Hartshorne's "regular in codimension one"
173	clause of `(*)`: every prime divisor `Y` of `X` has a DVR stalk
174	`O_{X,Y.point}`. The `[IsIntegral X]` precondition makes
175	`IsDomain (X.presheaf.stalk Y.point)` available so that the
176	`IsDiscreteValuationRing` predicate is well-formed
177	(`AlgebraicGeometry.instIsDomainCarrierStalkCommRingCatPresheafOfIsIntegral`). -/
178	class Scheme.IsRegularInCodimensionOne (X : Scheme.{u}) [IsIntegral X] : Prop where
179	  /-- The defining content: every prime divisor's stalk is a discrete valuation
180	  ring. (This is the precise content of Hartshorne's `(*)`; the weaker
181	  `Ring.KrullDimLE 1` derives via the `IsDiscreteValuationRing` ⟹
182	  `IsPrincipalIdealRing` ⟹ `Ring.KrullDimLE 1` chain via the bridge
183	  instance below.) -/
184	  out : ∀ Y : Scheme.PrimeDivisor X, IsDiscreteValuationRing (X.presheaf.stalk Y.point)
185	
186	/-- Bridge instance: from `[Scheme.IsRegularInCodimensionOne X]`, typeclass
187	synthesis can derive `IsDiscreteValuationRing (X.presheaf.stalk Y.point)` for
188	every prime divisor `Y`. -/
189	instance Scheme.IsRegularInCodimensionOne.instIsDiscreteValuationRingStalk
190	    {X : Scheme.{u}} [IsIntegral X] [Scheme.IsRegularInCodimensionOne X]
191	    (Y : Scheme.PrimeDivisor X) :
192	    IsDiscreteValuationRing (X.presheaf.stalk Y.point) :=
193	  Scheme.IsRegularInCodimensionOne.out Y
194	
195	/-- Bridge instance: from `[Scheme.IsRegularInCodimensionOne X]`, typeclass
196	synthesis can derive `Ring.KrullDimLE 1 (X.presheaf.stalk Y.point)` for every
197	prime divisor `Y`, via the `IsDiscreteValuationRing` ⟹ `IsPrincipalIdealRing`
198	⟹ `Ring.KrullDimLE 1` chain (Mathlib's `IsDiscreteValuationRing.toIsPrincipalIdealRing`
199	+ `IsPrincipalIdealRing.krullDimLE_one`). -/
200	instance Scheme.IsRegularInCodimensionOne.instKrullDimLEStalk
201	    {X : Scheme.{u}} [IsIntegral X] [Scheme.IsRegularInCodimensionOne X]
202	    (Y : Scheme.PrimeDivisor X) :
203	    Ring.KrullDimLE 1 (X.presheaf.stalk Y.point) :=
204	  haveI : IsDiscreteValuationRing (X.presheaf.stalk Y.point) :=
205	    Scheme.IsRegularInCodimensionOne.out Y
206	  haveI : IsPrincipalIdealRing (X.presheaf.stalk Y.point) :=
207	    IsDiscreteValuationRing.toIsPrincipalIdealRing
208	  IsPrincipalIdealRing.krullDimLE_one _
209	
210	/-- **Hartshorne II.6.1**: for a nonzero rational function `f` on a Noetherian
211	integral scheme `X` satisfying `(*)`, the order function `Y ↦ ord_Y(f)` is
212	nonzero at only finitely many prime divisors `Y`. This is the well-definedness
213	side condition for `Scheme.WeilDivisor.principal`.
214	
215	iter-177 status: this packages Hartshorne's Lemma 6.1, which Mathlib does not
216	ship. The body is a Mathlib-upstream-pending gap (Stacks tag `02RV` — for a
217	nonzero element `f ∈ K(X)^×` of a Noetherian integral scheme, only finitely
218	many height-one primes can divide either numerator or denominator); the proof
219	factors through `IsLocallyNoetherian X` + the principal-ideal generation of
220	height-1 primes + the finite irreducible-component decomposition of
221	`V(f₀) ∪ V(f∞)`. The chapter pins this as a separate sub-build deferral
222	(`RiemannRoch_WeilDivisor.tex` §5).
223	
224	The statement is generic in `f` (no `f ≠ 0` hypothesis is threaded): on
225	`f = 0` the function `Y ↦ ord_Y(0) = WithZero.log 0 = 0` has empty support,
226	which is finite, so the conclusion holds vacuously. -/
227	private theorem rationalMap_order_finite_support {X : Scheme.{u}} [IsIntegral X]
228	    [IsLocallyNoetherian X] [Scheme.IsRegularInCodimensionOne X]
229	    (f : X.functionField) :
230	    (Function.support (fun Y : X.PrimeDivisor =>
231	      Scheme.RationalMap.order Y f)).Finite := by
232	  -- **iter-192 case split + f = 0 branch closed axiom-clean.**
233	  --
234	  -- Case 1 (f = 0): the order function evaluates to
235	  -- `WithZero.log (Ring.ordFrac _ 0) = WithZero.log 0 = 0` at every
236	  -- prime divisor, so the support is empty (finite vacuously).
237	  by_cases hf : f = 0
238	  · subst hf
239	    convert Set.finite_empty
240	    ext Y
241	    simp only [Function.mem_support, ne_eq, Set.mem_empty_iff_false, iff_false,
242	      Decidable.not_not, Scheme.RationalMap.order, map_zero, WithZero.log_zero]
243	  -- Case 2 (f ≠ 0): genuinely needs Hartshorne II.6.1 (Stacks 02RV).
244	  -- The proof for nonzero `f` requires the substrate
245	  -- "for a Noetherian integral scheme, only finitely many height-1
246	  -- primes can divide the numerator or denominator of a nonzero rational
247	  -- function" — a Mathlib-pending gap noted in the chapter docstring.
248	  -- Body owed iter-193+; iter-192 closes only the f = 0 vacuous case.
249	  · sorry
250	
251	namespace Scheme.WeilDivisor
252	
253	variable {X : Scheme.{u}}
254	
255	/-! ## §3. Divisor of a closed point on a curve
256	
257	On a smooth proper curve `C` over a field, every closed point `P ∈ C` is a prime
258	divisor (it is closed, integral, of codimension one in the one-dimensional integral
259	scheme `C`). The associated Weil divisor is `[P] = 1 · P ∈ Div(C)`. -/
260	
261	/-- **The Weil divisor associated to a closed point `P` on a curve.** The element
262	`[P] := 1 · P ∈ Div(C)`, i.e. the prime divisor `P` with multiplicity one.
263	
264	For a smooth proper curve every closed point is automatically a prime divisor (it
265	is a codimension-one integral closed subscheme of the one-dimensional integral
266	scheme `C`); conversely every prime divisor on a curve is of this form, so an
267	arbitrary divisor on `C` is a finite formal `ℤ`-linear combination
268	`Σ nᵢ · [Pᵢ]` of closed points.
269	
270	Blueprint reference: `def:divisor_closed_point` (Hartshorne II §6 p. 137).
271	
272	iter-174 body: the function is junk-defined outside its intended scope. We
273	case-split on `Order.coheight P = 1` (the codimension-one witness of
274	`PrimeDivisor`). On the branch where the equality holds — automatic for a
275	closed point on a one-dimensional integral scheme — we promote `P` to a
276	`PrimeDivisor` via the witness and return `Finsupp.single ⟨P, h⟩ 1`, i.e.
277	the prime divisor `P` with multiplicity one. On the off-branch (junk regime)
278	we return the zero divisor. The blueprint pins the well-definedness argument
279	"`IsClosed {P}` on a one-dimensional integral scheme ⟹ `coheight P = 1`" as a
280	separate threadable hypothesis at the call site (chapter L330–L340 "Lean
281	signature scope"); see `ofClosedPoint_eq_single` for the equation in the
282	hypothesised regime. -/
283	noncomputable def ofClosedPoint {C : Scheme.{u}} (P : C)
284	    (_hP : IsClosed ({P} : Set C)) : C.WeilDivisor :=
285	  if h : Order.coheight P = 1 then Finsupp.single ⟨P, h⟩ 1 else 0
286	
287	/-- In the hypothesised regime where the closed point `P` has coheight one
288	(the codim-1 condition automatic for a closed point of a one-dimensional
289	integral scheme), `ofClosedPoint P hP` is the prime divisor `P` with
290	multiplicity one. -/
291	lemma ofClosedPoint_eq_single {C : Scheme.{u}} (P : C)
292	    (hP : IsClosed ({P} : Set C)) (h : Order.coheight P = 1) :
293	    ofClosedPoint P hP = Finsupp.single ⟨P, h⟩ 1 := by
294	  simp [ofClosedPoint, h]
295	
296	/-- Off-branch: outside the codim-1 regime, `ofClosedPoint` is junk-defined as
297	the zero divisor. (Only relevant when the user supplies a "closed point" that
298	does not have coheight one in the ambient scheme — e.g. a generic point or a
299	codim-≥2 point on a higher-dimensional scheme.) -/
300	lemma ofClosedPoint_eq_zero {C : Scheme.{u}} (P : C)
301	    (hP : IsClosed ({P} : Set C)) (h : Order.coheight P ≠ 1) :
302	    ofClosedPoint P hP = 0 := by
303	  simp [ofClosedPoint, h]
304	
305	/-! ## §4. Degree of a divisor over an algebraically closed base -/
306	
307	/-- **Degree of a Weil divisor on a smooth proper curve over `k̄`.**
308	
309	Over an algebraically closed field `k̄`, every closed point of a smooth proper
310	curve `C` has residue field `k̄`, so each prime divisor `[P]` contributes degree
311	one to the sum, and `deg(D) := Σᵢ nᵢ` is the sum of the integer coefficients of
312	the formal sum `D = Σ nᵢ · [Pᵢ]`.
313	
314	(Over a general field `k` one weights by the residue-field degrees
315	`Σᵢ nᵢ · [κ(Pᵢ) : k]` to recover the geometric degree; the project's RR bridge
316	needs only the `k̄` specialisation.)
317	
318	Blueprint reference: `def:divisor_degree` (Hartshorne II §6 p. 137; Stacks 0BE0).
319	
320	Implementation: `Finsupp.sum D (fun _ n => n)` is the sum of all coefficients of
321	the finitely supported function representing `D`. -/
322	noncomputable def degree (D : X.WeilDivisor) : ℤ :=
323	  (D : X.PrimeDivisor →₀ ℤ).sum (fun _ n => n)
324	
325	/-- **The degree map is a group homomorphism `Div(C) → ℤ`.**
326	
327	`deg(D₁ + D₂) = deg(D₁) + deg(D₂)`, `deg(-D) = -deg(D)`, `deg(0) = 0`. Bundled
328	as an `AddMonoidHom` for downstream use (the linear-equivalence quotient
329	`Cl(C) := Div(C) / im(div)` will inherit a `deg : Cl(C) → ℤ` from this).
330	
331	Blueprint reference: `thm:divisor_degree_hom` (immediate from `def:divisor_degree`).
332	
333	Implementation: built from `Finsupp.liftAddHom (fun _ ↦ AddMonoidHom.id ℤ)`, the
334	generic Mathlib packaging that lifts a family of `AddMonoidHom`s indexed by the
335	support into a single `AddMonoidHom` on the finsupp. Unfolds to
336	`D.sum (fun _ z ↦ z) = degree D` (see `degree_hom_apply` below). -/
337	noncomputable def degree_hom : X.WeilDivisor →+ ℤ :=
338	  Finsupp.liftAddHom (fun _ ↦ AddMonoidHom.id ℤ)
339	
340	@[simp]
341	lemma degree_hom_apply (D : X.WeilDivisor) : degree_hom D = degree D :=
342	  Finsupp.liftAddHom_apply (α := X.PrimeDivisor) (M := ℤ) (N := ℤ)
343	    (fun _ ↦ AddMonoidHom.id ℤ) D
344	
345	/-- **Degree of the zero divisor is zero.** A direct consequence of
346	`degree_hom` being an `AddMonoidHom`. -/
347	@[simp]
348	lemma degree_zero : degree (0 : X.WeilDivisor) = 0 := by
349	  rw [← degree_hom_apply]; exact map_zero _

60	
61	namespace AlgebraicGeometry
62	
63	/-! ## §1. Codim-1 cycle group / Weil divisor group
64	
65	Hartshorne's condition `(*)` (II §6, p. 130) is: `X` is a Noetherian integral
66	separated scheme that is regular in codimension one. Under `(*)`, prime divisors
67	are closed integral subschemes of codimension one, and the local ring at the
68	generic point of a prime divisor is a DVR with quotient field the function field
69	`K(X)`.
70	
71	A prime divisor is encoded by its **generic point** together with the
72	codimension-one witness `Order.coheight point = 1`: the data field `point : X`
73	selects the generic point of the closed integral subscheme, and the predicate
74	field `coheight` enforces codimension one in the specialisation preorder
75	(Hartshorne II §6 p. 130; blueprint pin `def:prime_divisor`). Integrality of the
76	closure is automatic, so no separate integrality witness is needed.
77	-/
78	
79	/-- A **prime divisor** on a scheme `X`: a closed integral subscheme of codimension
80	one, encoded (for a scheme satisfying Hartshorne's condition `(*)`) by its generic
81	point. On a curve, prime divisors correspond bijectively to closed points.
82	
83	The codimension-one witness is the predicate `Order.coheight point = 1` on the
84	specialisation preorder of `X.carrier` (Mathlib's convention is
85	`x ≤ y ↔ y ⤳ x`, so the generic point is the unique maximal element and a
86	generic point of a codim-1 closed integral subscheme has a length-one chain
87	above it). The closure `{point}̄` is automatically irreducible (so the reduced
88	induced subscheme structure is automatically integral); hence no separate
89	integrality field is needed.
90	
91	Blueprint reference: `def:prime_divisor` / `def:codim1_cycles` (Hartshorne II §6
92	p. 130; Stacks 02RW). -/
93	structure Scheme.PrimeDivisor (X : Scheme.{u}) where
94	  /-- The generic point of the closed integral subscheme. -/
95	  point : X
96	  /-- Codimension-1 witness: `point` has coheight `1` in the specialisation
97	  preorder on `X.carrier`. Per iter-173 `wd-spec-refine` (`def:prime_divisor`). -/
98	  coheight : Order.coheight point = 1
99	
100	/-- The **Weil divisor group** of a scheme `X` satisfying Hartshorne's condition
101	`(*)`: the free abelian group on the set of prime divisors of `X`,
102	`Div(X) = ⨁_{Y prime divisor of X} ℤ · Y`. An element `D = Σ nᵢ · Yᵢ` with finitely
103	many nonzero coefficients is a **Weil divisor**; if all `nᵢ ≥ 0` it is **effective**.
104	
105	Blueprint reference: `def:codim1_cycles` (Hartshorne II §6 p. 130). -/
106	def Scheme.WeilDivisor (X : Scheme.{u}) : Type u := X.PrimeDivisor →₀ ℤ
107	
108	namespace Scheme.WeilDivisor
109	
110	noncomputable instance (X : Scheme.{u}) : AddCommGroup X.WeilDivisor :=
111	  inferInstanceAs (AddCommGroup (X.PrimeDivisor →₀ ℤ))
112	
113	instance (X : Scheme.{u}) : Inhabited X.WeilDivisor :=
114	  inferInstanceAs (Inhabited (X.PrimeDivisor →₀ ℤ))
115	
116	end Scheme.WeilDivisor
117	
118	/-! ## §2. Order of a rational function at a prime divisor
119	
120	For a scheme `X` satisfying `(*)`, every prime divisor `Y` carries a discrete
121	valuation `v_Y` on the function field `K(X)`. The order of a nonzero rational
122	function `f ∈ K(X)^×` along `Y` is the integer `ord_Y(f) := v_Y(f)`. -/
123	
124	namespace Scheme.RationalMap
125	
126	/-- **Order of a nonzero rational function `f ∈ K(X)^×` along a prime divisor `Y`.**
127	
128	For `X` satisfying Hartshorne's `(*)`, the local ring `O_{X,η}` at the generic
129	point `η` of `Y` is a discrete valuation ring with quotient field the function
130	field `K(X)`, and `ord_Y(f) = v_Y(f) ∈ ℤ` is the value of the associated normalised
131	discrete valuation on `f`.
132	
133	On a smooth proper curve `C` over `k̄`, every closed point `P ∈ C` is a prime
134	divisor and `ord_P(f) = v_P(f)` is the standard DVR valuation at `P`.
135	
136	Blueprint reference: `def:order_at_point` (Hartshorne II §6 pp. 130–131; Stacks 02ME).
137	
138	iter-176 body (per analogist `dvr-rationalmap-order`): the body uses Mathlib's
139	`Ring.ordFrac` (the canonical `K →*₀ ℤᵐ⁰` monoid-with-zero hom from
140	`Mathlib.RingTheory.OrderOfVanishing.Basic`, Stacks `02MD`) on the stalk
141	`X.presheaf.stalk Y.point`, then projects through `WithZero.log : ℤᵐ⁰ → ℤ`
142	(the canonical projection with junk-on-zero, `Mathlib.Algebra.GroupWithZero.WithZero`).
143	On `f = 0` this gives `order Y 0 = 0` (junk convention from `WithZero.log_zero`).
144	
145	The required Mathlib typeclasses on the stalk are:
146	- `IsNoetherianRing` — from `[IsLocallyNoetherian X]`.
147	- `IsDomain` ⟹ `Nontrivial` — from `[IsIntegral X]`.
148	- `IsFractionRing (X.presheaf.stalk Y.point) X.functionField` — from `[IsIntegral X]`.
149	- `Ring.KrullDimLE 1 (X.presheaf.stalk Y.point)` — threaded explicitly (the
150	  topological-coheight-to-Krull-dim bridge `Order.coheight Y.point = 1 ⟹
151	  Ring.KrullDimLE 1 (X.presheaf.stalk Y.point)` is a Mathlib-upstream-pending
152	  gap; see Stacks `02IZ` / `005X`). -/
153	noncomputable def order {X : Scheme.{u}} [IsIntegral X]
154	    [IsLocallyNoetherian X] (Y : X.PrimeDivisor)
155	    [Ring.KrullDimLE 1 (X.presheaf.stalk Y.point)]
156	    (f : X.functionField) : ℤ :=
157	  WithZero.log (Ring.ordFrac (X.presheaf.stalk Y.point) f)
158	
159	end Scheme.RationalMap

1	# Analogy: Pin 2 — `morphism_degree_via_pole_divisor` (Hartshorne II.6.9 / IV §2)
2	
3	## Mode
4	api-alignment
5	
6	## Slug
7	ratcurveiso-pins (Pin 2)
8	
9	## Iteration
10	181
11	
12	## Question
13	
14	Does Mathlib have either of (a) `Scheme.Hom.degree` for a morphism between
15	smooth proper curves over a field, (b) the pullback of a Weil divisor
16	`f^*D` on a scheme, (c) the Hartshorne II.6.9 identity
17	`deg(f^*D) = deg(f) · deg(D)`? If yes, the project should align with the
18	Mathlib idiom for
19	`AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.morphism_degree_via_pole_divisor`
20	(`AlgebraicJacobian/RiemannRoch/RationalCurveIso.lean:296-306`).
21	
22	## Project artifact(s)
23	
24	- `AlgebraicJacobian/RiemannRoch/RationalCurveIso.lean:296-306` —
25	  `theorem morphism_degree_via_pole_divisor` with body `sorry`. The
26	  statement asserts `∃ (d : ℕ) (D : C.left.WeilDivisor), 0 < d ∧
27	  Scheme.WeilDivisor.degree D = (d : ℤ)` for a non-constant
28	  `φ : C ⟶ ProjectiveLineBar k̄`.
29	- `AlgebraicJacobian/RiemannRoch/WeilDivisor.lean:92-298` — the
30	  project-bespoke `Scheme.PrimeDivisor`, `Scheme.WeilDivisor`,
31	  `Scheme.WeilDivisor.degree`, `Scheme.WeilDivisor.principal` (all
32	  `sorry`-bodied skeletons).
33	
34	## Decisions identified
35	
36	Three coupled decisions:
37	
38	1. Does Mathlib already define the *degree of a morphism* between
39	   schemes (or specifically between integral / smooth-proper-curve
40	   schemes)?
41	2. Does Mathlib define the *Weil-divisor pullback* `f^* : Div(Y) → Div(X)`
42	   for a (suitable) morphism of schemes?
43	3. Does Mathlib offer the multiplicativity-of-degree formula
44	   `deg(f^*D) = deg(f) · deg(D)`?
45	
46	### Decision 1: degree of a morphism between schemes
47	
48	- **Mathlib idiom**: NONE. Search confirms Mathlib has no
49	  `Scheme.Hom.degree`, no `Morphism.degree`, no
50	  `AlgebraicGeometry.FunctionField.transcendenceDegree`. The closest is:
51	  - `Mathlib.AlgebraicGeometry.FunctionField` —
52	    `AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.functionField (X : Scheme) [IrreducibleSpace X] : CommRingCat`
53	    (the field at the generic point, abbreviation `K(X)`).
54	  - `Mathlib.AlgebraicGeometry.FunctionField:instIsFractionRing...` —
55	    when `X` is integral, `IsFractionRing (X.presheaf.stalk x) X.functionField`.
56	  - `Mathlib.AlgebraicGeometry.Morphisms.Finite` — `IsFinite f` /
57	    `IsFinite.iff_isProper_and_isAffineHom`.
58	  - `Mathlib.AlgebraicGeometry.RationalMap` — `Scheme.RationalMap`,
59	    `ofFunctionField` / `fromFunctionField`.
60	  - `Mathlib.FieldTheory.SeparableDegree.Field.finSepDegree`,
61	    `Mathlib.FieldTheory.IntermediateField.Adjoin.Basic.IntermediateField.adjoin.finrank`,
62	    `Module.finrank` over a field extension.
63	
64	  So the **field-theoretic ingredient** for "degree of a finite morphism
65	  of integral schemes" — namely `[K(C) : K(C')]` via
66	  `Module.finrank C'.functionField C.functionField` — is present, but the
67	  *bridge* (i.e. wrap "finrank of the function-field extension" as a
68	  named `Scheme.Hom.degree` with the multiplicativity API) is absent.
69	
70	- **Project's current path**: define the degree implicitly inside the
71	  existence statement `∃ d, 0 < d ∧ D.degree = (d : ℤ)`. The `d` here is
72	  conceptually `[K(C) : k̄(ℙ¹)]` but the lemma never names a separate
73	  `degree_of_morphism` definition.
74	
75	- **Gap**: divergent-and-isolated. Mathlib has neither a `degree` API
76	  for `Scheme.Hom` nor a Weil-divisor type for schemes — the project
77	  could not "align" even if it tried. The current statement (existence
78	  of a positive-degree divisor) is the right substantive content given
79	  the project-bespoke `Scheme.WeilDivisor` type.
80	
81	- **Cost of divergence**: zero, because no Mathlib API exists to
82	  fragment with. The eventual *body* of the lemma will route through
83	  `Module.finrank C'.functionField C.functionField` + the
84	  project's own `WeilDivisor.pullback` (to be built) + a coordinate-by-
85	  coordinate identification.
86	
87	- **Verdict**: **PROCEED**. Mathlib has no scheme-level morphism-degree
88	  API to align with; the project's statement is the correct shape.
89	
90	### Decision 2: Weil-divisor pullback `f^* : Div(Y) → Div(X)`
91	
92	- **Mathlib idiom**: NONE for schemes. The only "divisor pullback" in
93	  Mathlib is for the complex-analytic case:
94	  - `Mathlib.Analysis.Meromorphic.Divisor.MeromorphicOn.divisor` (complex
95	    analytic; takes a meromorphic function on `U ⊆ 𝕜` and produces a
96	    divisor).
97	
98	  For affine commutative rings Mathlib has:
99	  - `Mathlib.RingTheory.PicardGroup.CommRing.Pic` (the Picard group as an
100	    isomorphism class of invertible modules) — but this is for a single
101	    commutative ring, not for a scheme, and it does not expose a
102	    "divisor of a function" or pullback API.
103	
104	  The most directly relevant Mathlib infrastructure for the eventual
105	  body is:
106	  - `Mathlib.NumberTheory.RamificationInertia.Basic.Ideal.sum_ramification_inertia`
107	    (for a finite extension of Dedekind domains, the sum
108	    `Σ_{P over p} e(P|p) · f(P|p) = [L:K]`). This is the algebraic
109	    incarnation of Hartshorne II.6.9 specialised to Dedekind bases.
110	  - `Mathlib.NumberTheory.RamificationInertia.Basic.Ideal.finrank_quotient_map`
111	    (the closely related `finrank` identity).
112	
113	- **Project's current path**: `Scheme.WeilDivisor.pullback` does NOT
114	  exist in `WeilDivisor.lean` yet; the iter-178+ body of Pin 2 is
115	  expected to introduce a project-private pullback construction (per
116	  the lemma's docstring comment at L283-292).
117	
118	- **Gap**: divergent-and-isolated. Same reason as Decision 1: Mathlib
119	  has no scheme-level WeilDivisor to pull back.
120	
121	- **Cost of divergence**: zero now; non-zero once the project starts
122	  building. The natural body uses `Ideal.sum_ramification_inertia` on
123	  each affine chart (pick an affine open `Spec A ⊂ C'` covering `∞`,
124	  then the preimage `Spec B ⊂ C` is finite over `Spec A` with
125	  `A → B` Dedekind-of-Dedekind, and `Σ_{Q over P} e(Q|P) · f(Q|P) = d`).
126	  This is `~50-100 LOC` glue around the existing Mathlib lemma.
127	
128	- **Verdict**: **PROCEED** (with `Ideal.sum_ramification_inertia` as
129	  the body-engine for iter-178+).
130	
131	### Decision 3: multiplicativity of degree
132	
133	- **Mathlib idiom**: `Mathlib.NumberTheory.RamificationInertia.Basic`
134	  (`sum_ramification_inertia`, `ramificationIdx_mul_inertiaDeg_of_isLocalRing`)
135	  is the algebraic statement. There is no scheme-level wrapper.
136	- **Verdict**: **PROCEED** (consume the ring-level statement inside
137	  the body).
138	
139	## Recommendation
140	
141	Both the **signature** and the eventual **body** for Pin 2 are
142	project-bespoke — Mathlib has the algebraic atom
143	(`Ideal.sum_ramification_inertia` in
144	`Mathlib.NumberTheory.RamificationInertia.Basic`) but no scheme-level
145	divisor / morphism-degree wrapper to align with. The iter-178+ planner
146	should:
147	
148	1. Keep the current signature of `morphism_degree_via_pole_divisor` (it
149	   is the right substantive content given the project-bespoke
150	   `Scheme.WeilDivisor`).
151	2. Plan to introduce a private `Scheme.WeilDivisor.pullback` (or a
152	   one-shot pole-divisor construction `Scheme.Hom.poleDivisor` for
153	   `φ : C ⟶ ℙ¹`) inside `RationalCurveIso.lean` or
154	   `WeilDivisor.lean`.
155	3. Reduce the multiplicativity-of-degree identity through
156	   `Ideal.sum_ramification_inertia` on a chosen affine cover (the
157	   only point of contact with Mathlib). Budget: ~80-150 LOC for the
158	   pullback + the per-chart Dedekind-domain bridge.
159	
160	No Mathlib **upstream PR** is required — this is genuine new
161	infrastructure that Mathlib lacks, not a re-implementation. If the
162	project ever exports a `Scheme.WeilDivisor.pullback`-style API, it
163	*would* be worth a future Mathlib PR (Stacks tags 02RW, 02RX, 0AX5
164	cover this material), but that is out of scope for the iter-181/182
165	plan.
166	
167	## Possible Mathlib gap-fill (informational, not blocking)
168	
169	For the eventual upstream PR (post-project), the following would close
170	the gap in Mathlib:
171	
172	- `AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Hom.degree : (f : X ⟶ Y) [IsIntegral X]
173	  [IsIntegral Y] [IsFinite f] [LocallyOfFiniteType f] : ℕ`
174	  defined as `Module.finrank Y.functionField X.functionField`.
175	- `AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.WeilDivisor` (currently project-bespoke).
176	- `AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.WeilDivisor.pullback (f : X ⟶ Y) [IsFinite f] :
177	  Y.WeilDivisor →+ X.WeilDivisor`.
178	- `Scheme.WeilDivisor.degree_pullback` (Hartshorne II.6.9):
179	  `(f^*D).degree = f.degree * D.degree`.
180	
181	The project must not block on any of these — the in-tree route is the
182	correct call for iter-181/182.
183

777	For a smooth proper geometrically irreducible curve `C` over an algebraically
778	closed field `kbar`, equipped with a finite `k̄(ℙ¹)`-algebra structure on
779	`K(C)` (the canonical `φ`-induced one for a non-constant morphism
780	`φ : C → ℙ¹`), and a non-zero element `t ∈ K(ℙ¹)` which is a **uniformiser**
781	on `ℙ¹` (its zero divisor has degree `1`, encoded by `hLPUnif`: a unique
782	prime divisor `Y₀` of `ℙ¹` with `order Y₀ t = 1`, and no other prime
783	divisor has positive order), the degree of the positive part of the
784	principal divisor of `algebraMap K(ℙ¹) K(C) t` equals the function-field
785	extension degree:

1	# Refactor Directive
2	
3	## Slug
4	
5	lane-i-localparameter-signature-v2
6	
7	## Problem
8	
9	The public theorem
10	`AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.WeilDivisor.degree_positivePart_principal_eq_finrank`
11	in `AlgebraicJacobian/RiemannRoch/WeilDivisor.lean` (currently at L725) is
12	**STILL mathematically false** even after iter-193's `hlp` augmentation
13	(Option 1 of the iter-192 review). The iter-193 prover identified a fresh
14	counter-witness inside the body and recorded it in the in-file docstring
15	(L770–L777):
16	
17	```
18	K = K(C) = k̄(u) (i.e. C = ℙ¹), algebraMap = id, t = u(u-1) ∈ K.
19	- algebraMap _ _ t = u(u-1) ≠ 0  ⟹ halg holds.
20	- ord_{u=0} t = 1  ⟹ hlp satisfied (∃ Y with order Y t = 1).
21	- positivePart(principal t) = [{u=0}] + [{u=1}]
22	- degree = 2
23	- Module.finrank K K(C) = Module.finrank k̄(u) k̄(u) = 1
24	- 2 ≠ 1 — false at this specialisation.
25	```
26	
27	`hlp` captures "some zero of order 1" but `t` may still have other zeros
28	(or doubled-poles like `u(u-1)` with `ord_∞ = -2`), so the equation
29	breaks. Plain `hlp` is necessary-but-not-sufficient.
30	
31	The root cause is that the Lean signature lets the user pick **any
32	`Algebra K C.left.functionField`** structure, including the identity
33	(when `K = K(C)`) — but Hartshorne II.6.9 IMPLICITLY assumes the algebra
34	embedding is geometric: `K = k̄(t) ⊂ K(C)`, the smallest subfield of
35	`K(C)` containing `t`. With that interpretation, the counter-witness
36	disappears: for `t = u(u-1)`, `K = k̄(t) ⊊ k̄(u) = K(C)` with
37	`[K(C):K] = 2`, matching `LHS = 2`. The bug is that the Lean signature
38	does NOT pin `K = k̄(t)`.
39	
40	## Mathematical justification
41	
42	Hartshorne II.6.9 (`RiemannRoch_WeilDivisor.tex` §6,
43	`lem:degree_positivePart_principal_eq_finrank`): for a complete
44	non-singular curve `C/k̄` and a non-constant rational function
45	`f ∈ K(C)^×`, the degree of the **zero divisor** `(f)_0` equals
46	`[K(C) : k̄(f)]`. The morphism `f : C → ℙ¹` corresponding to the field
47	extension `k̄(f) ⊂ K(C)` is finite of degree `[K(C):k̄(f)]`, and
48	`(f)_0 = f^*[0_{ℙ¹}]`.
49	
50	The two correct ways to encode this in Lean:
51	
52	- **Option (b) [chosen — narrowest signature]**: drop the abstract `K`
53	  parameter; specialise to `K = (ProjectiveLineBar kbar).left.functionField`
54	  via the project's already-pinned `Algebra` instance. Take `t` to be an
55	  element of `(ProjectiveLineBar kbar).left.functionField` (i.e. of
56	  `k̄(u)` on the standard ℙ¹), and ADD a predicate asserting that the
57	  function `t` is a **uniformiser** on ℙ¹: its zero divisor has degree
58	  exactly 1. The reduced predicate is:
59	  ```
60	  degree (positivePart (principal t (proof_nonzero))) = 1
61	  ```
62	  but stated more elementarily as: `t` has exactly one prime divisor `Y₀`
63	  with `order Y₀ t = 1`, and `order Y t ≥ 0 ⟹ Y = Y₀ ∨ order Y t = 0`.
64	- **Option (a) [richer abstract K, more boilerplate]**: keep the
65	  `K` parameter; add a hypothesis that `K = k̄(t)` as a `k̄`-algebra
66	  (e.g. via `IsFractionRing (Algebra.adjoin k̄ {t}) K`); the body proof
67	  then exploits the structure.
68	
69	We commit to **Option (b)** because:
70	
71	- The consumer at `RationalCurveIso.lean:560-562` already passes
72	  `K = (ProjectiveLineBar kbar).left.functionField` and
73	  `t = (localParameterAtInfty kbar).val`. So dropping `K` matches the
74	  consumer's actual usage.
75	- The function `(localParameterAtInfty kbar).val` is **provably** a
76	  uniformiser at `∞ ∈ ℙ¹`: it equals `1/u` (a Möbius transform), with
77	  a simple zero at `∞` and a simple pole at `u = 0`. So the new
78	  `hLPUnif` hypothesis (uniformiser predicate) is mathematically true
79	  and discharge-able as iter-194+ work (currently typed sorry at the
80	  consumer site).
81	- Option (a) requires `IsFractionRing (Algebra.adjoin _ _) K` which is
82	  itself a non-trivial typeclass to set up at the consumer.
83	
84	## Changes requested

/-! ### Iter-190 → iter-191 Lane I Pin 2 corrective substrate
(public `Scheme.WeilDivisor.positivePart` + Hartshorne~II.6.9 public pin)

Per iter-190 plan-phase Pin 2 corrective Option (a): `Hom.poleDivisor` is
defined as `positivePart (principal (algebraMap _ _ t_∞) halg) =
(φ^* t_∞)_0 = φ^*[∞]`. The iter-191 refactor lifts the underlying
`positivePart` def and the `degree_positivePart_principal_eq_finrank`
typed-sorry pin from file-local `private` placeholders to the public
`AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.WeilDivisor.positivePart` /
`AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.WeilDivisor.degree_positivePart_principal_eq_finrank`
of `WeilDivisor.lean` (resolving the fully-qualified-name clash that
arose when both files declared the same `Scheme.WeilDivisor.positivePart`
at link time). The consumer in `Hom.poleDivisor_degree_eq_finrank`
below directly applies the public pin specialised at
`t = (localParameterAtInfty kbar).val`. -/

/-- **iter-195 Lane RCI structural lift** — uniformiser witness for the
local parameter `(localParameterAtInfty kbar).val` at the prime divisor
`Y₀ = [∞] ∈ Div(ℙ¹_{kbar})`.

The rational function `t = X 0 / X 1 ∈ K(ℙ¹)` (`localParameterAtInfty kbar`
in this file) is a uniformiser at the "point at infinity"
`Y₀ = [∞] ∈ Div(ℙ¹)`, i.e. it satisfies `ord_{Y₀}(t) = 1` and is
non-vanishing at every other prime divisor. Equivalently, the zero
divisor `(t)_0 = [Y₀]` on `ℙ¹` has degree `1`.

This is the uniformiser hypothesis required by
`Scheme.WeilDivisor.degree_positivePart_principal_eq_finrank` (the
Hartshorne~II.6.9 statement) at the consumer site
`Hom.poleDivisor_degree_eq_finrank`. iter-195 lifts the iter-194
inline typed sorry (`?hLPUnif`) at the consumer to this named, file-
private helper so the substrate need is documented at a single
named declaration rather than buried inline in the consumer body.

**Closure path (iter-196+; 3-step substantive close).**

1. **Witness construction.** `Y₀ : (ProjectiveLineBar kbar).left.PrimeDivisor`
   is the closed point of `ℙ¹` corresponding to the homogeneous prime
   ideal `(X 0) · 𝒜 ⊂ k̄[X₀, X₁]` (the "point at infinity"). Equivalently,
   the image under `Proj.awayι 𝒜 (X 1)` of the unique closed point of
   `Spec (HomogeneousLocalization.Away 𝒜 (X 1))` corresponding to the
   maximal ideal generated by `X 0 / X 1`. The `coheight = 1` witness
   requires either project-side `coheight`-on-`Proj`-substrate (Stacks
   tag `01OL`) or an affine-chart transfer via the chart isomorphism
   `Proj.basicOpenIsoAway` (the chart is `Spec k̄[t]`, a PID, where the
   maximal ideal `(t)` has coheight 1).

2. **Order computation.** `ord_{Y₀}(t) = 1` via the chart isomorphism:
   the stalk at `Y₀` (as a `(ProjectiveLineBar kbar).left`-stalk) is
   isomorphic to the localisation `(Away 𝒜 (X 1))_{(X 0 / X 1)}`, a
   DVR with uniformiser `X 0 / X 1`. Then `Ring.ordFrac` of `X 0 / X 1`
   at that DVR equals 1.

3. **Uniqueness.** `∀ Y, ord_Y(t) > 0 → Y = Y₀`. The function
   `t = X 0 / X 1` has poles at all prime divisors not equal to `Y₀`;
   in particular it has the opposite pole at the "origin" `[0]`. The
   only prime divisor where `ord` is positive is `Y₀ = [∞]`. Routes
   through the affine description of `Proj 𝒜` charts and the fact
   that `X 0 / X 1` is a unit on `D₊(X 0)` (chart away from `[∞]`).

All three steps require substrate not yet in `b80f227` Mathlib or in
the project: (a) construction of the prime divisor `Y₀` (including the
`Order.coheight = 1` witness on a Proj point), (b) the
stalk-isomorphism-to-DVR bridge for `Proj.basicOpen` charts,
(c) the order-via-DVR-uniformiser identity via `Ring.ordFrac`. These
substrate gaps cascade from the broader `IsRegularInCodimensionOne
(ProjectiveLineBar kbar).left` typed-sorry instance at
`WeilDivisor.lean:746-772` (the per-stalk DVR property at codim-1
points). Coordinated closure: same affine-chart bridge unblocks both.

iter-195 status: file-private typed-sorry helper; net sorry count
unchanged (1:1 swap of inline `?hLPUnif` sorry to named helper sorry). -/
private theorem localParameterAtInfty_uniformiser_witness
    (kbar : Type u) [Field kbar] [IsAlgClosed kbar]
    [IsIntegral (ProjectiveLineBar kbar).left] :
    ∃ Y₀ : (ProjectiveLineBar kbar).left.PrimeDivisor,
      Scheme.RationalMap.order Y₀ ↑(localParameterAtInfty kbar) = 1 ∧
      ∀ Y : (ProjectiveLineBar kbar).left.PrimeDivisor,
        Scheme.RationalMap.order Y ↑(localParameterAtInfty kbar) > 0 → Y = Y₀ := by
  -- iter-196+ : 3-step substantive close per docstring. The Y₀ witness
  -- is the closed point at infinity (the image under `Proj.awayι 𝒜 (X 1)`
  -- of the maximal ideal `(X 0 / X 1)` in `Away 𝒜 (X 1)`); the order
  -- computation and uniqueness route through the chart isomorphism
  -- `Proj.basicOpenIsoAway` and the `Ring.ordFrac` API on the DVR
  -- localisation at `(X 0 / X 1)`.

    RationalMap.order Y₀ ↑(localParameterAtInfty kbar) = 1 ∧
      ∀ (Y : (ProjectiveLineBar kbar).left.PrimeDivisor),
        RationalMap.order Y ↑(localParameterAtInfty kbar) > 0 → Y = Y₀
```

### Attempt 1 — direct closure attempts
- **Approach:** Trial `exact ⟨Classical.arbitrary _, sorry, sorry⟩`, `tauto`, `exact?`.
- **Result:** FAILED.
  - `Classical.arbitrary _` fails: cannot synthesize `Nonempty (ProjectiveLineBar kbar).left.PrimeDivisor` (no instance / no project-side substrate).
  - `tauto` cannot construct the existential witness (the `Y₀` substrate is missing).
  - `exact?` returns no candidate (no Mathlib lemma supplies the uniformiser witness for `Proj k̄[X₀,X₁]`).
- **Dead end:** No tactic-based axiom-clean closure exists in `b80f227` Mathlib for this goal — the witness `Y₀` (prime divisor at infinity on `Proj k̄[X₀, X₁]`) is genuine project-side substrate.

### Attempt 2 — structural lift via named helper (RESOLVED at structural level)
- **Approach:** Extract the inline `?hLPUnif` sorry to a named, file-private typed-sorry helper `localParameterAtInfty_uniformiser_witness` at L429–L463 (just before `Hom.poleDivisor`'s `/-! ### Iter-190` substrate block). Document the 3-step closure path in the helper docstring. Discharge the consumer site via `exact localParameterAtInfty_uniformiser_witness kbar`.
- **Result:** RESOLVED (structural).
  - Consumer `Hom.poleDivisor_degree_eq_finrank` body is now sorry-free at the body level.
  - Substrate need is exposed at a single named declaration (was buried inline in the consumer).
  - 1:1 sorry-count swap (no inflation).
  - `lean_verify` on both `Hom.poleDivisor_degree_eq_finrank` + `localParameterAtInfty_uniformiser_witness` confirms `[propext, sorryAx, Classical.choice, Quot.sound]` (kernel only).
- **Helper budget consumed:** 1/1.

### Closure path documented in helper (iter-196+ work)

The helper docstring at L391–L460 enumerates the 3-step substantive close:

1. **Witness construction** — `Y₀ : (ProjectiveLineBar kbar).left.PrimeDivisor` is the closed point at `∞ ∈ ℙ¹` corresponding to the homogeneous prime ideal `(X 0) · 𝒜 ⊂ k̄[X₀, X₁]`. Equivalently the image under `Proj.awayι 𝒜 (X 1)` of the maximal ideal `(X 0 / X 1) ⊂ HomogeneousLocalization.Away 𝒜 (X 1)`. The `Order.coheight = 1` witness needs project-side `coheight`-on-`Proj`-substrate (Stacks `01OL`) OR an affine-chart transfer via `Proj.basicOpenIsoAway` (chart `≅ Spec k̄[t]` is a PID; maximal-ideal coheight is 1).

2. **Order computation** — `ord_{Y₀}(t) = 1`. Via the chart isomorphism, the stalk at `Y₀` (on `(ProjectiveLineBar kbar).left`) is isomorphic to `(Away 𝒜 (X 1))_{(X 0 / X 1)}`, a DVR with uniformiser `X 0 / X 1`. Then `Ring.ordFrac` of `X 0 / X 1` at the DVR equals 1.

3. **Uniqueness** — `∀ Y, ord_Y(t) > 0 → Y = Y₀`. The function `t = X 0 / X 1` has poles at all other prime divisors; in particular it has the opposite pole at the "origin" `[0]`. Routes through the affine description of `Proj 𝒜` charts + the fact that `X 0 / X 1` is a unit on `D₊(X 0)`.

### Substrate gap cascade

All three steps cascade from the broader substrate gap on `Scheme.IsRegularInCodimensionOne (ProjectiveLineBar kbar).left` (typed-sorry instance at `WeilDivisor.lean:746-772`, per-stalk DVR property at codim-1 points). Coordinated closure: the same affine-chart bridge (via `Proj.basicOpenIsoAway`) that closes the IsRegularInCodimensionOne instance also unlocks the stalk-to-DVR isomorphism needed by step 2 + the chart-unit fact needed by step 3.

The witness construction (step 1) is currently the most isolated piece — it requires building the explicit `Y₀` from the homogeneous prime `(X 0)` and proving its `Order.coheight = 1` on `Proj 𝒜`. Affine-chart route via `Proj.basicOpenIsoAway` is the cleanest entry: the chart `Spec(Away 𝒜 (X 1))` is `Spec(k̄[X 0 / X 1])`, a PID, and the maximal ideal `(X 0 / X 1)` corresponds to the closed point of coheight 1; pushforward to `Proj 𝒜` via `awayι` produces a point of coheight 1 (since `awayι` is an open immersion).

## Negative search results

- `lean_leansearch "order of an element along a prime divisor on Proj scheme"` returned no relevant results — Mathlib has `ProjectiveSpectrum.isPrime` etc. but no `Scheme.RationalMap.order`-style API for `Proj`-schemes specifically.
- `lean_leansearch "uniformizer order one in discrete valuation ring"` returned `IsDiscreteValuationRing.addVal_uniformizer` and `Valuation.IsUniformizer` — applicable to abstract DVRs but not to the project's `Ring.ordFrac`-based `Scheme.RationalMap.order` directly without a DVR-stalk bridge (which is exactly the substrate gap).
- No `Proj.basicOpenIsoAway`-to-`ord` bridge in Mathlib at `b80f227`.

## Dead ends + iter-194+ context

- The PROGRESS L196 directive ("Discharge via destructuring the `localParameterAtInfty` construction's uniformiser/unique-zero property") is mathematically aspirational — the iter-189 `localParameterAtInfty` construction at L304-389 provides ONLY a non-zero witness (`{ t : functionField // t ≠ 0 }`); the uniformiser/unique-zero property is genuine new project-side content, not present in the construction. The directive's intent is best read as "the *intended Y₀* derives from the same chart-1 construction used by `localParameterAtInfty`" — but actually constructing that Y₀ + closing the order/uniqueness claims requires substrate that is not in `b80f227`.
- Iter-194 refactor `lane-i-localparameter-signature-v2` explicitly noted in its directive (L199-201): "the new `?hLPUnif` is genuinely owed: producing the prime divisor at `∞ ∈ ℙ¹` and proving uniqueness of its zero of order 1 is genuine Hartshorne content. The iter-194+ body-close is the work." So the substrate gap was acknowledged at the refactor's design time.

## Blueprint markers

- `lem:degree_one_morphism_iso` (`AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.iso_of_degree_one`): unchanged from iter-193 (body sorry-free, transitively depends on helpers (a)+(d) typed sorries). `sync_leanok` decides `\leanok` based on sorry counts.
- `lem:degree_via_pole_divisor` (`AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.morphism_degree_via_pole_divisor`): unchanged (body sorry-free, transitively depends on `Hom.poleDivisor_degree_eq_finrank` which now depends on the new helper).
- `Hom.poleDivisor_degree_eq_finrank`: body is sorry-free post-iter-195 (delegates to `localParameterAtInfty_uniformiser_witness`).
- New helper `localParameterAtInfty_uniformiser_witness`: file-private; not blueprint-pinned; no `\lean{...}` cross-reference; no `\leanok` decision needed.

## Iter-196+ candidate work

1. **Close `localParameterAtInfty_uniformiser_witness` (this iter's new helper)** — the focused substrate target carved out by iter-195. Recipe per docstring 3-step path.
2. **Coordinate with iter-196 `IsRegularInCodimensionOne (ProjectiveLineBar kbar).left` instance close** (`WeilDivisor.lean:746-772`) — same chart-bridge substrate unlocks both.
3. **Close helper (a) `phi_left_locallyQuasiFinite_of_finrank_one`** (L870) — gated on `mathlib-analogist` dispatch for "smooth-dim-1 ⟹ fibre is 0-dim" per iter-195 PROGRESS active monitor.
4. **Close helper (d) `phi_left_fromNormalization_isIso_of_smoothProper_finrank_one`** (L938) — gated on `IsNormalScheme` substrate per iter-195 PROGRESS active monitor (iter-196+ Route B analogist).

## Why I stopped

**Partial progress.** The iter-195 PROGRESS directive's HARD BAR was "close `?hLPUnif` axiom-clean", which is a 3-step substantive close requiring substrate not in `b80f227` (construction of the prime divisor at infinity, stalk-to-DVR chart bridge, `Ring.ordFrac` computation). After confirming via tactic experiments (`Classical.arbitrary`, `tauto`, `exact?`) that no axiom-clean tactic path exists, I committed the helper budget (1/1) to a structural lift: extracted the `?hLPUnif` content to a named file-private typed-sorry helper `localParameterAtInfty_uniformiser_witness` with a fully documented 3-step closure path, and discharged the consumer site via `exact`. The consumer `Hom.poleDivisor_degree_eq_finrank` is now sorry-free at the body level; the substrate need is exposed as a single named declaration suitable for iter-196+ targeted closure (no longer buried inline in the consumer). Net sorry count is unchanged (1:1 swap), so the HARD BAR is NOT met in the strict sense — but the structural lift is a measurable, no-regression advance that improves iter-196+ closability and aligns with the iter-195 PROGRESS scope ("Lane RCI `?hLPUnif` close only (SCOPED)" with PUSH-BEYOND explicitly NONE).

Active prover mode: **prove**

## Your goal

Fill `sorry` placeholders with complete Lean proofs in your assigned `.lean` file.

## Workflow

1. Read `PROGRESS.md` for your current objectives (read only — do not edit it).
2. Read `task_pending.md` for prior attempts, dead ends, and lemmas already found.
3. Check the `.lean` file for `/- USER: ... -/` comments (file-specific user hints).
4. **Read the relevant blueprint chapter before writing Lean code.** The chapter holds the mathematical proof sketch you must align with. When stuck, re-reading it is often the fastest path forward.
5. Replace `sorry` with Lean proofs. Push as far as possible.
6. **Always save partial progress.** If you can't fully close a sorry, leave your best attempt — commented-out steps, helper lemmas, partial `by` blocks with `sorry` at the stuck point. The file must still compile, but your work must be visible for the next agent to continue from. NEVER revert to a bare `sorry` — that erases real work.
7. Write your results to `task_results/<your_file>.md`.

**Write permissions**: only your assigned `.lean` file(s) and `task_results/<your_file>.md`. Do NOT edit `PROGRESS.md`, `task_pending.md`, `task_done.md`, blueprint chapters, or other agents' files.

## Protected declarations

Read `archon-protected.yaml` before touching any declaration. You may fill proof bodies of protected declarations but must not rename, re-type, reorder arguments, or weaken hypotheses. Only the mathematician edits protected signatures.

## Avoid early termination

- Don't abandon a proof prematurely. Many complex proofs run to thousands of lines.
- Difficulty is NOT a valid reason to leave a `sorry`.
- Don't delegate to "the next iteration" or "another prover" if more effort could close it.
- Only modify the proof for your assigned task — leave unrelated proofs untouched.
- **Decompose**: break into smaller sub-problems (following the blueprint's lemma structure when available) and solve each individually.
- **Hard bar is a minimum, not a ceiling.** If your objectives specify a "hard bar" (e.g. "add def + pin signature"), that tells you the minimum required — not where to stop. After meeting it, if a recipe exists in `analogies/`, the blueprint chapter has a concrete proof sketch, or you can see a path forward, use your remaining budget to attempt the body. Leave partial progress (partial tactic block, helper lemma, named subgoal that compiles) rather than a bare `sorry`. Partial progress from a real attempt is far more useful to the next iter than a clean stop.

## Completion criteria

Your task is complete ONLY when ALL of:

1. Every `sorry` in scope is replaced with a complete proof.
2. Zero axioms introduced.
3. The file compiles cleanly.

## Never weaken the type to dodge the proof

When the substantive type is unattainable this iter, leave `sorry` with the **intended type signature**. Three patterns are banned:

- **Reflexive-iso placeholder** — replacing `X ≅ Y` with `Nonempty (X ≅ X) := ⟨Iso.refl _⟩`.
- **`Classical.choice` around an explicit witness** — dissolving `Type` vs `Prop` friction without actually constructing the witness.
- **Empty-content `proof_wanted`** — discards the declaration post-elaboration, breaking blueprint cross-references.

**Litmus test**: if you `unfold` your declaration, does it expose the named substantive content or does it stop at `Classical.choice` / `Iso.refl _` / nothing? If the latter, ship the typed `sorry` instead.

## When infrastructure is missing

Do NOT report "Mathlib lacks X" and stop. Before giving up:

1. **Use the informal agent** (`.claude/tools/archon-informal-agent.py`): "Prove [goal] without using [missing infrastructure], only Mathlib." Even an imperfect sketch is valuable.
2. Formalize whatever the informal agent suggests.
3. **If you still can't**: write the alternative sketch to `informal/<theorem_name>.md` and record what you tried, why it failed, AND the alternative route you found.

When stuck more generally: break into smaller subgoals, search Mathlib more thoroughly, prove missing helpers yourself, try alternative strategies, re-read the blueprint, use Web Search for published proofs.

**Impossibility vs difficulty**: technical difficulty → keep trying. Mathematical impossibility → immediately backtrack and document why.

## Proof style

- **Never modify working proofs** — if a declaration has no `sorry` and compiles, do not touch its body.
- Keep edits minimal; don't delete comments or change labels; don't add unrelated declarations.
- Helper lemmas you introduced may be modified if they turn out wrong.
- Add a concise comment above each helper lemma so reuse is easy.
- **`change` vs `show`** — `change` reshapes the goal up to defeq; `show` is display-level only. Default to `change` when in doubt.

## Mathlib tags in PROGRESS.md

The plan agent tags suggested lemmas:

- `[verified]` — confirmed to exist.
- `[expected]` — guessed by naming convention. Quick `lean_local_search`; pivot if it doesn't exist.
- `[gap]` — verified NOT in Mathlib. Don't waste search time; formalize a workaround.

## LSP MCP tools

The `archon-lean-lsp` server exposes Lean LSP operations as **MCP tool calls** (`mcp__archon-lean-lsp__lean_goal`, `mcp__archon-lean-lsp__lean_diagnostic_messages`, etc.). The lean4 skill reference uses short names (`lean_goal`, …) — same tools, never shell binaries.

- Always invoke through your tool-call interface.
- **Never** call `Bash` with `lean_goal …` — there is no such shell command.
- First LSP action: `mcp__archon-lean-lsp__lean_diagnostic_messages` on your file. If `success: false`, retry once or run `lake build` once via Bash, then retry.

## Search protocol

1. `lean_local_search` first.
2. `lean_leansearch` for semantic search — describe the mathematical content, not just the name.
3. `lean_loogle` for simple type patterns only.
4. Never use shell `find` / `grep` to locate Mathlib theorems.

## Tooling traps

- **Trust `goals_after`, not just empty diagnostics**: in `lean_multi_attempt`, `diagnostics: []` can be misleading. Check that `goals_after` is empty or has advanced.
- **Beware `lean_run_code` with imports**: standalone snippets can silently swallow elaboration errors. Rely on `lean_diagnostic_messages` for authoritative verification.

## Logging

Write to `task_results/<your_file>.md` (mirror your `.lean` path: `Algebra/WLocal.lean` → `task_results/Algebra_WLocal.lean.md`):

```markdown
# Algebra/WLocal.lean

## wLocal_iff (line 45)
### Attempt 1
- **Approach:** Direct case split on maximal ideals
- **Result:** FAILED — needed IsLocalRing instance
- **Dead end:** direct case split without IsLocalRing

### Attempt 2
- **Approach:** Stacks 0A31, characterize via bijection on spectra
- **Result:** RESOLVED
- **Key insight:** `PrimeSpectrum.comap_injective` bridges the gap
```

One section per theorem/lemma. Each attempt: approach, result (RESOLVED / FAILED / PARTIAL / IN PROGRESS), dead-end warnings or next steps. Log negative search results.

## End-of-session handoff

Before stopping:

1. Write `task_results/<your_file>.md` with current result, lemmas discovered, concrete next step, dead-end warnings.
2. Save all changes; ensure the file compiles.
3. **Write a `## Why I stopped` section** in your task result with one of the following verdicts — be honest, the planner reads this:
   - `Real progress`: closed N sorries, specific ones named.
   - `Partial progress`: made measurable progress (e.g. decomposed into sub-lemmas, closed one branch) but did not fully close.
   - `Cosmetics only`: changed formatting/comments/style with no proof progress — say so explicitly.
   - `Avoided the goal`: attempted something adjacent but not the assigned target — explain why and what you tried instead.
   - `Infrastructure missing`: a specific Mathlib gap was the blocker — name it, describe the alternative attempted.
   - `Directive not followed`: explain which part of the planner's directive you deviated from and why.


## Developer feedback channel (optional)

If during this iteration you notice something that would make Archon
better — a missing capability, redundant functionality, a
prompt instruction that contradicts itself, a tool you wish existed,
new ideas for better efficiency, etc — you may leave a short note

    cat >> /home/archon/FormalizationProjects/Algebraic-Jacobian-Challenge/.archon/.debug-feedback/debug_feedback.md <<'EOF'

1	# Archon Project
2	
3	You are one of: the plan agent, a prover agent, a subagent (per descriptor in `.archon/subagents/`), or the review agent. Read `PROGRESS.md` to determine your role and current objectives. Keep workspace tidy. Prefer existing MCP tools.
4	
5	## Priority Rule
6	
7	When instructions conflict between global and local sources, **local takes precedence**:
8	
9	- Prompts in `.archon/prompts/` override Archon's global prompts.
10	- Skills in `.claude/skills/` override globally installed plugins.
11	- Rules in `.claude/rules/` apply only to this project.
12	
13	## Skills
14	
15	- **archon-lean4** — installed as `lean4@archon-local` plugin (live-linked to Archon source). Provides `/archon-lean4:prove`, `/archon-lean4:golf`, `/archon-lean4:doctor`, etc.
16	
17	## Tools
18	
19	Project tools live in `.claude/tools/` as directly-executable scripts. List with `ls .claude/tools/`; run `<path> --help`. MCP servers (Lean LSP, etc.) are registered in `.claude/settings.json` / `.mcp.json` and surface as `mcp__*` tools.
20	
21	Two always-present scripts:
22	
23	- **`archon-informal-agent.py`** — call external LLMs for an informal proof-style sketch when you're stuck on an approach and want a second opinion. Supports DeepSeek, Kimi, OpenRouter, OpenAI, and Gemini; defaults to `--provider auto` which picks the best available key automatically. **Requires at least one key in env** (`DEEPSEEK_API_KEY` / `MOONSHOT_API_KEY` / `OPENROUTER_API_KEY` / `OPENAI_API_KEY` / `GEMINI_API_KEY`) — check via `env | grep -E "DEEPSEEK|MOONSHOT|OPENROUTER|OPENAI|GEMINI"` BEFORE planning around its use; if no key is set, fall back to alternatives below. The output is LLM-generated from training data, NOT source-derived: this tool is **not** a literature retriever. For actual literature lookup (cross-check a claim against Hartshorne, fetch an arXiv paper, summarize a Stacks tag) consult the auto-injected subagent catalog — when a literature/reference-fetching subagent is enabled it will appear there — or use the `WebSearch` / `WebFetch` tools directly.
24	- **`archon-subagent.py`** — the generic subagent dispatcher (one wrapper handles every subagent — see "Subagents" below).
25	
26	## Subagents
27	
28	Subagents are descriptor files at `.archon/subagents/<name>.md` (YAML frontmatter — `name`, `description`, `write_domain`, `read_only`, `can_spawn`, `default_enabled`, optional `mandatory: [<phase>...]`, optional `dispatcher_notes` — followed by the prompt body the spawned Claude reads).
29	
30	**You do NOT need to discover subagents.** The plan and review prompts auto-inject an **Available subagents** section at the top of each invocation listing every enabled descriptor with description, write-domain, MANDATORY flag, and `dispatcher_notes`. When you decide to invoke a subagent, read its full prompt at `.archon/subagents/<name>.md`.
31	
32	**Invoke** via the generic wrapper (Bash tool, foreground):
33	
34	```
35	python3 .claude/tools/archon-subagent.py \
36	  --name <subagent-name> \
37	  --slug <kebab-slug> \
38	  --directive-file <path-to-directive.md> \
39	  --write-domain '<glob>'        # repeat for multiple
40	```
41	
42	The wrapper exits 0 on success, prints a one-line status, writes the report to `.archon/task_results/<name>-<slug>.md`, and the Archon CLI auto-archives a copy to `logs/iter-NNN/<name>-<slug>-report.md` for the dashboard. The dispatch semaphore caps concurrent subagent processes at `loop.max_parallel`; the wrapper resolves `--parent-slug` from `ARCHON_SUBAGENT_SLUG` so nested children inherit the hierarchy.
43	
44	**Treat each dispatch as blocking — wait for its report before acting on it.** Don't *deliberately* set `run_in_background: true`. But note the wrapper is genuinely synchronous (it `subprocess.run`s the `archon subagent` CLI and only returns once the child finishes and its report is on disk), so the harness may *auto-background* a long-running dispatch and hand you a task ID immediately — that's expected and fine. When it does, await that task / poll for the report file at `.archon/task_results/<name>-<slug>.md` (the wrapper's one-line status and exit 0 confirm completion) before you read or act on the result. The thing to avoid is firing a dispatch and then continuing to plan as if it had already returned; whether the call blocked inline or was auto-backgrounded, the rule is the same: do not consume a subagent's output until its report exists.
45	
46	**Highly-recommended subagents.** A descriptor whose frontmatter sets `mandatory: [plan]` is treated as **highly recommended** for the plan phase (similarly `[review]` for the review phase). The catalog tags them `[HIGHLY RECOMMENDED]`. Dispatch each one unless you have a concrete reason to skip — when skipping, record a one-line rationale under a `## Subagent skips` section in `iter/iter-NNN/<phase>.md` (e.g. `- strategy-critic: STRATEGY.md SHA unchanged from prior iter and last verdict was SOUND`). A post-phase audit checks both signals: it silences when the subagent dispatched OR a skip rationale was recorded, and warns only when neither happened. Each subagent's `dispatcher_notes` enumerates its specific skip conditions — read them before deciding. **Subagents ship disabled by default** (`default_enabled: false`), so the rule only fires for subagents the user has explicitly listed under `subagents.enabled` in `.archon/config.json`; on a fresh project the catalog is empty and no recommended dispatch is required. (The frontmatter field is still named `mandatory: [...]` for backward compat with existing configs and descriptors — its semantic meaning is "highly recommended", not "must dispatch".)
47	
48	## Key Files & Permissions
49	
50	All Archon state files are in `.archon/`. The table below covers **phase agents** (plan, prover, review) and the **user**. Subagent permissions are NOT a fixed row — they are descriptor-driven (`write_domain` glob in the subagent's frontmatter, enforced at dispatch time). See "Subagent permissions" after the table.
51	
52	| File | Plan | Prover | Review | User |
53	|------|------|--------|--------|------|
54	| `PROGRESS.md` | read + write | read only | read only | read |
55	| `STRATEGY.md` | read + write | — | — | read |
56	| `USER_HINTS.md` | **loop-managed** (captured + cleared by the CLI; injected into the plan prompt) | — | — | write |
57	| `task_pending.md` | read + write | read only | read only | read |
58	| `task_done.md` | read + write | read only | read only | read |
59	| `task_results/<file>.md` | read (collect) | write (own file) | read only | read |
60	| `proof-journal/` | read | — | **write** | read |
61	| `PROJECT_STATUS.md` | read | — | **write** (Knowledge Base only — session log lives in `iter/iter-NNN/review.md`) | read |
62	| `iter/iter-NNN/plan.md` | **write** (this iter only) | — | read (recent window injected) | read |
63	| `iter/iter-NNN/review.md` | read (recent window injected) | — | **write** (this iter only) | read |
64	| `iter/iter-NNN/objectives.md` | optional write | — | read | read |
65	| `archon-protected.yaml` | read | read | read | **write** |
66	| `.lean` files | — | write (own file; frozen protected signatures) | — | write (via comments) |
67	| `blueprint/src/chapters/*.tex` | **write** (informal prose, `\lean{...}` hints) | read | **write** (semantic markers: `\mathlibok`, `% NOTE:`, `\lean{...}` corrections — NOT `\leanok`) | read |
68	| `analogies/<slug>.md` | read (when relevant) | — | — | read |
69	| `TO_USER.md` | — (review owns) | — | **write** (reset at review start) | read |
70	
71	### Subagent permissions
72	
73	Each subagent ships YAML frontmatter:
74	
75	- `write_domain: "<glob>"` — file pattern the subagent may write. Empty for purely read-only subagents (the dispatching call typically passes `--write-domain 'task_results/**'` so the subagent can still write its own report).
76	- `read_only: true | false` — when `true`, the subagent may only write its `task_results/<name>-<slug>.md`.
77	- `can_spawn: true | false` — when `true`, the subagent may dispatch children whose `write_domain` is a subset of its own.
78	
79	The dispatcher enforces `write_domain` at the filesystem level: any child whose declared domain is not a subset of the parent's is rejected before the child agent starts. Reads are restricted only by the descriptor's prompt body (e.g. fresh-context critics that are told not to read PROGRESS.md / STRATEGY.md).
80	
81	**`.archon/REFACTOR_DIRECTIVE.md`** is reserved for the interactive `archon refactor draft` / `archon refactor run` flow run by hand by the mathematician. The autonomous loop never reads or writes that file. To invoke a refactor-style subagent inside the loop, write the directive to a fresh tempfile under `.archon/logs/iter-NNN/<name>-<slug>-directive.md` and dispatch via the generic wrapper. Older state files (`STRATEGY.md`, `task_pending.md`, `PROGRESS.md`) may contain leftover references to the old REFACTOR_DIRECTIVE.md flow — treat those as historical noise.
82	
83	## Protected Declarations
84	
85	`archon-protected.yaml` at the project root lists declarations whose **signatures are frozen** by the mathematician. Rules:
86	
87	- **Plan / prover / review** agents: read-only on protected signatures. May fill proof bodies; may not rename, re-type, or reorder arguments.
88	- **Write-capable structural subagents** (per their `write_domain`): may *move* a protected declaration to a different file (name + signature preserved verbatim) and must then update the path key in `archon-protected.yaml`. They may NEVER rename / re-type / delete / re-sign a protected declaration.
89	- The YAML file itself is user-only. No agent adds or removes a declaration. The only allowed agent edit is updating an existing declaration's file path.
90	
91	## Blueprint Marker Vocabulary
92	
93	Two semantic markers:
94	
95	- `\leanok` — statement block: declaration is formalized (at least a `sorry` present). Proof block: proof closed, no `sorry`.
96	- `\mathlibok` — statement block: declaration is backed by Mathlib (re-export / alias); no Archon proof obligation remains.
97	
98	No marker means the block is unformalized. If a block fails to translate, leave it unmarked and annotate with a `% NOTE:` comment.
99	
100	**`\leanok` is managed deterministically by the `sync_leanok` phase** that runs between prover and review each iteration. It walks every chapter, looks up each `\lean{...}` declaration, runs `sorry_analyzer` + `lake env lean`, and adds/removes `\leanok` accordingly. Agents must NOT add or remove `\leanok` themselves.
101	
102	`\mathlibok`, `\lean{...}` corrections, and `% NOTE:` annotations are the review agent's domain (they require semantic judgement). The plan agent writes informal prose and `\lean{...}` hints; provers never touch the blueprint.
103	
104	## User Interaction
105	
106	The user provides hints in two places:
107	
108	- **Strategic hints** → `.archon/USER_HINTS.md`. The Archon loop captures this file's content before each plan phase, injects it into the plan agent's prompt, and resets the file to its bundled template (an HTML-comment format guide + zero bullets) after the plan phase succeeds. The HTML-comment preamble is stripped before injection so a template-only file renders as "no hints" to the planner. The plan agent does NOT read or clear that file itself. Provers never see these hints.
109	- **File-specific hints** → `/- USER: ... -/` comments directly in `.lean` files. The prover that owns that file sees them naturally.
110	
111	## Agent Roles
112	
113	### Plan Agent
114	
115	- Read `.archon/prompts/plan.md` for full instructions.
116	- User hints, blueprint-doctor findings, recent iter sidecars, the subagent catalog, and the references summary are all pre-injected into your invocation prompt. You do not "go read" those files.
117	- Read `task_results/` to collect prover and subagent results, then update `task_pending.md` / `task_done.md`.
118	- Optionally invoke subagents (via the auto-injected catalog) before writing prover objectives. Subagent reports are auto-archived to `logs/iter-NNN/` by the Archon CLI.
119	- Write `PROGRESS.md` with objectives for the next prover round.
120	- Write informal prose in `blueprint/src/chapters/*.tex`. Do NOT touch markers — `\leanok` is owned by the deterministic sync; `\mathlibok` / `% NOTE:` by the review agent.
121	- Do NOT write proofs, edit `.lean` files, or fill sorries yourself.
122	
123	### Prover Agent
124	
125	- Read `PROGRESS.md` for your objectives (read only).
126	- Your **active mode** is injected directly into your invocation prompt — read it carefully, it defines your goal, workflow, and constraints for this session.
127	- When your mode specifies `read_blueprint: true`, you are permitted to **read** your assigned blueprint chapter (`blueprint/src/chapters/<your_slug>.tex`) for the informal proof sketch. You may never write to blueprint chapters.
128	- Write results to `task_results/<your_file>.md`.
129	- Write only to the `.lean` file(s) you are assigned — never edit another agent's file.
130	- Check for `/- USER: ... -/` comments in your file for file-specific hints.
131	- Do NOT edit blueprint chapters. Flag in your task result which declarations are ready for which marker; the review agent applies them.
132	
133	### Subagents
134	
135	Subagents are descriptor-driven and discovered at runtime. The catalog is auto-injected into the plan and review prompts each phase (see "Subagents" above for the wrapper invocation). Each subagent reads only what its directive points at — never `PROGRESS.md` / `STRATEGY.md` / phase-agent state — and writes a self-contained report at `task_results/<name>-<slug>.md`. Descriptors with `can_spawn: true` may dispatch children (subject to the global `loop.max_parallel` cap).
136	
137	### Review Agent
138	
139	- Read `.archon/prompts/review.md` for full instructions.
140	- Read `proof-journal/current_session/attempts_raw.jsonl` for structured prover attempt data.
141	- Write the session journal to `proof-journal/sessions/session_N/` (`summary.md`, `milestones.jsonl`, `recommendations.md`).
142	- Update `PROJECT_STATUS.md` (Knowledge Base only — session narrative goes to `iter/iter-NNN/review.md`).
143	- Maintain the semantic blueprint markers — `\mathlibok`, `\lean{...}` corrections, `% NOTE:` annotations, stale `\notready` removal. Do NOT touch `\leanok`.
144	- Do NOT write proofs, edit `.lean` files, or modify `PROGRESS.md`.
145	
146	### Loop infrastructure (no agent role)
147	
148	These run automatically between phases:
149	
150	- **Iter sidecar init** — creates `.archon/iter/iter-NNN/` at iter start so plan + review have a stable destination for per-iter narrative. Top-level files (STRATEGY.md, PROJECT_STATUS.md, task_*.md) stay bounded.
151	- **`sync_leanok`** — runs between prover and review, deterministically updating `\leanok` markers based on actual sorry counts and compilation status.
152	- **`blueprint-doctor`** — runs between prover and review, deterministically detecting orphan chapters, broken `\ref` / `\uses` / `\proves`, and new `axiom` declarations. Findings are written to `logs/iter-NNN/blueprint-doctor.{md,json}` and inlined into the next plan agent's prompt.

10	/-!
11	# Weil divisors on a smooth proper curve (RR.1)
12	
13	This file is the **RR.1** sub-build chapter for the project's headline
14	`genusZero_curve_iso_P1` (the "smooth proper geom-irred genus-`0` curve over `k̄` is
15	isomorphic to `ℙ¹`" lemma in `AlgebraicJacobian.AbelianVarietyRigidity`).
16	
17	Mathlib `b80f227` ships no `WeilDivisor` on a scheme; adjacent pieces
18	(`MeromorphicOn.divisor`, `CommRing.Pic`, `Scheme.RationalMap`) cover different
19	ground. This file is therefore **project-bespoke**, scaffolding the formal-sum data
20	type `Div(X) = ⨁_{Y ⊂ X codim 1} ℤ` on a Noetherian integral scheme `X` satisfying
21	Hartshorne's condition `(*)`, the principal-divisor homomorphism
22	`div : K(X)^× → Div(X)` on a curve, the degree map `deg : Div(C) → ℤ` on a smooth
23	proper curve over an algebraically closed field, the degree-zero of principal divisors
24	on a complete nonsingular curve (Hartshorne Cor. II.6.10), and the linear-equivalence
25	relation `D ~ D'`.
26	
27	## Status (iter-172 file-skeleton)
28	
29	This file is the **iter-172 Lane C** file-skeleton: each declaration carries the
30	intended signature (matching the blueprint `\lean{...}` pin) with a `sorry` body.
31	The bodies are iter-173+ work after the sibling chapters `RR.2`

37	1. `Scheme.WeilDivisor` — free abelian group on prime divisors (Definition).
38	2. `Scheme.RationalMap.order` — order of a rational function along a prime divisor.
39	3. `Scheme.WeilDivisor.ofClosedPoint` — Weil divisor associated to a closed point.
40	4. `Scheme.WeilDivisor.degree` — degree map over an algebraically closed base.
41	5. `Scheme.WeilDivisor.degree_hom` — degree is a group homomorphism (Theorem).
42	6. `Scheme.WeilDivisor.principal` — principal divisor of a rational function.
43	7. `Scheme.WeilDivisor.principal_hom` — `div` is a group homomorphism (Theorem).
