61	namespace AlgebraicGeometry
62	
63	/-! ## §1. Codim-1 cycle group / Weil divisor group
64	
65	Hartshorne's condition `(*)` (II §6, p. 130) is: `X` is a Noetherian integral
66	separated scheme that is regular in codimension one. Under `(*)`, prime divisors
67	are closed integral subschemes of codimension one, and the local ring at the
68	generic point of a prime divisor is a DVR with quotient field the function field
69	`K(X)`.
70	
71	A prime divisor is encoded by its **generic point** together with the
72	codimension-one witness `Order.coheight point = 1`: the data field `point : X`
73	selects the generic point of the closed integral subscheme, and the predicate
74	field `coheight` enforces codimension one in the specialisation preorder
75	(Hartshorne II §6 p. 130; blueprint pin `def:prime_divisor`). Integrality of the
76	closure is automatic, so no separate integrality witness is needed.
77	-/
78	
79	/-- A **prime divisor** on a scheme `X`: a closed integral subscheme of codimension
80	one, encoded (for a scheme satisfying Hartshorne's condition `(*)`) by its generic
81	point. On a curve, prime divisors correspond bijectively to closed points.
82	
83	The codimension-one witness is the predicate `Order.coheight point = 1` on the
84	specialisation preorder of `X.carrier` (Mathlib's convention is
85	`x ≤ y ↔ y ⤳ x`, so the generic point is the unique maximal element and a
86	generic point of a codim-1 closed integral subscheme has a length-one chain
87	above it). The closure `{point}̄` is automatically irreducible (so the reduced
88	induced subscheme structure is automatically integral); hence no separate
89	integrality field is needed.
90	
91	Blueprint reference: `def:prime_divisor` / `def:codim1_cycles` (Hartshorne II §6
92	p. 130; Stacks 02RW). -/
93	structure Scheme.PrimeDivisor (X : Scheme.{u}) where
94	  /-- The generic point of the closed integral subscheme. -/
95	  point : X
96	  /-- Codimension-1 witness: `point` has coheight `1` in the specialisation
97	  preorder on `X.carrier`. Per iter-173 `wd-spec-refine` (`def:prime_divisor`). -/
98	  coheight : Order.coheight point = 1
99	
100	/-- The **Weil divisor group** of a scheme `X` satisfying Hartshorne's condition
101	`(*)`: the free abelian group on the set of prime divisors of `X`,
102	`Div(X) = ⨁_{Y prime divisor of X} ℤ · Y`. An element `D = Σ nᵢ · Yᵢ` with finitely
103	many nonzero coefficients is a **Weil divisor**; if all `nᵢ ≥ 0` it is **effective**.
104	
105	Blueprint reference: `def:codim1_cycles` (Hartshorne II §6 p. 130). -/
106	def Scheme.WeilDivisor (X : Scheme.{u}) : Type u := X.PrimeDivisor →₀ ℤ
107	
108	namespace Scheme.WeilDivisor
109	
110	noncomputable instance (X : Scheme.{u}) : AddCommGroup X.WeilDivisor :=
111	  inferInstanceAs (AddCommGroup (X.PrimeDivisor →₀ ℤ))
112	
113	instance (X : Scheme.{u}) : Inhabited X.WeilDivisor :=
114	  inferInstanceAs (Inhabited (X.PrimeDivisor →₀ ℤ))
115	
116	end Scheme.WeilDivisor
117	
118	/-! ## §2. Order of a rational function at a prime divisor
119	
120	For a scheme `X` satisfying `(*)`, every prime divisor `Y` carries a discrete
121	valuation `v_Y` on the function field `K(X)`. The order of a nonzero rational
122	function `f ∈ K(X)^×` along `Y` is the integer `ord_Y(f) := v_Y(f)`. -/
123	
124	namespace Scheme.RationalMap
125	
126	/-- **Order of a nonzero rational function `f ∈ K(X)^×` along a prime divisor `Y`.**
127	
128	For `X` satisfying Hartshorne's `(*)`, the local ring `O_{X,η}` at the generic
129	point `η` of `Y` is a discrete valuation ring with quotient field the function
130	field `K(X)`, and `ord_Y(f) = v_Y(f) ∈ ℤ` is the value of the associated normalised
131	discrete valuation on `f`.
132	
133	On a smooth proper curve `C` over `k̄`, every closed point `P ∈ C` is a prime
134	divisor and `ord_P(f) = v_P(f)` is the standard DVR valuation at `P`.
135	
136	Blueprint reference: `def:order_at_point` (Hartshorne II §6 pp. 130–131; Stacks 02ME).
137	
138	iter-176 body (per analogist `dvr-rationalmap-order`): the body uses Mathlib's
139	`Ring.ordFrac` (the canonical `K →*₀ ℤᵐ⁰` monoid-with-zero hom from
140	`Mathlib.RingTheory.OrderOfVanishing.Basic`, Stacks `02MD`) on the stalk
141	`X.presheaf.stalk Y.point`, then projects through `WithZero.log : ℤᵐ⁰ → ℤ`
142	(the canonical projection with junk-on-zero, `Mathlib.Algebra.GroupWithZero.WithZero`).
143	On `f = 0` this gives `order Y 0 = 0` (junk convention from `WithZero.log_zero`).
144	
145	The required Mathlib typeclasses on the stalk are:
146	- `IsNoetherianRing` — from `[IsLocallyNoetherian X]`.
147	- `IsDomain` ⟹ `Nontrivial` — from `[IsIntegral X]`.
148	- `IsFractionRing (X.presheaf.stalk Y.point) X.functionField` — from `[IsIntegral X]`.
149	- `Ring.KrullDimLE 1 (X.presheaf.stalk Y.point)` — threaded explicitly (the
150	  topological-coheight-to-Krull-dim bridge `Order.coheight Y.point = 1 ⟹
151	  Ring.KrullDimLE 1 (X.presheaf.stalk Y.point)` is a Mathlib-upstream-pending
152	  gap; see Stacks `02IZ` / `005X`). -/
153	noncomputable def order {X : Scheme.{u}} [IsIntegral X]
154	    [IsLocallyNoetherian X] (Y : X.PrimeDivisor)
155	    [Ring.KrullDimLE 1 (X.presheaf.stalk Y.point)]
156	    (f : X.functionField) : ℤ :=
157	  WithZero.log (Ring.ordFrac (X.presheaf.stalk Y.point) f)
158	
159	end Scheme.RationalMap
160	
161	/-! ### Regular-in-codimension-one bridge class
162	
163	Hartshorne's condition `(*)` includes "regular in codimension one", i.e.\ every
164	prime divisor `Y` of `X` has a DVR stalk `O_{X,Y.point}`. Mathlib has no direct
165	typeclass for this; we package it as the project-bespoke class
166	`Scheme.IsRegularInCodimensionOne`, with an instance synthesising the per-`Y`
167	Krull-dim-≤-1 condition required by `Scheme.RationalMap.order`. Blueprint pin
168	("Iter-173+ may introduce a `Scheme.IsRegularInCodimensionOne` predicate to
169	abbreviate this"; chapter `RiemannRoch_WeilDivisor.tex` §2 "Standing hypothesis
170	`(*)` in the Lean encoding"). -/
171	
172	/-- Project-bespoke class encoding Hartshorne's "regular in codimension one"
173	clause of `(*)`: every prime divisor `Y` of `X` has a DVR stalk
174	`O_{X,Y.point}`. The `[IsIntegral X]` precondition makes
175	`IsDomain (X.presheaf.stalk Y.point)` available so that the
176	`IsDiscreteValuationRing` predicate is well-formed
177	(`AlgebraicGeometry.instIsDomainCarrierStalkCommRingCatPresheafOfIsIntegral`). -/
178	class Scheme.IsRegularInCodimensionOne (X : Scheme.{u}) [IsIntegral X] : Prop where
179	  /-- The defining content: every prime divisor's stalk is a discrete valuation
180	  ring. (This is the precise content of Hartshorne's `(*)`; the weaker
181	  `Ring.KrullDimLE 1` derives via the `IsDiscreteValuationRing` ⟹
182	  `IsPrincipalIdealRing` ⟹ `Ring.KrullDimLE 1` chain via the bridge
183	  instance below.) -/
184	  out : ∀ Y : Scheme.PrimeDivisor X, IsDiscreteValuationRing (X.presheaf.stalk Y.point)
185	
186	/-- Bridge instance: from `[Scheme.IsRegularInCodimensionOne X]`, typeclass
187	synthesis can derive `IsDiscreteValuationRing (X.presheaf.stalk Y.point)` for
188	every prime divisor `Y`. -/
189	instance Scheme.IsRegularInCodimensionOne.instIsDiscreteValuationRingStalk
190	    {X : Scheme.{u}} [IsIntegral X] [Scheme.IsRegularInCodimensionOne X]
191	    (Y : Scheme.PrimeDivisor X) :
192	    IsDiscreteValuationRing (X.presheaf.stalk Y.point) :=
193	  Scheme.IsRegularInCodimensionOne.out Y
194	
195	/-- Bridge instance: from `[Scheme.IsRegularInCodimensionOne X]`, typeclass
196	synthesis can derive `Ring.KrullDimLE 1 (X.presheaf.stalk Y.point)` for every
197	prime divisor `Y`, via the `IsDiscreteValuationRing` ⟹ `IsPrincipalIdealRing`
198	⟹ `Ring.KrullDimLE 1` chain (Mathlib's `IsDiscreteValuationRing.toIsPrincipalIdealRing`
199	+ `IsPrincipalIdealRing.krullDimLE_one`). -/
200	instance Scheme.IsRegularInCodimensionOne.instKrullDimLEStalk
201	    {X : Scheme.{u}} [IsIntegral X] [Scheme.IsRegularInCodimensionOne X]
202	    (Y : Scheme.PrimeDivisor X) :
203	    Ring.KrullDimLE 1 (X.presheaf.stalk Y.point) :=
204	  haveI : IsDiscreteValuationRing (X.presheaf.stalk Y.point) :=
205	    Scheme.IsRegularInCodimensionOne.out Y
206	  haveI : IsPrincipalIdealRing (X.presheaf.stalk Y.point) :=
207	    IsDiscreteValuationRing.toIsPrincipalIdealRing
208	  IsPrincipalIdealRing.krullDimLE_one _
209	
210	/-- **Hartshorne II.6.1**: for a nonzero rational function `f` on a Noetherian
211	integral scheme `X` satisfying `(*)`, the order function `Y ↦ ord_Y(f)` is
212	nonzero at only finitely many prime divisors `Y`. This is the well-definedness
213	side condition for `Scheme.WeilDivisor.principal`.
214	
215	iter-177 status: this packages Hartshorne's Lemma 6.1, which Mathlib does not
216	ship. The body is a Mathlib-upstream-pending gap (Stacks tag `02RV` — for a
217	nonzero element `f ∈ K(X)^×` of a Noetherian integral scheme, only finitely
218	many height-one primes can divide either numerator or denominator); the proof
219	factors through `IsLocallyNoetherian X` + the principal-ideal generation of
220	height-1 primes + the finite irreducible-component decomposition of
221	`V(f₀) ∪ V(f∞)`. The chapter pins this as a separate sub-build deferral
222	(`RiemannRoch_WeilDivisor.tex` §5).
223	
224	The statement is generic in `f` (no `f ≠ 0` hypothesis is threaded): on
225	`f = 0` the function `Y ↦ ord_Y(0) = WithZero.log 0 = 0` has empty support,
226	which is finite, so the conclusion holds vacuously. -/
227	private theorem rationalMap_order_finite_support {X : Scheme.{u}} [IsIntegral X]
228	    [IsLocallyNoetherian X] [Scheme.IsRegularInCodimensionOne X]
229	    (f : X.functionField) :
230	    (Function.support (fun Y : X.PrimeDivisor =>
231	      Scheme.RationalMap.order Y f)).Finite := by
232	  -- **iter-192 case split + f = 0 branch closed axiom-clean.**
233	  --
234	  -- Case 1 (f = 0): the order function evaluates to
235	  -- `WithZero.log (Ring.ordFrac _ 0) = WithZero.log 0 = 0` at every
236	  -- prime divisor, so the support is empty (finite vacuously).
237	  by_cases hf : f = 0
238	  · subst hf
239	    convert Set.finite_empty
240	    ext Y
241	    simp only [Function.mem_support, ne_eq, Set.mem_empty_iff_false, iff_false,
242	      Decidable.not_not, Scheme.RationalMap.order, map_zero, WithZero.log_zero]
243	  -- Case 2 (f ≠ 0): genuinely needs Hartshorne II.6.1 (Stacks 02RV).
244	  -- The proof for nonzero `f` requires the substrate
245	  -- "for a Noetherian integral scheme, only finitely many height-1
246	  -- primes can divide the numerator or denominator of a nonzero rational
247	  -- function" — a Mathlib-pending gap noted in the chapter docstring.
248	  -- Body owed iter-193+; iter-192 closes only the f = 0 vacuous case.
249	  · sorry
250	
251	namespace Scheme.WeilDivisor
252	
253	variable {X : Scheme.{u}}
254	
255	/-! ## §3. Divisor of a closed point on a curve
256	
257	On a smooth proper curve `C` over a field, every closed point `P ∈ C` is a prime
258	divisor (it is closed, integral, of codimension one in the one-dimensional integral
259	scheme `C`). The associated Weil divisor is `[P] = 1 · P ∈ Div(C)`. -/
260	
261	/-- **The Weil divisor associated to a closed point `P` on a curve.** The element
262	`[P] := 1 · P ∈ Div(C)`, i.e. the prime divisor `P` with multiplicity one.
263	
264	For a smooth proper curve every closed point is automatically a prime divisor (it
265	is a codimension-one integral closed subscheme of the one-dimensional integral
266	scheme `C`); conversely every prime divisor on a curve is of this form, so an
267	arbitrary divisor on `C` is a finite formal `ℤ`-linear combination
268	`Σ nᵢ · [Pᵢ]` of closed points.
269	
270	Blueprint reference: `def:divisor_closed_point` (Hartshorne II §6 p. 137).
271	
272	iter-174 body: the function is junk-defined outside its intended scope. We
273	case-split on `Order.coheight P = 1` (the codimension-one witness of
274	`PrimeDivisor`). On the branch where the equality holds — automatic for a
275	closed point on a one-dimensional integral scheme — we promote `P` to a
276	`PrimeDivisor` via the witness and return `Finsupp.single ⟨P, h⟩ 1`, i.e.
277	the prime divisor `P` with multiplicity one. On the off-branch (junk regime)
278	we return the zero divisor. The blueprint pins the well-definedness argument
279	"`IsClosed {P}` on a one-dimensional integral scheme ⟹ `coheight P = 1`" as a
280	separate threadable hypothesis at the call site (chapter L330–L340 "Lean
281	signature scope"); see `ofClosedPoint_eq_single` for the equation in the
282	hypothesised regime. -/
283	noncomputable def ofClosedPoint {C : Scheme.{u}} (P : C)
284	    (_hP : IsClosed ({P} : Set C)) : C.WeilDivisor :=
285	  if h : Order.coheight P = 1 then Finsupp.single ⟨P, h⟩ 1 else 0
286	
287	/-- In the hypothesised regime where the closed point `P` has coheight one
288	(the codim-1 condition automatic for a closed point of a one-dimensional
289	integral scheme), `ofClosedPoint P hP` is the prime divisor `P` with
290	multiplicity one. -/
291	lemma ofClosedPoint_eq_single {C : Scheme.{u}} (P : C)
292	    (hP : IsClosed ({P} : Set C)) (h : Order.coheight P = 1) :
293	    ofClosedPoint P hP = Finsupp.single ⟨P, h⟩ 1 := by
294	  simp [ofClosedPoint, h]
295	
296	/-- Off-branch: outside the codim-1 regime, `ofClosedPoint` is junk-defined as
297	the zero divisor. (Only relevant when the user supplies a "closed point" that
298	does not have coheight one in the ambient scheme — e.g. a generic point or a
299	codim-≥2 point on a higher-dimensional scheme.) -/
300	lemma ofClosedPoint_eq_zero {C : Scheme.{u}} (P : C)
301	    (hP : IsClosed ({P} : Set C)) (h : Order.coheight P ≠ 1) :
302	    ofClosedPoint P hP = 0 := by
303	  simp [ofClosedPoint, h]
304	
305	/-! ## §4. Degree of a divisor over an algebraically closed base -/
306	
307	/-- **Degree of a Weil divisor on a smooth proper curve over `k̄`.**
308	
309	Over an algebraically closed field `k̄`, every closed point of a smooth proper
310	curve `C` has residue field `k̄`, so each prime divisor `[P]` contributes degree
311	one to the sum, and `deg(D) := Σᵢ nᵢ` is the sum of the integer coefficients of
312	the formal sum `D = Σ nᵢ · [Pᵢ]`.
313	
314	(Over a general field `k` one weights by the residue-field degrees
315	`Σᵢ nᵢ · [κ(Pᵢ) : k]` to recover the geometric degree; the project's RR bridge
316	needs only the `k̄` specialisation.)
317	
318	Blueprint reference: `def:divisor_degree` (Hartshorne II §6 p. 137; Stacks 0BE0).
319	
320	Implementation: `Finsupp.sum D (fun _ n => n)` is the sum of all coefficients of
321	the finitely supported function representing `D`. -/
322	noncomputable def degree (D : X.WeilDivisor) : ℤ :=
323	  (D : X.PrimeDivisor →₀ ℤ).sum (fun _ n => n)
324	
325	/-- **The degree map is a group homomorphism `Div(C) → ℤ`.**
326	
327	`deg(D₁ + D₂) = deg(D₁) + deg(D₂)`, `deg(-D) = -deg(D)`, `deg(0) = 0`. Bundled
328	as an `AddMonoidHom` for downstream use (the linear-equivalence quotient
329	`Cl(C) := Div(C) / im(div)` will inherit a `deg : Cl(C) → ℤ` from this).
330	
331	Blueprint reference: `thm:divisor_degree_hom` (immediate from `def:divisor_degree`).
332	
333	Implementation: built from `Finsupp.liftAddHom (fun _ ↦ AddMonoidHom.id ℤ)`, the
334	generic Mathlib packaging that lifts a family of `AddMonoidHom`s indexed by the
335	support into a single `AddMonoidHom` on the finsupp. Unfolds to
336	`D.sum (fun _ z ↦ z) = degree D` (see `degree_hom_apply` below). -/
337	noncomputable def degree_hom : X.WeilDivisor →+ ℤ :=
338	  Finsupp.liftAddHom (fun _ ↦ AddMonoidHom.id ℤ)
339	
340	@[simp]
341	lemma degree_hom_apply (D : X.WeilDivisor) : degree_hom D = degree D :=
342	  Finsupp.liftAddHom_apply (α := X.PrimeDivisor) (M := ℤ) (N := ℤ)
343	    (fun _ ↦ AddMonoidHom.id ℤ) D
344	
345	/-- **Degree of the zero divisor is zero.** A direct consequence of
346	`degree_hom` being an `AddMonoidHom`. -/
347	@[simp]
348	lemma degree_zero : degree (0 : X.WeilDivisor) = 0 := by
349	  rw [← degree_hom_apply]; exact map_zero _
350	
351	/-- **Degree is additive.** A direct consequence of `degree_hom` being an
352	`AddMonoidHom`. -/
353	lemma degree_add (D₁ D₂ : X.WeilDivisor) :
354	    degree (D₁ + D₂) = degree D₁ + degree D₂ := by
355	  rw [← degree_hom_apply, ← degree_hom_apply, ← degree_hom_apply]
356	  exact map_add _ _ _
357	
358	/-! ## §5. Principal divisors -/
359	
360	/-- **The principal divisor of a nonzero rational function `f ∈ K(X)^×`.**
361	
362	By Hartshorne's Lemma 6.1, `ord_Y(f) = 0` for all but finitely many prime
363	divisors `Y`, so the formal sum
364	`div(f) := Σ_{Y prime divisor} ord_Y(f) · Y ∈ Div(X)`
365	has finite support and is a well-defined Weil divisor.
366	
367	On a smooth proper curve `C` over `k̄`, this specialises to
368	`div(f) = Σ_{P closed point} ord_P(f) · [P]`.
369	
370	Blueprint reference: `def:principal_divisor` (Hartshorne II §6 Lemma 6.1 +
371	following definition, p. 131).
372	
373	iter-177 body: the construction uses `Finsupp.ofSupportFinite` with the
374	finite-support witness `rationalMap_order_finite_support`. The latter is a
375	private theorem packaging Hartshorne 6.1; its body is a Mathlib-pending gap
376	(see chapter `RiemannRoch_WeilDivisor.tex` §5 sub-build note) and is left as
377	a `sorry` for an iter-178+ Mathlib-upstream PR. -/
378	noncomputable def principal [IsIntegral X] [IsLocallyNoetherian X]
379	    [Scheme.IsRegularInCodimensionOne X] (f : X.functionField)
380	    (_hf : f ≠ 0) : X.WeilDivisor :=
381	  Finsupp.ofSupportFinite
382	    (fun Y : X.PrimeDivisor => Scheme.RationalMap.order Y f)
383	    (rationalMap_order_finite_support f)
384	
385	/-- **The coefficient of `principal f hf` at a prime divisor `Y` is the order of
386	`f` along `Y`.** This is the basic structural unfolding of the
387	`Finsupp.ofSupportFinite` packaging in `principal`; one-line via
388	`Finsupp.ofSupportFinite_coe` from `Mathlib.Data.Finsupp.Defs`.
389	
390	iter-193 substrate helper for the Lane I body close
391	(`degree_positivePart_principal_eq_finrank`). -/
392	lemma principal_apply [IsIntegral X] [IsLocallyNoetherian X]
393	    [Scheme.IsRegularInCodimensionOne X] (f : X.functionField) (hf : f ≠ 0)
394	    (Y : X.PrimeDivisor) :
395	    (show (X.PrimeDivisor →₀ ℤ) from principal f hf) Y =
396	      Scheme.RationalMap.order Y f := by
397	  change (Finsupp.ofSupportFinite
398	      (fun Y : X.PrimeDivisor => Scheme.RationalMap.order Y f)
399	      (rationalMap_order_finite_support f)) Y = _
400	  rw [Finsupp.ofSupportFinite_coe]
401	
402	/-- **`Scheme.RationalMap.order Y 1 = 0`** — the order of the constant
403	function `1` is `0` at every prime divisor. Direct from
404	`map_one` of `Ring.ordFrac` + `WithZero.log_one`.
405	
406	iter-193 substrate helper for further structural results
407	(`principal_one_eq_zero` and similar). -/
408	@[simp]
409	lemma _root_.AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.RationalMap.order_one
410	    {X : Scheme.{u}} [IsIntegral X] [IsLocallyNoetherian X]
411	    (Y : X.PrimeDivisor)
412	    [Ring.KrullDimLE 1 (X.presheaf.stalk Y.point)] :
413	    Scheme.RationalMap.order Y (1 : X.functionField) = 0 := by
414	  unfold Scheme.RationalMap.order
415	  rw [map_one, WithZero.log_one]
416	
417	/-- **The principal divisor of the constant function `1` is the zero
418	divisor.** A direct consequence of `Scheme.RationalMap.order_one`. -/
419	@[simp]
420	lemma principal_one [IsIntegral X] [IsLocallyNoetherian X]
421	    [Scheme.IsRegularInCodimensionOne X] :
422	    principal (1 : X.functionField) one_ne_zero = 0 := by
423	  change (_ : X.PrimeDivisor →₀ ℤ) = (0 : X.PrimeDivisor →₀ ℤ)
424	  apply Finsupp.ext
425	  intro Y
426	  rw [principal_apply]
427	  exact Scheme.RationalMap.order_one Y
428	
429	/-- **The principal-divisor map is a group homomorphism `K(X)^× → Div(X)`.**
430	
431	Concretely `div(fg) = div(f) + div(g)`, `div(f⁻¹) = -div(f)`, `div(1) = 0`. Bundled
432	as a `MonoidHom` from the multiplicative units of `K(X)` to `Multiplicative (Div(X))`
433	(equivalently: an additive map `(K(X)^×, ·) → (Div(X), +)`).
434	
435	Blueprint reference: `thm:principal_hom` (Hartshorne II §6 p. 131).
436	
437	iter-177 body: closes coordinate-wise from the DVR identities
438	`v_Y(fg) = v_Y(f) + v_Y(g)`, `v_Y(1) = 0`. The per-`Y` identities live in
439	`Scheme.RationalMap.order` via `Ring.ordFrac` (a `K →*₀ ℤᵐ⁰` monoid-with-zero
440	hom) and `WithZero.log_mul` / `WithZero.log_one`. -/
441	noncomputable def principal_hom [IsIntegral X] [IsLocallyNoetherian X]
442	    [Scheme.IsRegularInCodimensionOne X] :
443	    (X.functionField)ˣ →* Multiplicative X.WeilDivisor where
444	  toFun u :=
445	    Multiplicative.ofAdd (principal (↑u : X.functionField) (Units.ne_zero u))
446	  map_one' := by
447	    -- Goal: Multiplicative.ofAdd (principal ↑1 _) = 1.
448	    -- Use `← ofAdd_zero` to rewrite RHS as `Multiplicative.ofAdd 0`,
449	    -- then `congr 1` reduces to `principal ↑1 _ = 0` (Finsupp equality),
450	    -- and `Finsupp.ext` peels to the per-`Y` coordinate identity, which
451	    -- closes by unfolding `order` to `WithZero.log (Ring.ordFrac _ 1) = 0`
452	    -- via `map_one` and `WithZero.log_one`.
453	    rw [← ofAdd_zero]
454	    congr 1
455	    apply Finsupp.ext
456	    intro Y
457	    change Scheme.RationalMap.order Y
458	        (((1 : (X.functionField)ˣ) : X.functionField)) = 0
459	    rw [Units.val_one]
460	    unfold Scheme.RationalMap.order
461	    rw [map_one, WithZero.log_one]
462	  map_mul' u v := by
463	    -- Goal: Multiplicative.ofAdd (principal ↑(u*v) _) =
464	    --       Multiplicative.ofAdd (principal ↑u _) * Multiplicative.ofAdd (principal ↑v _).
465	    -- Use `← ofAdd_add` to rewrite RHS, then `congr 1` reduces to
466	    -- `principal ↑(u*v) _ = principal ↑u _ + principal ↑v _` (Finsupp
467	    -- equality), and `Finsupp.ext` peels to the per-`Y` coordinate
468	    -- identity `order Y (uv) = order Y u + order Y v`, which closes by
469	    -- `Ring.ordFrac (uv) = Ring.ordFrac u * Ring.ordFrac v` (`map_mul`)
470	    -- and `WithZero.log_mul` (the nonzero hypotheses come from
471	    -- `Units.ne_zero` and `map_ne_zero`).
472	    rw [← ofAdd_add]
473	    congr 1
474	    apply Finsupp.ext
475	    intro Y
476	    change Scheme.RationalMap.order Y ((↑(u * v) : X.functionField))
477	      = Scheme.RationalMap.order Y (↑u : X.functionField)
478	      + Scheme.RationalMap.order Y (↑v : X.functionField)
479	    rw [Units.val_mul]
480	    unfold Scheme.RationalMap.order
481	    rw [map_mul]
482	    exact WithZero.log_mul
483	      ((map_ne_zero _).mpr (Units.ne_zero u))
484	      ((map_ne_zero _).mpr (Units.ne_zero v))
485	
486	/-- **Principal divisors on a complete nonsingular curve have degree zero**
487	(Hartshorne Corollary II.6.10, Stacks 0BE3).
488	
489	For every nonzero rational function `f ∈ K(C)^×` on a smooth proper curve `C`
490	over an algebraically closed field `k̄`,
491	`deg(div(f)) = 0 ∈ ℤ`.
492	
493	Blueprint reference: `thm:principal_deg_zero` (Hartshorne II.6 Cor. 6.10 p. 138).
494	
495	The proof (Hartshorne 6.10): if `f ∈ k̄` is constant then `div(f) = 0` and the
496	claim is trivial. Otherwise the inclusion `k̄(f) ⊂ K(C)` exhibits `K(C)` as a
497	finite extension of `k̄(f) ≅ k̄(t)`, so the corresponding morphism
498	`φ : C → ℙ¹_{k̄}` is finite, `div(f) = φ^*([0] - [∞])`, and pullback along a
499	finite morphism multiplies degree by `deg(φ)`. Two auxiliary sub-lemmas
500	(finite morphism induced by a non-constant rational function; multiplicativity
501	of degree under finite pullback, Hartshorne II.6.9) are deferred to follow-up
502	iters of `RR.1`. -/
503	theorem principal_degree_zero {kbar : Type u} [Field kbar] [IsAlgClosed kbar]
504	    (C : Over (Spec (.of kbar))) [IsProper C.hom]
505	    [SmoothOfRelativeDimension 1 C.hom] [GeometricallyIrreducible C.hom]
506	    [IsIntegral C.left] [IsLocallyNoetherian C.left]
507	    [Scheme.IsRegularInCodimensionOne C.left]
508	    (f : C.left.functionField) (hf : f ≠ 0) :
509	    degree (principal f hf) = 0 := by
510	  -- Hartshorne II.6.10 (iter-178 partial). Case-split on whether every
511	  -- prime-divisor order of `f` vanishes. On a complete nonsingular curve,
512	  -- this case split exactly recovers Hartshorne's constant-vs-non-constant
513	  -- split: `(∀ Y, ord_Y f = 0)` ⟺ `f ∈ k̄ \ {0}` (a nowhere-vanishing
514	  -- rational function on a complete curve is constant; that direction is
515	  -- Hartshorne II.6.10's "if `f ∈ k̄` then `(f) = 0`").
516	  by_cases hconst : ∀ Y : C.left.PrimeDivisor, Scheme.RationalMap.order Y f = 0
517	  · -- Constant branch: every order vanishes ⟹ `principal f hf = 0` ⟹
518	    -- `degree (principal f hf) = degree 0 = 0`.
519	    have hprincipal_zero : principal f hf = 0 := by
520	      apply Finsupp.ext
521	      intro Y
522	      change Scheme.RationalMap.order Y f = 0
523	      exact hconst Y
524	    rw [hprincipal_zero]
525	    unfold degree
526	    exact Finsupp.sum_zero_index
527	  · -- Non-constant branch (Hartshorne II.6.10 `f ∉ k̄`): the inclusion
528	    -- `k̄(f) ⊂ K(C)` defines a finite morphism `φ : C → ℙ¹_{k̄}` via the
529	    -- function-field-determines-curve correspondence (Hartshorne I.6.12),
530	    -- and `principal f hf = φ^*([0] - [∞])`. Pullback along a finite
531	    -- morphism of curves multiplies degrees by `deg(φ)` (Hartshorne
532	    -- II.6.9), so `deg(principal f hf) = deg(φ) · deg([0] - [∞]) =
533	    -- deg(φ) · 0 = 0`.
534	    -- This branch is gated on (i) the `φ : C → ℙ¹` construction (Lane 5
535	    -- `RationalCurveIso.lean:morphismToP1OfGlobalSections`, iter-178+),
536	    -- and (ii) the degree-multiplicativity-under-finite-pullback bridge
537	    -- (Hartshorne II.6.9, Mathlib gap).
538	    sorry
539	
540	/-! ## §6. Positive part of a Weil divisor
541	
542	Iter-190 plan-phase Lane I Pin 2 corrective substrate. The carrier
543	`X.WeilDivisor = X.PrimeDivisor →₀ ℤ` is a finitely supported integer-valued
544	function on prime divisors; the *positive part* `(D)_0` is obtained by
545	replacing each coefficient `n_Y` with `max(n_Y, 0)`. Equivalently this is
546	the lattice join `D ⊔ 0` in the pointwise lattice structure (the
547	`semilatticeSup` instance on `Finsupp` is noncomputable). The complementary
548	*negative part* `(D)_∞ := (-D)_0` then gives the canonical decomposition
549	`D = (D)_0 - (D)_∞` into a difference of effective divisors.
550	
551	Blueprint reference: `def:WeilDivisor_positivePart` /
552	`lem:degree_positivePart_principal_eq_finrank` of
553	`RiemannRoch_WeilDivisor.tex` §6 (iter-190 plan-phase additions).
554	-/
555	
556	/-- **Positive part of a Weil divisor**.
557	
558	`positivePart D` is the divisor obtained from `D` by replacing each
559	coefficient `n_Y` with `max(n_Y, 0)` (equivalently `D ⊔ 0` in the
560	pointwise lattice structure on `Finsupp`). For `D = div(f)` on a smooth
561	proper curve `C`, this recovers the divisor-of-zeros `(f)_0` — the
562	formal sum of the zeros of `f` with their multiplicities.
563	
564	The complementary `negativePart D := positivePart (-D)` then gives
565	`D = positivePart D - negativePart D` as a difference of effective
566	divisors.
567	
568	Implementation: `Finsupp.mapRange (fun n : ℤ => n ⊔ 0) (by simp)`, the
569	generic Mathlib packaging that maps a finsupp's coefficients through a
570	zero-preserving function. (The `D ⊔ 0` lattice form is equivalent but
571	requires the noncomputable `Finsupp.semilatticeSup` synthesis on
572	`PrimeDivisor →₀ ℤ`; the explicit `mapRange` form is more transparent
573	to typeclass synthesis.)
574	
575	Blueprint reference: `def:WeilDivisor_positivePart` (project-bespoke;
576	chapter `RiemannRoch_WeilDivisor.tex` §6, Hartshorne II.6.10 phrasing). -/
577	noncomputable def positivePart (D : X.WeilDivisor) : X.WeilDivisor :=
578	  Finsupp.mapRange (fun n : ℤ => n ⊔ 0) (by simp) D
579	
580	/-- The positive part of the zero divisor is zero. -/
581	@[simp]
582	lemma positivePart_zero : positivePart (0 : X.WeilDivisor) = 0 := by
583	  change Finsupp.mapRange (fun n : ℤ => n ⊔ 0) (by simp)
584	    (0 : X.PrimeDivisor →₀ ℤ) = (0 : X.PrimeDivisor →₀ ℤ)
585	  exact Finsupp.mapRange_zero
586	
587	/-- **Degree of positive part as a sum of capped coefficients.** A purely
588	symbolic Mathlib manipulation: unfolding `positivePart`
589	(= `Finsupp.mapRange (max · 0)`) and `degree` (= `Finsupp.sum (fun _ n => n)`)
590	identifies `degree (positivePart D)` with the sum of `max (D Y) 0` over
591	the support of `D`.
592	
593	This is the iter-192 structural-reduction helper consumed by
594	`degree_positivePart_principal_eq_finrank` below. The proof is one line
595	via `Finsupp.sum_mapRange_index` from
596	`Mathlib.Algebra.BigOperators.Finsupp.Basic`. -/
597	lemma degree_positivePart_eq_sum_max (D : X.WeilDivisor) :
598	    degree (positivePart D) = D.sum (fun _ n => max n 0) := by
599	  unfold positivePart degree
600	  exact Finsupp.sum_mapRange_index (h := fun _ b => b) (by intro _; rfl)
601	
602	/-- **Positive part of a `Finsupp.single`.** Pointwise extraction of the
603	mapRange definition of `positivePart`: for a one-point-supported Weil divisor
604	`Finsupp.single Y n`, the positive part is `Finsupp.single Y (max n 0)`.
605	A direct consequence of `Finsupp.mapRange_single` (with `max 0 0 = 0` as
606	the zero-preservation witness).
607	
608	iter-193 substrate helper paving the way for the Lane I body close. -/
609	@[simp]
610	lemma positivePart_single (Y : X.PrimeDivisor) (n : ℤ) :
611	    positivePart (Finsupp.single Y n : X.WeilDivisor) =
612	      Finsupp.single Y (max n 0) := by
613	  change Finsupp.mapRange (fun n : ℤ => n ⊔ 0) (by simp)
614	      (Finsupp.single Y n) = _
615	  rw [Finsupp.mapRange_single]
616	
617	/-- **Degree of a `Finsupp.single` Weil divisor.** The degree of the
618	one-point-supported Weil divisor `Finsupp.single Y n` is `n`. A direct
619	consequence of `Finsupp.sum_single_index`.
620	
621	iter-193 substrate helper for `degree_positivePart_principal_eq_finrank`. -/
622	@[simp]
623	lemma degree_single (Y : X.PrimeDivisor) (n : ℤ) :
624	    degree (Finsupp.single Y n : X.WeilDivisor) = n := by
625	  unfold degree
626	  change (Finsupp.single Y n : X.PrimeDivisor →₀ ℤ).sum (fun _ n => n) = n
627	  exact Finsupp.sum_single_index rfl
628	
629	/-- **Sum-over-prime-divisors lower bound via a single contributing point.**
630	If `f` has order `1` at some prime divisor `Y₀`, then the degree of the positive
631	part of `principal f hf` is at least `1`.
632	
633	iter-193 substrate helper consumed by `degree_positivePart_principal_eq_finrank`:
634	it formalises Step 2 of the Hartshorne II.6.9 recipe (`hlp` produces
635	the local-parameter prime divisor `Y₀` with coefficient `1`). -/
636	lemma one_le_degree_positivePart_principal_of_order_one
637	    [IsIntegral X] [IsLocallyNoetherian X]
638	    [Scheme.IsRegularInCodimensionOne X]
639	    (f : X.functionField) (hf : f ≠ 0) (Y₀ : X.PrimeDivisor)
640	    (h₀ : Scheme.RationalMap.order Y₀ f = 1) :
641	    1 ≤ degree (positivePart (principal f hf)) := by
642	  classical
643	  rw [degree_positivePart_eq_sum_max]
644	  -- The sum is over a finset; isolate the Y₀ contribution.
645	  have hY₀_supp :
646	      Y₀ ∈ (show (X.PrimeDivisor →₀ ℤ) from principal f hf).support := by
647	    rw [Finsupp.mem_support_iff]
648	    rw [principal_apply]
649	    rw [h₀]
650	    exact one_ne_zero
651	  -- For the sum to be ≥ 1, isolate the Y₀ term (= 1) and the rest (≥ 0).
652	  have h_split :
653	      (show (X.PrimeDivisor →₀ ℤ) from principal f hf).sum
654	          (fun _ n => max n 0) =
655	        max ((show (X.PrimeDivisor →₀ ℤ) from principal f hf) Y₀) 0 +
656	          ((show (X.PrimeDivisor →₀ ℤ) from principal f hf).support.erase Y₀).sum
657	            (fun Y => max
658	              ((show (X.PrimeDivisor →₀ ℤ) from principal f hf) Y) 0) := by
659	    rw [show (show (X.PrimeDivisor →₀ ℤ) from principal f hf).sum
660	            (fun _ n => max n 0) =
661	          (show (X.PrimeDivisor →₀ ℤ) from principal f hf).support.sum
662	            (fun Y => max
663	              ((show (X.PrimeDivisor →₀ ℤ) from principal f hf) Y) 0)
664	        from rfl]
665	    rw [Finset.sum_erase_eq_sub hY₀_supp]
666	    ring
667	  rw [h_split]
668	  -- The Y₀ term equals max 1 0 = 1.
669	  have hY₀_val :
670	      (show (X.PrimeDivisor →₀ ℤ) from principal f hf) Y₀ = 1 := by
671	    rw [principal_apply]; exact h₀
672	  rw [hY₀_val]
673	  -- The remainder is a sum of non-negative integers, hence ≥ 0.
674	  have h_nonneg :
675	      0 ≤ ((show (X.PrimeDivisor →₀ ℤ) from principal f hf).support.erase Y₀).sum
676	        (fun Y => max
677	          ((show (X.PrimeDivisor →₀ ℤ) from principal f hf) Y) 0) :=
678	    Finset.sum_nonneg (fun Y _ => le_max_right _ _)
679	  -- Conclude: 1 = max 1 0 ≤ max 1 0 + 0 ≤ LHS + remainder.
680	  have : (max (1 : ℤ) 0) = 1 := by norm_num
681	  rw [this]
682	  linarith

684	/-! ### Iter-194 typed-sorry instance scaffolding for `ProjectiveLineBar kbar`
685	
686	The `degree_positivePart_principal_eq_finrank` theorem below specialises
687	`K = (ProjectiveLineBar kbar).left.functionField` and the uniformiser
688	witness `hLPUnif` quantifies over `(ProjectiveLineBar kbar).left.PrimeDivisor`
689	with `Scheme.RationalMap.order Y₀ t`, which requires the underlying
690	scheme to support the order machinery. The required typeclasses are
691	`IsIntegral`, `IsLocallyNoetherian`, and `Scheme.IsRegularInCodimensionOne`
692	on `(ProjectiveLineBar kbar).left`. These are mathematically true
693	(ProjectiveLineBar is `Proj` of `k̄[X₀, X₁]`, finite type over the field
694	`k̄`, smooth of dimension 1 over `k̄`, hence locally Noetherian, integral,
695	and regular at every point), but Mathlib does not currently ship them as
696	free instances. We declare them as named typed-sorry instances scheduled
697	for iter-194+ closure.
698	-/
699	
700	/-- **Iter-194 axiom-clean closure**: `(ProjectiveLineBar kbar).left` is locally
701	Noetherian. Derivation chain: `(ProjectiveLineBar kbar).hom` is proper (existing
702	`projectiveLineBar_isProper`), hence locally of finite type
703	(`IsProper.toLocallyOfFiniteType`); the base `Spec (.of kbar)` is locally
704	Noetherian because the field `kbar` is Noetherian; locally-finite-type morphism
705	over a locally Noetherian base has locally Noetherian source
706	(`LocallyOfFiniteType.isLocallyNoetherian`). -/
707	instance instIsLocallyNoetherianProjectiveLineBar (kbar : Type u) [Field kbar] :
708	    IsLocallyNoetherian (ProjectiveLineBar kbar).left := by
709	  haveI : IsLocallyNoetherian (Spec (.of kbar)) := inferInstance
710	  haveI : LocallyOfFiniteType (ProjectiveLineBar kbar).hom :=
711	    IsProper.toLocallyOfFiniteType
712	  exact LocallyOfFiniteType.isLocallyNoetherian (ProjectiveLineBar kbar).hom
713	
714	/-- **Typed-sorry instance** (iter-194+ owed):
715	`(ProjectiveLineBar kbar).left` is regular in codimension one (Hartshorne's
716	`(*)`). Derivation: smoothness ⟹ regular ⟹ DVR stalks at codim-1
717	points; the bridge from `SmoothOfRelativeDimension 1` to
718	`Scheme.IsRegularInCodimensionOne` is iter-194+ work. -/
719	instance instIsRegularInCodimOneProjectiveLineBar (kbar : Type u) [Field kbar]
720	    [IsIntegral (ProjectiveLineBar kbar).left] :
721	    Scheme.IsRegularInCodimensionOne (ProjectiveLineBar kbar).left := by
722	  -- iter-194+: derive from `SmoothOfRelativeDimension 1` on
723	  -- `(ProjectiveLineBar kbar).hom` (smooth 1-dim ⟹ DVR stalks at every
724	  -- codim-1 prime divisor, via `Algebra.smooth_iff_regular` + Krull-dim
725	  -- chase).
726	  sorry
727	
728	/-- **Hartshorne II.6.9 specialised to `D = [∞]`** — typed-sorry pin (body
729	iter-194+).
730	
731	For a smooth proper geometrically irreducible curve `C` over an algebraically
732	closed field `kbar`, equipped with a finite `k̄(ℙ¹)`-algebra structure on
733	`K(C)` (the canonical `φ`-induced one for a non-constant morphism
734	`φ : C → ℙ¹`), and a non-zero element `t ∈ K(ℙ¹)` which is a **uniformiser**
735	on `ℙ¹` (its zero divisor has degree `1`, encoded by `hLPUnif`: a unique
736	prime divisor `Y₀` of `ℙ¹` with `order Y₀ t = 1`, and no other prime
737	divisor has positive order), the degree of the positive part of the
738	principal divisor of `algebraMap K(ℙ¹) K(C) t` equals the function-field
739	extension degree:
740	\[
741	  \deg\bigl((\mathrm{div}\,(\varphi^{\#}\,t))_0\bigr)
742	  \;=\; [K(C) : k̄(\mathbb{P}^1)] \;=\;
743	   \mathrm{Module.finrank}_{k̄(\mathbb{P}^1)} K(C).
744	\]
745	Equivalently, `(div (φ^# t))_0 = φ^*[Y₀]` and `deg(φ^*[Y₀]) = deg(φ)`
746	(Hartshorne II.6.9 specialised at the divisor `[Y₀] ∈ Div(ℙ¹)`).
747	
748	**Iter-194 refactor v2** (lane-i-localparameter-signature-v2):
749	the signature now drops the abstract `K` parameter and pins to
750	`(ProjectiveLineBar kbar).left.functionField`, with the uniformiser
751	hypothesis `hLPUnif` enforcing the local-parameter constraint
752	correctly (iter-193 `hlp` was insufficient; counter-witness
753	`K=K(C), t=u(u-1)` is now excluded because `t` lives strictly in
754	`K(ℙ¹)` and `hLPUnif` requires `t` to have a single zero of order
755	`1` on ℙ¹, ruling out functions with multiple zeros). The body
756	(~50-80 LOC owed iter-194+) chains
757	`Ideal.sum_ramification_inertia` + `Ideal.finrank_quotient_map` on
758	the Dedekind extension `A → B` at the maximal ideal
759	`m_{Y₀} = (t) ⊂ A`, per `analogies/ratcurveiso-pin2.md` Decision 2.
760	
761	Blueprint reference: `lem:degree_positivePart_principal_eq_finrank`
762	(Hartshorne II.6.9, p. 137; chapter `RiemannRoch_WeilDivisor.tex` §6). -/
763	theorem degree_positivePart_principal_eq_finrank
764	    {kbar : Type u} [Field kbar] [IsAlgClosed kbar]
765	    (C : Over (Spec (.of kbar))) [IsProper C.hom]
766	    [SmoothOfRelativeDimension 1 C.hom] [GeometricallyIrreducible C.hom]
767	    [IsIntegral C.left] [IsLocallyNoetherian C.left]
768	    [Scheme.IsRegularInCodimensionOne C.left]
769	    [IsIntegral (ProjectiveLineBar kbar).left]
770	    [IsLocallyNoetherian (ProjectiveLineBar kbar).left]
771	    [Scheme.IsRegularInCodimensionOne (ProjectiveLineBar kbar).left]
772	    [Algebra (ProjectiveLineBar kbar).left.functionField
773	       C.left.functionField]
774	    [Module.Finite (ProjectiveLineBar kbar).left.functionField
775	       C.left.functionField]
776	    (t : (ProjectiveLineBar kbar).left.functionField)
777	    (halg : algebraMap (ProjectiveLineBar kbar).left.functionField
778	       C.left.functionField t ≠ 0)
779	    (hLPUnif : ∃ Y₀ : (ProjectiveLineBar kbar).left.PrimeDivisor,
780	       Scheme.RationalMap.order Y₀ t = 1 ∧
781	       ∀ Y : (ProjectiveLineBar kbar).left.PrimeDivisor,
782	         Scheme.RationalMap.order Y t > 0 → Y = Y₀) :
783	    degree (positivePart
784	      (principal (algebraMap _ C.left.functionField t) halg)) =
785	      (Module.finrank (ProjectiveLineBar kbar).left.functionField
786	                       C.left.functionField : ℤ) := by
787	  -- **Iter-194 refactor v2** (lane-i-localparameter-signature-v2):
788	  -- the signature now drops the abstract `K` parameter and pins to
789	  -- `(ProjectiveLineBar kbar).left.functionField`, with the uniformiser
790	  -- hypothesis `hLPUnif` enforcing the local-parameter constraint
791	  -- correctly (iter-193 `hlp` was insufficient; counter-witness
792	  -- `K=K(C), t=u(u-1)` is now excluded because `t` lives strictly in
793	  -- `K(ℙ¹)` and `hLPUnif` requires `t` to have a single zero of order
794	  -- 1 on ℙ¹, ruling out functions with multiple zeros).
795	  --
796	  -- **Iter-194 partial structural advance.** We unpack the uniformiser
797	  -- witness to expose `Y₀ : PrimeDivisor(ℙ¹)`, but the body's main chain
798	  -- (`Ideal.sum_ramification_inertia` + `Ideal.finrank_quotient_map` on
799	  -- the Dedekind extension `A → B` at the maximal ideal `m_Y₀ = (t) ⊂ A`)
800	  -- requires the scheme-level morphism `φ : C → ℙ¹` and affine-chart
801	  -- transfer from the function-field embedding `algebraMap`. The
802	  -- function-field-determines-curve correspondence (Hartshorne I.6.12)
803	  -- and the affine-chart pullback bridge are themselves a Mathlib-pending
804	  -- substrate (no `Scheme.Hom.ofFunctionFieldEmbedding` constructor or
805	  -- `IsLocalization.AtPrime` ↔ `presheaf.stalk` bridge ships in
806	  -- `b80f227`). The full body is therefore deferred to iter-195+, contingent
807	  -- on the affine-chart-bridge substrate landing in `AbelianVarietyRigidity`
808	  -- (Lane E) via `pullbackSpecIso`.
809	  obtain ⟨Y₀, hY₀_one, hY₀_unique⟩ := hLPUnif
810	  -- `Y₀ : PrimeDivisor(ℙ¹)` is the unique zero of `t` on ℙ¹ with order 1.
811	  -- `hY₀_one : Scheme.RationalMap.order Y₀ t = 1`
812	  -- `hY₀_unique` : every other prime divisor with positive order = Y₀.
813	  -- (Both used downstream when the affine-chart bridge lands.)
814	  sorry
815	
816	/-! ## §7. Linear equivalence and the divisor class group -/
817	
818	/-- **Linear equivalence of Weil divisors.**
819	
820	Two Weil divisors `D, D' ∈ Div(X)` are linearly equivalent, written `D ~ D'`, if
821	and only if there exists a nonzero rational function `f ∈ K(X)^×` with
822	`D - D' = div(f) ∈ Div(X)`.
823	
824	`~` is an equivalence relation (reflexivity from `div(1) = 0`, symmetry from
825	`div(f⁻¹) = -div(f)`, transitivity from `div(fg) = div(f) + div(g)`, all via
826	`thm:principal_hom`). The quotient `Cl(X) := Div(X) / im(div)` is the
827	**divisor class group** of `X`.
828	
829	On a smooth proper curve `C` over `k̄`, `thm:principal_deg_zero` shows the
830	degree map descends to `deg : Cl(C) → ℤ`.
831	
832	Blueprint reference: `def:linear_equivalence` (Hartshorne II §6 p. 131). -/
833	def LinearEquivalence [IsIntegral X] [IsLocallyNoetherian X]
834	    [Scheme.IsRegularInCodimensionOne X] (D D' : X.WeilDivisor) : Prop :=
835	  ∃ (f : X.functionField) (hf : f ≠ 0), D - D' = principal f hf
836	
837	end Scheme.WeilDivisor
838	
839	end AlgebraicGeometry

2	\chapter{Weil divisors on a smooth proper curve (RR.1)}
3	\label{chap:RiemannRoch_WeilDivisor}
4	% SOURCE: Hartshorne, \emph{Algebraic Geometry}, II \S6, pp.~130--137 +
5	% IV.1, pp.~294--296 (read from references/hartshorne-algebraic-geometry.pdf).
6	% Stacks pointers (tag-level, not verbatim-quoted): 02RW (divisors),
7	% 02ME (order at a point on a regular 1-dimensional scheme),
8	% 0BE0 (degree of a divisor on a curve), 0BE3 (principal divisors have
9	% degree zero on a complete nonsingular curve).
10	\section{Setup and motivation}
11	This chapter is the first of four sub-build chapters \texttt{RR.1}--\texttt{RR.4}
12	that close the project's headline \emph{RR bridge}
13	\[
14	  \mathtt{genusZero\_curve\_iso\_P1}
15	  \,\colon\,
16	  \text{a smooth proper geometrically irreducible curve \(C / \bar k\) with
17	  \(g(C) = 0\) is isomorphic to \(\mathbb P^1_{\bar k}\).}
18	\]
19	The bridge as proved in Hartshorne (Example~IV.1.3.5) chains together:
20	divisor of a closed point on a curve (\texttt{RR.1}, this chapter); the
21	Riemann--Roch dimension formula \(\ell(D) = \deg D + 1\) for \(\deg D \geq 0\) on
22	a genus-\(0\) curve (\texttt{RR.2}); the global-sections argument identifying
23	\(\dim_k H^0(C, \mathcal O_C(P)) = 2\) via \(1\) and a non-constant function
24	(\texttt{RR.3}); the ``rational curve \(\Rightarrow \cong \mathbb P^1\)''
25	classification recovered as a degree-\(1\) morphism into \(\mathbb P^1\) via
26	\texttt{Proj.fromOfGlobalSections} (\texttt{RR.4}).
27	Mathlib ships no \texttt{WeilDivisor} on a scheme. Adjacent pieces exist, but
28	none of them is the data structure we need: \texttt{MeromorphicOn.divisor}
29	(\texttt{Mathlib.Analysis.Meromorphic.Divisor}) is the order function of a
30	meromorphic function valued in a \emph{normed} field, not a formal sum of
31	codimension-\(1\) cycles on a scheme; \texttt{CommRing.Pic}
32	(\texttt{Mathlib.RingTheory.PicardGroup}) is the ring-level Picard group of
33	invertible modules under tensor product, with no scheme-level analogue;
34	\texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.RationalMap}
35	(\texttt{Mathlib.AlgebraicGeometry.Birational.RationalMap}) packages rational
36	maps but not divisors. Consequently this chapter, and the three siblings, are
37	\emph{project-bespoke}: the only Mathlib tools they rely on are
38	discrete-valuation rings, the function field, and the ambient scheme API.
39	The audit grounding this scope decision is in
40	\texttt{analogies/rrbridge-survey.md}.
41	This chapter establishes the formal-sum data type
42	\(\mathrm{Div}(X) = \bigoplus_{\substack{Z \subset X \\ \mathrm{codim} 1}} \mathbb Z\) on a
43	Noetherian integral scheme \(X\) satisfying Hartshorne's condition \((*)\), the
44	principal-divisor homomorphism \(\mathrm{div} \colon K(X)^{\times} \to \mathrm{Div}(X)\) on a
45	curve, the degree map \(\deg \colon \mathrm{Div}(C) \to \mathbb Z\) on a smooth proper
46	curve over an algebraically closed field, and the linear-equivalence
47	relation. The \emph{degree-zero of a principal divisor} statement
48	(\ref{thm:principal_deg_zero}) is included for completeness; its proof
49	decomposes into a separate sub-build inside \texttt{RR.1} (it uses the
50	auxiliary lemma that any non-constant rational function on \(C\) defines a
51	finite morphism \(C \to \mathbb P^1\)). The line-bundle construction $\mathcal
52	O_C(D)\( and the associated cohomology computation \)H^0(C, \mathcal O_C(D))$
53	live in the sibling chapter \texttt{RR.3}; the Riemann--Roch dimension
54	formula lives in \texttt{RR.2}; the genus-\(0\) classification proper lives in
55	\texttt{RR.4}.
56	Throughout this chapter, the standing convention is Hartshorne's condition
57	\((*)\):
58	% SOURCE QUOTE: "\((\ast)\) \(X\) is a noetherian integral separated scheme which
59	% is regular in codimension one."
60	\textit{Source: Hartshorne, II \S6, p.~130, the conditional \((*)\).}
61	\[
62	  (\ast)\qquad
63	  X \text{ is a noetherian integral separated scheme which is regular in
64	  codimension one.}
65	\]
66	For the degree map and for principal-divisor statements on a curve, we
67	additionally assume \(X\) is a complete (i.e.\ proper) nonsingular curve over
68	an algebraically closed field \(\bar k\), where Hartshorne's condition \((*)\) is
69	automatic.
70	\paragraph{Standing hypothesis \((*)\) in the Lean encoding (iter-173 pin).}
71	Mathlib (as of \texttt{b80f227}) does not bundle Hartshorne's four-clause
72	\((\ast)\) as a single typeclass. The chapter pins \((*)\) to a \emph{layered}
73	typeclass set that the prover-agent threads through the Lean signatures in
74	\texttt{AlgebraicJacobian/RiemannRoch/WeilDivisor.lean}:
75	\begin{itemize}
76	  \item \textbf{Basic formal-sum layer} (carrier of \ref{def:prime_divisor} and
77	    \ref{def:codim1_cycles}, the additive group \(\mathrm{Div}(X)\), the degree
78	    \ref{def:divisor_degree}, the degree-homomorphism
79	    \ref{thm:divisor_degree_hom}, and \ref{def:divisor_closed_point}): the
80	    only Mathlib typeclass invoked is \texttt{[IsIntegral X]}
81	    (\texttt{Mathlib.AlgebraicGeometry.AffineScheme} via
82	    \texttt{Mathlib.AlgebraicGeometry.Properties}). Integrality of \(X\) already
83	    delivers \texttt{IrreducibleSpace X.carrier} (the carrier-level instance
84	    chain), so \(X\) has a unique generic point and the function field
85	    \texttt{X.functionField} (\texttt{Mathlib.AlgebraicGeometry.FunctionField})
86	    is a field; separatedness and noetherianness are \emph{not} required for
87	    the bare formal-sum data type.
88	  \item \textbf{Order/principal layer} (\ref{def:order_at_point},
89	    \ref{def:principal_divisor}, \ref{thm:principal_hom}): the Lean
90	    signature additionally threads
91	    \texttt{[IsLocallyNoetherian X]}
92	    (\texttt{Mathlib.AlgebraicGeometry.Noetherian}) and Hartshorne's
93	    regular-in-codimension-one clause. The latter has \emph{no} Mathlib
94	    typeclass shipping it directly; the chapter's pin is the project-side
95	    predicate ``for every \(x \in X\) with \(\mathrm{codim}_X \overline{\{x\}} = 1\) the
96	    stalk \(\mathcal O_{X,x}\) is a discrete valuation ring'', encodable as
97	    the conjunction
98	    $\forall x,\; \texttt{Order.coheight}\,x = 1 \Rightarrow
99	    \texttt{IsDiscreteValuationRing}\,(\mathcal O_{X,x})$. Iter-173+ may
100	    introduce a \texttt{Scheme.IsRegularInCodimensionOne} predicate to
101	    abbreviate this; until then the order/principal lemmas thread the DVR
102	    hypothesis explicitly at each codim-1 point.
103	  \item \textbf{Curve layer} (\ref{thm:principal_deg_zero}): the standing
104	    hypothesis tightens to a smooth proper geometrically irreducible curve
105	    over \(\bar k\) with explicit integrality witness, namely
106	    \begin{quote}
107	      \texttt{\{kbar : Type u\} [Field kbar] [IsAlgClosed kbar]\\
108	       (C : Over (Spec (.of kbar))) [IsProper C.hom]\\
109	       \phantom{}\quad[SmoothOfRelativeDimension 1 C.hom]\\
110	       \phantom{}\quad[GeometricallyIrreducible C.hom] [IsIntegral C.left]}
111	    \end{quote}
112	    matching the standing typeclass set used by the rest of the project's
113	    curve API (\texttt{AlgebraicJacobian.Genus},
114	    \texttt{AlgebraicJacobian.AbelianVarietyRigidity}). Hartshorne's \((*)\) is
115	    automatic for this layer (\texttt{[IsLocallyNoetherian]} from finite type
116	    over a field; regular-in-codim-1 from smoothness; separatedness from
117	    properness; integrality threaded).
118	\end{itemize}
119	\noindent
120	The \texttt{[IsIntegral X]} hypothesis is the only one threaded through the
121	\emph{statement} of every Lean signature in
122	\texttt{AlgebraicJacobian/RiemannRoch/WeilDivisor.lean} that mentions a
123	function field, principal divisor, or order. The noetherian-and-DVR
124	content lives inside the proof bodies (sub-build), where the prover threads
125	the appropriate stronger hypothesis at the use site.
126	\section{Codim-\(1\) cycle group}
127	\begin{definition}
128	\leanok
129	[Prime divisor of a scheme]
130	  \label{def:prime_divisor}
131	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.PrimeDivisor}
132	  % SOURCE: [Hartshorne], II.6, p.~130 (definition of "prime divisor" under
133	  % condition \((*)\)) (read from references/hartshorne-algebraic-geometry.pdf,
134	  % PDF page 147). Stacks pointer: tag 02RW (divisors).
135	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Definition. Let \(X\) satisfy \((\ast)\). A \emph{prime
136	  % divisor} on \(X\) is a closed integral subscheme \(Y\) of codimension one."
137	  \textit{Source: Hartshorne, II.6, p.~130 (definition of prime divisor).}
138	  Let \(X\) satisfy \((*)\). A \emph{prime divisor} on \(X\) is a closed integral
139	  subscheme \(Y \subset X\) of codimension one. Equivalently, by the standard
140	  generic-point correspondence (Hartshorne II \S6, p.~130, ``let $\eta \in
141	  Y\( be its generic point\ldots''), \)Y$ is determined by its generic point
142	  \(\eta \in X\): closed integral subschemes of codimension one of \(X\) are in
143	  natural bijection with points \(x \in X\) such that the closure
144	  \(\overline{\{x\}} \subset X\) has codimension one. The closure
145	  \(\overline{\{x\}}\), equipped with the \emph{reduced induced subscheme
146	  structure}, is automatically integral (irreducible, because the closure
147	  of a single point is irreducible, and reduced by construction); the only
148	  non-automatic condition is the codimension.
149	  \paragraph{Lean encoding (iter-173 pin).} The Lean structure
150	  \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.PrimeDivisor} bundles a point
151	  \texttt{point : X} together with a codimension-one witness, encoded via
152	  the Mathlib predicate
153	  \[
154	    \texttt{Order.coheight\ point\ =\ 1}
155	  \]
156	  on the specialisation preorder of the topological space underlying \(X\).
157	  Concretely, Mathlib's preorder on \texttt{X.carrier} is defined by
158	  ``\(x \leq y \iff y \rightsquigarrow x\)'' (i.e.\ \(x \in \overline{\{y\}}\);
159	  see \texttt{Mathlib.Topology.Specialization} and
160	  \texttt{Mathlib.Order.KrullDimension}). Under this convention the generic
161	  point of an integral scheme is the unique \emph{maximal} element of the
162	  preorder, so for the generic point \(\eta_Y\) of a prime divisor \(Y\) the
163	  only point strictly above \(\eta_Y\) is the generic point \(\eta_X\) of \(X\),
164	  and the chain \(\eta_Y < \eta_X\) has length one, giving
165	  \texttt{Order.coheight\ \(\eta_Y\)\ =\ 1}. The DVR/integrality content of
166	  ``the closure of \texttt{point} with the reduced subscheme structure is
167	  integral'' is automatic from \(\overline{\{\,\texttt{point}\,\}}\) being
168	  irreducible, so the Lean structure does not need a separate integrality
169	  field. The iter-172 placeholder \texttt{isCodim1AndIntegral : True} in
170	  \texttt{AlgebraicJacobian/RiemannRoch/WeilDivisor.lean:84--90} is to be
171	  refined to \texttt{coheight : Order.coheight point = 1} (named field, type
172	  \texttt{Prop}) in the iter-173 prover step, retaining the same
173	  \texttt{point} field for downstream defeq.
174	  \paragraph{Reference predicates considered and rejected.}
175	  Two alternatives were considered and rejected at the iter-172
176	  lean-vs-blueprint-checker stage:
177	  \begin{itemize}
178	    \item \texttt{IsIntegral\ (X.closedSubschemeOfPoint\ x)}: rejected
179	      because Mathlib (\texttt{b80f227}) ships \emph{no}
180	      \texttt{closedSubschemeOfPoint} constructor. The closest available
181	      object is \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.IdealSheafData}
182	      (\texttt{Mathlib.AlgebraicGeometry.IdealSheaf.Basic}), which packages
183	      a quasi-coherent ideal sheaf but does not produce a reduced induced
184	      subscheme on the closure of a point. Building that constructor is
185	      itself a Mathlib-upstream-scale project (Stacks tag 01J9 ``reduced
186	      induced scheme structure''), outside the scope of \texttt{RR.1}.
187	    \item A bundled \texttt{Scheme.IdealSheafData}-based prime-divisor
188	      carrier: rejected because the chapter only needs the generic-point
189	      witness for downstream order/principal-divisor consumption; carrying
190	      an \texttt{IdealSheafData} adds redundant data the chapter never
191	      uses.
192	  \end{itemize}
193	  The \texttt{Order.coheight}-based encoding is therefore the
194	  minimum-Mathlib-dependence pin compatible with the chapter's downstream
195	  needs.
196	\end{definition}
197	\begin{definition}
198	\leanok
199	[Codim-\(1\) cycle group / Weil divisor group of a scheme]
200	  \label{def:codim1_cycles}
201	  
202	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.WeilDivisor}
203	  \uses{def:prime_divisor}
204	  % SOURCE: [Hartshorne], II.6, p.~130 (definition of "Weil divisor" under
205	  % condition \((*)\)) (read from references/hartshorne-algebraic-geometry.pdf,
206	  % PDF page 147). Stacks pointer: tag 02RW.
207	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "A \emph{Weil divisor} is an element of the free abelian
208	  % group \(\operatorname{Div} X\) generated by the prime divisors. We write a
209	  % divisor as \(D = \sum n_i Y_i\), where the \(Y_i\) are prime divisors, the
210	  % \(n_i\) are integers, and only finitely many \(n_i\) are different from
211	  % zero. If all the \(n_i \geq 0\), we say that \(D\) is \emph{effective}."
212	  \textit{Source: Hartshorne, II.6, p.~130 (Weil divisor group).}
213	  Let \(X\) be a Noetherian integral separated scheme that is regular in
214	  codimension one (i.e.\ \(X\) satisfies \((*)\)). The \emph{Weil divisor group}
215	  \[
216	    \mathrm{Div}(X) \;=\; \bigoplus_{\substack{Y \subset X \\ \text{prime divisor}}}
217	    \mathbb Z \cdot Y
218	  \]
219	  is the free abelian group on the set of prime divisors of \(X\)
220	  (\ref{def:codim1_cycles}); an element \(D = \sum n_i Y_i\) with finitely
221	  many nonzero coefficients \(n_i \in \mathbb Z\) is called a \emph{Weil
222	  divisor}, and is \emph{effective} when all \(n_i \geq 0\).
223	  \paragraph{Lean signature scope.} The Lean definition
224	  \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.WeilDivisor X} is given for an arbitrary
225	  scheme \texttt{X : Scheme} as the formal-sum data type
226	  \texttt{X.PrimeDivisor \(\to_0\) \(\mathbb Z\)}; integrality of \(X\) is
227	  \emph{not} required to form the additive group \(\mathrm{Div}(X)\) (it becomes
228	  necessary only at the order/principal-divisor layer where the function
229	  field appears). Hartshorne's \((*)\) is the standing convention in the
230	  prose but is not threaded into the typeclass arguments of this base
231	  declaration. See ``Standing hypothesis \((*)\) in the Lean encoding'' above
232	  for the per-layer typeclass discipline.
233	\end{definition}
234	\section{Order of a rational function at a prime divisor}
235	\begin{definition}
236	\leanok
237	[Order of a rational function at a prime divisor]
238	  \label{def:order_at_point}
239	  
240	  \uses{def:codim1_cycles}
241	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.RationalMap.order}
242	  % SOURCE: [Hartshorne], II.6, p.~130--131 (valuation \(v_Y\) of a prime
243	  % divisor; "zero/pole of \(f\) along \(Y\)") (read from
244	  % references/hartshorne-algebraic-geometry.pdf, PDF pages 147--148).
245	  % Stacks pointer: tag 02ME.
246	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "If \(Y\) is a prime divisor on \(X\), let \(\eta \in Y\) be its
247	  % generic point. Then the local ring \(\mathcal O_{\eta, X}\) of \(X\) at
248	  % \(\eta\) is a discrete valuation ring with quotient field \(K\), the
249	  % function field of \(X\). We call the corresponding discrete valuation
250	  % \(v_Y\) the \emph{valuation of \(Y\)}."
251	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Now let \(f \in K^\ast\) be any nonzero rational function
252	  % on \(X\). Then \(v_Y(f)\) is an integer. If it is positive, we say \(f\) has
253	  % a \emph{zero} along \(Y\), of that order; if it is negative, we say \(f\)
254	  % has a \emph{pole} along \(Y\), of order \(-v_Y(f)\)."
255	  \textit{Source: Hartshorne, II.6, pp.~130--131 (valuation \(v_Y\)).}
256	  Let \(X\) satisfy \((*)\). For a prime divisor \(Y \subset X\) with generic
257	  point \(\eta \in Y\), the local ring \(\mathcal O_{X, \eta}\) is a discrete
258	  valuation ring (by regularity in codimension one) with quotient field the
259	  function field \(K(X)\). For a nonzero rational function \(f \in K(X)^{\times}\)
260	  the \emph{order of \(f\) along \(Y\)} is the integer
261	  \[
262	    \ord_Y(f) \;:=\; v_Y(f) \;\in\; \mathbb Z,
263	  \]
264	  where \(v_Y\) is the normalised discrete valuation associated to
265	  \(\mathcal O_{X, \eta}\). If \(\ord_Y(f) > 0\), \(f\) has a \emph{zero of
266	  order \(\ord_Y(f)\) along \(Y\)}; if \(\ord_Y(f) < 0\), \(f\) has a \emph{pole
267	  of order \(-\ord_Y(f)\) along \(Y\)}.
268	  Specialisation to a curve. If \(C\) is a smooth proper curve over \(\bar k\)
269	  and \(P \in C\) is a closed point, then \(P\) is automatically a prime
270	  divisor (it is closed, integral, of codimension one in the \(1\)-dimensional
271	  scheme \(C\)); the local ring \(\mathcal O_{C, P}\) is a discrete valuation
272	  ring by smoothness, and \(\ord_P(f) = v_P(f)\) is the standard DVR
273	  valuation of \(f\) at \(P\).
274	  \paragraph{Lean signature scope (iter-175 pin, per analogist
275	  \texttt{dvr-rationalmap-order}).} The iter-175 Lean signature is
276	  \begin{quote}
277	    \texttt{noncomputable def order \{X : Scheme.\{u\}\} [IsIntegral X]\\
278	    \phantom{}\quad[IsLocallyNoetherian X] (Y : X.PrimeDivisor)\\
279	    \phantom{}\quad[Ring.KrullDimLE 1 (X.presheaf.stalk Y.point)]\\
280	    \phantom{}\quad(f : X.functionField) : \(\mathbb Z\)}
281	  \end{quote}
282	  with \emph{no} \(f \neq 0\) hypothesis (junk-on-\(f = 0\) convention,
283	  see below). The body is
284	  \[
285	    \texttt{order}\ Y\ f \;:=\;
286	      \texttt{WithZero.log}\ \bigl(
287	        \texttt{Ring.ordFrac}\ (X.\texttt{presheaf.stalk}\ Y.\texttt{point})\ f
288	      \bigr).
289	  \]
290	  \emph{Mathlib API path.}
291	  \begin{itemize}
292	    \item \texttt{Ring.ordFrac R : K \(\to\!\!*_0\) \(\mathbb Z^{m_0}\)}
293	      (with \(\mathbb Z^{m_0} = \texttt{WithZero (Multiplicative}\ \mathbb Z)\)),
294	      from \texttt{Mathlib.RingTheory.OrderOfVanishing.Basic} (Stacks
295	      tag~\texttt{02MD}), is Mathlib's canonical monoid-with-zero hom
296	      extending the length-of-quotient valuation
297	      \texttt{Ring.ord} from a Noetherian Krull-dim-\(\leq 1\) ring to
298	      its fraction field via \texttt{Submonoid.LocalizationMap.lift\textsubscript{0}}.
299	      For a DVR-uniformised local ring its value on \(r \in R\) is the
300	      length of \(R/(r)\) (the DVR valuation); on \(a/b \in K\), it is
301	      \(\texttt{ord}(a) - \texttt{ord}(b)\). The hypotheses
302	      \texttt{[CommRing R] [Nontrivial R] [IsNoetherianRing R]\\
303	      [Ring.KrullDimLE 1 R]} are exactly the typeclasses the body
304	      synthesises from \([\texttt{IsIntegral}\ X]\) (delivers
305	      \texttt{IsDomain} hence \texttt{Nontrivial} on the stalk via
306	      \texttt{instIsDomainCarrierStalkCommRingCatPresheafOfIsIntegral},
307	      and \texttt{IsFractionRing (X.presheaf.stalk Y.point)
308	      X.functionField} via
309	      \texttt{instIsFractionRingCarrierStalkCommRingCatPresheafFunctionField}),
310	      from \([\texttt{IsLocallyNoetherian}\ X]\) (delivers
311	      \texttt{IsNoetherianRing} on the stalk via
312	      \texttt{instIsNoetherianRingCarrierStalkCommRingCatPresheafOfIsLocallyNoetherian}),
313	      and from the explicitly threaded
314	      \texttt{[Ring.KrullDimLE 1 (X.presheaf.stalk Y.point)]}.
315	    \item \texttt{WithZero.log : \(\mathbb Z^{m_0} \to \mathbb Z\)}
316	      from \texttt{Mathlib.Algebra.GroupWithZero.WithZero} is Mathlib's
317	      canonical projection to the additive value group with
318	      junk-on-zero: \texttt{log\_zero}: \(\log 0 = 0\);
319	      \texttt{log\_mul}: \(\log(x \cdot y) = \log x + \log y\) for
320	      \(x, y \neq 0\). This makes the downstream additive identities
321	      \(v_Y(fg) = v_Y(f) + v_Y(g)\), \(v_Y(f^{-1}) = -v_Y(f)\),
322	      \(v_Y(1) = 0\) available as named lemmas for
323	      \ref{def:order_at_point}.
324	  \end{itemize}
325	  \emph{Junk-on-\(f = 0\) convention.} Setting \(f = 0\) in
326	  \texttt{X.functionField} feeds \(0\) into
327	  \texttt{Ring.ordFrac (X.presheaf.stalk Y.point)} (zero of the
328	  monoid-with-zero hom; \texttt{Ring.ordFrac} maps \(0\) to
329	  \(0 \in \mathbb Z^{m_0}\)); then \texttt{WithZero.log 0 = 0} so
330	  \(\texttt{order}\ Y\ 0 = 0\). This is the canonical Mathlib junk
331	  choice; no separate \(f \neq 0\) hypothesis is threaded on the
332	  signature.
333	  \emph{In-tree gap-fill (project-side; Mathlib upstream opportunity).}
334	  Mathlib (as of \texttt{b80f227}) has no direct bridge from the
335	  topological condition \(\texttt{Order.coheight}\ Y.\texttt{point} = 1\)
336	  on \texttt{X.carrier} to the algebraic condition
337	  \(\texttt{Ring.KrullDimLE 1 (X.presheaf.stalk Y.point)}\) on the
338	  stalk. The missing fact (Stacks tag~\texttt{02IZ} / \texttt{005X}) is
339	  the topological identity ``coheight of \(x\) in the scheme's
340	  specialisation preorder equals the height of the prime ideal
341	  corresponding to \(x\) in any affine chart''; the algebraic step
342	  ``ideal height \(=\) stalk Krull dim'' is in Mathlib as
343	  \texttt{IsLocalization.AtPrime.ringKrullDim\_eq\_height}
344	  (\texttt{Mathlib.RingTheory.Ideal.Height}) and
345	  \texttt{IsAffineOpen.isLocalization\_stalk}
346	  (\texttt{Mathlib.AlgebraicGeometry.AffineScheme}). The iter-175
347	  workaround is to thread the explicit instance
348	  \texttt{[Ring.KrullDimLE 1 (X.presheaf.stalk Y.point)]} on
349	  \texttt{order} (and downstream consumers); a future two-line
350	  Mathlib upstream PR pinning the coheight-height bridge would let
351	  the project drop the explicit argument in favour of synthesis from
352	  \texttt{coheight Y.point = 1} via the \texttt{PrimeDivisor} carrier.
353	\end{definition}
354	\section{Divisor of a closed point on a curve}
355	\begin{definition}
356	\leanok
357	[Divisor of a closed point on a curve]
358	  \label{def:divisor_closed_point}
359	  \uses{def:codim1_cycles}
360	  
361	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.WeilDivisor.ofClosedPoint}
362	  % SOURCE: [Hartshorne], II.6 ("Divisors on Curves"), p.~137 (read from
363	  % references/hartshorne-algebraic-geometry.pdf, PDF page 154):
364	  % "A prime divisor is just a closed point, so an arbitrary divisor can
365	  % be written \(D = \sum n_i P_i\), where the \(P_i\) are closed points, and
366	  % \(n_i \in \mathbf Z\)."
367	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Now we come to the study of divisors on curves. If \(X\)
368	  % is a nonsingular curve, then \(X\) satisfies the condition \((\ast)\) used
369	  % above, so we can talk about divisors on \(X\). A prime divisor is just
370	  % a closed point, so an arbitrary divisor can be written $D = \sum n_i
371	  % P_i\(, where the \)P_i\( are closed points, and \)n_i \in \mathbf Z$."
372	  \textit{Source: Hartshorne, II.6, p.~137 (``Divisors on Curves'').}
373	  Let \(C\) be a smooth proper curve over a field \(k\) and let \(P \in C\) be a
374	  closed point. The \emph{Weil divisor associated to \(P\)} is the element
375	  \[
376	    [P] \;:=\; 1 \cdot P \;\in\; \mathrm{Div}(C)
377	  \]
378	  i.e.\ the prime divisor \(P\) with multiplicity one. The assignment is
379	  well-defined because the closed point \(P\) is automatically a codimension-\(1\)
380	  integral closed subscheme of the \(1\)-dimensional integral scheme \(C\).
381	  Conversely, every prime divisor on \(C\) is of this form, so an arbitrary
382	  divisor on \(C\) is a finite formal \(\mathbb Z\)-linear combination
383	  \(\sum n_i [P_i]\) of closed points.
384	  \paragraph{Lean signature scope (iter-174 pin).} The Lean signature
385	  \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.WeilDivisor.ofClosedPoint} accepts a
386	  scheme \texttt{C : Scheme.\{u\}}, a point \texttt{P : C}, and a
387	  hypothesis \texttt{(\_hP : IsClosed (\{P\} : Set C))}. It does
388	  \emph{not} typeclass-pin ``smooth proper curve over \(\bar k\)'' at the
389	  carrier level, and it does \emph{not} thread the
390	  one-dimensional-integral-scheme content into the declaration head.
391	  Instead the iter-174 encoding is \emph{junk-defined} outside the
392	  codim-\(1\) regime:
393	  \[
394	    \texttt{ofClosedPoint}\ P\ \_\!hP\ :=\
395	      \begin{cases}
396	        \texttt{Finsupp.single}\ \langle P, h\rangle\ 1 &
397	          \text{if } h : \texttt{Order.coheight}\ P = 1,\\
398	        0 & \text{otherwise.}
399	      \end{cases}
400	  \]
401	  On the off-branch --- where \texttt{P} is not a codimension-one point
402	  (e.g.\ \texttt{P} is the generic point of \texttt{C}, or a closed point
403	  of codimension \(\geq 2\) on a higher-dimensional scheme) ---
404	  \texttt{ofClosedPoint} returns the zero divisor \(0 \in \mathrm{Div}(C)\). This
405	  convention lets the Lean signature avoid threading
406	  \texttt{[OneDimensional C]} / \texttt{[IsIntegral C]} typeclass payload
407	  at the declaration head; the well-definedness consumer must instead
408	  invoke the bridge equation lemmas
409	  \ref{lem:ofClosedPoint_eq_single} and \ref{lem:ofClosedPoint_eq_zero}
410	  to expose the two branches at the point of use.
411	  On the intended regime (one-dimensional integral scheme \(C\), \(P\) a
